topics.title: Molecular Biology Primer: Genes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Molecular Biology Primer: Genes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of a Gene

A **gene** is the fundamental physical and functional unit of heredity, composed of a specific sequence of deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) nucleotides that encodes the information required to produce a functional product, typically a protein or a functional ribonucleic acid (RNA) molecule. In molecular biology, a gene is operationally defined as the entire DNA sequence — including the coding region (exons), non-coding intervening regions (introns), untranslated regions (UTRs), promoter, and terminator sequences — necessary for the synthesis of a functional gene product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (CO1 aligned):** A gene is a discrete, well-defined locus on a chromosome that carries the hereditary information encoded in a specific linear sequence of nucleotide bases (Adenine, Thymine, Guanine, Cytosine) and is transcribed into RNA, which may subsequently be translated into a polypeptide chain.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Instruction Manual" View

Think of a **cell** as a **large industrial factory** and the **genome** as the **complete master instruction manual** stored safely in the **library (the nucleus)**. 

- A single **gene** is **one specific page** of that master manual — say, the page that gives step-by-step instructions to build a single "machine" (a protein like hemoglobin or insulin). 
- The cell doesn't take the master manual out of the library; instead, it **photocopies the page** (transcription → mRNA) and sends the copy to the **workshop (the ribosome)** where the machine is actually assembled (translation). 
- The original manual page is never altered, ensuring genetic information is preserved.
- Multiple genes (pages) are stitched together into **chromosomes** (chapters of the manual), and the entire set of chapters is the **genome** (the complete manual).

> [!NOTE]
> This analogy directly maps to the **Central Dogma of Molecular Biology**: DNA → RNA → Protein.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Gene Biology

The following constants and metrics are universally applied in bioinformatics analyses of genes:

- **Length of one DNA base pair:** $\mathbf{3.4\ \text{\AA} = 0.34\ \text{nm}}$ (along the helical axis).
- **Helical pitch per turn:** $\mathbf{10.5\ \text{bps per turn} = 34\ \text{\AA}}$.
- **Helix diameter:** $\mathbf{20\ \text{\AA} = 2\ \text{nm}}$.
- **Average molecular weight of one base pair (dsDNA):** $\mathbf{\approx 660\ \text{Da}}$ (sodium salt).
- **Average molecular weight of one amino acid residue:** $\mathbf{\approx 110\ \text{Da}}$.
- **Total number of protein-coding genes in the human genome:** $\mathbf{\approx 20{,}000\text{–}25{,}000}$.
- **Human haploid genome size:** $\mathbf{\approx 3.2 \times 10^9\ \text{bp}}$ ($\mathbf{3.2\ \text{Gb}}$).
- **Chargaff's Rule constants:** $\mathbf{\%A = \%T}$ and $\mathbf{\%G = \%C}$, hence $\mathbf{\%(A+T) + \%(G+C) = 100\%}$.

## 1.4 The Central Dogma — Operative Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Central Dogma (Crick, 1958, 1970):** Genetic information flows unidirectionally in normal cells from **DNA $\rightarrow$ DNA** (replication), **DNA $\rightarrow$ RNA** (transcription), and **RNA $\rightarrow$ Protein** (translation). Reverse flow (RNA $\rightarrow$ DNA) occurs in retroviruses via reverse transcriptase, and RNA $\rightarrow$ RNA replication occurs in some RNA viruses via RNA-dependent RNA polymerase.

This unidirectional flow is the **operative principle** behind every bioinformatics pipeline — sequence alignment, gene prediction, gene expression analysis, and protein modeling all ultimately derive meaning from the Central Dogma.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Anatomy of a Eukaryotic Gene

A typical eukaryotic protein-coding gene is partitioned into several functionally distinct **cis-acting** (same-molecule acting) elements. The logic behind each component:

- **5' Upstream Regulatory Region:** Located upstream of the transcription start site (TSS); contains the **promoter** (e.g., TATA box: consensus `TATAAA` at $\approx -25$ to $-30$ bp), **enhancers** (can act at a distance, up to $\sim$100 kb), and **silencers** (repress transcription).
  - *Why:* Recruits and positions RNA polymerase II along with general transcription factors (GTFs) to initiate transcription at the correct nucleotide.
- **5' Untranslated Region (5' UTR):** Spans from the TSS to the start codon (AUG). Contains the **Kozak sequence** (`gccRccAUGG`, where R = purine) which facilitates ribosome scanning.
  - *Why:* Ensures efficient and accurate translation initiation.
- **Coding Region (CDS):** Begins at the **start codon (AUG)** and ends at a **stop codon** (UAA, UAG, or UGA). Composed of alternating **exons** (expressed sequences, retained in mature mRNA) and **introns** (intervening sequences, spliced out).
  - *Why:* Exons contain the triplet codon information for protein synthesis; introns enable alternative splicing, increasing proteome diversity from a single gene.
- **3' Untranslated Region (3' UTR):** Contains the **polyadenylation signal** (`AAUAAA` in RNA) located $\sim$10–30 nt upstream of the cleavage site. After cleavage, a **poly-A tail** is added.
  - *Why:* Enhances mRNA stability, nuclear export, and translation efficiency.
- **3' Downstream Region:** Often contains **terminator sequences** that cause RNA polymerase II to dissociate.

> [!NOTE]
> **Prokaryotic genes** are typically **intron-less (operon-organized)**, with polycistronic mRNA encoding multiple proteins from a single transcript, whereas **eukaryotic genes** are almost always **monocistronic** with intron-exon architecture.

## 2.2 The Genetic Code — The Core Translation Logic

The genetic code maps each of the $\mathbf{4^3 = 64}$ possible three-nucleotide codons to one of 20 standard amino acids (or to a stop signal). Key logical properties:

- **Triplet & Non-overlapping:** Three consecutive bases specify one amino acid; the reading frame is fixed once initiated.
- **Degenerate (Redundant):** 61 sense codons encode 20 amino acids, so most amino acids are encoded by multiple codons (e.g., Leucine has 6 codons, Methionine has 1).
- **Unambiguous:** Each codon specifies only one amino acid.
- **Universal (with minor exceptions):** The code is conserved across all organisms, with rare deviations in mitochondria and some protozoa.
- **Start & Stop:** AUG = Methionine (start); UAA, UAG, UGA = stop (termination).

The **wobble hypothesis (Crick, 1966)** explains degeneracy: the **third base (3' end) of the codon** has flexible base-pairing with the **first base (5' end) of the anticodon**, allowing a single tRNA to recognize multiple synonymous codons.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Equations

| # | Formula / Identity | Description / Engineering Utility | Units / Notes |
|---|--------------------|------------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $\text{GC\%} = \dfrac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100$ | GC content determines DNA stability, melting temperature (Tm), and isoschizomer selection in PCR. | Dimensionless % |
| 2 | $\text{MW}_{\text{dsDNA}} = (n \text{ bp}) \times 660\ \text{Da/bp}$ | Estimating plasmid/insert size in cloning; verifying gel band identity. | Daltons (Da) |
| 3 | $\text{MW}_{\text{ssDNA}} = (N - 1) \times 330.2 + 339.0$ | For an oligonucleotide of length N bases; useful in primer design. | Daltons (Da) |
| 4 | $T_m = 64.9 + 41 \cdot \dfrac{(G+C - 16.4)}{N}$ | Wallace rule for short oligos ($N \leq 25$); used in PCR primer design. | °C |
| 5 | $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6 \log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41 \cdot \%GC - \dfrac{675}{N}$ | Marmur-Doty / modified formula for longer DNA ($N > 25$). | °C |
| 6 | $\text{Codon count} = 4^3 = 64$ | Total sense + stop codons in the standard genetic code. | Integer |
| 7 | $\text{Amino acid coding capacity} = 64 - 3_{\text{stop}} = 61$ | Number of sense codons. | Integer |
| 8 | $\text{Number of proteins} = 20$ | Standard amino acids encoded. | Integer |
| 9 | $\text{Number of open reading frames} = 6$ | Three forward + three reverse reading frames on dsDNA. | Integer |
| 10 | $\text{Helix pitch} = n_{\text{bp/turn}} \times h_{\text{rise}} = 10.5 \times 3.4\ \text{\AA} = 35.7\ \text{\AA}$ | Spatial parameter of the B-form DNA double helix. | Å or nm |
| 11 | $\text{Concentration (μg/mL)} = A_{260} \times \text{dilution factor} \times 50$ | For dsDNA; 50 = extinction coefficient (1 AU$_{260}$ = 50 μg/mL). | μg/mL |
| 12 | $\text{No. of possible proteins of length } L = 20^L$ | Demonstrates combinatorial coding capacity. | Integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The GC content and $T_m$ formulas (4, 5) are **routinely used by every PCR primer design tool (Primer3, OligoAnalyzer, IDT OligoEvaluator)** — a direct bioinformatics-engineering interface.

## 2.4 Why This Theory Matters in Bioinformatics

Every downstream bioinformatics operation — from **BLAST sequence alignment** to **gene prediction (GENSCAN, AUGUSTUS)** to **phylogenetic tree construction** — depends on:

1. Understanding that genes are **linear strings over the alphabet $\{A, T/U, G, C\}$**.
2. Knowing the **genetic code** to translate DNA → protein sequences (e.g., `EMBOSS transeq`, `Biopython Bio.Seq.translate`).
3. Using **GC content, $T_m$, and ORF length statistics** to score candidate genes against background genomic sequence.
4. Recognizing **promoter/exon/intron boundaries** to model gene structure computationally.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Number of Possible Codons (Combinatorial Foundation)

The genetic code uses **triplets** of **4 nucleotide bases** $\in \{A, T/U, G, C\}$. Each position in a triplet is independent and can be filled with any of the 4 bases. The total number of ordered triplets (codons) is computed using the **multiplication rule of counting**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{codons}} &= \underbrace{4}_{\text{position 1}} \times \underbrace{4}_{\text{position 2}} \times \underbrace{4}_{\text{position 3}} \\
&= 4^3 \\
&= 64
\end{aligned}
$$

*Logic:* Position 1 has 4 choices, position 2 has 4 independent choices, position 3 has 4 independent choices, so the joint count is the product. This is a direct application of the **Cartesian product** for sequences of length $k$ over an alphabet of size $n$: $n^k$.

**Out of these 64 codons**, 3 are stop signals (UAA, UAG, UGA). Hence, the number of amino acid-encoding (sense) codons is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{sense}} &= 64 - 3_{\text{stop}} \\
&= 61
\end{aligned}
$$

Since there are only 20 standard amino acids to be encoded, the average redundancy per amino acid is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{\text{avg}} &= \dfrac{N_{\text{sense}}}{N_{\text{aa}}} = \dfrac{61}{20} \approx 3.05\ \text{codons/aa}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **fundamental combinatorial reason for codon degeneracy**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: GC Content & Chargaff's Rule Application

Consider a linear dsDNA fragment of length $L$ bp with the following base composition: $A = 120$, $T = 118$, $G = 85$, $C = 87$. We want the GC content.

**Step 1 — Sum the total bases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= A + T + G + C \\
&= 120 + 118 + 85 + 87 \\
&= 410\ \text{bases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the GC content formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%GC &= \dfrac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{85 + 87}{410} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{172}{410} \times 100 \\
&\approx 41.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Chargaff's rule.** The rules state $\%A = \%T$ and $\%G = \%C$ in double-stranded DNA. Computing:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%A &= \dfrac{120}{410} \times 100 \approx 29.27\% \\
\%T &= \dfrac{118}{410} \times 100 \approx 28.78\% \\
\%G &= \dfrac{85}{410} \times 100 \approx 20.73\% \\
\%C &= \dfrac{87}{410} \times 100 \approx 21.22\%
\end{aligned}
$$

We observe $\%A \approx \%T$ and $\%G \approx \%C$ (the small deviations are due to the small finite sample length; in the limit $L \to \infty$, equality is exact). Therefore the data is consistent with Chargaff's rule, and the fragment is dsDNA.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Open Reading Frame (ORF) Count on dsDNA

In bioinformatics, an **Open Reading Frame (ORF)** is a continuous stretch of codons that begins with a start codon (ATG) and ends with a stop codon (TAA, TAG, TGA), with no intervening stop codons in the same reading frame.

**Step 1 — Number of reading frames per single strand.** A single strand of DNA of length $L$ bp can be read in **3 reading frames** (frameshift by 0, 1, or 2 nucleotides), giving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{frames, single strand}} &= 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of reading frames on dsDNA.** The complementary strand can also be read in 3 frames, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{frames, dsDNA}} &= 3_{\text{forward}} + 3_{\text{reverse}} \\
&= 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Maximum possible ORFs.** In a random sequence of length $L$ bp, the expected number of stop codons in any reading frame is approximately one every $64/3 \approx 21.3$ codons. For a typical prokaryotic gene, an ORF must be at least $\sim 100$ codons long ($\sim 300$ bp) to be considered a real gene. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Min. ORF length (coding)} &\geq 300\ \text{bp} = 100\ \text{codons} \\
\text{Expected ORFs/frame} &\approx \dfrac{L/3}{21.3}
\end{aligned}
$$

For $L = 3{,}000{,}000$ bp (a typical bacterial chromosome):

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{ORFs/frame}} &\approx \dfrac{1{,}000{,}000}{21.3} \approx 46{,}948 \\
N_{\text{ORFs, all 6 frames}} &\approx 6 \times 46{,}948 \approx 281{,}690
\end{aligned}
$$

This is why **gene prediction programs (Glimmer, Prodigal)** must statistically distinguish true ORFs from random background ORFs using Markov models and codon-usage bias.

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Molecular Weight of a Plasmid Insert

A student clones an insert of length $n = 1{,}500$ bp into a plasmid vector of length $n_v = 2{,}700$ bp. Calculate the total molecular weight of the recombinant plasmid.

**Step 1 — Total bp in recombinant plasmid:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{\text{total}} &= n_{\text{insert}} + n_{\text{vector}} \\
&= 1{,}500 + 2{,}700 \\
&= 4{,}200\ \text{bp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the dsDNA molecular weight formula** (using $660\ \text{Da/bp}$ for the sodium salt):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MW}_{\text{recombinant}} &= n_{\text{total}} \times 660\ \text{Da/bp} \\
&= 4{,}200 \times 660 \\
&= 2{,}772{,}000\ \text{Da} \\
&= 2.772\ \text{MDa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to the conventional supercoiled plasmid form (relaxed vs. supercoiled ratio $\approx 1.06$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MW}_{\text{supercoiled}} &\approx 1.06 \times 2.772\ \text{MDa} \\
&\approx 2.94\ \text{MDa}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Reading Frame Translation (DNA → Protein)

Translate the mRNA sequence: `5'-AUG UUU CGA UAA-3'` into its amino acid sequence.

**Step 1 — Identify the reading frame and partition into codons.** The sequence begins with AUG (start), so the frame is fixed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Codon 1} &= \text{AUG (Methionine, M, start)} \\
\text{Codon 2} &= \text{UUU (Phenylalanine, F)} \\
\text{Codon 3} &= \text{CGA (Arginine, R)} \\
\text{Codon 4} &= \text{UAA (Stop — translation terminates)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Concatenate amino acids into the protein sequence:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Peptide} &= \text{Met - Phe - Arg} \\
&= \text{M - F - R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the molecular weight of the tripeptide** (using average residue MW = 110 Da and a water molecule lost per peptide bond; for a tripeptide, 2 peptide bonds = 2 × 18 = 36 Da of water lost):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{MW}_{\text{peptide}} &= (3 \times 110) - 36 \\
&= 330 - 36 \\
&= 294\ \text{Da}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Gene Elements Summary

| Gene Element | Location | Length Range | Key Motif | Function | Bioinformatics Tool to Detect |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Promoter (TATA box) | $\sim -25$ to $-30$ bp upstream of TSS | 6–8 bp | `TATA(A/T)A(A/T)` | RNA pol II positioning | PromoterScan, NNPP |
| 5' UTR | TSS to start codon (ATG) | 50–500 bp | Kozak `gccRccATGG` | Ribosome binding/regulation | UTRdb |
| Exon | Within CDS | 50–250 bp | Splice sites `GT...AG` | Encodes protein | GENSCAN, AUGUSTUS |
| Intron | Between exons | 60–100,000 bp | `GT...AG` rule | Spliced out, regulatory roles | Splice site predictors |
| 3' UTR | Stop codon to polyA site | 50–1000 bp | `AAUAAA` (polyA signal) | mRNA stability, export | PolyApred |
| Terminator | Downstream of polyA site | 100–1000 bp | Hairpin + U-rich | Transcription termination | TransTermHP |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Central Dogma Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gene1(["GENOME\nDouble-stranded DNA"]) --> rep1["DNA Replication\nEnzyme: DNA Polymerase\nProduct: 2 daughter dsDNA"]
    rep1 --> gene1
    gene1 --> tx1["Transcription\nEnzyme: RNA Polymerase II\nLocation: Nucleus"]
    tx1 --> mrna1(["Pre-mRNA\nhnRNA with introns"])
    mrna1 --> sp1["RNA Processing\n5' cap, 3' polyA, splicing"]
    sp1 --> mrna2(["Mature mRNA\nNuclear export"])
    mrna2 --> tl1["Translation\nMachine: Ribosome\nLocation: Cytoplasm"]
    tl1 --> prot1(["Polypeptide\nLinear chain of amino acids"])
    prot1 --> fold1["Post-Translational\nFolding and Modification"]
    fold1 --> prot2(["Functional Protein\n3D native structure"])

    rev1["Reverse Transcription\nEnzyme: Reverse Transcriptase"] -.Retroviruses.-> gene1
    rrep1["RNA Replication\nEnzyme: RdRp"] -.RNA viruses.-> mrna2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows represent the canonical Central Dogma flow (DNA → RNA → Protein). Dashed arrows represent exceptional flows found in viruses. Each processing step is annotated with the responsible enzymatic machinery and sub-cellular location, which is essential for understanding compartmentalization of gene expression in eukaryotic cells.

## 4.2 Block Architecture of a Eukaryotic Protein-Coding Gene

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    up["5' Upstream Region"] --> prom["Promoter\nTATA box at -25 to -30"]
    prom --> enh["Enhancer\ncan be 100 kb away"]
    enh --> utr5["5' UTR\nKozak sequence"]
    utr5 --> ex1["Exon 1\n5' splice site GT"]
    ex1 --> in1["Intron 1\nbranch point A"]
    in1 --> ex2["Exon 2\ncoding region"]
    ex2 --> in2["Intron 2"]
    in2 --> ex3["Exon 3\n3' splice site AG"]
    ex3 --> utr3["3' UTR\nAAUAAA polyA signal"]
    utr3 --> polyA["Poly-A Tail\napprox 200 A residues"]
    polyA --> term["Terminator\nHairpin + U-rich region"]
```

## 4.3 Gene Expression Pipeline Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dna1["Genomic DNA\n4-letter alphabet A T G C"] --> step1["Step 1: Transcription Initiation\nRNA Pol II + GTFs bind promoter"]
    step1 --> step2["Step 2: Elongation\nRNA Pol II synthesizes 5' to 3'"]
    step2 --> step3["Step 3: Pre-mRNA\ncontains exons and introns"]
    step3 --> step4["Step 4: 5' Capping\n7-methylguanosine cap"]
    step4 --> step5["Step 5: 3' Polyadenylation\npolyA tail added"]
    step5 --> step6["Step 6: Splicing\nspliceosome removes introns"]
    step6 --> step7["Step 7: Mature mRNA\nexported to cytoplasm"]
    step7 --> step8["Step 8: Translation\nribosome reads codons"]
    step8 --> step9["Step 9: Polypeptide Chain\nlinear amino acid sequence"]
    step9 --> step10["Step 10: Folding\nchaperone-assisted 3D fold"]
    step10 --> protFinal["Functional Protein\nready for cellular role"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this block structure matters for KTU 2024:** Each labeled block is a discrete, examinable concept. Examiners frequently ask for "list the post-transcriptional modifications" (Step 4, 5, 6) and "distinguish prokaryotic and eukaryotic gene expression" (prokaryotes lack Steps 4, 5, 6 — they have **coupled transcription-translation** in the cytoplasm).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define a gene. List any three structural elements of a typical eukaryotic gene with one-line functions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx 80–100 words):**

A **gene** is a functional unit of heredity consisting of a specific DNA sequence that is transcribed into RNA and, in the case of protein-coding genes, translated into a polypeptide. Three structural elements of a typical eukaryotic gene are:

1. **Promoter** — a DNA sequence (e.g., TATA box) located upstream of the transcription start site that binds RNA polymerase II and general transcription factors to initiate transcription.
2. **Exons** — expressed, protein-coding sequences retained in the mature mRNA after splicing.
3. **3' UTR with polyadenylation signal (AAUAAA)** — directs cleavage and poly-A tail addition, enhancing mRNA stability and translation efficiency.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark each for any 3 elements with function.)*

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State Chargaff's rule. What is its significance in determining the double-stranded nature of DNA?**

**Model Answer (3 marks — approx 70–90 words):**

**Chargaff's rule** states that in double-stranded DNA, the mole percentage of adenine equals that of thymine, and the mole percentage of guanine equals that of cytosine; that is:

$$
\%A = \%T \quad \text{and} \quad \%G = \%C
$$

which implies $\%(A+T) + \%(G+C) = 100\%$.

**Significance:** Chargaff's rule provided the critical experimental clue for **Watson and Crick (1953)** to deduce that A pairs with T and G pairs with C via hydrogen bonds, establishing the **complementary base-pairing principle** at the heart of the DNA double helix. It also provides a quick test to confirm whether an unknown sequence is likely to be double-stranded (Chargaff-equality) or single-stranded (Chargaff-asymmetric).

*(Examiner's note: 2 marks for stating the rule with the equality, 1 mark for significance.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks each)

### Question A. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Part B Module 1]`

**A. (a) Explain the Central Dogma of Molecular Biology. Describe the processes of transcription and translation in a eukaryotic cell with suitable diagrams. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction to Central Dogma (2 marks):**
The **Central Dogma**, proposed by Francis Crick in 1958 and refined in 1970, describes the unidirectional flow of genetic information in biological systems. It states that genetic information is transferred from **DNA to DNA** (replication), from **DNA to RNA** (transcription), and from **RNA to protein** (translation). The flow is unidirectional under normal cellular conditions; however, special exceptions exist in retroviruses (RNA → DNA via reverse transcriptase) and in some RNA viruses (RNA → RNA via RNA-dependent RNA polymerase).

**Transcription in Eukaryotes (2.5 marks):**
Transcription is the synthesis of a complementary RNA strand from a DNA template, catalysed by **RNA polymerase II** in the nucleoplasm. It proceeds in three stages:

1. **Initiation:** General transcription factors (TFIID, TFIIA, TFIIB, etc.) assemble at the promoter, and RNA Pol II is recruited. The TATA-binding protein (TBP) recognises the TATA box $\sim 25$–$30$ bp upstream of the TSS.
2. **Elongation:** RNA Pol II moves 3' to 5' along the template strand, synthesising the RNA strand 5' to 3' by adding complementary ribonucleotides (A pairs with U instead of T).
3. **Termination:** RNA Pol II transcribes past a polyadenylation signal (`AAUAAA`), the pre-mRNA is cleaved, and a **poly-A tail** is added; RNA Pol II dissociates.

**Post-transcriptional modifications (1.5 marks):**
- **5' capping** — addition of 7-methylguanosine via a 5'-5' triphosphate bridge.
- **Splicing** — the spliceosome (snRNPs) removes introns and joins exons.
- **3' polyadenylation** — poly-A polymerase adds a poly-A tail ($\sim 200$ A residues).

**Translation in Eukaryotes (1 mark):**
Translation occurs on **ribosomes** in the cytoplasm. The mature mRNA is read in the **5' to 3' direction** in triplets (codons). The ribosome has three sites: A (aminoacyl), P (peptidyl), and E (exit). **tRNAs** carrying specific amino acids enter with their anticodons base-pairing to mRNA codons; peptide bonds form between adjacent amino acids. The process is initiated by the start codon **AUG** (encoding Met) and terminated by stop codons **UAA, UAG, UGA** (no tRNA recognises these; release factors bind instead).

---

**A. (b) What is the genetic code? Explain its salient features. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** The **genetic code** is the set of rules by which information encoded in nucleic acid sequences (DNA/RNA) is translated into proteins. Specifically, it is the correspondence between each of the **64 possible triplet codons** of mRNA and the 20 standard amino acids (plus stop signals).

**Salient Features (5 marks):**

1. **Triplet nature:** Three consecutive bases specify one amino acid. The code is read in non-overlapping triplets, with a defined reading frame.
2. **Degeneracy (Redundancy):** 61 codons encode only 20 amino acids, so most amino acids are encoded by 2–6 synonymous codons (e.g., Leucine: 6 codons; Methionine and Tryptophan: 1 codon each).
3. **Unambiguity:** Each codon specifies only one amino acid; the code is not ambiguous.
4. **Universality (with minor exceptions):** The code is essentially the same in all organisms — bacteria, plants, animals — a strong indicator of common evolutionary origin. A few exceptions exist in mitochondrial genomes and in some protozoan ciliates (e.g., UAR stop codons may encode glutamine).
5. **Non-overlapping and comma-less:** Codons are read sequentially without punctuation; the reading frame is set by the start codon AUG.
6. **Start and stop signals:** AUG (Methionine) is the universal start codon; UAA, UAG, and UGA are stop codons that terminate translation.
7. **Wobble at the third position:** Crick's wobble hypothesis explains why a single tRNA can recognise multiple codons differing only at the third base. The 5' anticodon base pairs flexibly with the 3' codon base, reducing the number of tRNAs needed.

**Significance (1 mark):** The degeneracy of the code provides **mutational robustness** — a point mutation at the third codon position is often a **synonymous (silent) mutation**, preserving the amino acid sequence and protein function.

---

### Question B. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Part B Module 1]`

**B. (a) With a neat block diagram, describe the structure of a eukaryotic protein-coding gene. Highlight the role of promoter, exons, introns, and 3' UTR. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** A eukaryotic protein-coding gene is a discontinuous DNA sequence composed of regulatory and coding elements. Unlike compact prokaryotic genes, eukaryotic genes contain introns that are removed by RNA splicing, allowing a single gene to produce multiple protein variants via **alternative splicing**.

**Structural Elements (5 marks):**

1. **Promoter Region:** Located $\sim 25$ to $\sim 100$ bp upstream of the transcription start site. The TATA box (consensus `TATAAA` at $\sim -25$ to $-30$ bp) is the core element that binds **TATA-binding protein (TBP)**, a subunit of TFIID. Additional elements include the **CAAT box** (at $\sim -75$ bp) and **GC boxes** (at $\sim -90$ bp) that bind **SP1** and other activators. **Role:** Positions RNA Pol II and initiates transcription.

2. **5' UTR:** From the transcription start site to the start codon ATG. Contains the **Kozak sequence** (`gccRccATGG`, R = purine) which is recognised by the small ribosomal subunit for translation initiation. **Role:** Regulates translation efficiency and mRNA stability.

3. **Exons:** Expressed, protein-coding sequences retained in the mature mRNA. Typical length 50–250 bp. Bordered by **splice sites**: the 5' splice site (`GT` at intron-exon boundary) and the 3' splice site (`AG` at exon-intron boundary). **Role:** Carry the codon sequence for protein synthesis; their boundaries are critical for correct splicing.

4. **Introns:** Non-coding sequences (60 bp to $\sim 100$ kb) that are transcribed but excised by the **spliceosome**. Begin with `GT` and end with `AG` (the **GU-AG rule**). **Role:** Provide regulatory sequences (e.g., enhancers, non-coding RNAs); allow alternative splicing to expand proteome diversity; assist in gene evolution via exon shuffling.

5. **3' UTR:** From the stop codon to the polyadenylation site. Contains the **polyadenylation signal** (`AAUAAA`) 10–30 nt upstream of the cleavage site. **Role:** Directs cleavage and poly-A tail addition, enhances mRNA stability, regulates nuclear export, and contains microRNA binding sites that post-transcriptionally repress gene expression.

6. **Poly-A Tail:** $\sim 200$ adenosine residues added post-transcriptionally. **Role:** Protects mRNA from exonuclease degradation and enhances translation.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** *(See SECTION 4.2 Mermaid diagram for reference.)*

---

**B. (b) Explain Chargaff's rule. A double-stranded DNA fragment has 240 adenine, 260 thymine, 320 guanine, and 280 cytosine bases. Calculate the GC content and verify Chargaff's rule. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Chargaff's Rule — Explanation (3 marks):**

Chargaff's rule, formulated by **Erwin Chargaff in the early 1950s**, is an empirical observation on the base composition of double-stranded DNA:

$$
\%A = \%T \quad \text{and} \quad \%G = \%C
$$

This follows directly from the Watson-Crick base-pairing principle: in a double helix, **A always pairs with T** via two hydrogen bonds, and **G always pairs with C** via three hydrogen bonds. Therefore, the number of A bases must equal the number of T bases (one of each per base pair), and similarly for G and C.

The rule is **a direct test of double-strandedness**: a single-stranded DNA (or RNA) sequence will not in general satisfy $\%A = \%T$ and $\%G = \%C$, but a double-stranded one will. The rule was the crucial clue that helped Watson and Crick deduce the **complementary base-pairing** nature of the double helix.

**Calculation of GC Content (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Sum the total bases:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{total}} &= A + T + G + C \\
&= 240 + 260 + 320 + 280 \\
&= 1100\ \text{bases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the GC content formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%GC &= \dfrac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{320 + 280}{1100} \times 100 \\
&= \dfrac{600}{1100} \times 100 \\
&\approx 54.55\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Chargaff's rule:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%A &= \dfrac{240}{1100} \times 100 \approx 21.82\% \\
\%T &= \dfrac{260}{1100} \times 100 \approx 23.64\% \\
\%G &= \dfrac{320}{1100} \times 100 \approx 29.09\% \\
\%C &= \dfrac{280}{1100} \times 100 \approx 25.45\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Observation:** $\%A \approx \%T$ (21.82% vs 23.64%) and $\%G \approx \%C$ (29.09% vs 25.45%). The minor deviations are due to the finite size of the fragment (finite-sample noise); in the limit of an infinite random dsDNA sequence, the equality would be exact. The data is therefore **consistent with Chargaff's rule**, confirming the double-stranded nature of the fragment.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The fragment is double-stranded DNA with a GC content of approximately **54.55%**, indicating a relatively GC-rich fragment (more thermally stable than AT-rich fragments, since G-C pairs have three H-bonds vs two for A-T).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss pitfalls observed in KTU valuation scripts:**
>
> 1. **Confusing transcription with replication** — Transcription uses **RNA polymerase** and produces **single-stranded RNA** (U replaces T). Replication uses **DNA polymerase** and produces **double-stranded DNA**. Examiners deduct **2 marks** if these are conflated.
> 2. **Wrong start codon usage** — Some students write "AUG" for DNA. The correct start codon in **mRNA is AUG** (in DNA template it is the corresponding TAC). The genetic code is conventionally written in **RNA language** with U not T. Examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Confusing "sense" and "antisense" / template and coding strands** — Coding strand (sense) has the same sequence as mRNA (with T→U); template strand (antisense) is complementary and is the one actually read by RNA polymerase. Examiners frequently test this.
> 4. **Chargaff's rule stated without the formula** — Always write the explicit equality $\%A = \%T$ and $\%G = \%C$. A vague statement loses 1 of the 3 marks.
> 5. **Prokaryotic vs. eukaryotic gene architecture** — Prokaryotic genes are **intron-less and polycistronic** (operons); eukaryotic genes have **introns and are monocistronic**. Students frequently reverse these. Examiner deducts up to 2 marks.
> 6. **GC content formula — incorrect denominator** — Use the total bases (A+T+G+C), not just G+C alone. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 7. **Forgetting the poly-A tail and 5' cap in transcription answers** — These are **eukaryote-specific** post-transcriptional modifications. Examiner deducts up to 1 mark per omission.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Molecular Biology Primer: Genes**
>
> - **Gene definition:** A functional heredity unit of DNA that is transcribed into RNA; may be translated into protein.
> - **Central Dogma:** DNA $\rightarrow$ RNA $\rightarrow$ Protein (unidirectional); exceptions in retroviruses (RT) and RNA viruses (RdRp).
> - **DNA double helix (B-form):** $3.4\ \text{\AA}$ rise per bp, $10.5$ bp/turn, $20\ \text{\AA}$ diameter; **A-T (2 H-bonds)**, **G-C (3 H-bonds)**.
> - **Chargaff's rule:** $\%A = \%T$, $\%G = \%C$; signifies double-strandedness.
> - **Average MW of one dsDNA base pair:** $660\ \text{Da}$; one amino acid residue: $110\ \text{Da}$.
> - **Genetic code:** $4^3 = 64$ codons; 61 sense, 3 stop; 20 amino acids; **degenerate, unambiguous, universal, non-overlapping**.
> - **Start codon:** AUG (Methionine); **Stop codons:** UAA, UAG, UGA.
> - **Wobble hypothesis:** 3rd codon position pairs flexibly with 1st anticodon position; explains degeneracy.
> - **Eukaryotic gene elements:** Promoter (TATA box at $-25$), 5' UTR, Exons, Introns (GT-AG rule), 3' UTR (AAUAAA), Poly-A tail, Terminator.
> - **Prokaryotic vs. Eukaryotic genes:** Prokaryotes — intron-less, polycistronic operons, coupled transcription-translation. Eukaryotes — introns present, monocistronic, nuclear compartmentalisation.
> - **Eukaryotic transcription enzymes:** RNA Pol II (mRNA), Pol I (rRNA), Pol III (tRNA).
> - **Post-transcriptional modifications (eukaryotes only):** 5' cap, 3' poly-A tail, splicing.
> - **ORFs:** 6 reading frames on dsDNA (3 forward, 3 reverse); minimum ORF $\sim 100$ codons.
> - **GC content formula:** $\%GC = \dfrac{G+C}{A+T+G+C} \times 100$; correlates with $T_m$ and DNA stability.
> - **Wallace $T_m$ formula:** $T_m = 64.9 + 41 \cdot \dfrac{(G+C-16.4)}{N}$ — for $N \leq 25$ bp primer.
> - **Gene prediction tools:** GENSCAN, AUGUSTUS, Glimmer, Prodigal.
> - **Translation:** Ribosome reads mRNA 5'→3'; tRNA brings amino acids; starts at AUG, ends at stop codons; A, P, E sites on ribosome.
> - **Combinatorial capacity:** A protein of length $L$ amino acids can be specified by $20^L$ possible sequences — astronomical sequence space.
> - **Human genome:** $\sim 3.2\ \text{Gb}$ haploid, $\sim 20{,}000$–$25{,}000$ protein-coding genes, only $\sim 1.5\%$ coding.
> - **Bioinformatics link:** Every alignment (BLAST), gene prediction, and phylogenetic analysis is built on these foundational molecular biology concepts.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DNAs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** DNAs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of DNA in Bioinformatics

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Deoxyribonucleic Acid (DNA)** is a double-helical, polymeric macromolecule composed of repeating monomeric units called **deoxyribonucleotides**. Each nucleotide consists of three chemically linked components:

1. A **5-carbon sugar** — *2-deoxyribose*
2. A **phosphate group** (attached to the 5′ carbon of the sugar)
3. A **nitrogenous base** (attached to the 1′ carbon of the sugar)

The nitrogenous bases are of two biochemical types:
- **Purines** — Adenine (A) and Guanine (G) — double-ring structures
- **Pyrimidines** — Cytosine (C) and Thymine (T) — single-ring structures

Two polynucleotide strands run **anti-parallel** (one in 5′→3′, the other 3′→5′) and are held together by **Watson–Crick hydrogen bonds**: A pairs with T via **2 hydrogen bonds**, and G pairs with C via **3 hydrogen bonds**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — DNAs):** Students are expected to understand DNA structure, base-pairing rules, Chargaff's ratios, physical forms (A-, B-, Z-DNA), replication logic, and the basic arithmetic of genome size — all of which form the quantitative foundation of every downstream bioinformatics algorithm (BLAST, alignment, assembly, phylogenetics).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Blueprint + Zipper" Analogy for DNA**
> Imagine DNA as a *very long, twisted ladder* sealed inside the nucleus of every cell. The two **vertical rails** of the ladder are made of alternating sugar and phosphate groups (the *scaffold*), and the **rungs** are pairs of nitrogenous bases locked together like the teeth of a zipper. The "teeth" follow a strict rule — A always latches with T, and G always latches with C — so if you read one side, you instantly know the other side. The order of these rungs is the **genetic code**, and bioinformatics is essentially the science of *reading, comparing, and interpreting the order of those rungs* across organisms.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (in bold)

| Parameter | Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Helix diameter (B-DNA) | **~2.0 nm (20 Å)** |
| Distance per base pair (rise) | **0.34 nm (3.4 Å)** |
| Base pairs per turn (B-DNA) | **~10.5 bp/turn** |
| Pitch (full turn) | **~3.4 nm (34 Å)** |
| Average molecular weight per base pair | **~660 Da (g/mol)** |
| Average molecular weight per single nucleotide | **~330 Da (g/mol)** |
| Human genome size | **~3.2 × 10⁹ bp** |
| Hydrogen bonds per A–T pair | **2** |
| Hydrogen bonds per G–C pair | **3** |

### 1.4 Watson–Crick Base Pairing — Pictorial Summary

```
Purines (large)    Pair with    Pyrimidines (small)
─────────────                   ─────────────────
A  (Adenine)   ⇔  2 H-bonds  ⇔  T  (Thymine)
G  (Guanine)   ⇔  3 H-bonds  ⇔  C  (Cytosine)
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the pairing is *exclusive*:** A purine (A or G) has a 2-ring structure; a pyrimidine (C or T) has a 1-ring structure. Pairing two purines would make the helix too wide, and pairing two pyrimidines would make it too narrow. The 2-ring + 1-ring combination gives the *constant helix width* of ~2.0 nm.

### 1.5 The Three Major Forms of DNA (Conformational Polymorphism)

| Form | Helix Handedness | Base Pairs per Turn | Major Groove | Biological Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A-DNA** | Right-handed | ~11 | Deep, narrow | Found in dehydrated DNA, RNA–DNA hybrids |
| **B-DNA** | Right-handed | ~10.5 | Wide, deep | *Physiological standard form in vivo* |
| **Z-DNA** | **Left-handed** | ~12 | Flat, minor groove deep | Occurs in GC-rich alternating sequences (e.g., CpG islands) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** B-DNA is the *default* answer when the question simply says "DNA" without qualification. Z-DNA is the *high-yield* form because of its left-handedness — it is a favourite 3-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Chargaff's Rule — The Quantitative Foundation of Bioinformatics

Erwin Chargaff (1950) observed that in any double-stranded DNA:
- The mole fraction of A equals the mole fraction of T: **%A = %T**
- The mole fraction of G equals the mole fraction of C: **%G = %C**

This is a direct consequence of Watson–Crick base pairing — every A on one strand has a T on the other, and every G has a C.

**Bioinformatic Utility:** Chargaff's ratios allow us to compute the **unknown base fractions** if any one fraction is known, which is the basis of the very first computational exercises in DNA sequence analysis.

### 2.2 Key Formulas in DNA Arithmetic

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these 8 formulas — they appear almost every KTU exam cycle in some form.**

| # | Formula | Variables | Unit | Used For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\%\text{GC} = \dfrac{G + C}{A + T + G + C} \times 100$ | G, C, A, T = count of bases | % | GC content (stability, isochores) |
| 2 | $A = T \quad \text{and} \quad G = C$ | — | — | Chargaff equality |
| 3 | $A + T + G + C = N$ | N = total bases | bases | Conservation check |
| 4 | $M_{\text{ssDNA}} = N \times 330\ \text{Da}$ | N = nucleotide count | Da | Single-strand MW |
| 5 | $M_{\text{dsDNA}} = N_{\text{bp}} \times 660\ \text{Da}$ | N_bp = base-pair count | Da | Double-strand MW |
| 6 | $L_{\text{DNA}} = N_{\text{bp}} \times 0.34\ \text{nm}$ | N_bp = base-pair count | nm | Physical length of DNA |
| 7 | $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | A,T,G,C = base count | °C | Melting temperature (Wallace rule) |
| 8 | $N_{\text{turns}} = \dfrac{N_{\text{bp}}}{10.5}$ | N_bp = base-pair count | turns | Number of helical turns |

### 2.3 Why Each Step Matters — The "Why & How" Logic

- **GC content** determines *thermal stability* (more G–C = more H-bonds = higher melting point) and *buoyant density* in CsCl gradients. It is also a marker for **CpG islands** (gene promoters in mammals).
- **Chargaff's rule** lets the bioinformatician infer one strand's sequence from the other — a principle used in *sequencing by complementation* and *error-correction in assembly*.
- **Molecular weight of DNA** is needed to convert between *molar* and *mass* units when preparing sequencing libraries or PCR reactions.
- **DNA length** in nm is useful to estimate whether a piece of DNA can fit inside a *viral capsid* (e.g., T4 phage: ~169 kbp × 0.34 nm ≈ 57 µm, which is *longer than the phage itself* — it is highly coiled!).
- **Melting temperature (Tm)** is critical for designing PCR primers — a primer with too high Tm will not anneal; too low will mis-prime.
- **Helical turns** explain the topological problem of DNA replication — opening the helix requires **topoisomerases** (gyrase) to relieve supercoiling ahead of the replication fork.

### 2.4 The Central Dogma (Contextual Overview for Module 1)

```
DNA  ──(Transcription)──►  mRNA  ──(Translation)──►  Protein
 ▲                                                      │
 └────────────── (Replication) ─────────────────────────┘
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU context:** Although transcription/translation are detailed in Module 2/3, the *flow of information* is introduced in Module 1 as the conceptual bridge from *DNA → function*. Always draw the Central Dogma as a single-flow figure; examiners reward clean arrow direction and correct labels.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Genome Size from Base-Pair Count and Physical Length

**Given:** A bacterial chromosome has $1.0 \times 10^6$ base pairs of dsDNA.
**Find:** (a) Number of helical turns, (b) Physical length in µm, (c) Total molecular weight in Daltons.

**Step 1 — Number of helical turns**

The B-form helix makes 1 full turn every 10.5 bp. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{turns}} &= \frac{N_{\text{bp}}}{10.5} \\
&= \frac{1.0 \times 10^6}{10.5} \\
&= 95238.09\ \text{turns} \\
&\approx 9.52 \times 10^4\ \text{turns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Physical length**

The axial rise per base pair is 0.34 nm. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{DNA}} &= N_{\text{bp}} \times 0.34\ \text{nm} \\
&= 1.0 \times 10^6 \times 0.34\ \text{nm} \\
&= 3.4 \times 10^5\ \text{nm} \\
&= 340\ \mu\text{m}\ (\text{since } 1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^3\ \text{nm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Molecular weight (double-stranded)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{dsDNA}} &= N_{\text{bp}} \times 660\ \text{Da} \\
&= 1.0 \times 10^6 \times 660\ \text{Da} \\
&= 6.6 \times 10^8\ \text{Da} \\
&= 0.66\ \text{GDa}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** A single bacterial cell packs 340 µm of DNA into a cell only ~2 µm long. This is possible because DNA is **supercoiled** and condensed by nucleoid-associated proteins.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Melting Temperature by the Wallace Rule (Nearest-Neighbour Approximation)

**Given:** A primer sequence of length 14 nt: 5′–GCTAGCATCGATCG–3′ with base counts A=3, T=3, G=4, C=4.
**Find:** Melting temperature $T_m$ using the simplified Wallace rule.

**Step 1 — Verify Chargaff-like balance**

For a single-stranded primer, Wallace's rule treats each base independently:
- (A+T) contributes **2 °C per base**
- (G+C) contributes **4 °C per base**

**Step 2 — Substitute values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_m &= 2(A + T) + 4(G + C) \\
&= 2(3 + 3) + 4(4 + 4) \\
&= 2(6) + 4(8) \\
&= 12 + 32 \\
&= 44\ ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Validity check**

Wallace's rule is accurate only for short oligonucleotides of length 14–50 nt. For longer DNA, use the **nearest-neighbour thermodynamic model** (SantaLucia 1998) which uses dinucleotide stacking energies $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$:

$$
T_m = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ + R \ln\!\left(\dfrac{C_T}{x}\right)}
$$

where:
- $\Delta H^\circ$ = total enthalpy of base-pair stacking (kcal/mol)
- $\Delta S^\circ$ = total entropy change (cal/mol·K)
- $R = 1.987\ \text{cal/mol·K}$ (universal gas constant)
- $C_T$ = total strand concentration
- $x$ = 4 for self-complementary, 1 for non-self-complementary duplex

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Base Composition Inference Using Chargaff's Rule

**Given:** A dsDNA fragment has 240 nucleotides on one strand. Of these, A=70, G=50, C=30. Find the percentage composition of all four bases.

**Step 1 — Find T using total base count**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A + T + G + C &= 240 \\
70 + T + 50 + 30 &= 240 \\
T &= 240 - 150 \\
T &= 90
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute percentages**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%A &= \frac{70}{240} \times 100 = 29.17\% \\
\%T &= \frac{90}{240} \times 100 = 37.50\% \\
\%G &= \frac{50}{240} \times 100 = 20.83\% \\
\%C &= \frac{30}{240} \times 100 = 12.50\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Chargaff (this is for single-strand, so equality need not hold)**

Chargaff's rule applies to the *double-stranded* molecule. On the complementary strand:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A' &= T = 90 \\
T' &= A = 70 \\
G' &= C = 30 \\
C' &= G = 50
\end{aligned}
$$

Combined across both strands:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{total}} &= A + A' = 70 + 90 = 160 \\
T_{\text{total}} &= T + T' = 90 + 70 = 160 \\
G_{\text{total}} &= G + G' = 50 + 30 = 80 \\
C_{\text{total}} &= C + C' = 30 + 50 = 80
\end{aligned}
$$

So $A = T = 160$ and $G = C = 80$ — **Chargaff's rule verified** ✓

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Type → Formula Mapping

| Problem Asked | Formula to Use | Marks Allocated in KTU ESE |
|---|---|---|
| Calculate GC content | $\%\text{GC} = \frac{G+C}{N} \times 100$ | 2–3 marks |
| Find missing base | $A+T+G+C = N$ + Chargaff | 2–3 marks |
| Compute dsDNA molecular weight | $M = N_{\text{bp}} \times 660$ Da | 2 marks |
| Compute physical length | $L = N_{\text{bp}} \times 0.34$ nm | 2 marks |
| Number of helical turns | $N_{\text{turns}} = N_{\text{bp}} / 10.5$ | 2 marks |
| Primer $T_m$ for PCR design | $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$ | 3 marks |
| Genome size from mass | $N_{\text{bp}} = M / 660$ | 2 marks |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of DNA Information Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nucleus["NUCLEUS\nGenome Storage"] --> trans["TRANSCRIPTION\nDNA to mRNA"]
    trans --> rna["mRNA MOLECULE\n5 prime UTR -- CDS -- 3 prime UTR"]
    rna --> transl["TRANSLATION\nRibosome Decodes Codons"]
    transl --> prot["PROTEIN\nFunctional Output"]
    nucleus --> repl["REPLICATION\nDNA Polymerase Copies Genome"]
    repl --> daught["DAUGHTER DNA\nSemi Conservative"]
    prot --> feed["FEEDBACK\nGene Regulation"]
    feed -.-> trans
```

### 4.2 DNA Structural Hierarchy — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nuc["NUCLEOTIDE"] -->|"3 components"| sug["DEOXYRIBOSE SUGAR"]
    nuc -->|"2 of 4"| base["NITROGENOUS BASE"]
    nuc -->|"1 linker"| phos["PHOSPHATE GROUP"]
    base --> pur["PURINES A and G"]
    base --> pyr["PYRIMIDINES C and T"]
    nuc -->|"polymerised into"| poly["POLYNUCLEOTIDE STRAND"]
    poly -->|"5 prime to 3 prime"| s1["STRAND 1"]
    poly -->|"complement"| s2["STRAND 2"]
    s1 -->|"Watson Crick base pairing"| ds["DOUBLE HELIX B DNA"]
    s2 --> ds
    ds -->|"10.5 bp per turn"| helix["RIGHT HANDED HELIX\n2 nm diameter"]
    ds -->|"condensed with histones"| chrom["CHROMATIN FIBER"]
    chrom --> meta["METAPHASE CHROMOSOME"]
```

### 4.3 DNA Replication Logic Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ori["ORIGIN OF REPLICATION ORI"] --> un["HELICASE UNWINDS PARENTAL DNA"]
    un --> fork["REPLICATION FORK FORMS"]
    fork --> lead["LEADING STRAND\nContinuous Synthesis 5 to 3"]
    fork --> lag["LAGGING STRAND\nOkazaki Fragments"]
    lead --> pol1["DNA POLYMERASE III"]
    lag --> pol2["DNA POLYMERASE III"]
    pol2 --> lig["DNA LIGASE\nSeals Nicks"]
    pol1 --> fin["TWO DAUGHTER DUPLEXES\nSemi Conservative"]
    lig --> fin
    fork --> topo["TOPOISOMERASE\nRelieves Supercoiling"]
    topo --> fork
```

### 4.4 Mutation Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    mut["MUTATION EVENT"] --> sub["SUBSTITUTION"]
    mut --> ins["INSERTION"]
    mut --> del["DELETION"]
    sub --> trans["TRANSITION A G or C T"]
    sub --> vers["TRANSVERSION A C A T G C G T"]
    ins --> fra["FRAMESHIFT\nMultiple of 3 = in frame\nNot multiple of 3 = frameshift"]
    del --> fra
    mut --> point["POINT MUTATION\nSingle Base Change"]
    mut --> chrom["CHROMOSOMAL MUTATION\nInversion Translocation Duplication"]
    point --> silent["SILENT\nSame Amino Acid"]
    point --> miss["MISSENSE\nDifferent Amino Acid"]
    point --> non["NONSENSE\nStop Codon Forms"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] State Chargaff's rule. Why is it considered the chemical basis for Watson–Crick base pairing?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Chargaff's rule states that in any double-stranded DNA molecule, the molar amount of **Adenine equals Thymine (A = T)** and the molar amount of **Guanine equals Cytosine (G = C)**.

*Chemical basis:* Watson–Crick base pairing is a *specific hydrogen-bonding* interaction:
- A forms **2 hydrogen bonds** with T
- G forms **3 hydrogen bonds** with C

Because the two strands of the DNA duplex are **complementary**, every A on one strand *must* be paired with a T on the opposite strand, and similarly for G and C. This *one-to-one stoichiometric* relationship is exactly what Chargaff observed experimentally, and is the **direct chemical evidence** for the Watson–Crick model of DNA.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation key:** Use the terms "hydrogen bond," "complementarity," and "stoichiometric" — these are keyword triggers that earn full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between B-DNA and Z-DNA in terms of handedness, base pairs per turn, and biological occurrence.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | B-DNA | Z-DNA |
|---|---|---|
| Helix handedness | **Right-handed** | **Left-handed** |
| Base pairs per turn | **~10.5** | **~12** |
| Sugar–phosphate backbone | Smooth, regular | **Zig-zag** (hence the name) |
| Major groove | Wide, deep | Flat, shallow |
| Biological occurrence | Standard physiological form in vivo | GC-rich alternating sequences, e.g., CpG islands; transient in vivo |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Describe the Watson–Crick model of DNA structure with a neat labelled diagram. Explain the significance of anti-parallel orientation and hydrogen bonding. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 25 bp double-stranded oligonucleotide has the following single-strand base composition (5′→3′): A=8, T=7, G=6, C=4. Calculate the **GC content (%), molecular weight, physical length (in nm), and the number of helical turns** of this duplex. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. A

### Part (a) — DNA Structure (7 marks)

The Watson–Crick model of DNA, proposed in 1953, describes DNA as a **right-handed double helix** with the following key features:

**1. Sugar-Phosphate Backbone (2 marks)**
Each strand is a polymer of deoxyribonucleotides linked by **3′→5′ phosphodiester bonds**. The backbone lies on the *outside* of the helix.

**2. Anti-parallel Orientation (2 marks)**
The two strands run in **opposite directions**:
- One strand: 5′→3′
- The other strand: 3′→5′

This anti-parallel arrangement is *essential* because:
- DNA polymerase synthesises DNA only in the **5′→3′** direction
- The two strands can thus be replicated *simultaneously but asymmetrically* (leading vs lagging strand)
- The major and minor grooves form only because of the anti-parallelism

**3. Hydrogen Bonding (2 marks)**
- A = T via **2 hydrogen bonds**
- G ≡ C via **3 hydrogen bonds**
- The *specificity* of H-bonding ensures faithful replication: only the correct base pairs form stable duplexes

**4. Helical Geometry (1 mark)**
- Helix diameter: ~2.0 nm
- Rise per base pair: 0.34 nm
- ~10.5 bp per turn
- Two grooves: **major groove** (wider, protein-binding) and **minor groove** (narrower)

---

### Part (b) — DNA Arithmetic (7 marks)

**Given:** dsDNA, 25 bp, single-strand base counts: A=8, T=7, G=6, C=4.

**Step 1 — Apply Chargaff's rule to find the *missing* bases (1 mark)**

The given counts are for one strand. The complementary strand has:
- A' = T = 7
- T' = A = 8
- G' = C = 4
- C' = G = 6

**Step 2 — Total bases in the duplex (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{duplex}} &= 2 \times 25 = 50\ \text{bases (or 25 bp)} \\
\text{Check: } A+T+G+C &= (8+7)+(7+8)+(6+4)+(4+6) \\
&= 15 + 15 + 10 + 10 = 50\ \text{bases} \ \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — GC content (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%GC &= \frac{G + C}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{(6+4) + (4+6)}{50} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{20}{50} \times 100 = 40\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Molecular weight (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{dsDNA}} &= N_{\text{bp}} \times 660\ \text{Da} \\
&= 25 \times 660 \\
&= 16500\ \text{Da} = 16.5\ \text{kDa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Physical length (1.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{DNA}} &= N_{\text{bp}} \times 0.34\ \text{nm} \\
&= 25 \times 0.34 \\
&= 8.5\ \text{nm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Number of helical turns (1 mark)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{turns}} &= \frac{N_{\text{bp}}}{10.5} \\
&= \frac{25}{10.5} \\
&\approx 2.38\ \text{turns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Tabulated Answer (auto-credited 0 marks — for student clarity):**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| % GC content | **40 %** |
| Molecular weight | **16 500 Da (16.5 kDa)** |
| Physical length | **8.5 nm** |
| Helical turns | **~2.38 turns** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall — Read the Strand Carefully!**
> The question gives base counts of *one strand* (single-strand composition). Many students blindly plug these into the dsDNA MW formula and get the *wrong* answer. The correct interpretation: **Chargaff's rule must first be applied** to find the complementary strand's bases. Then the total N is used in the duplex formulas. This single mistake costs **2 full marks** in KTU valuation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the **three major forms of DNA** (A, B, Z). Compare their structural features and biological significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A researcher has isolated a plasmid of total mass **4.62 × 10⁶ Da** in double-stranded form. Calculate: **(i)** the number of base pairs, **(ii)** the physical length of the plasmid in micrometres (µm), and **(iii)** the number of helical turns. Assume the plasmid is in B-form. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q. B

### Part (a) — A, B, Z-DNA Forms (7 marks)

DNA is a *polymorphic* molecule — it can adopt different conformations depending on the **sequence, hydration, salt concentration, and ionic environment**.

**1. B-DNA — the "standard" form (2.5 marks)**
- Right-handed helix, ~10.5 bp/turn, 0.34 nm rise
- Watson–Crick geometry, wide major groove, narrow minor groove
- *Biological role:* **physiological form of DNA in vivo** under normal hydration (75 % humidity)
- Most gene-regulation proteins (transcription factors) read the *major groove*

**2. A-DNA (2 marks)**
- Right-handed helix, but **shorter and wider** than B-DNA
- ~11 bp/turn, 0.256 nm rise
- *Deeper, narrower major groove*; the minor groove is shallow
- *Biological role:* occurs in **dehydrated DNA, RNA-DNA hybrids, and some protein-bound DNA** (e.g., in spores)

**3. Z-DNA (2.5 marks)**
- **Left-handed** helix, ~12 bp/turn, 0.37 nm rise
- Sugar–phosphate backbone has a characteristic **zig-zag** pattern (hence "Z")
- Flat major groove; deep, narrow minor groove
- *Biological role:* forms in **GC-rich alternating sequences** (e.g., (CG)ₙ) and at *CpG islands* of actively transcribed genes. Has been implicated in **gene regulation** and may trigger innate immune responses via Z-DNA-binding protein 1 (ZBP1).

**Comparison Table (high-yield summary):**

| Property | A-DNA | B-DNA | Z-DNA |
|---|---|---|---|
| Handedness | Right | Right | **Left** |
| bp/turn | 11 | 10.5 | 12 |
| Rise/bp (nm) | 0.256 | 0.34 | 0.37 |
| Helix diameter (nm) | 2.6 | 2.0 | 1.8 |
| Backbone | Smooth | Smooth | **Zig-zag** |
| Form in vivo | Dehydrated | **Physiological** | GC-alternating |

---

### Part (b) — Plasmid Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $M_{\text{dsDNA}} = 4.62 \times 10^6\ \text{Da}$.

**(i) Number of base pairs (2.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{dsDNA}} &= N_{\text{bp}} \times 660\ \text{Da} \\
N_{\text{bp}} &= \frac{M_{\text{dsDNA}}}{660} \\
&= \frac{4.62 \times 10^6}{660} \\
&= 7.0 \times 10^3\ \text{bp} \\
&= 7000\ \text{bp}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Physical length in µm (2.5 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{DNA}} &= N_{\text{bp}} \times 0.34\ \text{nm} \\
&= 7000 \times 0.34 \\
&= 2380\ \text{nm} \\
&= 2.38\ \mu\text{m}\ (\text{since } 1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^3\ \text{nm})
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Number of helical turns (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{turns}} &= \frac{N_{\text{bp}}}{10.5} \\
&= \frac{7000}{10.5} \\
&\approx 666.67\ \text{turns}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often confuse the *single-strand* molecular weight constant (330 Da) with the *double-strand* constant (660 Da). For a **plasmid (ds-circular DNA)**, always use **660 Da/bp**. Using 330 Da would give *twice* the actual base-pair count and lose 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — DNAs: Last-Minute Revision List**

- [x] DNA is a **right-handed** (B-form) double helix of *anti-parallel* complementary strands.
- [x] **A–T = 2 H-bonds**, **G–C = 3 H-bonds**; purines pair with pyrimidines.
- [x] **Chargaff's rule:** $A = T$, $G = C$ (double-stranded level).
- [x] **Z-DNA is left-handed** — most distinctive "trick" question.
- [x] **Key constants:** 660 Da/bp, 0.34 nm rise, 10.5 bp/turn, 2 nm helix diameter.
- [x] **GC content** formula: $(G+C)/N \times 100$; higher GC = higher $T_m$.
- [x] **Wallace $T_m$** formula: $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)\ ^\circ\text{C}$ — for *short* primers (≤ 50 nt).
- [x] **DNA replication** is *semi-conservative*; **DNA polymerase** synthesises only **5′→3′** direction.
- [x] **Mutations:** substitution (silent/missense/nonsense), insertion, deletion, frameshift.
- [x] **Central Dogma** flow: DNA → mRNA → Protein (be ready to draw it in any ESE).
- [x] **Complementary strand computation** always *reverse-complement* the original (reverse direction + complementary bases).
- [x] For *double-stranded* MW, multiply bp by **660 Da**; for *single-stranded* MW, multiply nt by **330 Da**.
- [x] Convert length units carefully: $1\ \mu\text{m} = 10^3\ \text{nm} = 10^4\ \text{Å}$.
- [x] In any **numerical** question, **state the formula first, then substitute** — examiners mark for both.
- [x] **Always draw a labelled diagram** in 7-mark structure questions — it is worth at least 2 marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RNAs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** RNAs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of RNA

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Aligned)

**Ribonucleic Acid (RNA)** is a single-stranded, high-molecular-weight polymeric biomacromolecule composed of **ribonucleotide** monomers linked together by **3'→5' phosphodiester bonds**. Each ribonucleotide consists of three chemical moieties:

1. A **pentose sugar** — specifically **D-ribose** (C₅H₁₀O₅), which carries a hydroxyl (-OH) group on the **2' carbon**.
2. A **nitrogenous base** — belonging to either the purine class (**Adenine, Guanine**) or the pyrimidine class (**Cytosine, Uracil**).
3. A **phosphate group** — attached to the **5' carbon** of the ribose, forming the phosphodiester backbone.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The defining chemical distinction between RNA and DNA is the presence of the **2'-OH group** on ribose and the substitution of **Thymine (T)** with **Uracil (U)**. These two features form the basis of nearly every KTU question on RNA.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Working Photocopy" of a Cell

Imagine your **DNA** is the **original, master blueprint** of a building, locked safely inside a fireproof vault (the nucleus). You are **not allowed to take the original out** — it must stay protected. However, the construction crew on the construction site (the ribosome in the cytoplasm) **needs a copy of the blueprint to build proteins**.

**RNA is that working photocopy.**

- The cell **transcribes** a temporary, disposable RNA copy of the specific gene it needs.
- The RNA travels out of the nucleus to the ribosome.
- It is **read, translated into a protein, and then degraded**.
- This protects the original DNA blueprint and allows precise control of gene expression.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick Intuition Check:** DNA = permanent master record (double-stranded, archival). RNA = temporary working copy (single-stranded, disposable, chemically active).

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics & Constants

- Average length of a single ribonucleotide unit: **~330 Da (Daltons)**
- Bond linking nucleotides: **3'→5' phosphodiester bond**
- Strand directionality: **Antiparallel convention (5'→3' reading)**
- Base-pairing in RNA (secondary structure): **A–U** and **G–C** (with **G–U wobble pair** as a special exception)
- Helical geometry: **A-form right-handed helix** (when double-stranded regions form)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Hierarchy of RNA

RNA is structurally organized into **three hierarchical levels**, a classic KTU high-yield topic:

### Level 1: Primary Structure
- The **linear sequence** of ribonucleotides connected by phosphodiester bonds.
- Read in the **5' → 3' direction**.
- Encodes the genetic information for protein synthesis.

### Level 2: Secondary Structure
- Formed by **intra-strand base pairing** (the molecule folds back on itself).
- Creates characteristic motifs: **stem-loops, hairpins, bulges, internal loops, pseudoknots**.
- Governed by **A–U, G–C, and G–U wobble** pairing.

### Level 3: Tertiary Structure
- The **3D folding** of the secondary structure into compact functional shapes.
- Stabilized by **non-canonical base pairs, base stacking, and metal ion (Mg²⁺) coordination**.
- Example: the **L-shaped tertiary fold of tRNA**.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the core quantitative relationships and structural parameters for RNA — directly testable in KTU examinations.

| Parameter | Formula / Value | Unit | Engineering / Biological Significance |
|-----------|-----------------|------|---------------------------------------|
| Molecular weight of one ribonucleotide | $\approx 330$ | Da (g/mol) | Used in electrophoresis & sequencing calculations |
| Phosphate–sugar bond | 3'→5' phosphodiester | — | Defines directionality of strand |
| Chargaff-equivalent rule (RNA) | $A = U$ and $G = C$ | mole ratio | For double-stranded RNA regions only |
| RNA base composition | $\%A + \%U + \%G + \%C = 100$ | percent | Identity constraint for any RNA sample |
| Moles of nucleotide per OD₂₆₀ | $\approx 40 \, \mu g/mL$ per 1 OD | µg/mL | Spectrophotometric quantification |
| Tm (RNA duplex) relation | $T_m = 81.5 + 16.6\log_{10}[Na^+] + 0.41(\%G+C) - 600/L$ | °C | Predicts melting temperature of RNA duplexes |
| UV absorbance maximum (RNA) | $\lambda_{max} = 260$ | nm | Used for purity check (A260/A280 ratio) |
| Optical density ratio (pure RNA) | $A_{260}/A_{280} \approx 2.0$ | ratio | Purity benchmark (lower = protein contamination) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Use $\vert x \vert$ notation instead of $|x|$ inside any cell to avoid breaking the markdown table. Always remember that **RNA uses Uracil, not Thymine** — this single fact can cost 3 marks in Part A.

## 2.3 Why These Formulas Matter in Bioinformatics

- **Tm calculation** predicts the stability of RNA secondary structures (critical for **siRNA/miRNA drug design**, **antisense oligonucleotide therapeutics**, and **PCR primer design** for cDNA).
- **A260/A280 ratio** is a routine QC check in any molecular biology or bioinformatics wet-lab pipeline (e.g., before NGS library preparation).
- **Base composition statistics** are foundational for **GC-content analysis, codon usage bias, and RNA secondary structure prediction algorithms** (e.g., Mfold, RNAfold).

## 2.4 Types of RNA — The KTU Master Classification

| RNA Type | Full Name | Function | Size (nt) | KTU Relevance |
|----------|-----------|----------|-----------|---------------|
| **mRNA** | Messenger RNA | Carries protein-coding info from DNA to ribosome | 400–12,000 | Central Dogma |
| **tRNA** | Transfer RNA | Adapter — delivers amino acids to the ribosome | 73–93 | Translation |
| **rRNA** | Ribosomal RNA | Catalytic & structural core of the ribosome | 120–5,000 | Peptidyl transferase |
| **snRNA** | Small Nuclear RNA | Splicing of pre-mRNA (with proteins = snRNPs) | 100–200 | Post-transcriptional |
| **miRNA** | Micro RNA | Post-transcriptional gene silencing | 21–23 | RNA interference |
| **siRNA** | Small Interfering RNA | Defense against viruses & transposons | 20–25 | RNAi pathway |
| **lncRNA** | Long Non-Coding RNA | Chromatin remodeling, transcription regulation | > 200 | Epigenetics |
| **piRNA** | Piwi-interacting RNA | Transposon silencing in germ cells | 24–31 | Genome defense |
| **snoRNA** | Small Nucleolar RNA | Guides rRNA modification (methylation/pseudouridylation) | 60–300 | rRNA maturation |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** The syllabus emphasizes **mRNA, tRNA, rRNA** as the classical three, plus **miRNA and siRNA** for their bioinformatics significance. Expect a comparative question in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Chargaff-Type Rule Applied to RNA Duplexes

For any **double-stranded RNA** region (e.g., the stems of tRNA, or the RNA duplexes formed in RNAi), the **Chargaff analogue** for RNA can be derived directly from base-pairing stoichiometry.

### Given
- A double-stranded RNA (dsRNA) region of length $N$ base pairs.
- Each base pair consists of one purine hydrogen-bonded to one pyrimidine.
- Watson–Crick rules apply: A pairs with U, and G pairs with C.

### Step 1: Define base counts on the sense strand
Let:
- $a$ = number of Adenine bases on sense strand
- $u$ = number of Uracil bases on sense strand
- $g$ = number of Guanine bases on sense strand
- $c$ = number of Cytosine bases on sense strand

### Step 2: Apply the conservation constraint
The total number of bases on the sense strand equals the total length:

$$a + u + g + c = N$$

### Step 3: Apply Watson–Crick pairing on the antisense strand
Every A on the sense strand pairs with a U on the antisense strand, and vice versa. The same logic applies for G and C. Therefore, on the **antisense strand**:

- Number of U bases = number of A bases on the sense strand = $a$
- Number of A bases = number of U bases on the sense strand = $u$
- Number of C bases = number of G bases on the sense strand = $g$
- Number of G bases = number of C bases on the sense strand = $c$

### Step 4: Compute total counts over both strands

The total count of each base in the **entire dsRNA duplex** is the sum of both strands:

$$A_{total} = a + u$$

$$U_{total} = u + a$$

$$G_{total} = g + c$$

$$C_{total} = c + g$$

### Step 5: Deduce the equivalences
By inspection of the totals above, we directly obtain the **Chargaff-equivalent rule for RNA**:

$$A_{total} = U_{total}$$

$$G_{total} = C_{total}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Identity (KTU Exam Ready):**
> $$\boxed{A = U \quad \text{and} \quad G = C}$$
> This is the **RNA Chargaff rule** and applies **only to double-stranded RNA regions** — never to single-stranded mRNA, tRNA, or rRNA in their unfolded form.

## 3.2 Derivation: RNA Concentration from UV Absorbance

A standard wet-lab and bioinformatics quantification step.

### Step 1: Beer–Lambert Law

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$

where:
- $A$ = absorbance (unitless)
- $\varepsilon$ = molar extinction coefficient (L · mol⁻¹ · cm⁻¹)
- $c$ = concentration (mol/L)
- $l$ = path length (cm, typically 1 cm)

### Step 2: Standard absorbance for RNA

For a single-stranded RNA, an absorbance of **1.0 at 260 nm (A₂₆₀ = 1.0)** in a 1 cm cuvette corresponds to a concentration of approximately **40 µg/mL**.

### Step 3: Practical working formula

$$C_{RNA} \, [\mu g/mL] = A_{260} \times 40 \times \text{Dilution Factor}$$

### Step 4: Worked numerical example (typical KTU 3-mark problem)

**Problem:** A 5 µL RNA sample is diluted into 495 µL of nuclease-free water and measured at A₂₆₀ = 0.325. Calculate the RNA concentration in the original stock.

**Solution:**

- Dilution factor = $(495 + 5) / 5 = 500 / 5 = 100$
- Concentration in diluted sample = $0.325 \times 40 = 13 \, \mu g/mL$
- Concentration in original stock = $13 \times 100 = \mathbf{1300 \, \mu g/mL}$ (or **1.3 mg/mL**)

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Codon–Amino Acid Mapping (Central to tRNA Function)

A complete comparison of codon–anticodon recognition using the **Wobble Hypothesis** at the 3' anticodon position:

| 5' Codon Base | Possible 3' Anticodon Bases | Number of Codons Read |
|---------------|------------------------------|------------------------|
| A | U | 1 |
| C | G | 1 |
| G | C or U (Wobble) | 2 |
| U | A, G, or I (Inosine) | 3 |

This explains why the **3' end of tRNA** can use modified bases like **Inosine (I)** to broaden its pairing capacity, allowing **61 sense codons to be decoded by only ~31 tRNA isoacceptors** in bacteria.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Relevance:** The Wobble Hypothesis is a frequently repeated Part A question (2–3 marks) and occasionally appears in Part B as a sub-question (7 marks).

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Transcription (RNA Synthesis)

A canonical KTU 14-mark derivation topic.

**Step 1 — Initiation**
RNA polymerase (RNAP) binds the **promoter region** (e.g., TATA box at -10 and -35 in *E. coli*) with the help of sigma (σ) factor.

**Step 2 — Unwinding**
DNA double helix locally unwinds (~17 bp) to form the **transcription bubble**, exposing the template strand.

**Step 3 — Elongation**
RNAP moves 3'→5' along the **template strand** (antisense strand), synthesizing RNA 5'→3'. Incoming NTPs pair with template bases: A↔U, T↔A, G↔C, C↔G. The 2'-OH of the incoming NTP attacks the α-phosphate, releasing pyrophosphate (PPi).

**Step 4 — Termination**
Either **ρ (rho)-dependent** (in bacteria) or **ρ-independent** (hairpin + U-rich tract) termination signals release the nascent RNA.

**Step 5 — Processing (in eukaryotes)**
- 5' capping (7-methylguanosine)
- 3' polyadenylation (poly-A tail)
- **Splicing** of introns by the **spliceosome** (snRNAs U1, U2, U4, U5, U6)

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Flow of Genetic Information — Central Dogma

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["DNA MASTER BLUEPRINT"] -->|TRANSCRIPTION BY RNAP| nodeB["PREMESSENGER RNA"]
    nodeB -->|5 CAP AND 3 POLY A TAIL| nodeC["MATURE MRNA"]
    nodeC -->|TRANSLATION AT RIBOSOME| nodeD["PROTEIN"]
    nodeC -->|RNA INTERFERENCE| nodeE["GENE SILENCING COMPLEX"]
    nodeE -->|DEGRADATION| nodeF["MRNA DEGRADATION"]
    nodeD -->|FEEDBACK| nodeA
```

## 4.2 RNA Polymerase Catalytic Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["PROMOTER BINDING BY SIGMA FACTOR"] --> nodeS2["DNA UNWINDING TRANSCRIPTION BUBBLE"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["INITIATION NTP ADDITION"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["ELONGATION 5 TO 3 RNA GROWTH"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["TERMINATION HAIRPIN OR RHO"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6["PRIMARY RNA TRANSCRIPT RELEASED"]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7["POST TRANSCRIPTIONAL PROCESSING"]
    nodeS7 --> nodeS8["MATURE FUNCTIONAL RNA"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of the Three Major RNA Classes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TRANS["TRANSCRIPTION UNIT"]
        nodeT1["DNA TEMPLATE STRAND"]
    end
    subgraph TRANS2["PROCESSING NUCLEUS"]
        nodeP1["PREMESSENGER RNA"]
        nodeP2["SPLICEOSOME SNRNA"]
    end
    subgraph TRANS3["CYTOPLASM"]
        nodeR1["MATURE MRNA"]
        nodeR2["TRANSFER TRNA"]
        nodeR3["RIBOSOMAL RRNA"]
        nodeR4["RIBOSOME COMPLEX"]
    end
    nodeT1 -->|TRANSCRIBE| nodeP1
    nodeP1 -->|SPLICED BY| nodeP2
    nodeP1 -->|CAPPED AND POLYADENYLATED| nodeR1
    nodeR1 -->|BINDS| nodeR4
    nodeR2 -->|DELIVERS AMINO ACID TO| nodeR4
    nodeR3 -->|STRUCTURAL AND CATALYTIC CORE OF| nodeR4
    nodeR4 -->|SYNTHESIZES| nodeF1["POLYPEPTIDE CHAIN"]
```

## 4.4 RNA Interference (RNAi) Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeR1["DOUBLE STRANDED RNA PRECURSOR"] -->|DICER ENDONUCLEASE| nodeR2["SHORT SIRNA DUPLEX 21 TO 23 NT"]
    nodeR2 -->|LOADED INTO| nodeR3["RISC COMPLEX"]
    nodeR3 -->|STRAND SEPARATION| nodeR4["GUIDE STRAND RETAINED"]
    nodeR4 -->|BASE PAIR WITH TARGET MRNA| nodeR5["ARGONAUTE MEDIATED CLEAVAGE"]
    nodeR5 -->|RESULT| nodeR6["TRANSLATIONAL SILENCING OR MRNA DEGRADATION"]
```

## 4.5 tRNA Secondary Structure (Cloverleaf)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph nodeL1["ACCEPTOR STEM 7 BP 3 END CCA"]
    end
    subgraph nodeL2["D ARM DIHYDROURIDINE LOOP"]
    end
    subgraph nodeL3["ANTICODON ARM READS CODON"]
    end
    subgraph nodeL4["VARIABLE LOOP"]
    end
    subgraph nodeL5["TPSI C ARM RIBOTHYMIDINE"]
    end
    nodeL1 --- nodeL2
    nodeL2 --- nodeL3
    nodeL3 --- nodeL4
    nodeL4 --- nodeL5
    nodeL5 --- nodeL1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Reading Tip for KTU:** When asked to "diagram the cloverleaf structure of tRNA," examiners expect **all four arms labeled**, the **3' CCA acceptor end**, the **anticodon loop (positioned 34–36)**, and the **D loop and TΨC loop** identified by their conserved modified bases.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Differentiate between DNA and RNA at the chemical level. Why is uracil used in RNA instead of thymine?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | DNA | RNA |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Sugar | 2'-deoxyribose | D-ribose (2'-OH present) |
| Base replacing U/T | Thymine (5-methyluracil) | Uracil |
| Strands | Double-stranded (B-form helix) | Single-stranded (A-form when duplexed) |
| Function | Long-term genetic storage | Temporary, functional roles |

**Why Uracil and not Thymine?**
- Chemically, uracil is **simpler and energetically cheaper** to synthesize than thymine (which requires an extra methylation step using N⁵,N¹⁰-methylenetetrahydrofolate).
- Since RNA is **transient and produced in large quantities**, using uracil saves cellular energy.
- Thymine in DNA acts as a **protective methyl group** that guards against spontaneous deamination of cytosine (which would otherwise produce uracil and be misinterpreted). RNA is short-lived, so this protection is unnecessary.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the Wobble Hypothesis and its significance in tRNA function.**

**Model Answer:**

The Wobble Hypothesis was proposed by **Francis Crick (1966)** to explain how **61 sense codons** can be decoded by only **~31–40 tRNAs** in most organisms.

**Core Idea:**
The base at the **5' end of the anticodon** (the "wobble position," position 34) can form **non-standard base pairs** with the 3' end of the codon.

**Key Wobble Pairings at the 3' Codon Position:**

- G in anticodon → pairs with **C or U** in codon
- U in anticodon → pairs with **A or G** in codon
- **Inosine (I)** in anticodon (deaminated adenosine) → pairs with **A, U, or C** in codon

**Significance:**
- Reduces the number of tRNA genes required
- Provides **translational efficiency** and **error tolerance**
- Explains degeneracy of the genetic code

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe the structural features of transfer RNA (tRNA) with a neat, labeled cloverleaf diagram. Explain how the L-shaped tertiary structure is achieved.**

**Model Solution:**

**Structural Features of tRNA:**

1. **Length:** 73–93 nucleotides (average ~76 nt in eukaryotes).
2. **Single-stranded** RNA that folds into a characteristic **cloverleaf secondary structure**.
3. Contains **10–15 modified bases** (e.g., dihydrouridine, ribothymidine, pseudouridine, inosine).
4. The 3' end always terminates in the **CCA sequence**, which is the site of **amino acid attachment**.

**The Four Arms of the Cloverleaf:**

| Arm / Loop | Function |
|------------|----------|
| Acceptor stem (7 bp) | Carries the amino acid at 3' CCA |
| D arm (3–4 bp + D loop) | Contains dihydrouridine; recognized by aminoacyl-tRNA synthetase |
| Anticodon arm (5 bp + 7-nt loop) | Contains the anticodon (positions 34–36) that reads the mRNA codon |
