| TΨC arm (5 bp + 7-nt loop) | Contains the conserved sequence T-Ψ-C; binds to ribosomal RNA |
| Variable loop | 3–21 nt; classifies tRNAs into Class I (3–5 nt) or Class II (>5 nt) |

**Tertiary L-shaped Structure:**

- The cloverleaf folds into an **L-shape** of approximately 70 Å × 70 Å.
- This folding is stabilized by:
  - **Base stacking interactions**
  - **Non-Watson–Crick tertiary base pairs** (e.g., G19–C56)
  - **Mg²⁺ ion coordination**
- Functionally, the **acceptor stem** lies at one end of the L and the **anticodon** lies at the other end, with a **separation of ~70 Å** — the precise distance between the A site and the P site of the ribosome.

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare the biogenesis and cellular functions of mRNA, tRNA, and rRNA in eukaryotic cells.**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | mRNA | tRNA | rRNA |
|---------|------|------|------|
| Polymerase | RNA Pol II | RNA Pol III | RNA Pol I (28S, 18S, 5.8S); Pol III (5S) |
| Primary transcript | pre-mRNA | pre-tRNA | pre-rRNA (45S in humans) |
| Processing | 5' cap, 3' poly-A tail, splicing | 5' leader, 3' trailer removal, intron splicing (some), CCA addition, base modifications | Cleavage into 18S, 5.8S, 28S; 2'-O-methylation; pseudouridylation |
| Final length | Variable (few hundred to ~10,000 nt) | ~76 nt | 28S (~5,000), 18S (~1,900), 5.8S (~160), 5S (~120) |
| Function | Template for protein synthesis at the ribosome | Adapter molecule delivering amino acids to the ribosome | Catalytic (peptidyl transferase) and structural core of the ribosome |
| Site of action | Cytoplasm (translation) | Cytoplasm | Nucleolus (assembly) → cytoplasm |
| Stability | Highly variable (min to hours) | Moderately stable | Long-lived |
| % of total cellular RNA | ~3–5% | ~10–15% | ~80% |

**Key Synthesis Insight:**
- **rRNA** accounts for the **majority of cellular RNA mass** because each cell contains ~10⁷ ribosomes.
- **tRNA** has the most **chemical modifications per nucleotide**, making it a biochemical "Rosetta stone" for translation fidelity.
- **mRNA** is the **most diverse** in sequence and the **least stable**, allowing dynamic gene expression control.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the process of transcription in prokaryotes with a neat, labeled diagram. Highlight the role of sigma (σ) factor.**

**Model Solution:**

**Transcription in Prokaryotes — Three Stages:**

**Stage 1 — Initiation:**
- The **RNA polymerase core enzyme** (α₂ββ'ω subunits) binds the σ factor to form the **holoenzyme**.
- The σ factor recognizes the **promoter** at the **-35 (TTGACA)** and **-10 (TATAAT)** consensus sequences.
- The DNA locally unwinds, forming the **transcription bubble** (~17 bp).
- The first phosphodiester bond forms (typically a purine — A or G).

**Stage 2 — Elongation:**
- The σ factor dissociates after synthesis of the first ~8–10 nt.
- RNA polymerase moves 3'→5' along the **template (antisense) strand**.
- New NTPs are added to the 3'-OH of the growing chain.
- The RNA emerges in the **5'→3' direction**.
- A short **RNA-DNA hybrid** (~8 bp) forms transiently in the active site.

**Stage 3 — Termination:**
- **ρ-independent (intrinsic) termination:** A G-C-rich hairpin followed by a U-rich tract destabilizes the RNA-DNA hybrid, releasing the transcript.
- **ρ-dependent termination:** The **rho (ρ) helicase** binds a C-rich "rut" site, translocates 5'→3' on the RNA, and unwinds the hybrid using ATP hydrolysis.

**Role of σ Factor:**
- **Promoter recognition** and specificity
- **Strand separation** at the -10 region
- **Dissociation** after initiation to allow processive elongation
- Different σ factors (σ⁷⁰ housekeeping, σ³² heat shock, σ⁵⁴ nitrogen) regulate gene expression under different conditions

**(b) [7 Marks] Describe the mechanism of RNA interference (RNAi). Compare miRNA and siRNA in terms of origin, mechanism, and biological function.**

**Model Solution:**

**RNA Interference Mechanism:**

1. **Trigger:** Introduction of exogenous dsRNA (e.g., viral, transposon) or endogenous hairpin precursors.
2. **Dicing:** The **Dicer** endonuclease (RNase III family) cleaves dsRNA into **21–23 nt siRNA/miRNA duplexes** with 2-nt 3' overhangs.
3. **Loading:** Duplex is loaded into the **RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC)**, with **Argonaute (Ago)** as the catalytic core.
4. **Strand selection:** The **passenger strand** is discarded; the **guide strand** is retained.
5. **Target recognition:** The guide strand base-pairs with complementary mRNA.
6. **Silencing:**
   - **Perfect complementarity** → Ago endonuclease cleaves the mRNA (siRNA mode)
   - **Partial complementarity** → translational repression + mRNA destabilization (miRNA mode)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | miRNA | siRNA |
|---------|-------|-------|
| Origin | Endogenous (introns, intergenic) | Exogenous (transfection) or endogenous transposons |
| Precursor | pre-miRNA (stem-loop, ~70 nt hairpin) | Long dsRNA or bimolecular duplex |
| Dicer product | ~22 nt imperfect duplex | ~21 nt perfect duplex |
| Complementarity to target | Partial (seed region nt 2–8) | Perfect / near-perfect |
| Genomic targets | Hundreds of mRNAs (pleiotropic) | Single specific mRNA |
| Biological role | Developmental gene regulation, cell differentiation | Defense against viruses & transposons |
| Therapeutic use | Anti-cancer, anti-metabolic | Antiviral, anti-oncogene (e.g., siRNA drugs) |
| Pathway location | Nucleus → cytoplasm | Mostly cytoplasmic |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing miRNA and siRNA** — these are the most confused pair in KTU papers. Memorize the **origin, complementarity, and pleiotropy** distinctions.
> 2. **Forgetting the Wobble position number (34)** — writing "anticodon loop" is incomplete; specify the **5' end of the anticodon** as the wobble site.
> 3. **Writing "RNA polymerase" without specifying Pol I, II, or III** in eukaryotic transcription questions — examiners expect the **specific polymerase** for full marks.
> 4. **Using Thymine instead of Uracil** in RNA base-pairing questions — automatic loss of at least 1 mark.
> 5. **Omitting the CCA tail** when diagramming tRNA — this is the **aminoacylation site** and is mandatory in any labeled structure.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Exam Day Notes — RNAs**

- ☐ RNA is **single-stranded**, with **2'-OH on ribose** and **Uracil replacing Thymine**.
- ☐ The **phosphodiester bond** links the **3'-OH of one ribose** to the **5'-phosphate of the next**.
- ☐ **A-form helix** when dsRNA forms (not B-form as in DNA).
- ☐ **G–U wobble pair** is a unique RNA secondary structure feature, absent in DNA.
- ☐ **Three classical RNA types:** mRNA (template), tRNA (adapter), rRNA (catalytic + structural).
- ☐ **Chargaff's rule for dsRNA:** A = U, G = C.
- ☐ **Tertiary structure of tRNA** is an **L-shape** with the **acceptor end** and **anticodon** at opposite termini, separated by ~70 Å.
- ☐ **Wobble position = position 34** of the anticodon (the 5' end).
- ☐ **Inosine (I)** at the wobble position can pair with **A, U, or C** in the codon.
- ☐ **Transcription:** RNA is synthesized **5'→3'**, template read **3'→5'**.
- ☐ **Eukaryotic mRNA processing:** 5' cap + 3' poly-A tail + splicing (by **spliceosome** with snRNAs U1, U2, U4, U5, U6).
- ☐ **Prokaryotic transcription:** σ factor for promoter recognition; ρ factor for termination.
- ☐ **RNAi pathway:** Dicer cleaves dsRNA → RISC loaded → guide strand → Ago-mediated silencing.
- ☐ **miRNA = partial complementarity, pleiotropic (many targets); siRNA = perfect complementarity, single target.**
- ☐ **A₂₆₀ = 1.0 ↔ 40 µg/mL RNA** (single-stranded) — standard quantification rule.
- ☐ **Pure RNA A₂₆₀/A₂₈₀ ratio ≈ 2.0**; lower indicates protein contamination.
- ☐ **rRNA constitutes ~80% of total cellular RNA mass.**
- ☐ **Catalytic activity of the ribosome (peptidyl transferase) is performed by rRNA, not protein** — the ribosome is a **ribozyme**.
- ☐ The **5' cap is a 7-methylguanosine** connected by a **5'-5' triphosphate bridge** — a unique biochemical linkage.
- ☐ **Poly-A tail** enhances mRNA stability and translation efficiency via **PABP (Poly-A Binding Protein)**.
- ☐ Always specify the **correct RNA polymerase** (I, II, III) when answering eukaryotic transcription questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proteins
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Proteins ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Definition of Proteins (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned) > [!NOTE] > **Formal Definition (KTU Standard):** > Proteins are **macromolecular biopolymers** composed of one or more **polypeptide chains**, each built from a linear sequence of **α-amino acids** linked together by **peptide (amide) bonds**. They are the **principal functional and structural molecules** of the cell, executing catalysis, transport, signaling, immune defense, and mechanical support. From a bioinformatics perspective, proteins are modeled as **1D sequences over a 20-letter alphabet** (the 20 standard amino acids) that fold into specific **3D conformations** under the influence of physico-chemical forces. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (The "Origami Letter" Analogy) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain English Intuition:** > Think of a protein as a **letter written on a long strip of paper**, where the alphabet has only **20 special letters** (the 20 amino acids). > - The **sequence of letters** = **Primary structure** (which letter comes first, second, third...). > - The way the strip **curls into a spiral** as you hold it = **Secondary structure** (α-helices, β-sheets). > - The way the curled strip **crumples into a 3D ball or shape** = **Tertiary structure**. > - Multiple crumpled balls **stacking together** = **Quaternary structure**. > Just like the meaning of the letter depends on how you fold the paper, the **function of the protein depends on its 3D shape**, which in turn depends on the sequence. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Key Numerical Standards in Protein Chemistry:** > - **Average molecular weight of an amino acid residue ≈ 110 Da (Daltons)** > - **Peptide bond length = 1.33 Å** (intermediate between single and double bond) > - **α-amino acids in nature = 20 (standard, encoded by genetic code)** > - **Bond angles:** φ (phi) torsion around N–Cα, ψ (psi) torsion around Cα–C' > - **Resonance energy of peptide bond ≈ 85 kJ/mol** (gives it partial double-bond character) ### 1.4 The 20 Standard Amino Acids – The Protein Alphabet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Concept:** > All proteins in living organisms are constructed from the same set of **20 standard α-amino acids**. Each amino acid shares a **common backbone** (–NH₂–CαH(R)–COOH–) and differs only in the **side chain (R-group)**. It is the R-group that determines each amino acid's chemistry — its size, charge, hydrophobicity, and ability to form hydrogen bonds or disulfide bridges. **General Structure of an α-Amino Acid:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{H}_2\text{N} - \overset{\alpha}{\text{C}}\text{H(R)} - \text{COOH} \end{aligned} $$ Where: - **H₂N–** = amino group (proton donor/acceptor) - **–COOH** = carboxyl group (acidic proton) - **Cα** = central (alpha) carbon — the chiral center - **R** = variable side chain (defines identity) > [!NOTE] > **Classification of 20 Amino Acids by R-Group Polarity (Bioinformatics View):** > - **Non-polar (Hydrophobic):** Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Pro, Phe, Trp, Met > - **Polar Uncharged:** Ser, Thr, Cys, Tyr, Asn, Gln > - **Positively Charged (Basic):** Lys, Arg, His > - **Negatively Charged (Acidic):** Asp, Glu ## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET ### 2.1 The Peptide Bond – The Fundamental Linker > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** > A **peptide bond** is the **amide bond (–CO–NH–)** formed by a **condensation (dehydration) reaction** between the **α-carboxyl group of one amino acid** and the **α-amino group of the next**, releasing one molecule of water. **Formation Reaction:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_1\text{–COOH} \; + \; \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_2\text{–COOH} \\ \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{N–CHR}_1\text{–CO–NH–CHR}_2\text{–COOH} \; + \; \text{H}_2\text{O} \end{aligned} $$ **Why Peptide Bonds Are Planar and Rigid (KTU Favorite Question):** - The peptide bond exhibits **partial double-bond character** due to **resonance** between the carbonyl oxygen and the amide nitrogen. - This restricts free rotation and forces the **six atoms (Cα, C, O, N, H, Cα') to lie in a single plane** — known as the **peptide plane**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{C=O} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{C–O}^- \\ \text{C–N} \;\;\longleftrightarrow\;\; \text{C=N}^+ \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Resulting Polarity:** The peptide bond is **electrically polar** — the oxygen carries a partial negative charge (δ⁻) and the nitrogen carries a partial positive charge (δ⁺). This polarity is the basis for **hydrogen bonding in secondary structure**. ### 2.2 The Four Levels of Protein Architecture (Anfinsen's Hierarchy) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Central Dogma of Protein Folding (Anfinsen, 1973 — Nobel Prize 1972):** > *"The amino acid sequence of a protein contains all the information needed to specify its native 3D structure."* This is the **thermodynamic hypothesis** — sequence → structure → function. | Level | Description | Stabilizing Forces | Example | |-------|-------------|---------------------|---------| | **Primary (1°)** | Linear sequence of amino acids linked by peptide bonds | **Covalent peptide bonds** | Insulin A-chain, Myoglobin sequence | | **Secondary (2°)** | Local regular folding into α-helices and β-sheets | **Hydrogen bonds** between backbone C=O and N–H | α-helix of keratin, β-sheet of silk | | **Tertiary (3°)** | Overall 3D shape of a single polypeptide chain | **Hydrophobic interactions, disulfide bridges, ionic bonds, H-bonds, van der Waals** | Folded globular myoglobin, lysozyme | | **Quaternary (4°)** | Assembly of multiple folded polypeptide subunits | **Same as 3°** plus subunit-subunit interfaces | Hemoglobin (4 subunits), DNA polymerase | ### 2.3 Secondary Structure Elements (α-Helix & β-Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **α-Helix (Pauling & Corey, 1951):** > - **3.6 amino acid residues per turn** > - **Pitch (rise per turn) = 5.4 Å** > - **Rise per residue = 1.5 Å** > - **H-bond pattern:** C=O of residue *i* bonds to N–H of residue *i+4* > - **Side chains project outward** from the helical axis > [!NOTE] > **β-Sheet (Pauling & Corey, 1951):** > - **Strands are extended (not coiled)** > - **H-bonds are formed between adjacent strands (inter-strand)**, not within a strand > - **Two orientations:** > - **Parallel β-sheet:** strands run N→C in same direction > - **Antiparallel β-sheet:** strands run in opposite directions > - Side chains alternate **above and below** the plane of the sheet ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Equations and Parameters for Protein Calculations in KTU Exams:** | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Value / Unit | Application | |---------------------|------------|---------------|-------------| | Average residue weight | $M_{\text{residue}} \approx 110 \text{ Da}$ | Daltons (Da) | Estimating protein MW from sequence length $n$ | | Estimated protein MW | $M_{\text{protein}} \approx (n - 1) \times 110 + 18$ | Da (or kDa) | For a sequence of $n$ residues (water correction) | | Peptide bond length | $d_{\text{peptide}} = 1.33$ | Ångström (Å) | Structure of peptide plane | | α-Helix rise per residue | $h_{\alpha} = 1.5$ | Å | α-helix geometry | | α-Helix residues per turn | $n_{\alpha} = 3.6$ | residues/turn | α-helix geometry | | α-Helix pitch | $P_{\alpha} = 5.4$ | Å | α-helix geometry | | Number of water molecules released | $n_{\text{water}} = n - 1$ | molecules | Forming a peptide of $n$ residues | | pI (isoelectric point) | $\text{pI} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$ (for neutral AA) | dimensionless | Zwitterion pH; net charge = 0 | | Molar extinction coefficient | $\varepsilon_{280} = n_{\text{Trp}} \times 5500 + n_{\text{Tyr}} \times 1490$ | $\text{M}^{-1}\text{cm}^{-1}$ | UV absorbance at 280 nm (concentration) | | Henderson–Hasselbalch | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}\right)$ | dimensionless | Titration of ionizable groups | > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE FOR KTU ANSWERS:** > - When writing **absolute value** or **modulus** (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) in tables or plain text, always use **$\vert x \vert$** (LaTeX vert) — never use the unescaped pipe character $\vert$ in markdown tables, as it breaks the column structure. Same for set-builder notation: use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Bioinformatics Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where this knowledge applies in industry & research:** > - **Drug Design (CADD):** Knowing α-helix / β-sheet geometry helps design peptide-mimetic inhibitors. > - **Homology Modeling (Swiss-Model, MODELLER):** Aligning target sequence to a template with known secondary structure assigns α/β regions. > - **AlphaFold2 (DeepMind, 2020–2021):** Predicts 3D folding directly from sequence — uses the Anfinsen principle computationally. > - **Proteomics Mass Spec:** Peptide mass = Σ(residue masses) + 18 — same as the formula above. > - **Biopharma:** Recombinant insulin, monoclonal antibodies — sequence determines structure determines therapeutic function. ## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ### 3.1 Derivation: Estimating Molecular Weight of a Protein from Its Sequence > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** Given a polypeptide chain of **n** amino acid residues, derive an expression for the approximate molecular weight, accounting for the water molecules lost during peptide bond formation. --- **Step 1 — Total mass of free amino acids (before peptide formation):** Let the **average molecular weight of a free amino acid** be $M_{\text{AA}}$. For a chain of $n$ amino acids: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{total, free}} = n \cdot M_{\text{AA}} \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Each amino acid in its free (unbonded) form contributes its full mass. --- **Step 2 — Number of peptide bonds formed:** In a linear chain of $n$ amino acids, the number of peptide bonds is always **one less than the number of residues**: $$ \begin{aligned} n_{\text{peptide bonds}} = n - 1 \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** $n$ residues require $(n-1)$ connections to form a single linear chain (just like $n$ fence posts need $n-1$ segments). --- **Step 3 — Mass of water released:** Each peptide bond formation releases **exactly one water molecule (H₂O, MW = 18 Da)**: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{water released}} = (n - 1) \times 18 \text{ Da} \end{aligned} $$ --- **Step 4 — Net mass of the polypeptide:** The polypeptide's mass = total free mass − mass of water lost: $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{protein}} &= n \cdot M_{\text{AA}} - (n - 1) \times 18 \end{aligned} $$ --- **Step 5 — Substitute the standard average residue mass (110 Da):** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{protein}} &= n \times 110 - (n - 1) \times 18 \\ &= 110n - 18n + 18 \\ &= 92n + 18 \end{aligned} $$ --- **Final Compact Formula:** $$ \boxed{\,M_{\text{protein}} \approx 92\,n + 18 \; \text{Da}\,} $$ > **Numerical sanity check:** For $n = 100$ residues, $M \approx 9200 + 18 = 9218$ Da $\approx 9.2$ kDa — which is reasonable for a small globular protein. ### 3.2 Worked Example (Analytical Matrix Style) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sample KTU-Style Problem:** > A purified protein from *E. coli* is sequenced and found to contain **287 amino acid residues**. Calculate its approximate molecular weight in **kDa**. **Given:** $n = 287$ residues **Apply the formula:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{protein}} &= 92n + 18 \\ &= 92 \times 287 + 18 \\ &= 26404 + 18 \\ &= 26422 \text{ Da} \end{aligned} $$ **Convert to kDa:** $$ \begin{aligned} M_{\text{protein}} &= \dfrac{26422}{1000} = 26.422 \text{ kDa} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer:** $\boxed{M \approx 26.42 \text{ kDa}}$ > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** Always state the formula first, then substitute. Show units. Final answer should include the **kDa** conversion step for full marks. ### 3.3 Derivation: Why the Peptide Bond is Rigid and Planar (Resonance Argument) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Draw both resonance structures of the peptide bond:** The peptide bond (–C(=O)–N(H)–) can be written as two resonance contributors: **Structure A (major, neutral form):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{O=C–N–H} \quad (\text{40% weight}) \end{aligned} $$ **Structure B (minor, charge-separated form):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{}^-\text{O–C=N}^+\text{–H} \quad (\text{60% weight}) \end{aligned} $$ > **Logic:** Oxygen is more electronegative than nitrogen, so the lone pair on N delocalizes toward O, giving structure B significant weight. --- **Step 2 — Bond order consequences:** The **C–N bond** gains partial double-bond character: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bond order}_{\text{C–N}} = 1 + (\text{weight of B}) = 1 + 0.6 = 1.6 \end{aligned} $$ > **Result:** Rotation around this bond is **energetically forbidden** under physiological conditions (barrier ≈ 85 kJ/mol). --- **Step 3 — Geometric consequences:** - The C–N bond shortens from a typical single-bond length of **1.45 Å** to **1.33 Å** (the actual measured peptide bond length). - The six atoms **Cα–C(=O)–N(H)–Cα** are **coplanar** — this is the **peptide plane** (also called the **amide plane**). - Free rotation is only possible around the **N–Cα** bond (torsion angle **φ, phi**) and the **Cα–C** bond (torsion angle **ψ, psi**). > [!NOTE] > **Ramachandran Plot Connection:** The restriction to φ and ψ makes the 3D fold a function of just two dihedral angles per residue — visualized in the famous **Ramachandran plot** (sterically allowed φ/ψ regions). ### 3.4 Derivation: Isoelectric Point (pI) of a Neutral Amino Acid > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step 1 — Identify the two relevant pKₐ values:** > - $\text{p}K_{a1}$ = pKₐ of the α-carboxyl group (–COOH → –COO⁻ + H⁺), typically ≈ 2.0 > - $\text{p}K_{a2}$ = pKₐ of the α-amino group (–NH₃⁺ → –NH₂ + H⁺), typically ≈ 9.0 --- **Step 2 — Net charge at low pH (< pKₐ1):** The molecule is **+1** (protonated). **Step 3 — At pH between pKₐ1 and pKₐ2:** The COOH has deprotonated, NH₃⁺ is still protonated → **net charge = 0 (zwitterion).** **Step 4 — At high pH (> pKₐ2):** Both groups deprotonated → **net charge = −1.** > **Logic:** The zwitterion (charge = 0) exists at the **midpoint** between the two pKₐ values. --- **Step 5 — pI is the average of the two pKₐ values:** $$ \boxed{\,\text{pI} = \dfrac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}\,} $$ > **Example for Glycine:** pI = (2.34 + 9.60) / 2 = **5.97** ✓ (literature value: 5.97) ## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hierarchical Levels of Protein Structure ```mermaid flowchart TD A0["Primary Structure
Linear AA sequence
Peptide bonds"] --> B0["Secondary Structure
Alpha helix / Beta sheet
H-bonds in backbone"] B0 --> C0["Tertiary Structure
3D fold of one chain
Hydrophobic / disulfide / ionic"] C0 --> D0["Quaternary Structure
Multiple subunits
Subunit interfaces"] A0a["Sequence of 20 AAs"] --> A0 B0a["Local regular patterns"] --> B0 C0a["Global 3D shape"] --> C0 D0a["Oligomeric assembly"] --> D0 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Peptide Bond Formation (Condensation Reaction) ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeAA1["Amino Acid 1
H2N-CHR1-COOH"] -->|"Condensation
-H2O"| nodePB["Peptide Bond
-CO-NH-"] nodeAA2["Amino Acid 2
H2N-CHR2-COOH"] -->|"Condensation
-H2O"| nodePB nodePB --> nodeDipep["Dipeptide
H2N-CHR1-CO-NH-CHR2-COOH"] nodeH2O["Water H2O
Released"]:::waste nodeAA1 -.-> nodeH2O nodeAA2 -.-> nodeH2O classDef waste fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: α-Helix vs. β-Sheet Hydrogen-Bonding Topology ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph helixBlock["ALPHA HELIX - Intra-chain H-bonds"] h1["Residue i C=O"] -->|"H-bond"| h2["Residue i+4 N-H"] h2 -->|"H-bond"| h3["Residue i+8 N-H"] end subgraph sheetBlock["BETA SHEET - Inter-strand H-bonds"] s1["Strand 1 C=O"] -->|"H-bond"| s2["Strand 2 N-H"] s2 -->|"H-bond"| s3["Strand 1 N-H"] end helixBlock --> noteH["3.6 residues per turn
5.4 angstrom pitch
1.5 angstrom rise per residue"] sheetBlock --> noteS["Parallel or Antiparallel
Strands extended
Side chains alternate"] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture — From Gene to Functional Protein ```mermaid flowchart LR gene["DNA Coding Sequence"] -->|"Transcription"| mrna["mRNA Codons"] mrna -->|"Translation
Ribosome"| chain["Polypeptide Chain
Primary Structure"] chain -->|"Spontaneous Folding
Anfinsen Principle"| folded["3D Native Structure
Tertiary"] folded -->|"Often + Cofactors"| active["Functional Protein
Enzyme / Receptor / Structural"] folded -->|"Multi-subunit Assembly"| complex["Quaternary Complex
Hemoglobin / Polymerase"] active --> bio["Biological Function"] complex --> bio misfolded["Misfolded Protein"] -.->|"Misfolding Disease"| disease["Alzheimer / Parkinson / CJD"] folded -.-> misfolded ``` ### 4.5 Mermaid Diagram: Bioinformatics Analysis Pipeline for a Protein Sequence ```mermaid flowchart TD seq["Input: Protein FASTA Sequence"] --> blast["BLASTp
Homology Search"] seq --> pfam["Pfam / InterPro
Domain Detection"] seq --> pred2d["PSIPRED / JPred
Secondary Structure Prediction"] seq --> pred3d["AlphaFold2 / Swiss-Model
3D Structure Prediction"] seq --> sigp["SignalP / DeepSig
Subcellular Localization"] blast --> annot["Functional Annotation"] pfam --> annot pred2d --> annot pred3d --> annot sigp --> annot annot --> report["Bioinformatics Report"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** > **Define the following with respect to protein structure:** > **(a) Primary structure**    **(b) α-Helix**    **(c) Denaturation** **Model Answer:** **(a) Primary structure (1 mark):** The **linear sequence of amino acids** in a polypeptide chain, linked together by **peptide (covalent) bonds**. It is determined by the nucleotide sequence of the corresponding gene. Example: the 51-residue sequence of insulin. **(b) α-Helix (1 mark):** A common **secondary structure motif** in proteins, in which the polypeptide backbone is wound into a **right-handed helix** with **3.6 amino acid residues per turn** and a **pitch of 5.4 Å**. Stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** between the C=O of residue *i* and the N–H of residue *i+4*. Side chains project outward from the helical axis. **(c) Denaturation (1 mark):** The process by which a protein **loses its native 3D structure (and hence its function)** due to disruption of **non-covalent interactions** (and sometimes disulfide bonds) by external agents such as **heat, pH extremes, urea, or organic solvents**. The primary sequence (peptide bonds) remains intact. Often (but not always) **irreversible**, e.g., cooking egg white. --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** > **What is a peptide bond? Explain its partial double-bond character.** **Model Answer:** A **peptide bond** is the **amide linkage (–CO–NH–)** formed between the **α-carboxyl group of one amino acid** and the **α-amino group of the next**, with the **elimination of one water molecule** (condensation reaction). The peptide bond exhibits **partial double-bond character** (1 mark) due to **resonance** between two structures: - The **neutral form** O=C–N–H - The **charge-separated form** ⁻O–C=N⁺–H This resonance gives the C–N bond a **bond order of ~1.6** (1 mark), restricts rotation, and forces the six atoms of the peptide group into a **rigid planar configuration** called the **peptide plane**. As a result, the bond length is **1.33 Å**, intermediate between a C–N single bond (1.45 Å) and a C=N double bond (1.25 Å). ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **Choose ONE of the following two questions:** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main]** > **(a)** Describe in detail the **four levels of protein structure** with suitable diagrams. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** What is the **α-helix**? Explain its **geometric parameters** and the **hydrogen-bonding pattern** that stabilizes it. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Four levels of protein structure (7 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Primary structure (1.5 marks):** > - The **linear covalent sequence** of amino acids joined by **peptide bonds**. > - Determined by the gene (DNA → mRNA → protein). > - **Stabilized by:** strong covalent bonds only. > - Example: Insulin A-chain has 21 residues in a specific order. > - One change (e.g., Glu→Val at position 6) → sickle-cell anemia. > **Secondary structure (1.5 marks):** > - **Local, regular folding** of the backbone into recurring patterns. > - Two major types: **α-helix** and **β-sheet** (β-strands). > - **Stabilized by:** hydrogen bonds between **backbone** C=O and N–H groups. > - Example: α-keratin (helical), fibroin/silk (β-sheet). > **Tertiary structure (2 marks):** > - The **overall 3D shape** of a single polypeptide chain. > - Brings together distant residues into a compact **globular** or **fibrous** form. > - **Stabilized by:** hydrophobic interactions (core), H-bonds, ionic/salt bridges, disulfide bonds (–S–S– between cysteines), van der Waals forces. > - Example: Myoglobin — a single folded globular chain with a heme group. > **Quaternary structure (2 marks):** > - The **association of two or more folded polypeptide subunits** into a functional oligomer. > - **Stabilized by:** the same forces as tertiary structure, plus subunit–subunit interfaces. > - Example: **Hemoglobin (Hb)** = 2 α-chains + 2 β-chains (heterotetramer, α₂β₂). > - DNA polymerase III has 10 subunits in *E. coli*. **Diagram (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid Diagram):** All four levels may be drawn as a flowchart showing the hierarchical relationship. --- **(b) α-Helix: Geometry & Hydrogen Bonding (7 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** > The **α-helix** is a right-handed helical secondary structure discovered by **Pauling and Corey (1951)**. It is the most common regular secondary structure in globular proteins. > **Geometric Parameters (3 marks):** > - **Residues per turn:** $n_{\alpha} = 3.6$ > - **Pitch (rise per turn):** $P_{\alpha} = 5.4$ Å > - **Rise per residue:** $h_{\alpha} = 1.5$ Å > - **Helix sense:** right-handed (most common in nature; L-amino acids favor it). > - **Side-chain orientation:** project **outward** from the central helical axis, away from the backbone. > - **φ, ψ angles:** approximately φ ≈ −60°, ψ ≈ −45° (within the allowed region of the Ramachandran plot). > **Hydrogen-Bonding Pattern (3 marks):** > - **Donor:** N–H group of residue $i+4$ > - **Acceptor:** C=O group of residue $i$ > - All backbone C=O and N–H groups participate in H-bonds (except the first 4 N–H and last 4 C=O at the termini). > - H-bonds are **intrahelical** (within a single chain), aligned **parallel to the helix axis**. > - Net dipole moment: partial positive charge at the N-terminus and partial negative at the C-terminus. > - Disruption by **proline** (no N–H to donate) and **glycine** (too flexible) — these are "helix breakers." --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Alternative]** > **(a)** Explain the **structure of an amino acid**. List the **20 standard amino acids** classified by the polarity of their side chains. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** Derive the **molecular weight of a protein** of $n$ amino acid residues. A 250-residue protein is purified — what is its approximate molecular weight? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Structure of an amino acid & classification (7 marks):** > **General structure (2 marks):** > An α-amino acid has the form: > $$\text{H}_2\text{N–C}_{\alpha}\text{H(R)–COOH}$$ > - **Amino group (–NH₂):** basic, can accept a proton → –NH₃⁺ > - **Carboxyl group (–COOH):** acidic, can donate a proton → –COO⁻ > - **α-Carbon (Cα):** chiral (except glycine) — gives L- and D- stereoisomers; **only L-amino acids are incorporated into proteins** by ribosomal translation. > - **Side chain (R):** the variable group — the **only feature** that distinguishes the 20 standard amino acids. > **Classification by R-group polarity (5 marks — list all 20):** | Polarity Class | Amino Acids (3-letter / 1-letter) | Key Feature | |----------------|----------------------------------|-------------| | **Non-polar (Hydrophobic)** | Gly (G), Ala (A), Val (V), Leu (L), Ile (I), Pro (P), Phe (F), Trp (W), Met (M) | R-groups are hydrocarbon or aromatic; cluster in protein core | | **Polar Uncharged** | Ser (S), Thr (T), Cys (C), Tyr (Y), Asn (N), Gln (Q) | Can form H-bonds; Cys can form disulfide bonds | | **Positively Charged (Basic)** | Lys (K), Arg (R), His (H) | R-group has +ve charge at physiological pH | | **Negatively Charged (Acidic)** | Asp (D), Glu (E) | R-group has –ve charge at physiological pH | > **Special amino acids worth mentioning (bonus):** > - **Glycine (G):** R = H (smallest, no chirality) > - **Proline (P):** R bonds back to the amino N → creates a **rigid ring**; disrupts helices. > - **Cysteine (C):** Forms **disulfide (–S–S–) bonds** — only covalent side-chain crosslink in proteins. --- **(b) Molecular weight derivation (7 marks):** > **Derivation (4 marks):** see Section 3.1 for full step-by-step derivation, leading to: > $$\boxed{M_{\text{protein}} \approx 92n + 18 \text{ Da}}$$ > **Numerical application (3 marks):** > Given: $n = 250$ residues. > > Substitute into the formula: > $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{protein}} &= 92 \times 250 + 18 \\ &= 23000 + 18 \\ &= 23018 \text{ Da} \end{aligned}$$ > > Converting to **kDa**: > $$\begin{aligned} M_{\text{protein}} &= \dfrac{23018}{1000} \\ &= 23.018 \text{ kDa} \end{aligned}$$ > > **Final Answer:** $\boxed{M_{\text{protein}} \approx 23.02 \text{ kDa}}$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification with sanity check:** A 250-residue protein of ~23 kDa is in the typical size range of small monomeric enzymes (e.g., lysozyme is ~14 kDa, ribonuclease A is ~13.7 kDa; 23 kDa is reasonable). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams — Proteins Module:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract water mass:** Students often write $M = 110n$ and lose 2–3 marks. Always use $M \approx 92n + 18$ (or $M = 110n - 18(n-1)$). > 2. **Confusing α-helix pitch with rise per residue:** Pitch = 5.4 Å (per turn); rise per residue = 1.5 Å. The relationship: $5.4 = 3.6 \times 1.5$. > 3. **Mixing up "secondary" and "tertiary" structure:** Secondary = local regular patterns (α/β); Tertiary = full 3D fold of a single chain. This is a classic 1–2 mark trap. > 4. **Wrong H-bond direction in α-helix:** It is C=O of residue $i$ to N–H of residue $i+4$ — *not* $i-4$ or $i+3$. > 5. **Omitting diagrams:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for well-labeled flowcharts. Always include at least a block diagram of the four structure levels. > 6. **Forgetting units (Da vs. kDa):** Convert explicitly. $1 \text{ kDa} = 1000 \text{ Da}$. > 7. **Calling proline a "helix promoter":** Proline is a **helix breaker** because its cyclic structure prevents N–H donation. > 8. **Forgetting that denaturation is (often) irreversible:** Renaturation is possible (Anfinsen experiment with ribonuclease) but not always. Don't make blanket statements. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Proteins Module 1:** **🔹 Core Definitions** - **Amino acid:** $\text{H}_2\text{N–C}_{\alpha}\text{H(R)–COOH}$; 20 standard types, all L- (except in rare bacterial cases) - **Peptide bond:** –CO–NH– covalent amide linkage; formed by condensation (–H₂O) - **Residue:** an amino acid incorporated into a peptide (after losing H from –NH₂ and OH from –COOH, net loss of 18 Da per bond) - **Polypeptide:** chain of many residues; **Protein** = functional 3D unit (one or more polypeptides) **🔹 The 20 Amino Acids (Memory Aid)** - **Hydrophobic 9:** GAVLIMP-FW (Gly, Ala, Val, Leu, Ile, Met, Pro, Phe, Trp) - **Polar 6:** STYC-NQ (Ser, Thr, Tyr, Cys, Asn, Gln) - **Positive 3:** H-K-R (His, Lys, Arg) - **Negative 2:** D-E (Asp, Glu) - **Special:** Gly (no chirality), Pro (cyclic, helix breaker), Cys (disulfide bond) **🔹 Four Structure Levels** - **1°:** Sequence — peptide bonds - **2°:** α-helix (3.6 res/turn, 5.4 Å pitch, 1.5 Å rise, $i → i+4$ H-bond) | β-sheet (parallel / antiparallel, inter-strand H-bonds) - **3°:** 3D fold — hydrophobic core, H-bonds, ionic, disulfide, vdW - **4°:** Multimeric assembly — same forces + subunit interfaces **🔹 Key Numbers to Memorize** - Average residue mass = **110 Da** - Net peptide bond loss = **18 Da** per bond - α-helix: **3.6 / 5.4 / 1.5** (residues per turn / pitch / rise per residue) - Peptide bond length = **1.33 Å** - 1 **kDa** = 1000 **Da** **🔹 Critical Equations** - $M_{\text{protein}} \approx 92n + 18$ Da - $\text{pI} = (\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2})/2$ (neutral amino acids) - $\varepsilon_{280} = 5500\,n_{\text{Trp}} + 1490\,n_{\text{Tyr}}$ (UV quantitation) **🔹 Famous Principles** - **Anfinsen's Thermodynamic Hypothesis (1973):** Sequence → Structure → Function - **Pauling & Corey (1951):** α-helix and β-sheet proposed from model building - **Ramachandran Plot:** Allowed φ/ψ regions — visualizes steric constraints **🔹 Quick "Must-Draw" Diagrams for Exam** - General amino acid structure (with α-carbon labelled) - Peptide bond formation (condensation + H₂O release) - α-helix cross-section showing $i → i+4$ H-bond - Hierarchical 1°→2°→3°→4° flowchart - Ramachandran plot (allowed regions for α-helix, β-sheet, left-handed helix) **🔹 Bioinformatics Connection (Module Continuity)** - **BLASTp** — sequence homology search - **Pfam** — protein domain families - **PSIPRED** — secondary structure prediction - **AlphaFold2** — 3D structure prediction from sequence (Nobel-worthy, 2024 Chemistry Nobel awarded to Hassabis & Jumper) - **UniProt** — comprehensive protein knowledgebase - **PDB** — Protein Data Bank (3D structures, >200,000 entries) > **END OF NOTES — PROTEINS (MODULE 1) | KTU 2024 SCHEME | PEADT414**

topics.title: Genomics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Genomics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> **Genomics** is the comprehensive, interdisciplinary field of molecular biology dedicated to the **structure, function, evolution, mapping, and editing of complete genomes** (the entire set of DNA, including all of its genes and their interactions) of an organism. It contrasts with classical genetics, which generally studies single genes one at a time.

In KTU 2024-scheme terminology, genomics is subdivided into three canonical pillars:

| Branch | Scope |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Structural Genomics** | Physical nature of the genome (sequence, size, chromosome count, gene positions). |
| **Functional Genomics** | What each gene *does* — expression patterns, regulatory networks, phenotypes. |
| **Comparative Genomics** | Comparing genomes across species to trace evolution and conserved elements. |

A complementary branch, **Epigenomics**, studies heritable changes that do **not** alter the underlying DNA sequence (e.g., methylation, histone modification).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library and the Librarian" View

> [!TIP]
> **Library Analogy:** Imagine the **nucleus of a cell** is a giant library.
> - The **chromosomes** are the bookshelves.
> - The **DNA double helix** is the long scrolls of text.
> - The **genome** is the *entire* collection of text in *all* scrolls.
> - A **gene** is a single *chapter* containing instructions to build one specific "tool" (a protein).
> - **Genomics** is the work of the *head librarian*: cataloguing every letter, identifying every chapter, and understanding how chapters are cross-referenced. The **Human Genome Project** was the equivalent of cataloguing the largest, most complex library in existence — roughly **3.2 billion** base pairs, with about **20,000–25,000** protein-coding genes.

### 1.3 The Central Dogma of Molecular Biology

Genomics is anchored in Francis Crick's **Central Dogma**, which defines the directional flow of genetic information:

$$
\text{DNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Transcription}} \text{RNA} \xrightarrow{\text{Translation}} \text{Protein}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU exams, remember the **one-way flow** (DNA → RNA → Protein) and the *special cases*: **reverse transcription** (RNA → DNA, as in retroviruses) and **RNA replication** (RNA → RNA, as in some viruses). These exceptions are frequently asked in 2-mark and 3-mark questions.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Throughout Genomics

- **Genome size:** measured in **megabases (Mb)** or **gigabases (Gb)**.
- **Gene count:** typical human count is **\approx 20,000–25,000** protein-coding genes (a famous surprise from the HGP — far fewer than the 100,000 once predicted).
- **Haploid vs Diploid:** Humans have a **haploid** genome size of **\approx 3.2 Gb**; somatic cells are **diploid (\approx 6.4 Gb)**.
- **C-value paradox:** There is no clear correlation between organismal complexity and genome size (e.g., the amoeba *Polychaos dubium* has a genome orders of magnitude larger than the human genome).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Workflow of a Genomics Experiment

A typical genomics pipeline proceeds through five logical stages:

1. **Sample Preparation** — extract high-molecular-weight DNA, fragment, size-select.
2. **Library Construction** — ligate platform-specific adaptors to fragments.
3. **Sequencing** — generate raw reads (Sanger, Illumina, Nanopore, PacBio).
4. **Assembly / Alignment** — *de novo* assembly or read-mapping to a reference.
5. **Annotation & Interpretation** — predict genes, assign function, identify variants.

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU question frame: *"Differentiate between Structural and Functional Genomics."* Use the table above and emphasise that **structural = 'what is there?'** and **functional = 'what does it do?'**.

### 2.2 Genome Organisation — Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic

| Feature | Prokaryotes | Eukaryotes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Genome form | Single **circular** chromosome (often) | Multiple **linear** chromosomes |
| Size | **0.5–10 Mb** | **10 Mb – 100+ Gb** |
| Gene density | High (~1 gene per **\approx 1 kb**) | Low (~1 gene per **\approx 100 kb** in humans) |
| Introns | Rare | Common (especially in metazoans) |
| Repetitive DNA | Few transposons | Vast (LINEs, SINEs, microsatellites) |
| Plasmids | Common | Absent (mostly) |
| Histones | Archaeal histone-like proteins | Canonical **H2A, H2B, H3, H4** octamer |
| Ploidy | **Haploid** (chromosome copy) | Diploid (somatic) / haploid (gametes) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coding density** is a high-yield KTU concept. A bacterium like *E. coli* uses **\approx 88\%** of its genome for coding, while humans use only **\approx 1.5\%** (the rest is regulatory elements, introns, and — predominantly — repetitive and non-coding DNA).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for Part B (14-mark) numerical problems in KTU 2024-scheme ESE papers.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{1. Sequencing Coverage (Depth):} \quad
C &= \frac{N \times L}{G} \\[6pt]
\textbf{2. GC Content (\%):} \quad
\%GC &= \frac{n_G + n_C}{n_A + n_T + n_G + n_C} \times 100 \\[6pt]
\textbf{3. Phred Quality Score:} \quad
Q &= -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}}) \\[6pt]
\textbf{4. Probability a base is unsequenced (Lander–Waterman):} \quad
P_0 &= e^{-C} \\[6pt]
\textbf{5. Number of gaps expected:} \quad
N_{\text{gaps}} &= G \cdot e^{-C} \\[6pt]
\textbf{6. N50 Statistic (Assembly Contiguity):} \quad
N50 &= L_k \quad \text{where} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} L_i < \tfrac{G}{2} \le \sum_{i=1}^{k} L_i \\[6pt]
\textbf{7. Genome size (k-mer based):} \quad
G_{\text{est}} &= \frac{\text{Total k-mers}}{k\text{-mer coverage}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{8. Alignment identity (\%):} \quad
\%I &= \frac{\text{Matches}}{\text{Alignment length}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> In the table below, the **vertical bar symbol** has been replaced by `\vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser — a common KTU-student pitfall when copying formulas into exam scripts that use similar rendering.

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C$ | Mean coverage depth | dimensionless (×) |
| $N$ | Number of reads | count |
| $L$ | Read length | bp |
| $G$ | Genome size | bp |
| $P$ | Probability of base-calling error | dimensionless (0–1) |
| $Q$ | Phred quality score | dimensionless |
| $n_A, n_T, n_G, n_C$ | Counts of each nucleotide | count |
| $P_0$ | Probability a given genome base has **zero** coverage | dimensionless |
| $L_i$ | Length of the $i$-th contig, sorted descending | bp |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Bioinformatic Utility

- **Medical Genomics:** Identifying driver mutations in cancer (e.g., **BRCA1/2** in breast cancer) via whole-genome sequencing.
- **Pharmacogenomics:** Tailoring drug dosage to a patient's genetic makeup (e.g., **CYP2C19** for clopidogrel).
- **Agricultural Genomics:** Marker-assisted selection for drought resistance in rice, wheat.
- **Synthetic Biology & Industrial Biotech:** Designing minimal genomes (e.g., **JCVI-syn3.0**, 473 genes) for chassis organisms.
- **Forensic Genomics:** DNA fingerprinting using **STR** (short tandem repeat) profiling.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation — Lander–Waterman Coverage Model

This is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU bioinformatics modules. It models the probability that a genome position is covered by at least one read.

**Step 1 — Model the genome as a target of length $G$ bp.**
A single read of length $L$ is generated uniformly at random. It "covers" a contiguous $L$-bp window on the genome.

**Step 2 — Expected number of reads.**
The total number of independent reads produced is $N$. Each read starts at a position drawn uniformly from $\{1, 2, \dots, G\}$.

**Step 3 — Compute coverage.**
Each base of the genome lies in a window of size $L$ starting at any of the $L$ preceding read-start positions (or all positions where a read could begin and still cover that base). Assuming reads are independent and coverage is approximately Poisson-distributed (valid for large $G$, $N$ with $L \ll G$):

$$
\lambda = C = \frac{N \times L}{G}
$$

**Step 4 — Poisson probability of zero coverage.**
For a Poisson process with mean $\lambda = C$, the probability that a given base is covered by **zero** reads is:

$$
P_0 = \frac{\lambda^0 e^{-\lambda}}{0!} = e^{-C}
$$

**Step 5 — Probability of at least one read.**
Therefore, the probability that a base **is** covered:

$$
P_{\text{covered}} = 1 - e^{-C}
$$

**Step 6 — Expected number of uncovered gaps (contigs).**

$$
N_{\text{gaps}} = G \cdot P_0 = G \cdot e^{-C}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, the most important corollary to memorise: to cover **\approx 99\%** of the genome (i.e. $P_0 \le 0.01$), one needs $C \ge -\ln(0.01) \approx 4.6\times$ coverage. To reach **99.99\%**, one needs $C \ge 9.2\times$. This is why the standard human WGS target is **\ge 30×** (gives $P_0 = e^{-30} \approx 9.4 \times 10^{-14}$, effectively zero gaps).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

> **Problem (modeled on KTU ESE 2023):** A bacterial genome is **4.6 Mbp** in size. Sequencing produced **2.3 million reads**, each of length **200 bp**. Calculate: (a) mean coverage $C$, (b) probability that any given base is *not* covered, and (c) expected number of gaps.

**Step (a) — Compute coverage.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= 2.3 \times 10^{6} \text{ reads} \\
L &= 200 \text{ bp} \\
G &= 4.6 \times 10^{6} \text{ bp} \\
C &= \frac{N \times L}{G} = \frac{2.3 \times 10^{6} \times 200}{4.6 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{4.6 \times 10^{8}}{4.6 \times 10^{6}} = 100
\end{aligned}
$$

So the mean coverage is $C = 100\times$.

**Step (b) — Probability of zero coverage.**

$$
P_0 = e^{-C} = e^{-100} \approx 3.72 \times 10^{-44}
$$

**Step (c) — Expected number of gaps.**

$$
N_{\text{gaps}} = G \cdot e^{-C} = 4.6 \times 10^{6} \times 3.72 \times 10^{-44} \approx 1.71 \times 10^{-37}
$$

> This is effectively **zero** — the genome is *fully covered* with astronomical margin. This is why modern microbial re-sequencing routinely targets $\ge 50\times$ for high-confidence variant calling.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Phred Score and Error Rate

> **Problem:** A base-call has Phred score $Q = 30$. Compute the probability of an erroneous call.

**Step 1 — Recall definition.**

$$
Q = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{error}})
$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange.**

$$
P_{\text{error}} = 10^{-Q/10}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q = 30$.**

$$
P_{\text{error}} = 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 = 0.1\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> Memorise the **Phred ladder** — frequently asked in 3-mark questions: $Q=10 \Rightarrow 10\%$, $Q=20 \Rightarrow 1\%$, $Q=30 \Rightarrow 0.1\%$, $Q=40 \Rightarrow 0.01\%$.

### 3.4 Worked Example — N50 Calculation

> **Problem:** A *de novo* assembly yielded contigs of lengths (bp): **12, 8, 6, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1** (sorted descending, Mbp). Total genome size $G = 37$ Mbp. Find the N50.

**Step 1 — Half the total length.**

$$
G/2 = 37/2 = 18.5 \text{ Mbp}
$$

**Step 2 — Cumulative sum.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
12 \rightarrow 12 \\
12+8 = 20 \rightarrow 20 \\
20+6 = 26 \rightarrow 26
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Find first cumulative sum $\ge 18.5$.**
This is the third contig (**6 Mbp**), because $20 \ge 18.5$ and $12 < 18.5$.

$$
\therefore N50 = 6 \text{ Mbp}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing a Sequencing Platform

| Platform | Read Length | Throughput | Error Profile | Best For |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sanger** | 500–1000 bp | Low | Low (\approx 0.1%, $Q \approx 30$) | Gold-standard validation, small amplicons |
| **Illumina (short-read)** | 50–300 bp | Very high | Very low ($\le 0.1\%$) | WGS, RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, population studies |
| **PacBio HiFi (CLR/CCS)** | 10–25 kb | High | Low (\approx 0.1% HiFi) | Structural variants, full-length isoforms |
| **Oxford Nanopore** | Up to **> 1 Mb** | Moderate | Higher (\approx 1–5% raw, **\approx 0.1%** with consensus) | Ultra-long, real-time, epigenetic base-calling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners love a table-comparison question in Part B. The above matrix, drawn into your answer script, is worth 7 marks by itself when supported with two or three sentences of explanation per row.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Set B, Q1(a)]**
> **Q1. Define genomics. Differentiate between structural and functional genomics.**

**Model Answer:**
Genomics is the comprehensive study of an organism's complete set of DNA (the genome), including the structure, function, evolution, and mapping of all genes and non-coding sequences.

| Aspect | Structural Genomics | Functional Genomics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Focus | Physical layout — sequence, size, gene positions | Gene activity, expression, interaction |
| Output | Genome maps, sequence assemblies, gene coordinates | Expression profiles, regulatory networks, phenotypes |
| Example | Human Genome Project (HGP) | ENCODE, GTEx, FANTOM projects |
| Key tool | Sequencing, assembly, annotation | RNA-seq, ChIP-seq, knockouts/CRISPR screens |

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Q1(b)]**
> **Q2. What is the Human Genome Project? Mention any two of its major outcomes.**

**Model Answer:**
The **Human Genome Project (HGP, 1990–2003)** was an international research effort to determine the sequence of the human genome and identify all of its genes. It was led by the **US Department of Energy, the NIH**, and partners including the **Wellcome Trust (UK)**, with parallel contributions from **Celera Genomics** (Venter *et al.*, 2001).

**Two major outcomes:**
1. The human genome is **\approx 3.2 Gbp** long and contains only **\approx 20,000–25,000** protein-coding genes (much fewer than the 100,000 once predicted).
2. **\approx 98.5\%** of the human genome does *not* code for proteins — most of the genome is regulatory, repetitive, or non-functional, which shifted the focus of biology toward **non-coding regulation**.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 1, Q2]** — Internal Choice. Attempt **either (A) or (B)**.

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the workflow of a typical **genome sequencing and assembly project**. Mention the role of coverage, read length, and the **N50 statistic** in evaluating assembly quality.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A research team sequences a novel bacterium with a **5.2 Mbp** genome. They obtain **\approx 3.9 million** reads, each **300 bp** long, on an Illumina platform. Calculate the **(i)** mean coverage, **(ii)** probability that a given base is uncovered, and **(iii)** expected number of gaps. Comment on the sufficiency of coverage for downstream variant calling.

**Model Solution (a) — Workflow:**
1. **DNA extraction and QC** — purity, integrity, concentration.
2. **Library preparation** — fragmentation (enzymatic or acoustic), end-repair, **adapter ligation**, size selection (typically 300–500 bp insert).
3. **Sequencing** — Illumina generates short paired-end reads stored as **FASTQ** files with Phred quality scores.
4. **Quality control** — tools such as **FastQC** and **Trimmomatic** remove adapters and low-quality bases.
5. **Assembly** — *de novo* (e.g., **SPAdes, MEGAHIT**) or reference-based (e.g., **BWA, Bowtie2**).
6. **Evaluation** — N50, L50, total length, number of contigs, BUSCO completeness, GC content.
7. **Annotation and submission** — GenBank/ENA/EMBL.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Coverage:**

$$
C = \frac{N \times L}{G} = \frac{3.9 \times 10^{6} \times 300}{5.2 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.17 \times 10^{9}}{5.2 \times 10^{6}} = 225
$$

**(ii) Probability of zero coverage (Lander–Waterman):**

$$
P_0 = e^{-C} = e^{-225} \approx 10^{-97.7}
$$

**(iii) Expected number of gaps:**

$$
N_{\text{gaps}} = G \cdot e^{-C} = 5.2 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-97.7} \approx 0
$$

**Comment:** $C = 225\times$ is *far* in excess of the standard **\ge 30\times** threshold for microbial variant calling. Coverage is **amply sufficient**, and the project can proceed to high-confidence SNP and indel detection.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain **comparative genomics**. List the three main types of genomic comparison and state **two bioinformatics tools** used for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A Phred base-call score is **Q = 20**. **(i)** What is the corresponding base-calling error probability? **(ii)** How many such errors would you expect in a **1 million bp** sequencing output? **(iii)** If a newer basecaller improves the score to **Q = 40** on the same data, how many errors are then expected, and what is the **fold-reduction** in errors?

**Model Solution (a):**

Comparative genomics is the study of similarities and differences between the genomes of different species or strains to identify conserved regions (functional importance), unique regions (lineage-specific adaptations), and evolutionary relationships.

**Three main types of comparison:**

1. **Pairwise Genome Alignment** — aligning two complete genomes.
   Tools: **MUMmer, LASTZ, MAVID, Blastn**.
2. **Synteny / Collinearity Analysis** — identifying conserved gene order and detecting rearrangements, inversions, translocations.
   Tools: **Mauve, SyRI, ACT (Artemis Comparison Tool)**.
3. **Phylogenomics** — building evolutionary trees using concatenated core genes or whole-genome SNPs.
   Tools: **RAxML, IQ-TREE, BEAST, PhyloBayes**.

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Error probability at $Q = 20$:**

$$
P = 10^{-Q/10} = 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01 = 1\%
$$

**(ii) Expected errors in 1 Mbp:**

$$
N_{\text{err}} = 1 \times 10^{6} \times 0.01 = 10{,}000
$$

**(iii) Error probability at $Q = 40$:**

$$
P' = 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001
$$

$$
N_{\text{err}}' = 1 \times 10^{6} \times 10^{-4} = 100
$$

**Fold-reduction:**

$$
\text{Fold reduction} = \frac{10{,}000}{100} = 100\text{-fold}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where students typically lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing "$L = 300$ bp" but substituting the numeric value only, leading to a coverage that is off by orders of magnitude. **Always state units explicitly** in the substitution line.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 10:** A common error is $P = 10^{-Q}$ instead of $10^{-Q/10}$. Memorise $Q = -10 \log_{10} P$.
> 3. **Misinterpreting N50:** N50 is *not* an average. It is the contig length such that **50% of the total assembly is contained in contigs of this length or longer**. Larger N50 = better assembly.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in Part (a):** KTU valuation keys assign **at least 2 marks** for a properly labelled workflow diagram. Even a hand-drawn box-and-arrow figure earns the marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the "comment" in numericals:** Always end with a 1–2 sentence **biological interpretation** of your numerical answer.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Card — Genomics, Module 1**

- **Definition:** Genomics = study of the *complete* genome (structure, function, evolution).
- **Three branches:** Structural, Functional, Comparative (+ Epigenomics).
- **Central Dogma:** DNA → RNA → Protein. Special cases: reverse transcription, RNA replication.
- **Human Genome:** **\approx 3.2 Gbp**, **\approx 20,000–25,000** protein-coding genes, **\approx 1.5\%** coding.
- **C-value paradox:** Genome size does *not* correlate with organismal complexity.
- **Prokaryotic vs Eukaryotic:** circular vs linear, dense vs intron-rich, plasmid vs chromosome-bound.
- **HGP:** 1990–2003, public + Celera, produced the reference human genome.
- **Coverage formula:** $C = N \cdot L \,/\, G$ — *must* be able to derive and use.
- **Lander–Waterman:** $P_0 = e^{-C}$, $N_{\text{gaps}} = G \cdot e^{-C}$.
- **Phred score:** $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$; $Q = 30 \Leftrightarrow P = 0.1\%$.
- **N50:** Larger is better; the contig length at which 50% of the assembly is covered.
- **Sequencing platforms:** Sanger (long, low throughput), Illumina (short, very high), PacBio/Nanopore (long, real-time, higher error raw).
- **Annotation pipeline:** Assembly → RepeatMasking → Gene Prediction → Homology → Functional assignment → GO/KEGG.
- **Comparative genomics tools:** MUMmer, Mauve, RAxML, IQ-TREE.
- **Caveat for KTU exams:** Always draw a **labelled block diagram** in workflow questions — it directly fetches 2 marks in the official valuation key.
- **Biomedical relevance:** BRCA1/2 (cancer), CYP450 (pharmacogenomics), STR profiling (forensics), JCVI-syn3.0 (synthetic biology).

> **End of Module 1 — Genomics Notes (PEADT414, KTU 2024 Scheme).**

topics.title: Sequencing Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sequencing Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is DNA Sequencing?

**DNA Sequencing** is the laboratory process of determining the precise order of nucleotides — **Adenine (A), Guanine (G), Cytosine (C), and Thymine (T)** — within a DNA molecule. It is the foundational technology that powers modern genomics, molecular biology, personalized medicine, and evolutionary studies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Sequencing is the analytical procedure used to decipher the linear arrangement of nucleotides in a DNA (or RNA via cDNA) fragment, thereby converting a biological polymer into a digital, character-based string amenable to computational analysis.

**The Human Genome Project (1990–2003)**, which cost nearly **$3 billion** and 13 years, is the landmark achievement that propelled sequencing from a manual, radioactive-labelled craft into the high-throughput, automated discipline it is today.

## 1.2 The Generations of Sequencing Technology

Sequencing is broadly classified into **three generations** plus the latest **fourth generation (Nanopore)**:

| Generation | Era | Representative Technology | Read Length | Throughput |
|------------|------|---------------------------|-------------|------------|
| **1st Generation** | 1977–2000s | Sanger, Maxam-Gilbert | **500–1000 bp** | Low |
| **2nd Generation (NGS)** | 2005–Present | Illumina, Ion Torrent, 454 | **50–600 bp** | Very High |
| **3rd Generation** | 2010–Present | PacBio SMRT | **10–100 kb** | High |
| **4th Generation** | 2014–Present | Oxford Nanopore | **>1 Mb (theoretical)** | Ultra High |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Book Reading" Metaphor

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Imagine a library of 23 books (the human chromosomes).** Each book has roughly **3 billion letters** with no spaces, no punctuation, and only four possible characters (A, T, G, C). Sequencing is the act of:
> 1. **Shredding the books** into tiny fragments (library preparation).
> 2. **Reading each fragment** in a sequencing machine.
> 3. **Computationally reassembling** the fragments into the original text.

The *method* of reading, the *length* of each fragment, and the *parallelism* of reading distinguish the generations from one another.

## 1.4 Core Chemical Players in Sequencing

Every sequencing chemistry — regardless of generation — relies on a small set of molecular workhorses:

- **dNTPs (Deoxynucleotide Triphosphates):** Normal building blocks. They have a **3'-OH group** that allows chain elongation.
- **ddNTPs (Dideoxynucleotide Triphosphates):** Chain-terminating analogues. They **lack the 3'-OH group**, so incorporation halts polymerization. This is the heart of Sanger sequencing.
- **DNA Polymerase:** The enzyme that copies the template strand.
- **Primers:** Short oligonucleotides (~18–25 nt) that initiate synthesis at a known position.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU:** A sequencing machine does not "see" DNA directly — it detects a *signal* (fluorescence, pH change, current blockage, or light) emitted when a nucleotide is incorporated. The chemistry of *how* that signal is generated is the primary differentiator between sequencing platforms.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 First-Generation Sequencing

### 2.1.1 Sanger Dideoxy Chain-Termination Method (1977)

Developed by **Frederick Sanger** (Nobel Prize, 1980), this method uses the strategic incorporation of **fluorescently labelled ddNTPs** to terminate DNA synthesis at each possible base position.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. The template DNA is denatured into single strands.
2. A primer is annealed to the 3' end of the template.
3. The reaction mixture contains: **DNA polymerase, all four dNTPs, and a small amount of each of the four ddNTPs (each labelled with a distinct fluorophore)**.
4. When the polymerase incorporates a ddNTP, the chain terminates because the **3'-OH is absent** and the next phosphodiester bond cannot form.
5. The result is a population of fragments of varying lengths, each ending at a specific base.
6. **Capillary electrophoresis** separates the fragments by size, and a laser detects the terminal fluorescent label.
7. The computer reconstructs the sequence from the order of emitted colors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Sanger Sequencing** uses a **single reaction tube** with four differently fluorescent ddNTPs, replacing the four separate radioactive gel lanes used historically. The automated **ABI 3730xl** remains the gold-standard Sanger instrument with **>99.999%** accuracy per base.

### 2.1.2 Maxam-Gilbert Chemical Sequencing (1977)

Developed by **Allan Maxam and Walter Gilbert**, this method uses **base-specific chemical cleavage** of a radioactively labelled DNA fragment, followed by polyacrylamide gel electrophoresis.

**The Four Cleavage Reactions:**
- **G reaction:** Dimethyl sulfate (DMS) methylates guanine → piperidine cleavage.
- **A + G reaction:** Formic acid depurinates A and G → piperidine cleavage.
- **C + T reaction:** Hydrazine opens C and T rings → piperidine cleavage.
- **C reaction:** Hydrazine + NaCl (salt inhibits T reaction) → cleavage only at C.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Sanger won over Maxam-Gilbert:** Sanger's method is *enzymatic* (cleaner), easier to automate, requires less hazardous chemicals, and scales better. Maxam-Gilbert is largely **obsolete in routine use** but is historically significant and occasionally used for short oligonucleotides.

## 2.2 Next-Generation Sequencing (NGS) — Second Generation

### 2.2.1 Illumina Sequencing by Synthesis (SBS)

The **market leader** (≈ **70% of global sequencing data**) uses **reversible terminator chemistry** on a glass flow cell.

**Workflow:**
1. **Library Preparation:** DNA is fragmented (≈ **300–500 bp**), end-repaired, and ligated with platform-specific **adapters**.
2. **Cluster Generation:** Fragments bind to the flow cell and undergo **bridge amplification (PCR)**, creating clusters of ≈ **1000 identical copies** per spot.
3. **Sequencing by Synthesis:** All four fluorescently labelled, **reversibly terminated** dNTPs are added simultaneously. After each incorporation, the flow cell is imaged.
4. **Cleavage:** The terminator and fluorophore are chemically cleaved, allowing the next cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Paired-End (PE) Sequencing:** Both ends of each fragment (typically **2 × 150 bp** or **2 × 300 bp** on modern platforms like NovaSeq X) are sequenced, providing superior alignment and structural variant detection.

### 2.2.2 Ion Torrent Semiconductor Sequencing

Detects **pH changes** when a hydrogen ion (H⁺) is released during nucleotide incorporation. **No fluorescence, no optics** — a semiconductor chip (like a CMOS) detects the pH shift.

- **Disadvantage:** Homopolymer runs (e.g., AAAAA) are difficult to quantify accurately.
- **Advantage:** Fast run times (≈ **2–4 hours**).

### 2.2.3 454 Pyrosequencing (Roche) — *Discontinued 2016*

Used **pyrophosphate (PPi)** release, converted by **luciferase** into light. Pioneered massively parallel sequencing. Discontinued due to high reagent cost and homopolymer errors.

## 2.3 Third-Generation Sequencing (Long Reads)

### 2.3.1 PacBio SMRT (Single Molecule Real-Time)

- Uses **Zero-Mode Waveguides (ZMWs)** — tiny wells that observe single polymerase molecules in real time.
- **Read length:** **10–25 kb**, sometimes **>100 kb**.
- **Circular Consensus:** The same molecule is read repeatedly (insert size ~10–20 kb), generating **HiFi reads** with **>99.9% accuracy**.

### 2.3.2 Oxford Nanopore Technologies (ONT)

- A **single-stranded DNA molecule is threaded through a biological nanopore** embedded in a membrane.
- Changes in **ionic current** are measured; the signal pattern is decoded into bases using neural network algorithms (e.g., **MinKNOW**, **Guppy**, **Dorado**).
- **Read length:** Theoretically **>1 Mb**; typical **10–100 kb**.
- **Portable device:** **MinION** is a **USB-sized sequencer**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Description |
|---|-------------------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | **Phred Quality Score** | $Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)$ | $P$ = probability of base being wrong. $Q20$ → 1% error; $Q30$ → 0.1% error. |
| 2 | **Accuracy (per base)** | $A = 1 - 10^{-Q/10}$ | E.g., $Q30$ gives $99.9\%$ accuracy. |
| 3 | **Sequencing Coverage (Depth)** | $C = \dfrac{N \times L}{G}$ | $N$ = number of reads, $L$ = read length, $G$ = genome size. |
| 4 | **Lander–Waterman Physical Coverage** | $C_{phys} = N \times L$ | Total bases sequenced, in bp. |
| 5 | **Fold Coverage** | $C_{fold} = \dfrac{N \times L}{G}$ | Same as formula 3; usually >30× for human WGS. |
| 6 | **GC Content** | $\%\!GC = \dfrac{G+C}{A+T+G+C} \times 100$ | Affects sequencing bias, especially in Illumina. |
| 7 | **Expected Heterozygous SNP detection (Poisson)** | $P(\text{covered} \geq k) = 1 - e^{-C} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \dfrac{C^i}{i!}$ | Probability a base is covered by at least $k$ reads. |
| 8 | **Sanger Chain Termination Constant** | $\text{termination fraction} = \dfrac{[ddNTP]}{[dNTP] + [ddNTP]}$ | Ratio determining ladder of fragment lengths. |
| 9 | **Nanopore Signal Sampling** | $I_{base} = f(\text{k-mer in pore})$ | Current level depends on the specific $k$-mer occupying the pore. |
| 10 | **Throughput (run)** | $\text{Output} = N_{reads} \times L_{read}$ | Total bases generated per run. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** In clinical genomics, **30× coverage** is the standard for human whole-genome sequencing to ensure accurate variant calling. In cancer genomics, **>100× coverage** of tumor DNA is often required to detect subclonal somatic mutations present at low allele frequencies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Phred Quality Score

The Phred quality score was originally developed by **Ewing & Green (1998)** at the University of Washington. It translates the raw signal-to-noise ratio of a sequencing trace into a logarithmic probability of error.

**Step 1 — Base Definition:**
Let $P$ be the probability that the called base is **incorrect**. We want a score that:
- Is **larger** for **more accurate** bases (intuitive).
- Grows **logarithmically** (so a 10× improvement in accuracy adds a fixed amount).

**Step 2 — Start with logarithmic accuracy:**
The natural quantity is $\log_{10}(1/P) = -\log_{10}(P)$.

**Step 3 — Multiply by 10 for convenience:**
The industry convention (codified in the **FASTQ format** and the **SAM/BAM specifications**) is to multiply by 10. This gives the integer Phred score $Q$.

$$
Q = -10 \log_{10}(P)
$$

**Step 4 — Express the accuracy:**
Starting from the definition and exponentiating both sides:

$$
\log_{10}(P) = -\dfrac{Q}{10}
$$

$$
P = 10^{-Q/10}
$$

$$
A = 1 - P = 1 - 10^{-Q/10}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical examples:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q = 20: \quad P &= 10^{-20/10} = 10^{-2} = 0.01 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = 99\% \\[4pt]
Q = 30: \quad P &= 10^{-30/10} = 10^{-3} = 0.001 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = 99.9\% \\[4pt]
Q = 40: \quad P &= 10^{-40/10} = 10^{-4} = 0.0001 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; A = 99.99\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** In FASTQ files, $Q$ values are stored as **ASCII characters**: $Q + 33$ in the older **Phred+33** (Sanger) encoding, used by modern Illumina (1.8+).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Coverage Formula

**Goal:** Find the average number of times a given base in the reference genome is covered by sequencing reads.

**Step 1 — Total sequenced bases:**
If the sequencer produces $N$ reads, each of length $L$ (in base pairs), the total number of sequenced bases is:

$$
T = N \times L \;\; \text{(bp)}
$$

**Step 2 — Divide by genome size:**
If the reference genome is $G$ base pairs long, the average coverage depth is:

$$
C = \dfrac{T}{G} = \dfrac{N \times L}{G}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked Example (Illumina NovaSeq, Human Genome):**
Given:
- Genome size $G = 3 \times 10^{9}$ bp
- Read length $L = 150$ bp (paired-end)
- Number of read pairs $N = 600$ million

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= 2 \times N \times L \quad \text{(paired-end, both mates)} \\
  &= 2 \times (6 \times 10^{8}) \times 150 \\
  &= 1.8 \times 10^{11} \text{ bp} \\[4pt]
C &= \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{11}}{3 \times 10^{9}} = 60 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **60× WGS** standard, sufficient for accurate SNP and small-indel calling in clinical workflows.

## 3.3 Lander–Waterman Statistics for Coverage

The actual coverage at any given base follows a **Poisson distribution** because sequencing reads sample positions randomly (after library preparation).

**Step 1 — Poisson parameter:**
The expected coverage $\lambda$ equals the average fold coverage $C$.

$$
\lambda = C = \dfrac{N \times L}{G}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability a base is covered by at least $k$ reads:**

$$
P(X \geq k) = 1 - e^{-\lambda} \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} \dfrac{\lambda^i}{i!}
$$

**Step 3 — Worked example: Probability a base is covered by ≥ 1 read at $C = 8\times$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(X \geq 1) &= 1 - e^{-8} \sum_{i=0}^{0} \dfrac{8^0}{0!} \\
&= 1 - e^{-8} \\
&= 1 - 0.000335 \\
&\approx 0.9997 \;\;\text{(99.97\% of bases covered)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why a coverage of **8–10×** is the bare minimum for *de novo* assembly, while **30×** is the standard for variant calling in resequencing projects.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Platform Comparison

| Property | Sanger | Illumina | Ion Torrent | PacBio HiFi | Oxford Nanopore |
|----------|--------|----------|-------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Read Length** | 500–1000 bp | 50–600 bp | 200–600 bp | 10–25 kb | 10 kb – 1 Mb |
| **Accuracy** | >99.999% (Q50) | >99.9% (Q30) | >99% (Q20–25) | >99.9% (HiFi Q30) | >99% (Q20) |
| **Throughput / Run** | ~0.5 Mb | **600 Gb–6 Tb** | 0.5–50 Gb | 30–500 Gb | 50 Gb – 2 Tb |
| **Cost per Mb (approx.)** | High (~$0.50) | Low (~$0.001) | Low (~$0.005) | Medium (~$0.05) | Low (~$0.01) |
| **Run Time** | 0.5–3 h | 12 h – 3 d | 2–4 h | 12–30 h | 1 min – 72 h |
| **Best For** | Single genes, validation | WGS, WES, RNA-Seq | Small genomes, amplicons | Iso-Seq, structural variants | Rapid ID, field use |
| **Error Type** | Substitution | Substitution | Indel (homopolymer) | Indel (random) | Indel (homopolymer-like) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sanger Sequencing Workflow (Dideoxy Chain Termination)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Template DNA ds] --> B[Denaturation 95C]
    B --> C[Primer Annealing 55C]
    C --> D[Four Reaction Tubes with ddNTPs]
    D --> E1[Tube A ddATP]
    D --> E2[Tube C ddCTP]
    D --> E3[Tube G ddGTP]
    D --> E4[Tube T ddTTP]
    E1 --> F1[DNA Polymerase Extension]
    E2 --> F2[DNA Polymerase Extension]
    E3 --> F3[DNA Polymerase Extension]
    E4 --> F4[DNA Polymerase Extension]
    F1 --> G1[Fragments ending at A]
    F2 --> G2[Fragments ending at C]
    F3 --> G3[Fragments ending at G]
    F4 --> G4[Fragments ending at T]
    G1 --> H[Capillary Electrophoresis]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
    H --> I[Laser Detection of Fluorophore]
    I --> J[Chromatogram electropherogram]
    J --> K[Base Calling Software]
    K --> L[Final DNA Sequence Text]
```

## 4.2 Illumina Sequencing by Synthesis Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input gDNA] --> B[Fragmentation 300 to 500 bp]
    B --> C[End Repair and A Tailing]
    C --> D[Adapter Ligation]
    D --> E[Size Selection]
    E --> F[Library Quantification]
    F --> G[Flow Cell Loading]
    G --> H[Bridge Amplification Cluster Generation]
    H --> I[Sequencing by Synthesis SBS]
    I --> J[Cycle 1 Incorporate Image Cleave]
    J --> K[Cycle 2 Incorporate Image Cleave]
    K --> L[Cycle N Linear Extension]
    L --> M[Base Calling and Quality Phred Q30]
    M --> N[FASTQ Output Files]
    N --> O[Alignment to Reference BWA MEM]
    O --> P[Variant Calling GATK]
    P --> Q[Annotated VCF Report]
```

## 4.3 Oxford Nanopore Sequencing Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Molecular Weight DNA] --> B[Adapter Ligation Motor Protein]
    B --> C[Library Loading on Flow Cell]
    C --> D[Membrane with Embedded Nanopores]
    D --> E[Applied Voltage Ionic Current]
    E --> F[Current Modulation by k-mer in Pore]
    F --> G[MinKNOW Basecalling Software]
    G --> H[Guppy Dorado Neural Network]
    H --> I[Raw FAST5 / POD5 Signal Files]
    I --> J[Basecalled FASTQ Files]
    J --> K[Real Time Analysis Metagenomics SNP]
    K --> L[Final Genome Assembly or Variant Set]
```

## 4.4 Comparison Map of Sequencing Generations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root[DNA Sequencing Technology Tree]
    Root --> G1[1st Generation 1977 to 2000s]
    Root --> G2[2nd Generation NGS 2005 to Present]
    Root --> G3[3rd Generation 2010 to Present]
    Root --> G4[4th Generation 2014 to Present]

    G1 --> S[Sanger Dideoxy Chain Termination]
    G1 --> MG[Maxam Gilbert Chemical Cleavage]

    G2 --> ILL[Illumina SBS Reversible Terminator]
    G2 --> IT[Ion Torrent pH Detection]
    G2 --> PY[454 Pyrosequencing Discontinued]

    G3 --> PB[PacBio SMRT ZMW Circular Consensus]
    G3 --> PB2[PacBio HiFi Long Read High Accuracy]

    G4 --> ONT[Oxford Nanopore MinION GridION PromethION]
    G4 --> ONT2[Single Molecule Electrical Signal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** Each block represents a discrete biochemical or computational step. The arrows indicate the unidirectional flow of the sample and the generated data. The boundary between *wet-lab* and *in-silico* analysis typically occurs at the **base-calling** step (e.g., step M in the Illumina diagram and step G/H in the Nanopore diagram).
