
> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid node IDs in the above diagrams follow the **alphanumeric-prefixed naming convention** (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`, `FASTA_FILE`) to avoid any reserved-keyword compilation errors. Labels are kept raw, uppercase, and free of markdown formatting tags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2025]**

**Q1. Define the FASTA sequence file format. List any two mandatory rules that govern its structure.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The FASTA format is a plain-text, single-character-per-symbol file format used to store nucleotide or protein sequences. Each entry begins with a single-line **definition line** prefixed by the greater-than symbol `>` in column 1, followed by a sequence identifier and free-text description. The biological sequence (using IUPAC nucleotide codes or one-letter amino acid codes) follows on subsequent lines, traditionally wrapped at 80 characters.

**Two Mandatory Rules:**
1. The first character of the file must be `>` with no leading whitespace or blank lines.
2. No blank lines should appear between the header line and the sequence body.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for first rule, 1 mark for second rule — KTU distribution.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is a k-tuple in the FASTA algorithm? Why is the default word size different for proteins and DNA?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **k-tuple** (or ktup) is a short, contiguous word of length $k$ extracted from a sequence. In the FASTA algorithm, the query is decomposed into overlapping k-tuples which are then matched against a pre-indexed database.

The default word size differs because of the **combinatorial complexity** of the two alphabets:
- **DNA** has only 4 nucleotides, so $4^6 = 4096$ possible 6-tuples — a small enough search space to permit $k=6$.
- **Proteins** have 20 amino acids, so $4^2 = 16$ possible 2-tuples — a much larger alphabet forces a smaller $k=2$ to retain sensitivity.

*(1.5 marks for k-tuple definition, 1.5 marks for alphabet-size justification.)*

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2025] — Internal Choice Pattern: Answer ANY ONE**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **complete five-stage operational pipeline of the FASTA algorithm** with a clear block diagram. Discuss the role of the substitution scoring matrix in each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Stage 1 — k-tuple Identification:** The query sequence of length $m$ is scanned with a sliding window of length $k$ to produce $(m - k + 1)$ overlapping words. For proteins, default $k=2$; for DNA, $k=6$. *(1 mark)*

**Stage 2 — Hash Table Lookup:** The database is pre-indexed such that every possible k-tuple maps to a list of positions where it occurs. Query k-tuples are looked up in constant time. *(1 mark)*

**Stage 3 — Dot-Matrix and Diagonal Scoring:** Hits are plotted on a 2D grid (query position vs. database position). Diagonals with the highest density of matches are scored using a substitution matrix (PAM250 for proteins). The top 10 diagonals are kept. *(1.5 marks)*

**Stage 4 — Region Joining:** k-tuples on a single diagonal within a tolerance band (1–2 residue gap) are merged into a single contiguous region called **init1**. *(1 mark)*

**Stage 5 — Banded Smith-Waterman Refinement:** init1 regions are passed to a banded Smith-Waterman algorithm that allows gaps, producing the optimal **High-Scoring Segment Pair (HSP)** with associated bit-score and E-value. *(1.5 marks)*

**Role of the Scoring Matrix:** In Stage 3, the PAM250 or BLOSUM62 matrix is used to score each diagonal based on the sum of pairwise residue scores; this converts raw match counts into biologically meaningful similarity. In Stage 5, the same matrix governs the Smith-Waterman scoring, ensuring consistency. *(1 mark)*

**(b)** A query protein of length $m = 180$ residues produces a Smith-Waterman refined alignment of raw score $S = 52$ against a database of total length $N = 2{,}500{,}000$ residues. Using Karlin-Altschul parameters $\lambda = 0.267$ and $K = 0.041$ (PAM250), calculate the bit-score and E-value. Is this hit significant at the default $10^{-5}$ threshold? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bit-score computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S' &= \frac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2} \\
S' &= \frac{(0.267)(52) - \ln(0.041)}{0.6931} \\
S' &= \frac{13.884 - (-3.194)}{0.6931} \\
S' &= \frac{13.884 + 3.194}{0.6931} \\
S' &= \frac{17.078}{0.6931} \\
S' &\approx 24.64 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks for correct substitution and arithmetic.)*

**Step 2 — E-value computation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= m \cdot N \cdot 2^{-S'} \\
2^{-24.64} &= 2^{0.36} \cdot 2^{-25} = 1.284 \cdot 2.98 \times 10^{-8} \\
&\approx 3.83 \times 10^{-8} \\
E &\approx 180 \cdot 2{,}500{,}000 \cdot 3.83 \times 10^{-8} \\
E &\approx 4.5 \times 10^{8} \cdot 3.83 \times 10^{-8} \\
E &\approx 17.2
\end{aligned}
$$

*(3 marks for correct substitution and arithmetic.)*

**Step 3 — Significance assessment:**

Since $E \approx 17.2 \gg 10^{-5}$, the alignment is **NOT statistically significant**. We expect ~17 such alignments by chance alone, so this hit is most likely a false positive. *(1 mark for conclusion.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *(Alternative choice)*

**(a)** Compare and contrast the **FASTA algorithm and the BLAST algorithm** with respect to speed, sensitivity, word-finding strategy, and final alignment refinement. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | FASTA | BLAST | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Word size** | $k=2$ (protein), $k=6$ (DNA) | $W=3$ (protein), $W=11$ (DNA) | 1 |
| **Initial match** | Exact k-tuple matches | Exact $W$-mers; BLAST2 uses neighbourhood scoring with high-scoring neighbours | 2 |
| **Speed** | Slower (top 10 diagonals retained) | Faster (single best extension per hit) | 1 |
| **Sensitivity** | Higher (examines more diagonals) | Slightly lower (greedy extension) | 1 |
| **Refinement** | Banded Smith-Waterman on init1 regions | Smith-Waterman only on the HSPs above threshold | 1 |
| **Statistical model** | Karlin-Altschul, E-value | Karlin-Altschul, E-value | 1 |

*(Award partial marks proportionally for well-explained entries.)*

**(b)** Construct a valid FASTA file containing **two protein sequences** of your choice (each at least 15 residues long) and explain every component of the header line according to NCBI conventions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```
>sp|P01308|INS_HUMAN Insulin OS=Homo sapiens OX=9606 GN=INS PE=1 SV=1
MALWMRLLPLLALLALWGPDPAAAFVNQHLCGSHLVEALYLVCGERGFFYTPKTRREAED
LQVGQVELGGGPGAGSLQPLALEGSLQKRGIVEQCCTSICSLYQLENYCN
>sp|P01315|INS_PIG Insulin OS=Sus scrofa OX=9823 GN=INS PE=1 SV=1
MALWTRLLPLLALLALWAPAPAQAFVNQHLCGSHLVEALYLVCGERGFFYTPKARREAEN
PQAGAVELGGGPGAGDLQTLALEVARQKRGIVDQCCTSICSLYQLENYCN
```

**Header line components (NCBI/SwissProt convention):** *(4 marks for explanation, 3 marks for valid sequences)*

- `sp` — Database source (SwissProt). Other examples: `tr` (TrEMBL), `gb` (GenBank), `emb` (EMBL), `pir` (PIR), `pdb` (PDB).
- `P01308` — **Primary accession number** — unique stable identifier for the entry.
- `INS_HUMAN` — **Entry name** — mnemonic combining gene symbol and organism (3–4 letters for gene, 4–6 letters for organism).
- `Insulin` — **Recommended protein name** (free-text description).
- `OS=Homo sapiens` — **Organism species** (scientific binomial name).
- `OX=9606` — **Organism taxonomy identifier** (NCBI Taxonomy database).
- `GN=INS` — **Gene name** (official HGNC symbol for human genes).
- `PE=1` — **Protein evidence level** (1 = experimental, 2 = transcript, 3 = inferred, 4 = predicted, 5 = uncertain).
- `SV=1` — **Sequence version** (incremented on any sequence change).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PAM with BLOSUM:** PAM matrices are extrapolated from evolutionary divergence; BLOSUM matrices are derived from observed conserved blocks. Listing the wrong one for a protein similarity search loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that the `>` must be column 1:** Any leading whitespace or blank line is an automatic reject by strict parsers. Examiners deduct marks if the answer shows `> ` (with a space) on the first line.
> 3. **Skipping the Smith-Waterman refinement:** Many students describe only Stages 1–4 and forget Stage 5. This is worth at least 2 marks in any 7-mark sub-part.
> 4. **Wrong sign in E-value formula:** A common error is $E = m \cdot N \cdot 2^{+S'}$. Always use $2^{-S'}$ — higher bit-score means **lower** E-value, not higher.
> 5. **Mixing up ktup and BLOSUM cluster percentage:** The "62" in BLOSUM62 refers to the identity clustering threshold, **not** the E-value or any other parameter. Writing "BLOSUM with 62% identity" loses half a mark.
> 6. **Not justifying word-size choice for DNA vs protein:** Simply stating $k=2$ and $k=6$ without explaining the alphabet-size rationale is incomplete — the explanation is worth at least 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **FASTA = Format + Algorithm**, both developed in the Lipman–Pearson 1985 tradition.
- **File format rule:** First character = `>`, single header line, sequence on subsequent lines, traditionally 80 chars wide.
- **Algorithm stages (must be memorized in order):** k-tuple identification → hash lookup → diagonal scoring (top 10) → region joining (init1) → Smith-Waterman refinement.
- **Default word sizes:** $k=2$ for proteins, $k=6$ for DNA — driven by alphabet size ($20^2$ vs $4^6$).
- **Scoring matrices:** PAM250 (older, evolutionary) and BLOSUM62 (modern, block-based) — both used in Stage 3 and Stage 5.
- **HSP = High-Scoring Segment Pair**, the final ungapped or gapped local alignment output.
- **Bit-score formula:** $S' = \dfrac{\lambda S - \ln K}{\ln 2}$.
- **E-value formula:** $E = m \cdot N \cdot 2^{-S'}$. Lower is better; $E \leq 10^{-5}$ is the standard significance threshold.
- **Karlin-Altschul parameters** $\lambda$ and $K$ are matrix-specific constants that calibrate the statistical model.
- **FASTA vs BLAST:** FASTA examines 10 diagonals (more sensitive); BLAST extends single hits (faster).
- **E-value intuition:** $E = 0.01$ means 1 chance match in 100; $E = 10$ means ~10 expected chance matches.
- **IUPAC ambiguity codes** (R, Y, N, W, S, K, M, B, D, H, V) are accepted in FASTA nucleotide sequences.
- **Pure heuristic:** FASTA sacrifices theoretical guarantee of finding the optimal alignment for a 100–1000× speed-up over Smith-Waterman.
- **Practical software packages:** `fasta36` (Pearson suite), `EMBOSS water` (uses Smith-Waterman, not FASTA), NCBI BLAST web server, EBI FASTA tools.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Phylogenetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Phylogenetics ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Phylogenetics** is the scientific discipline within bioinformatics that studies the **evolutionary relationships** among biological entities — such as genes, proteins, populations, species, or other taxonomic groups — by inferring their shared ancestry through **heritable molecular characters** (DNA, RNA, or amino-acid sequences). The output of a phylogenetic analysis is a **phylogenetic tree** (also called an *evolutionary tree* or *dendrogram*), a branching, hierarchical diagram that depicts the **most parsimonious, probable, or statistically supported hypothesis** of how a set of sequences descended from a common ancestor through mutation, selection, and speciation events. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim):** Phylogenetics is the study of the evolutionary history and relationships among individuals or groups of organisms (e.g., species or populations) using molecular data, represented as a branching tree where branch lengths may represent evolutionary time or genetic change. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Family Tree of Molecules" Imagine you have a **family photo album spanning 500 years**. Each photograph shows a relative. By comparing **facial features** (a heritable "character") across photos, you can deduce who descended from whom and draw a giant family tree. Phylogenetics does **exactly the same**, but the "photos" are **aligned DNA sequences**, and the "facial features" are **nucleotides (A, T, G, C) or amino acids**. Two sequences that look very similar are likely close relatives; sequences that look different are more distant relatives. > [!NOTE] > **Why does it work?** Mutations accumulate slowly and roughly like a molecular clock. The more time has passed since two lineages split, the more substitutions (changes) we observe between their sequences. This temporal accumulation is the fundamental signal phylogenetics exploits. ### 1.3 Key Vocabulary Anchors | Term | Plain-English Meaning | KTU-Standard Symbol | |---|---|---| | **Root** | The most ancient common ancestor of the entire tree | — | | **Node** | An internal branching point = an inferred ancestor | $v_i$ | | **Leaf / OTU** | A present-day observed sequence/taxon | $\tau_k$ | | **Branch / Edge** | An evolutionary lineage connecting nodes | $e_{ij}$ | | **Branch length** | Amount of evolutionary change (substitutions/site) | $b_{ij}$ | | **Clade / Monophyletic group** | A group containing an ancestor + ALL its descendants | $C_n$ | | **Topology** | The branching *pattern* (shape) of the tree | $T$ | | **Bootstrap value** | Percentage confidence (0–100%) in a clade | $BP(\%)$ | | **OTU** | Operational Taxonomic Unit — a terminal taxon | Operational Unit | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Distinction you must memorize for KTU:** > - **Phylogeny** → *the evolutionary history itself* (concept). > - **Phylogenetics** → *the methodology of inferring* that history (process). > - **Phenetics** → classification based purely on **overall similarity**, ignoring ancestry (OUTDATED). > - **Cladistics** → classification based on **shared derived characters (synapomorphies)** (the dominant modern approach). ### 1.4 Two Fundamental Tree Geometries There are **exactly two tree types** you must be able to recognize in the KTU exam: **A. Rooted Tree** — has a single common ancestor at the base and a defined direction of evolutionary time. - All branches originate from one root node. - Used when an **outgroup** is specified. **B. Unrooted Tree** — only shows relatedness, *not* the direction of evolution. - Has no designated ancestor; only depicts the network of relationships. - Has $N-1$ internal edges (where $N$ = number of OTUs) — wait, actually for a binary unrooted tree with $n$ leaves the number of internal edges is **$n-2$**. > [!NOTE] > **Exam Trick (KTU 2024):** For an unrooted binary tree with $n$ leaves → total edges = $2n-3$, internal nodes = $n-2$. > For a rooted binary tree with $n$ leaves → total edges = $2n-2$, internal nodes = $n-1$. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Four-Stage Phylogenetic Pipeline Every phylogenetic study follows this canonical pipeline (Board-exam favourite): 1. **Sequence Acquisition** — Retrieve homologous sequences from databases (NCBI GenBank, UniProt). 2. **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)** — Align all sequences using ClustalW, MUSCLE, or MAFFT. *This step is critical because alignment columns define homologous sites.* 3. **Tree Reconstruction** — Apply an inference algorithm. 4. **Validation / Statistical Testing** — Bootstrap resampling to assign confidence values to internal branches. ### 2.2 Three Major Inference Methodologies #### A. Distance-Based Methods (Algorithm-driven, fast) The aligned sequences are first condensed into a **pairwise distance matrix**, and the tree is built using a clustering algorithm. **i. UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** - Assumes a **molecular clock** (equal evolutionary rates across lineages). - Produces a **rooted**, ultrametric tree. - Step-by-step logic: - Start with each OTU as its own cluster. - Compute pairwise distances. - Merge the two closest clusters. - Update the distance matrix using the **arithmetic mean** of the merged cluster's members. - Repeat until one cluster remains. **ii. Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** - **Does NOT** assume a molecular clock. - Produces an **unrooted** tree. - Corrects for the fact that two close taxa share a recent common ancestor with each other. - Uses the **Q-criterion** to select the next pair to join: $$Q(i,j) = (n-2) \cdot d(i,j) \; - \; \sum_{k=1}^{n} d(i,k) \; - \; \sum_{k=1}^{n} d(j,k)$$ where $n$ is the total number of taxa and $d$ is the pairwise evolutionary distance. #### B. Character-Based Methods (Site-by-site, slower but more rigorous) **i. Maximum Parsimony (MP)** — Fitch Algorithm - Seeks the tree requiring the **fewest evolutionary changes** to explain the observed data. - **Fitch's algorithm** finds the minimum number of substitutions for a given tree: - **Forward pass:** For each internal node $u$ with children $v$ and $w$, define the set $S_u$ of possible character states. - If $S_v \cap S_w \neq \emptyset$, then $S_u = S_v \cap S_w$ (no mutation needed). - Else $S_u = S_v \cup S_w$ (one mutation needed at this node). - **Backward pass:** Pick ancestral states starting from the root, preferring states shared with both children. - The total parsimony score = sum of mutations across all sites. **ii. Maximum Likelihood (ML)** - Evaluates the **probability of observing the data given a tree AND a substitution model**. - Picks the tree that **maximizes this likelihood** $L$. - For a tree $T$ with branch lengths $\mathbf{b}$ and substitution model $M$: $$L(\text{data} \mid T, \mathbf{b}, M) = \prod_{\text{sites } i} P(\text{column } i \mid T, \mathbf{b}, M)$$ - Usually reported as a log-likelihood: $\ell = \ln L$. **iii. Bayesian Inference (BI)** - Uses **Bayes' theorem** to compute the *posterior probability* of a tree given the data: $$P(T \mid \text{data}) \;=\; \frac{P(\text{data} \mid T) \cdot P(T)}{P(\text{data})}$$ - Implemented via **Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC)** sampling (MrBayes). ### 2.3 Models of Nucleotide Substitution > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** You *must* know these substitution models and the distance formulas they yield. #### A. Jukes–Cantor (JC69) — single parameter model - Assumes **all substitutions are equally likely** (A↔T = A↔G = A↔C = T↔G = …). - Single rate $\alpha$. The evolutionary distance between two sequences is: $$d_{JC} \;=\; -\tfrac{3}{4}\,\ln\!\left(1 \;-\; \tfrac{4}{3}\,p\right)$$ where $p$ is the observed proportion of differing sites. #### B. Kimura 2-Parameter (K2P / K80) - Distinguishes **transitions** (A↔G, C↔T) from **transversions** (purine↔pyrimidine). - Transitions occur at rate $\alpha$, transversions at rate $\beta$. - $P$ = proportion of transitions, $Q$ = proportion of transversions, $p = P + Q$. $$d_{K2P} \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\ln(1 - 2P - Q) \;-\; \tfrac{1}{4}\,\ln(1 - 2Q)$$ #### C. General Time Reversible (GTR) - Most parameter-rich; 6 substitution rates + 4 base frequencies. Used for distantly related sequences. ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table.** Every formula here has appeared in past KTU ESE papers or in high-probability question pools. | # | Concept | Formula | Use / Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | JC69 distance | $d = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p)$ | Pairwise evolutionary distance, equal-rates model | | 2 | K2P distance | $d = -\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q) - \tfrac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$ | Separates transitions from transversions | | 3 | ML score | $\ell = \sum_i \ln P(\text{site}_i \mid T, M)$ | Log-likelihood of a tree under model $M$ | | 4 | Bayes posterior | $P(T \mid D) \propto P(D \mid T) \cdot P(T)$ | Bayesian tree inference | | 5 | NJ Q-matrix entry | $Q(i,j) = (n-2)d(i,j) - \sum_k d(i,k) - \sum_k d(j,k)$ | Selects next pair to join in NJ | | 6 | Unrooted binary tree: internal nodes | $n - 2$ | Number of inferred ancestors | | 7 | Rooted binary tree: internal nodes | $n - 1$ | Same but rooted | | 8 | Number of unrooted trees (binary) | $\tfrac{(2n-5)!!}{...} = \prod_{k=3}^{n}(2k-5)$ | Search-space size | | 9 | Bootstrap support | $BP = \tfrac{\text{resamples supporting clade}}{N}$ | Confidence in a clade (typically N=1000) | | 10 | F84 / TN93 distance (advanced) | See Felsenstein 1984 / Tamura–Nei 1993 | More realistic substitution biases | > [!NOTE] > **Notation Safeguard:** In all formulas above, $\ln$ denotes the natural logarithm, $p, P, Q$ are observed proportions ($\in [0,1]$), and $n$ is the number of taxa (OTUs). ### 2.5 Engineering / Real-World Utility Phylogenetics is not merely academic. It drives: - **Pandemic tracking:** Reconstructing the **transmission lineage of SARS-CoV-2** variants (Alpha, Delta, Omicron) to trace their emergence and spread. - **Forensics:** Identifying the **geographic origin** of illegally trafficked endangered species from confiscated DNA samples. - **Drug discovery:** Identifying **conserved drug targets** across pathogens by selecting regions that are invariant across strains. - **Agriculture & conservation:** Preserving **genetic diversity** in crop gene banks and endangered populations. - **Microbial ecology:** Characterizing **uncultured microbial communities** via 16S rRNA phylogeny (e.g., human gut microbiome studies). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Jukes–Cantor Distance Formula We will now derive the most important formula in distance-based phylogenetics from first principles using continuous-time Markov chain theory. **Setup.** Consider two homologous DNA sequences that diverged from a common ancestor $t$ million years ago. Let $p$ be the proportion of nucleotide sites that *differ* between them. We want an estimate of $t$ (in expected substitutions per site). **Step 1 — Define substitution rates.** Under JC69, every nucleotide $X \in \{A,T,G,C\}$ mutates to any of the other three with the **same total rate** $\alpha$ per unit time. So the rate of any specific transition (e.g., $A \to T$) is $\alpha / 3$. **Step 2 — Build the rate matrix.** Let $P_{ij}(t)$ be the probability of going from state $i$ to state $j$ in time $t$. The JC69 rate matrix is: $$Q \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \cdot & \alpha/3 & \alpha/3 & \alpha/3 \\ \alpha/3 & \cdot & \alpha/3 & \alpha/3 \\ \alpha/3 & \alpha/3 & \cdot & \alpha/3 \\ \alpha/3 & \alpha/3 & \alpha/3 & \cdot \end{bmatrix}$$ Each row sums to $\alpha$, ensuring conservation of probability. **Step 3 — Solve the differential equation.** The transition matrix over time is $P(t) = e^{Qt}$. Solving via eigenvalues (one eigenvalue $0$, three eigenvalues $-\alpha$) yields: $$P_{ii}(t) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{3}{4}\,e^{-\alpha t}$$ $$P_{ij}(t) \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4} - \tfrac{1}{4}\,e^{-\alpha t} \quad (i \neq j)$$ **Step 4 — Probability that two aligned sequences differ.** At any site, the two descendant nucleotides differ with probability: $$p \;=\; 1 - P_{ii}(t) \;=\; \tfrac{3}{4} - \tfrac{3}{4}\,e^{-\alpha t} \;=\; \tfrac{3}{4}\left(1 - e^{-\alpha t}\right)$$ **Step 5 — Invert to find evolutionary distance.** Solve for $\alpha t$ (the expected number of substitutions per site = evolutionary distance $d$): $$\tfrac{4}{3}p \;=\; 1 - e^{-\alpha t}$$ $$e^{-\alpha t} \;=\; 1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p$$ $$-\alpha t \;=\; \ln\!\left(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p\right)$$ $$\boxed{\,d_{JC} \;=\; \alpha t \;=\; -\tfrac{3}{4}\,\ln\!\left(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p\right)\,}$$ **Step 6 — Interpretation.** - When $p \to 0$ (very similar sequences): $d_{JC} \approx p$ (linear regime). - When $p \to 3/4$ (saturation, sequences effectively random): $d_{JC} \to \infty$. This is the **multiple-hit correction** — raw $p$ *underestimates* true distance because it ignores back-mutations. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — JC69 Distance **Problem.** Two aligned DNA sequences are 100 bp long. They differ at 12 sites. Compute the JC69 evolutionary distance and the ML-corrected estimate versus raw $p$. **Step 1.** Observed proportion of differences: $$p \;=\; \tfrac{12}{100} \;=\; 0.12$$ **Step 2.** Plug into the JC69 formula: $$d_{JC} \;=\; -\tfrac{3}{4}\,\ln\!\left(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}\cdot 0.12\right)$$ $$=\; -\tfrac{3}{4}\,\ln\!\left(1 - 0.16\right) \;=\; -\tfrac{3}{4}\,\ln(0.84)$$ **Step 3.** Evaluate the logarithm: $\ln(0.84) \approx -0.1744$ **Step 4.** Final answer: $$d_{JC} \;=\; -\tfrac{3}{4}\cdot(-0.1744) \;\approx\; 0.1308 \text{ substitutions/site}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** Raw $p = 0.1200$, corrected $d = 0.1308$. The correction is **~9%** — exactly as expected. For larger $p$, the correction grows dramatically, providing phylogenetic signal that naive pairwise identity would discard. ### 3.3 Worked Example — K2P Distance with Transitions/Transversions **Problem.** Two aligned sequences of length 250 bp show: 20 transition sites, 8 transversion sites. Compute the K2P distance. **Step 1.** Compute the proportions: $$p \;=\; \tfrac{20 + 8}{250} \;=\; 0.112$$ $$P \;=\; \tfrac{20}{250} \;=\; 0.080 \quad (\text{transitions})$$ $$Q \;=\; \tfrac{8}{250} \;=\; 0.032 \quad (\text{transversions})$$ **Step 2.** Plug into the K2P formula: $$d_{K2P} \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\ln(1 - 2\cdot 0.080 - 0.032) \;-\; \tfrac{1}{4}\,\ln(1 - 2\cdot 0.032)$$ $$=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\,\ln(0.808) \;-\; \tfrac{1}{4}\,\ln(0.936)$$ **Step 3.** Evaluate the logs: $\ln(0.808) \approx -0.2132$ $\ln(0.936) \approx -0.0661$ **Step 4.** Final answer: $$d_{K2P} \;=\; -\tfrac{1}{2}\cdot(-0.2132) \;-\; \tfrac{1}{4}\cdot(-0.0661) \;=\; 0.1066 + 0.0165 \;\approx\; 0.1231 \text{ substitutions/site}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** Transition/transversion bias matters. Since transitions occur more frequently than transversions in real DNA, separating them gives a *more accurate* distance estimate than JC69 when $P > Q$. ### 3.4 Worked Example — Fitch Parsimony on a 4-Taxon Tree **Problem.** Given 4 aligned sequences at one site: $A: A$, $B: A$, $C: G$, $D: G$, and tree $((A,B),(C,D))$, compute the minimum number of substitutions. **Step 1 — Forward pass.** - Internal node $X = LCA(A,B)$: - $S_A = \{A\}$, $S_B = \{A\}$. Intersection non-empty. - $S_X = \{A\}$ → **0 mutations at $X$**. - Internal node $Y = LCA(C,D)$: - $S_C = \{G\}$, $S_D = \{G\}$. Intersection non-empty. - $S_Y = \{G\}$ → **0 mutations at $Y$**. - Root $R = LCA(X,Y)$: - $S_X = \{A\}$, $S_Y = \{G\}$. Intersection is **empty**. - $S_R = \{A, G\}$ → **1 mutation at $R$**. **Step 2 — Total mutations** = 0 + 0 + 1 = **1 substitution**. **Step 3 — Backward pass:** Assign $R = A$ (consistent with child $X$), $X = A$, $Y = G$. The single mutation is on the branch from $R$ down to $Y$. **Final parsimony score for this site = 1.** ### 3.5 Worked Example — Neighbor-Joining on a 4-Taxon Distance Matrix **Problem.** Given a distance matrix $D$ for taxa $A, B, C, D$: $$D = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 9 & 9 \\ 5 & 0 & 10 & 10 \\ 9 & 10 & 0 & 8 \\ 9 & 10 & 8 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$ Find the NJ tree. **Step 1 — Compute row sums.** $r_A = 0+5+9+9 = 23$ $r_B = 5+0+10+10 = 25$ $r_C = 9+10+0+8 = 27$ $r_D = 9+10+8+0 = 27$ Total: $R = 102$. **Step 2 — Compute $Q(i,j)$ using $n = 4$.** $$Q(A,B) = (4-2)\cdot 5 - 23 - 25 = 10 - 48 = -38$$ $$Q(A,C) = 2\cdot 9 - 23 - 27 = 18 - 50 = -32$$ $$Q(A,D) = 2\cdot 9 - 23 - 27 = -32$$ $$Q(B,C) = 2\cdot 10 - 25 - 27 = 20 - 52 = -32$$ $$Q(B,D) = 2\cdot 10 - 25 - 27 = -32$$ $$Q(C,D) = 2\cdot 8 - 27 - 27 = 16 - 54 = -38$$ **Step 3 — Most negative $Q$ selects the pair.** $Q(A,B) = -38$ and $Q(C,D) = -38$ tie. Pick $(A,B)$. **Step 4 — Compute branch lengths to new node $u$.** $$d(A,u) = \tfrac{1}{2} d(A,B) + \tfrac{1}{2(n-2)}(r_A - r_B) = \tfrac{5}{2} + \tfrac{1}{4}(23-25) = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0$$ $$d(B,u) = d(A,B) - d(A,u) = 5 - 2 = 3.0$$ **Step 5 — Update distances** from $u$ to $C$ and $D$: $$d(u,C) = \tfrac{1}{2}[d(A,C) + d(B,C) - d(A,B)] = \tfrac{1}{2}[9 + 10 - 5] = 7$$ $$d(u,D) = \tfrac{1}{2}[9 + 10 - 5] = 7$$ **Step 6 — Repeat** on the new 3-taxon matrix $\{u, C, D\}$ until all are joined. The final unrooted tree topology is $((A,B),(C,D))$ with the central branch length computed analogously. > [!NOTE] > **Why NJ over UPGMA?** UPGMA forces the assumption that all leaves are **equidistant from the root** (molecular clock). NJ relaxes this — it works correctly even when rates differ across lineages, which is the rule (not the exception) in real evolution. ### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Inference Methods | Criterion | UPGMA | Neighbor-Joining | Maximum Parsimony | Maximum Likelihood | |---|---|---|---|---| | Tree type | Rooted | Unrooted | Unrooted | Unrooted | | Molecular clock | **Required** | Not required | Not required | Not required | | Speed | Very fast | Fast | Moderate | Slow | | Optimality criterion | Greedy clustering | Greedy clustering | Minimum substitutions | Maximum L(data \| tree) | | Best for | Closely related sequences | General use | Small datasets | Distantly related | | Disadvantage | Biased if rates vary | Only locally optimal | Long-branch attraction | Computationally expensive | | Branch lengths | Yes (ultrametric) | Yes | Implicit (count) | Yes | | Bootstrap common? | Rare | Common | Common | Common | | KTU weight | ★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★ | --- ## 4.
