In a rooted tree, the root marks the **direction of evolutionary time**. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Differentiate between rooted and unrooted phylogenetic trees. In what situation would you choose to construct a rooted tree?** **Model Answer:** A **rooted phylogenetic tree** has a single designated *root node* representing the **most recent common ancestor (MRCA)** of all included taxa. The root fixes a direction in evolutionary time, so every internal branch has a clear *ancestor-to-descendant* orientation. Mathematically, a rooted binary tree with $n$ leaves has **$n-1$ internal nodes** and **$2n-2$ edges**. An **unrooted phylogenetic tree** does not designate an ancestor; it only shows the **pattern of relatedness** among taxa as a free-branching network. It has $n-2$ internal nodes and $2n-3$ edges for the binary case. A rooted tree is **preferred when:** - An **outgroup taxon** is available — a species known to have diverged *before* the rest (the radiation's ancestor). - We want to infer **directionality of evolution** (e.g., which gene arose first). - Studies of **ancestral character reconstruction**. If the outgroup is unknown or the goal is purely to display similarity clusters (e.g., exploratory analysis), an unrooted tree suffices. > [!NOTE] > **Key marks (3-mark question breakdown):** > - Definition of rooted tree (1 mark). > - Definition of unrooted tree (1 mark). > - Justification of rooted-tree use (1 mark). --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Explain the term "molecular clock" and how UPGMA uses it. Why is Neighbor-Joining often preferred over UPGMA in practice?** **Model Answer:** The **molecular clock hypothesis**, proposed by Zuckerkandl and Pauling (1965), states that **substitutions accumulate at an approximately constant rate per unit time** across different evolutionary lineages. If true, the **branch lengths from the root to every tip are equal** — making the tree *ultrametric*. **UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** is a distance-based clustering algorithm that *explicitly relies* on the molecular clock. At each step it merges the two closest clusters (using arithmetic-mean linkage), and assigns the merge point at distance $d/2$ from each. The resulting tree is therefore **rooted and ultrametric**. **Why Neighbor-Joining is preferred over UPGMA in practice:** NJ relaxes the molecular clock assumption by introducing the **Q-criterion**, which corrects for the fact that close pairs of taxa share recent common ancestors. UPGMA will return a *biased* tree topology if rates vary across lineages (the **long-branch attraction artifact** is worsened). NJ produces **unrooted** trees and is **robust to unequal rates**, which is the biologically realistic case — different genes and species evolve at different rates. Hence NJ is the **default choice** in software like MEGA and PHYLIP for routine analysis. > [!NOTE] > **3-mark breakdown:** > - Definition of molecular clock (1 mark). > - UPGMA's reliance on it (1 mark). > - Reason NJ preferred (1 mark). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) **INTERNAL CHOICE — answer EITHER Question A OR Question B.** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 model]** **(a) Define the Jukes–Cantor (JC69) model of nucleotide substitution. Derive the JC69 evolutionary distance formula $d = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p)$ from first principles, clearly stating each assumption. (7 Marks)** **(b) Two aligned DNA sequences of length 500 bp differ at 75 sites. Compute the JC69 evolutionary distance and comment on whether multiple-hit correction has a meaningful effect here. (7 Marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution to A(a):** **Assumptions of JC69:** 1. **Equilibrium base frequencies** are all equal: $\pi_A = \pi_T = \pi_G = \pi_C = 1/4$. 2. **All substitution types are equally likely**: A↔T, A↔G, A↔C, T↔G, T↔C, G↔C all occur at the **same rate**. 3. Sites evolve **independently** (no linkage). 4. Substitution rate is **constant across sites and over time** (no rate heterogeneity). 5. There is **no overlap among substitutions** (Poisson process assumption). **Derivation:** Let $\alpha$ be the per-site substitution rate (so each specific nucleotide-to-nucleotide transition occurs at $\alpha/3$). Solve the Markov chain $P(t) = e^{Qt}$ with JC69 rate matrix $Q$. Eigen-decomposition yields: $$P_{ii}(t) = \tfrac{1}{4} + \tfrac{3}{4}e^{-\alpha t}$$ $$P_{ij}(t) = \tfrac{1}{4} - \tfrac{1}{4}e^{-\alpha t}, \quad (i \neq j)$$ Probability two descendants differ at a site: $$p = 1 - P_{ii}(t) = \tfrac{3}{4}(1 - e^{-\alpha t})$$ Rearrange: $$e^{-\alpha t} = 1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p$$ Take the natural log: $$-\alpha t = \ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p)$$ Since $d = \alpha t$ is the evolutionary distance: $$\boxed{d_{JC} = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p)}$$ > [!NOTE] > **7-mark breakdown for (a):** > - Listing all 5 JC69 assumptions (2 marks). > - Setting up the rate matrix / Markov equation (1 mark). > - Obtaining the transition probabilities (2 marks). > - Algebraic inversion to the final formula (2 marks). --- ##### **Model Solution to A(b):** **Step 1 — Compute raw proportion of differences.** $$p = \tfrac{75}{500} = 0.150$$ **Step 2 — Apply the JC69 formula.** $$d_{JC} = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}\cdot 0.150)$$ $$= -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - 0.200) = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(0.800)$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm.** $\ln(0.800) = -0.22314$ **Step 4 — Final substitution rate.** $$d_{JC} = -\tfrac{3}{4}\cdot(-0.22314) = 0.16736 \text{ substitutions/site}$$ **Step 5 — Compare to raw $p$.** - Raw $p = 0.1500$ sites/site. - Corrected $d_{JC} = 0.1674$ sites/site. - **Absolute correction** = $0.0174$ sites/site. - **Relative correction** = $\tfrac{0.0174}{0.1500}\cdot 100\% \approx 11.6\%$. **Comment.** Yes, the multiple-hit correction has a meaningful effect. At $p=0.15$, approximately 1 in every 9 apparent differences is actually the result of *undetected* double or triple substitutions at the same site. JC69 corrects for this hidden signal. As $p$ approaches $3/4$, the correction diverges — reflecting the breakdown of the alignment's phylogenetic informativeness due to saturation. > [!NOTE] > **7-mark breakdown for (b):** > - Correct computation of $p$ (1 mark). > - Substitution into formula (2 marks). > - Numerical evaluation to $0.1674$ (2 marks). > - Quantitative comment on the magnitude of the correction (2 marks). --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – model paper]** **(a) Compare distance-based and character-based methods of phylogenetic inference. List one algorithm for each category. (7 Marks)** **(b) Apply the Fitch algorithm to compute the minimum parsimony score for the following alignment column on the tree $((A,B),(C,D))$ where the leaves are $A:T$, $B:T$, $C:A$, $D:G$. (7 Marks)** --- ##### **Model Solution to B(a):** | Feature | Distance-Based | Character-Based | |---|---|---| | Input | Pairwise distance matrix | Aligned characters per site | | Output | Single tree by clustering | Tree(s) minimizing/maximizing an optimality criterion | | Optimality | Local greedy (clustering) | Global (searches tree space) | | Examples | UPGMA, Neighbor-Joining | Maximum Parsimony, Maximum Likelihood, Bayesian | | Speed | Very fast (polynomial) | Slow (NP-hard for parsimony) | | Information used | Only pairwise summary | Every site independently | | Best for | Quick exploratory trees | Publication-quality phylogenies | - **Distance-based algorithm:** **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** — uses Q-criterion to iteratively join closest pair while correcting for shared ancestry. - **Character-based algorithm:** **Maximum Parsimony (Fitch algorithm)** — selects the tree requiring the fewest character-state changes, computed site-by-site. **Why character methods dominate:** Distance methods lose information by collapsing the alignment into a single number per pair. Character methods retain the original signal and can recover correct topologies even under conditions of heterogeneity that mislead distance methods. They also provide an explicit optimality criterion (parsimony score, likelihood value) enabling statistical comparison. > [!NOTE] > **7-mark breakdown for (a):** > - 5-row comparison table (5 marks). > - One algorithm named per category (1 mark). > - Concluding sentence on which is preferred (1 mark). --- ##### **Model Solution to B(b):** Given the tree $((A,B),(C,D))$ and the leaf states $A:T$, $B:T$, $C:A$, $D:G$. **Step 1 — Initialize leaf sets.** $$S_A = \{T\}, \quad S_B = \{T\}, \quad S_C = \{A\}, \quad S_D = \{G\}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the internal node $X$ above $(A,B)$.** $S_A \cap S_B = \{T\}$. Intersection is non-empty. $$S_X = \{T\}, \quad \text{mutations at } X: 0$$ **Step 3 — Compute the internal node $Y$ above $(C,D)$.** $S_C \cap S_D = \{A\} \cap \{G\} = \emptyset$. Intersection is empty. $$S_Y = \{A, G\}, \quad \text{mutations at } Y: 1$$ **Step 4 — Compute the root $R$.** $S_X \cap S_Y = \{T\} \cap \{A,G\} = \emptyset$. Intersection is empty. $$S_R = \{A, G, T\}, \quad \text{mutations at } R: 1$$ **Step 5 — Total parsimony score.** $$\text{Score} = 0 + 1 + 1 = 2 \text{ substitutions}$$ **Step 6 — Backward pass for ancestral assignments.** - Choose $R = T$ (also in $S_X$, so valid). - $X = T$ (forced, since $S_X = \{T\}$). - $Y = A$ (choose arbitrarily; e.g., prefer state that minimizes future mutations, here both $A$ and $G$ are equally parsimonious). - The two mutations occur on branches $R \to Y$ and $Y \to D$ (or $Y \to C$, depending on $Y$'s choice). > [!NOTE] > **7-mark breakdown for (b):** > - Initialization of leaf sets (1 mark). > - Forward pass at $X$ (1 mark). > - Forward pass at $Y$ (1 mark). > - Forward pass at $R$ (1 mark). > - Correct parsimony score = 2 (2 marks). > - Backward pass / ancestral assignment (1 mark). --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places students LOSE marks in phylogenetics questions:** > > 1. **Confusing rooted vs unrooted tree node counts** — students often write "$n$ internal nodes" for both. Memorize: rooted binary = $n-1$, unrooted binary = $n-2$. > > 2. **Forgetting the Jukes–Cantor multiple-hit correction** — when asked for evolutionary distance, students give raw $p$ instead of $-\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p)$. This loses **3 marks** in a single 7-mark sub-question. > > 3. **Failing to distinguish transitions from transversions** — when a question gives separate counts, you MUST split them; otherwise you cannot use the K2P formula correctly. > > 4. **Calling NJ a "rooted" method** — it is unrooted. Only UPGMA guarantees a rooted tree (and only under molecular clock). > > 5. **Misidentifying Fitch's algorithm step** — at an internal node, students often write the *union* of children sets when the intersection is non-empty. This is wrong: **intersection first, union only as a fallback**. > > 6. **Skipping units** — always state substitutions/site or millions of years. Examiners deduct marks for unit-less answers. > > 7. **Writing "phenetics = phylogenetics"** — phenetics is OBSOLETE similarity-based classification; phylogenetics = cladistics + evolutionary analysis. Worth a full 3-mark question lost. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - [ ] Phylogenetics = study of evolutionary history via molecular characters. - [ ] Two tree types: **rooted** (with MRCA) and **unrooted** (relatedness only). - [ ] Binary rooted tree with $n$ leaves → $n-1$ internal nodes, $2n-2$ edges. - [ ] Binary unrooted tree with $n$ leaves → $n-2$ internal nodes, $2n-3$ edges. - [ ] Four-step pipeline: alignment → distance matrix / character matrix → tree inference → bootstrap validation. - [ ] **UPGMA** = rooted, molecular-clock required, ultrametric, arithmetic-mean linkage. - [ ] **Neighbor-Joining** = unrooted, no clock assumption, uses **Q-matrix** $Q(i,j)=(n-2)d(i,j)-r_i-r_j$. - [ ] **Maximum Parsimony** = fewest substitutions, Fitch's algorithm, NP-hard search. - [ ] **Maximum Likelihood** = maximizes $P(\text{data} \mid \text{tree}, \text{model})$. - [ ] **Bayesian Inference** = uses posterior $P(T \mid D) \propto P(D \mid T)P(T)$ via MCMC. - [ ] **JC69 formula:** $d = -\tfrac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \tfrac{4}{3}p)$, equal substitution rates. - [ ] **K2P formula:** $d = -\tfrac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q) - \tfrac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$, separates Ts from Tv. - [ ] Multiple-hit correction is the *key* advantage of model-based distances over raw $p$. - [ ] **Bootstrap** = column-resampling, typically N=1000 replicates, support shown as % on nodes. - [ ] Real-world uses: SARS-CoV-2 variant tracking, forensics, drug-target conservation, microbiome 16S rRNA. - [ ] **Distinguish:** Phenetics ≠ Phylogenetics; Phylogeny ≠ Phylogenetics.

topics.title: Phylogenetic Tree Basics and Construction Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Phylogenetic Tree Basics and Construction Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Phylogenetic Tree** (also called an **Evolutionary Tree** or **Dendrogram**) is a branching diagram that represents the inferred evolutionary relationships among a set of biological entities — most commonly species, genes, or proteins — based on similarities and differences in their physical or genetic characteristics.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A phylogenetic tree is a tree-like graphical representation of the evolutionary history of a group of organisms or sequences, where the leaves represent the current (extant) taxa, the internal nodes represent the hypothetical common ancestors, and the branch lengths represent the amount of evolutionary change (in substitutions per site) that has occurred along that lineage.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your **family pedigree chart** (the one traced by grandmothers). Your grandparents are at the top, your parents in the middle, and you and your cousins at the bottom. The closer two cousins are connected through the chart, the more recently they share a common ancestor. 

A phylogenetic tree works exactly the same way, but instead of tracking surnames, it tracks **DNA, RNA, or protein sequence changes** over millions of years. The "family" is a group of organisms or genes, and the "ancestors" are inferred hypothetical sequences.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Intuition:** A phylogenetic tree is essentially a *hypothesis* about history. It does not depict the *true* tree (which is unknown), but rather the *most likely* tree based on observed molecular data.

## 1.3 Core Terminology (Syllabus Highlights)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **OTU** | Operational Taxonomic Unit — a terminal leaf in the tree (e.g., a species or sequence). |
| **Root** | The common ancestor of all taxa; present only in rooted trees. |
| **Internal Node** | Hypothetical ancestral unit from which descendant taxa evolved. |
| **Branch (Edge)** | Represents a lineage evolving through time. |
| **Branch Length** | Amount of evolutionary change (substitutions per site). |
| **Topology** | The branching pattern of the tree, ignoring branch lengths. |
| **Clade** | A group of taxa sharing a common ancestor (monophyletic group). |
| **Bifurcation** | A node splitting into exactly two descendants. |
| **Multifurcation (Polytomy)** | A node with more than two descendants. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Evolutionary distance is typically measured in **substitutions per site per unit time**, with **1 PAM = 1% of amino acid positions changing over an evolutionary period of 10^8 years** (Dayhoff scale).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Phylogenetic Trees

Phylogenetic trees are classified along two primary dimensions:

### A. Rooted vs. Unrooted Trees
- **Rooted Tree:** Has a designated root node (assumed common ancestor) and a clear direction of evolutionary time. The root is usually placed using an **outgroup** — a taxon known to be evolutionarily distant from the ingroup.
- **Unrooted Tree:** Shows only the relationship pattern (topology) without assuming a direction of evolution. Contains one fewer node than a rooted tree for the same taxa.

### B. Bifurcating vs. Multifurcating
- **Bifurcating (Fully Resolved) Tree:** Every internal node has exactly two descendants. Number of internal nodes = $n - 1$ (for rooted tree with $n$ leaves).
- **Multifurcating Tree:** Contains polytomies; indicates uncertainty or simultaneous speciation events.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Tree Construction

The construction of a phylogenetic tree follows a logical pipeline:

1. **Sequence Acquisition:** Obtain homologous sequences (DNA/RNA/protein) from multiple species.
2. **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA):** Align sequences using tools like ClustalW, MUSCLE, or MAFFT.
3. **Model Selection:** Choose an appropriate substitution model (e.g., Jukes-Cantor, Kimura-2P).
4. **Tree Inference:** Apply a tree-building algorithm (distance-based or character-based).
5. **Tree Evaluation:** Assess reliability via **bootstrap analysis** (≥ 70% considered reliable).

## 2.3 Major Tree Construction Methods (Deep Dive)

### 2.3.1 Distance-Based Methods
These methods first convert the aligned sequences into a pairwise **distance matrix**, then reconstruct the tree from the matrix.

**(a) UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)**
- Assumes a **molecular clock** (constant rate of evolution).
- Produces a **rooted tree**.
- Step-by-step logic:
  1. Start with each taxon as its own cluster.
  2. Merge the two closest clusters.
  3. Recompute distances using arithmetic mean formula (see Section 3).
  4. Repeat until one cluster remains.

**(b) Neighbor-Joining (NJ) Method**
- Does **NOT** assume a molecular clock.
- Produces an **unrooted tree**.
- Corrects for rate variation by using the **Q-criterion** (sum of branch lengths in star tree minus observed distance).
- Widely used due to speed and good accuracy for closely related sequences.

### 2.3.2 Character-Based Methods
These methods use the actual aligned characters (nucleotides or amino acids) directly.

**(a) Maximum Parsimony (MP)**
- Finds the tree that requires the **fewest evolutionary changes** (substitutions).
- Logic: "The simplest explanation is the most likely."
- Searches tree space using algorithms like branch-and-bound or heuristic search.

**(b) Maximum Likelihood (ML)**
- Finds the tree that **maximizes the probability** of observing the data, given a specific substitution model.
- Computationally expensive but statistically rigorous.
- **Bayesian Inference (BI)** is a related method using posterior probabilities (MCMC sampling).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Purpose / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Jukes-Cantor Distance** | $d = -\frac{3}{4} \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{4}{3}p\right)$ | Single-parameter model; all substitutions equally likely. |
| **Kimura 2-Parameter (K2P)** | $d = -\frac{1}{2}\ln(1-2P-Q) - \frac{1}{4}\ln(1-2Q)$ | Distinguishes transitions (P) from transversions (Q). |
| **p-Distance** | $p = \frac{n_d}{n}$ | Proportion of observed differences per site. |
| **UPGMA Recombination** | $d_{(AB),C} = \frac{d_{A,C} + d_{B,C}}{2}$ | Arithmetic mean of distances from new cluster to others. |
| **NJ Q-Criterion** | $Q(i,j) = (n-2)\cdot d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$ | $u_i = \sum_k d_{ik}$; selects neighbors to join. |
| **Number of Unrooted Trees** | $N_u = \frac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-3}(n-3)!}$ | For $n \geq 3$ taxa. |
| **Number of Rooted Trees** | $N_r = \frac{(2n-3)!}{2^{n-2}(n-2)!}$ | For $n \geq 2$ taxa. |
| **Bootstrap Support** | $BS = \frac{\text{Times clade appears}}{N_{\text{replicates}}} \times 100\%$ | Reliability metric (≥ 70% = strong). |
| **PAM Unit** | 1 PAM $= 1\%$ amino acid change | Dayhoff evolutionary scale. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** Phylogenetic analysis is foundational in **drug target discovery** (tracking viral mutations like SARS-CoV-2 variants), **forensic identification**, **conservation biology**, and **molecular epidemiology**. In KTU valuation, drawing the **correct distance matrix and tree topology** carries 70% of the marks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Jukes-Cantor (JC69) Distance

The Jukes-Cantor model assumes that each nucleotide ($A, T, G, C$) can mutate to any other nucleotide at an **equal rate $\alpha$** per unit time.

**Setup:**
- Total substitution rate out of any nucleotide = $3\alpha$.
- The probability of a nucleotide being unchanged after time $t$:

$$P_{\text{unchanged}}(t) = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{3}{4}e^{-4\alpha t}$$

- The probability of being changed (i.e., observed difference $p$):

$$p(t) = \frac{3}{4}\left(1 - e^{-4\alpha t}\right)$$

**Deriving the distance formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \frac{3}{4}\left(1 - e^{-4\alpha t}\right) \\[4pt]
\frac{4p}{3} &= 1 - e^{-4\alpha t} \\[4pt]
e^{-4\alpha t} &= 1 - \frac{4p}{3} \\[4pt]
-4\alpha t &= \ln\!\left(1 - \frac{4p}{3}\right) \\[4pt]
d = 2\alpha t &= -\frac{1}{2}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{4p}{3}\right) \cdot \frac{1}{1} \\[4pt]
\therefore d &= -\frac{3}{4}\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{4p}{3}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of transitions:**
- Step 1: Start with the equilibrium expression for $p$.
- Step 2: Isolate the exponential term.
- Step 3: Apply natural logarithm to both sides.
- Step 4: Recall that $d = 2\alpha t$ (since distance is the total branch length over two lineages).
- Step 5: Final JC69 formula.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the factor 2?** The distance $d$ represents the **sum of branch lengths** from the root to two leaves, hence $d = \alpha t + \alpha t = 2\alpha t$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: UPGMA Tree Construction

**Given the following pairwise distance matrix for 4 taxa (A, B, C, D):**

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 2 | 4 | 6 |
| **B** | 2 | 0 | 4 | 6 |
| **C** | 4 | 4 | 0 | 6 |
| **D** | 6 | 6 | 6 | 0 |

**Step 1:** Identify the minimum distance. The smallest value is **d(A, B) = 2**.

**Step 2:** Merge A and B into a new cluster **(AB)** at height $h(AB) = 2/2 = 1$.

**Step 3:** Recompute distances using the UPGMA formula:
$$d_{(AB),C} = \frac{d_{A,C} + d_{B,C}}{2} = \frac{4+4}{2} = 4$$

$$d_{(AB),D} = \frac{d_{A,D} + d_{B,D}}{2} = \frac{6+6}{2} = 6$$

**Updated Matrix:**

| | (AB) | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(AB)** | 0 | 4 | 6 |
| **C** | 4 | 0 | 6 |
| **D** | 6 | 6 | 0 |

**Step 4:** Identify next minimum: **d((AB), C) = 4**.

**Step 5:** Merge (AB) and C into **(AB)C** at height $h((AB)C) = 4/2 = 2$.

**Step 6:** Recompute distance to D:
$$d_{((AB)C),D} = \frac{d_{(AB),D} + d_{C,D}}{2} = \frac{6+6}{2} = 6$$

**Step 7:** Final merge: ((AB)C) and D at height $h(((AB)C)D) = 6/2 = 3$.

**Final UPGMA Tree (with branch lengths):**
- Branch from root to D: $3 - 2 = 1$ (subtracting child height from parent height for each branch).
- Branch from ((AB)C) to C: $2 - 1 = 1$.
- Branches to A and B: $1 - 0 = 1$ each.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valuation Tip:** Always compute the **height of each node** as $h = d/2$ and use the rule "branch length = parent height − child height" — this is the most common UPGMA question in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Neighbor-Joining Q-Matrix

**Given 5 taxa with the following distance matrix D:**

| | A | B | C | D | E |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 5 | 9 | 9 | 8 |
| **B** | 5 | 0 | 10 | 10 | 9 |
| **C** | 9 | 10 | 0 | 8 | 7 |
| **D** | 9 | 10 | 8 | 0 | 3 |
| **E** | 8 | 9 | 7 | 3 | 0 |

**Step 1:** Compute the row sums $u_i = \sum_{k} d_{ik}$:
- $u_A = 0 + 5 + 9 + 9 + 8 = 31$
- $u_B = 5 + 0 + 10 + 10 + 9 = 34$
- $u_C = 9 + 10 + 0 + 8 + 7 = 34$
- $u_D = 9 + 10 + 8 + 0 + 3 = 30$
- $u_E = 8 + 9 + 7 + 3 + 0 = 27$

**Step 2:** Compute Q-matrix using $Q(i,j) = (n-2)\cdot d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$, where $n = 5$, so $n-2 = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(A,B) &= 3(5) - 31 - 34 = 15 - 65 = -50 \\
Q(A,C) &= 3(9) - 31 - 34 = 27 - 65 = -38 \\
Q(A,D) &= 3(9) - 31 - 30 = 27 - 61 = -34 \\
Q(A,E) &= 3(8) - 31 - 27 = 24 - 58 = -34 \\
Q(B,C) &= 3(10) - 34 - 34 = 30 - 68 = -38 \\
Q(B,D) &= 3(10) - 34 - 30 = 30 - 64 = -34 \\
Q(B,E) &= 3(9) - 34 - 27 = 27 - 61 = -34 \\
Q(C,D) &= 3(8) - 34 - 30 = 24 - 64 = -40 \\
Q(C,E) &= 3(7) - 34 - 27 = 21 - 61 = -40 \\
Q(D,E) &= 3(3) - 30 - 27 = 9 - 57 = -48
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** The minimum Q-value is **Q(A, B) = −50**. Therefore, A and B are **neighbors** to be joined.

**Step 4:** Branch lengths from new node U to A and B:
- $S_{UA} = \frac{d_{AB}}{2} + \frac{u_A - u_B}{2(n-2)} = \frac{5}{2} + \frac{31-34}{6} = 2.5 - 0.5 = 2.0$
- $S_{UB} = \frac{d_{AB}}{2} + \frac{u_B - u_A}{2(n-2)} = 2.5 + 0.5 = 3.0$

**Step 5:** Recompute distances from U to all other taxa and repeat the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** "Q finds Quality pairs" — the lowest (most negative) Q value indicates the **most likely neighbors** in the unrooted tree topology.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Phylogenetic Tree Architecture (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ROOT - Common Ancestor] --> B[Internal Node X]
    A --> C[Internal Node Y]
    B --> D[Taxon 1 - Leaf]
    B --> E[Taxon 2 - Leaf]
    C --> F[Taxon 3 - Leaf]
    C --> G[Taxon 4 - Leaf]
    B -.->|Branch Length| H[Substitutions per Site]
    C -.->|Branch Length| H
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#abebc6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Yellow = root, Blue = internal nodes (hypothetical ancestors), Green = observed taxa (leaves), Red dashed = the metric on each branch (substitutions per site).

## 4.2 Tree Construction Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: Sequence Retrieval from Databases NCBI PDB UniProt] --> P2[Step 2: Multiple Sequence Alignment ClustalW MUSCLE MAFFT]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Model Selection JC69 K2P PAM WAG]
    P3 --> P4{Step 4: Method Choice}
    P4 -->|Distance Based| P5a[UPGMA Neighbor Joining]
    P4 -->|Character Based| P5b[Maximum Parsimony Maximum Likelihood Bayesian]
    P5a --> P6[Step 5: Tree Inference]
    P5b --> P6
    P6 --> P7[Step 6: Bootstrap Validation 100 to 1000 replicates]
    P7 --> P8[Step 7: Final Tree Visualization FigTree iTOL MEGA]
    style P1 fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#1e8449
    style P2 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#2874a6
    style P3 fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#b7950b
    style P4 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21
    style P5a fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483
    style P5b fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483
    style P6 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#cb4335
    style P7 fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#9a7d0a
    style P8 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
```

## 4.3 Comparison of Tree-Building Methods

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[Phylogenetic Tree Methods] --> D[Distance Based]
    M --> C[Character Based]
    D --> U[UPGMA - Rooted, Molecular Clock]
    D --> N[NJ - Unrooted, No Clock Assumption]
    C --> MP[Max Parsimony - Fewest Changes]
    C --> ML[Max Likelihood - Highest Probability]
    C --> BI[Bayesian Inference - Posterior Probability]
    style M fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#d4efdf,stroke:#1e8449
    style C fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d
    style U fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#7d6608
    style N fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#7d6608
    style MP fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style ML fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
    style BI fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Takeaway:** Distance methods (UPGMA, NJ) are **fast and scalable**; character-based methods (MP, ML, BI) are **slower but more accurate**, especially for divergent sequences.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define phylogenetic tree. List any four basic terms used in phylogenetic tree representation.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A phylogenetic tree is a branching diagram representing the evolutionary relationships among a set of taxa (species, genes, or proteins) based on similarities and differences in their molecular sequences.

**Four basic terms:**
1. **Root:** The common ancestor of all taxa.
2. **Branch (Edge):** Represents a lineage evolving over time.
3. **Internal Node:** A hypothetical common ancestor.
4. **Leaf (OTU / Taxon):** The observed entity at the tip of a branch.

> [!NOTE]
> Each term = 0.5 marks; definition = 1 mark.

---

### **Question 2: What is the difference between rooted and unrooted phylogenetic trees?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Rooted Tree | Unrooted Tree |
|---|---|---|
| **Direction of Evolution** | Has a designated root and clear evolutionary direction. | No designated root; only shows relationship pattern. |
| **Common Ancestor** | Identifies a single common ancestor of all taxa. | Does not assume a common ancestor. |
| **Inference Method** | UPGMA, Maximum Parsimony (with outgroup). | Neighbor-Joining, ML (without outgroup). |
| **Number of Nodes (n taxa)** | Contains $2n - 1$ nodes (including root). | Contains $2n - 2$ nodes (no root). |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks - Internal Choice)

### **Question A: Construct the UPGMA tree for the following distance matrix and explain each step.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 2 | 5 | 7 |
| **B** | 2 | 0 | 5 | 7 |
| **C** | 5 | 5 | 0 | 5 |
| **D** | 7 | 7 | 5 | 0 |

#### **Part (a): Explain the UPGMA algorithm and its assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** is a distance-based hierarchical clustering algorithm that builds a **rooted tree** by iteratively merging the two closest clusters.

**Key Assumptions:**
1. **Molecular Clock Hypothesis:** All lineages evolve at a constant rate. Thus, the height of a node is directly proportional to its distance from the leaves.
2. **Ultrametricity:** The distance from the root to every leaf is equal.
3. Equal weighting of all pairs (hence "unweighted").

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Initialize each taxon as a singleton cluster.
2. Compute the pairwise distance matrix.
3. Identify the two clusters $(i, j)$ with the minimum distance $d_{ij}$.
4. Merge $i$ and $j$ into a new cluster $(ij)$ with height $h(ij) = d_{ij}/2$.
5. Recompute the distance from the new cluster to all other clusters $k$ using the arithmetic mean:
$$d_{(ij),k} = \frac{d_{i,k} + d_{j,k}}{2}$$
6. Repeat steps 3–5 until a single cluster remains.

**Limitations:**
- The molecular clock assumption often fails in real data.
- Inaccurate when substitution rates vary across lineages.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Definitions (2) + Assumptions (2) + Algorithm steps (2) + Limitation (1) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Part (b): Apply the UPGMA algorithm to the given matrix and draw the final tree with branch lengths. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Minimum distance is **d(A, B) = 2**. Merge A and B.

Height: $h(AB) = 2/2 = 1$.

**Step 2:** Recompute distances:
$$d_{(AB),C} = \frac{d_{A,C} + d_{B,C}}{2} = \frac{5+5}{2} = 5$$

$$d_{(AB),D} = \frac{d_{A,D} + d_{B,D}}{2} = \frac{7+7}{2} = 7$$

**Updated Matrix:**

| | (AB) | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(AB)** | 0 | 5 | 7 |
| **C** | 5 | 0 | 5 |
| **D** | 7 | 5 | 0 |

**Step 3:** Minimum distance is **d((AB), C) = 5** and **d(C, D) = 5** (tie). Choose (AB) and C.

Height: $h((AB)C) = 5/2 = 2.5$.

**Step 4:** Recompute distance to D:
$$d_{((AB)C),D} = \frac{d_{(AB),D} + d_{C,D}}{2} = \frac{7+5}{2} = 6$$

**Updated Matrix:**

| | ((AB)C) | D |
|---|---|---|
| **((AB)C)** | 0 | 6 |
| **D** | 6 | 0 |

**Step 5:** Final merge at height $h = 6/2 = 3$.

**Branch Lengths (parent height − child height):**
- Root to D: $3 - 0 = 3$
- ((AB)C) to (AB): $2.5 - 1 = 1.5$
- ((AB)C) to C: $2.5 - 0 = 2.5$
- (AB) to A: $1 - 0 = 1$
- (AB) to B: $1 - 0 = 1$

**Final Tree Sketch:**
```
         ((((AB)C)D)
        /             \
   ((AB)C)             D
   /       \          (3.0)
 (AB)       C
 /  \      (2.5)
A    B
(1) (1)
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to divide distance by 2** to get the node height: Costs 1 mark.
> - **Not recomputing the matrix** after each merge: Costs 2 marks.
> - **Missing branch lengths in the final tree**: Costs 1 mark.
> - **Choosing the wrong pair in case of ties** without showing the updated matrix: Costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question B: Explain the Neighbor-Joining (NJ) algorithm. Given the following distance matrix, compute the Q-matrix and identify the pair of neighbors.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | 0 | 4 | 6 | 8 |
| **B** | 4 | 0 | 8 | 10 |
| **C** | 6 | 8 | 0 | 8 |
| **D** | 8 | 10 | 8 | 0 |

#### **Part (a): Explain the NJ algorithm, its key formula, and advantages over UPGMA. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** method, proposed by **Saitou and Nei (1987)**, is a distance-based algorithm that constructs an **unrooted tree** by iteratively joining a pair of "neighbors" — taxa connected by a single internal node in the **star decomposition** of the tree.

**Key Idea:** Unlike UPGMA, NJ does **not** assume a molecular clock. It corrects for the fact that in a star tree, pairs with long branches tend to have inflated distances, by using the **Q-criterion**.

**Q-Criterion Formula:**
$$Q(i,j) = (n-2) \cdot d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$$

where $u_i = \sum_{k=1}^{n} d_{ik}$ is the row sum of distances for taxon $i$, and $n$ is the number of current taxa.

**Algorithm Steps:**
1. Compute the distance matrix $D$ for $n$ taxa.
2. Compute the $Q$-matrix using the formula above.
3. Select the pair $(i, j)$ with the **minimum (most negative) Q value** as neighbors.
4. Compute the branch lengths from the new internal node U to $i$ and $j$:
$$S(i,U) = \frac{d_{ij}}{2} + \frac{u_i - u_j}{2(n-2)}, \quad S(j,U) = d_{ij} - S(i,U)$$
5. Compute new distances from U to all remaining taxa $k$:
$$d_{U,k} = \frac{d_{i,k} + d_{j,k} - d_{i,j}}{2}$$
6. Replace $i$ and $j$ by U in the matrix and repeat until 3 taxa remain, then connect them directly.

**Advantages over UPGMA:**
- Does **not** require the molecular clock assumption.
- Produces **unrooted trees**, which are more accurate.
- Performs better when **substitution rates vary** across lineages.
- Widely used in tools like **MEGA, ClustalW, PAUP***.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** Concept (2) + Q-formula (1.5) + Algorithm (2) + Branch length formulas (1) + Advantages (0.5) = 7 marks.

---

#### **Part (b): Compute the Q-matrix and identify the neighbor pair. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Compute row sums $u_i$ for $n = 4$:**
- $u_A = 0 + 4 + 6 + 8 = 18$
- $u_B = 4 + 0 + 8 + 10 = 22$
- $u_C = 6 + 8 + 0 + 8 = 22$
- $u_D = 8 + 10 + 8 + 0 = 26$

**Step 2: Compute Q-matrix using $Q(i,j) = (n-2)\cdot d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$ where $n-2 = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(A,B) &= 2(4) - 18 - 22 = 8 - 40 = -32 \\
Q(A,C) &= 2(6) - 18 - 22 = 12 - 40 = -28 \\
Q(A,D) &= 2(8) - 18 - 26 = 16 - 44 = -28 \\
Q(B,C) &= 2(8) - 22 - 22 = 16 - 44 = -28 \\
Q(B,D) &= 2(10) - 22 - 26 = 20 - 48 = -28 \\
Q(C,D) &= 2(8) - 22 - 26 = 16 - 48 = -32
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Q-Matrix:**

| | A | B | C | D |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A** | — | -32 | -28 | -28 |
| **B** | -32 | — | -28 | -28 |
| **C** | -28 | -28 | — | -32 |
| **D** | -28 | -28 | -32 | — |

**Step 4: Identify the minimum Q-value:**
- Minimum = **−32**, occurring in **two pairs**: (A, B) and (C, D).
- Both pairs are valid neighbors. Conventionally, the first-encountered pair **(A, B)** is chosen.

**Step 5: Branch lengths from new node U to A and B:**
- $S(A, U) = \frac{4}{2} + \frac{18 - 22}{2(2)} = 2 + \frac{-4}{4} = 2 - 1 = 1$
- $S(B, U) = 4 - 1 = 3$

**Step 6: Distances from U to C and D:**
- $d_{U,C} = \frac{d_{A,C} + d_{B,C} - d_{A,B}}{2} = \frac{6 + 8 - 4}{2} = 5$
- $d_{U,D} = \frac{d_{A,D} + d_{B,D} - d_{A,B}}{2} = \frac{8 + 10 - 4}{2} = 7$

**Result:** A and B are joined as neighbors with an edge to U of length 1 (to A) and 3 (to B). Continue with the new 3×3 matrix $\{(U, C, D) = (0, 5, 7); (5, 0, 8); (7, 8, 0)\}$ for the final star topology.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Forgetting to multiply by (n-2)**: Common error; costs 2 marks.
> - **Choosing the most positive Q value instead of minimum**: Costs 2 marks.
> - **Not showing the Q-matrix in tabular form**: Costs 0.5 marks.
> - **Skipping the row sum calculation**: Costs 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ✅ A **phylogenetic tree** is a hypothesis of evolutionary history based on molecular data.
- ✅ Key terms: **root, internal node, leaf (OTU), branch, branch length, topology, clade, polytomy**.
- ✅ **Rooted trees** = have a common ancestor; **unrooted trees** = relationship pattern only.
- ✅ **UPGMA**: Distance-based, **rooted**, assumes **molecular clock**, uses arithmetic mean.
- ✅ **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)**: Distance-based, **unrooted**, no molecular clock assumption, uses **Q-criterion**.
- ✅ **Maximum Parsimony (MP)**: Character-based, minimizes the number of substitutions.
- ✅ **Maximum Likelihood (ML)**: Character-based, maximizes probability of observed data given a model.
- ✅ **Jukes-Cantor (JC69)**: $d = -\frac{3}{4}\ln(1 - \frac{4p}{3})$ — single parameter, equal substitution rates.
- ✅ **Kimura 2-Parameter (K2P)**: Distinguishes **transitions** (P) from **transversions** (Q).
- ✅ **UPGMA cluster height** = $d/2$; **branch length** = parent height − child height.
- ✅ **NJ Q-formula**: $Q(i,j) = (n-2)\cdot d_{ij} - u_i - u_j$; select **minimum** (most negative).
- ✅ **Bootstrap support** ≥ 70% indicates a **reliable clade**.
- ✅ For $n$ taxa: **rooted trees** = $\frac{(2n-3)!}{2^{n-2}(n-2)!}$, **unrooted trees** = $\frac{(2n-5)!}{2^{n-3}(n-3)!}$.
- ✅ Common tools: **MEGA, PAUP*, PhyML, RAxML, MrBayes, ClustalW, MUSCLE, MAFFT**.
- ✅ Always perform **Multiple Sequence Alignment (MSA)** before tree construction.
- ✅ **Outgroup** is used to root an otherwise unrooted tree.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UPGMA
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** UPGMA

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> **UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** is a simple, bottom-up (agglomerative) hierarchical clustering algorithm used to construct **rooted phylogenetic trees** from a symmetric distance matrix of pairwise evolutionary distances between taxonomic units (OTUs/taxa/sequences). At each iteration, the two clusters with the **smallest pairwise distance** are merged into a new composite cluster, and the branch length of the new internal node is set to **half** the distance between the merged clusters (i.e., the node height = d/2). Distances from the new cluster to all remaining clusters are then updated as the **unweighted arithmetic mean** of the component distances.

The method is formally described in **Sneath & Sokal (1973)** as a numerical taxonomy technique and is one of the most frequently tested clustering algorithms in KTU bioinformatics modules.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **teacher organizing students into a family tree based on how similar their answers are on a quiz**.

- **Step 1** — You scan the similarity matrix and find the two students whose answers are closest (most similar). You merge them into a small "study group."
- **Step 2** — You recompute the similarity of this new group to every other student by **averaging** the similarities of the two original students with each outsider (no weighting — every individual counts equally).
- **Step 3** — You repeat: find the next closest pair (it could be a single student, a group, or two groups), merge them, and re-average.
- **Step 4** — Keep going until **everyone is in one big group**. That final group is the root of the tree, and the order in which merges occurred defines the tree topology.

The **height** of each merge in the tree is half the distance at which the two groups came together — so two very similar sequences merge near the bottom (recent divergence), while very different sequences only merge near the top (ancient divergence).

> [!NOTE] **Key Intuition — Why "Unweighted"?**
> The word *unweighted* means every original taxon in a cluster contributes **equally** to the average distance calculation, regardless of how many descendants a sub-cluster has. If cluster X contains 1 sequence and cluster Y contains 50 sequences, the new distance is still the simple arithmetic mean — not weighted by cluster size. This is what distinguishes UPGMA from WPGMA (Weighted PGMA).

## 1.3 Important Assumptions Embedded in UPGMA

| Assumption | Consequence |
|---|---|
| **Molecular Clock** | All lineages evolve at the same constant rate. |
| **Ultrametric tree** | All tips (leaves) are equidistant from the root. |
| **Additivity of distances** | Pairwise distances are assumed to satisfy the ultrametric inequality: $d(x,z) \leq \max(d(x,y), d(y,z))$. |
| **Symmetric input matrix** | $d(i,j) = d(j,i)$ and $d(i,i) = 0$. |

> [!WARNING] **KTU Pitfall**
> UPGMA produces **incorrect trees when the molecular clock is violated** (i.e., when different lineages evolve at different rates). For real biological data where rates vary, **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** is preferred. Examiners frequently award marks for stating this limitation explicitly.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Symbols

- **Distance metric**: $d_{ij}$ — pairwise evolutionary distance between taxa $i$ and $j$.
- **Cluster height**: $h(\text{cluster}) = d_{\text{merged}}/2$.
- **Branch length**: $b = h(\text{parent}) - h(\text{child})$.
- **Number of taxa**: **n**.
- **Time complexity**: $O(n^3)$ in the naive form; $O(n^2 \log n)$ with a heap, $O(n^2)$ with the **nearest-neighbor** heuristic (Sneath, 1973).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Operating Principle

UPGMA is an **agglomerative** algorithm — it starts with $n$ singleton clusters (each containing one sequence/taxon) and successively merges the two closest clusters until only one cluster containing all taxa remains. The sequence of merges defines the tree topology.

### 2.1.1 The Two Core Operations

**Operation A — Cluster Selection (Minimum Search)**

Given the current distance matrix $\mathbf{D}^{(t)}$ at iteration $t$, find the pair $(i^*, j^*)$ that satisfies:

$$
(i^*, j^*) = \arg\min_{i<j} \, d_{ij}^{(t)}
$$

If multiple pairs share the minimum, **any one may be chosen** — the final tree may differ in topology but not in mathematical validity. KTU answers should explicitly state this tie-breaking convention.

**Operation B — Distance Update (Arithmetic Mean)**

After merging clusters $i$ and $j$ into a new cluster $(i \cup j)$, the distance from this new cluster to every other cluster $k$ is:

$$
d_{(i \cup j), k} = \frac{d_{i,k} + d_{j,k}}{2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] **Why divide by 2 (not by 2 + 1 + ...)?**
> Because **each original cluster contributes exactly one term** to the sum (regardless of how many taxa it internally contains). If we instead averaged all pairwise distances between members of $i$ and members of $k$, we would use a denominator of $|i| \cdot |k|$. The "unweighted" definition here means each **member cluster** of the new composite carries weight 1.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Symbol / Meaning | Used In |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $d_{(i\cup j), k} = \dfrac{d_{i,k} + d_{j,k}}{2}$ | Arithmetic mean update | Every iteration |
| 2 | $h(i \cup j) = \dfrac{d_{i,j}}{2}$ | Height of new internal node | Tree drawing |
| 3 | $b_v = h(\text{parent}(v)) - h(v)$ | Branch length of node $v$ | Final tree |
| 4 | $\sum_{x \in C_i} \sum_{y \in C_j} d_{xy} \big/ (\vert C_i \vert \cdot \vert C_j \vert)$ | Generalized form of mean for arbitrary cluster sizes | Theory only |
| 5 | Ultrametric condition: $d(x,z) \le \max(d(x,y), d(y,z))$ | Necessary for UPGMA validity | Assumption check |
| 6 | Time complexity | $O(n^3)$ naive; $O(n^2)$ optimized | Complexity question |
| 7 | Tie-breaking rule | Pick any pair with minimum $d_{ij}$ | Implementation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Never** use the vertical bar `|` inside formula table cells — KTU markdown rendering will break. Use `\vert` or `\mid` instead, as shown above in row 4.

## 2.3 Engineering & Biological Utility

- **Phylogenetics** — quick reconstruction of evolutionary trees from sequence distance matrices (e.g., 16S rRNA gene trees).
- **Microbial ecology** — clustering OTUs from amplicon sequencing into operational taxonomic units.
- **Gene expression analysis** — clustering co-expressed genes using expression-distance metrics.
- **Chemoinformatics** — clustering small molecules by Tanimoto or Euclidean distance.
- **Population genetics** — building haplotype trees.

## 2.4 Strengths and Limitations

| Strengths | Limitations |
|---|---|
| **Conceptually simple** — easy to implement by hand on the exam. | Assumes **molecular clock**; fails under unequal rates. |
| Produces a **rooted, ultrametric tree** — height is interpretable as time. | Can produce **incorrect topologies** with heterotachous data. |
| Deterministic given a fixed tie-breaking rule. | Sensitive to noise in distance estimates. |
| Works on any symmetric distance matrix. | $O(n^3)$ — slow for very large $n$ (e.g., $> 10{,}000$ taxa). |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The General UPGMA Update Equation (Full Derivation)

Let cluster $u$ be formed by merging clusters $i$ and $j$ at iteration $t$. Let cluster $k$ be any other cluster. We want to express $d_{u,k}^{(t+1)}$ in terms of distances among the **original taxa** $\mathcal{T} = \{T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n\}$.

### 3.1.1 Construction of the Update Rule

By definition, the new cluster $u = i \cup j$ contains **all taxa** from both $i$ and $j$. Define:

$$
\mathcal{C}_u = \mathcal{C}_i \cup \mathcal{C}_j
$$

where $\mathcal{C}_i$ is the set of original taxa inside cluster $i$, and likewise for $j$. By the definition of an arithmetic mean distance between two sets:

$$
d_{u,k} = \frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C}_u \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{C}_k \vert} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{C}_u} \sum_{y \in \mathcal{C}_k} d_{xy}
$$

Split the outer sum across the two source clusters:

$$
d_{u,k} = \frac{1}{(\vert \mathcal{C}_i \vert + \vert \mathcal{C}_j \vert) \cdot \vert \mathcal{C}_k \vert} \left[ \sum_{x \in \mathcal{C}_i} \sum_{y \in \mathcal{C}_k} d_{xy} + \sum_{x \in \mathcal{C}_j} \sum_{y \in \mathcal{C}_k} d_{xy} \right]
$$

Multiplying and dividing each numerator/denominator pair by the appropriate cluster size yields the original one-to-one arithmetic mean definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d_{u,k} &= \frac{\vert \mathcal{C}_i \vert}{(\vert \mathcal{C}_i \vert + \vert \mathcal{C}_j \vert)} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C}_i \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{C}_k \vert} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{C}_i} \sum_{y \in \mathcal{C}_k} d_{xy}}_{=\, d_{i,k}} \\
&\quad + \frac{\vert \mathcal{C}_j \vert}{(\vert \mathcal{C}_i \vert + \vert \mathcal{C}_j \vert)} \cdot \underbrace{\frac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C}_j \vert \cdot \vert \mathcal{C}_k \vert} \sum_{x \in \mathcal{C}_j} \sum_{y \in \mathcal{C}_k} d_{xy}}_{=\, d_{j,k}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For **UPGMA**, the convention is that each *immediate child cluster* ($i$ or $j$) of $u$ is given **equal weight** of $\dfrac{1}{2}$, regardless of how many taxa each contains. This forces $\vert \mathcal{C}_i \vert = \vert \mathcal{C}_j \vert = 1$ in the weighting formula, which collapses to:

$$
\boxed{\, d_{(i\cup j),\, k} \;=\; \frac{d_{i,k} + d_{j,k}}{2} \,}
$$

> [!NOTE] **Contrast with WPGMA**
> In WPGMA (Weighted PGMA), the weights are $\dfrac{1}{\vert \mathcal{C}_i \vert + \vert \mathcal{C}_j \vert}$ instead of $\dfrac{1}{2}$. Hence a singleton cluster and a 50-taxon cluster contribute proportionally. This is the single most testable theoretical distinction.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — A KTU 14-Mark Classic

> [!IMPORTANT] **Exam Setup**
> Given the pairwise distance matrix below for **five taxa** A, B, C, D, E, construct the UPGMA tree. Show all intermediate matrices, the merge sequence, and the final rooted tree with branch lengths.

### 3.2.1 Initial Distance Matrix $\mathbf{D}^{(0)}$

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(0)} = \begin{array}{c|ccccc}
 & A & B & C & D & E \\ \hline
A & 0 & 2 & 4 & 6 & 6 \\
B & 2 & 0 & 4 & 6 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 4 & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 6 & 0 & 4 \\
E & 6 & 6 & 6 & 4 & 0
\end{array}
$$

### 3.2.2 Iteration 1 — Merge the Closest Pair

**Minimum search:** Scanning the upper triangle, the smallest distance is $d(A,B) = 2$.

- **Merge:** Combine A and B into a new cluster $(AB)$.
- **Height:** $h(AB) = d(A,B)/2 = 2/2 = \mathbf{1}$.
- **Branch lengths from (AB) to its children:** both are $1 - 0 = \mathbf{1}$.

**Distance update** using the arithmetic mean formula for $k \in \{C, D, E\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d((AB), C) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d(A,C) + d(B,C)\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(4 + 4) = 4 \\
d((AB), D) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d(A,D) + d(B,D)\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6) = 6 \\
d((AB), E) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d(A,E) + d(B,E)\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix after Iteration 1, $\mathbf{D}^{(1)}$:**

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(1)} = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & (AB) & C & D & E \\ \hline
(AB) & 0 & 4 & 6 & 6 \\
C & 4 & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 0 & 4 \\
E & 6 & 6 & 4 & 0
\end{array}
$$

### 3.2.3 Iteration 2 — Merge the Next Closest Pair

**Minimum search:** The smallest entry is $d((AB), C) = 4$ (tie with $d(D,E)=4$; we pick $(AB),C$).

- **Merge:** Form cluster $((AB)C)$.
- **Height:** $h(((AB)C)) = 4/2 = \mathbf{2}$.
- **Branch length from $((AB)C)$ down to $(AB)$:** $2 - 1 = \mathbf{1}$.
- **Branch length from $((AB)C)$ down to $C$:** $2 - 0 = \mathbf{2}$.

**Distance update** for $k \in \{D, E\}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(((AB)C), D) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d((AB),D) + d(C,D)\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6) = 6 \\
d(((AB)C), E) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d((AB),E) + d(C,E)\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix after Iteration 2, $\mathbf{D}^{(2)}$:**

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(2)} = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & ((AB)C) & D & E \\ \hline
((AB)C) & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 0 & 4 \\
E & 6 & 4 & 0
\end{array}
$$

### 3.2.4 Iteration 3 — Merge D and E

**Minimum search:** $d(D,E) = 4$ is now the unique minimum.

- **Merge:** Form cluster $(DE)$.
- **Height:** $h(DE) = 4/2 = \mathbf{2}$.
- **Branch lengths:** both D and E have length $2 - 0 = \mathbf{2}$ from $(DE)$.

**Distance update** for $k = ((AB)C)$:

$$
d((DE), ((AB)C)) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d(D, ((AB)C)) + d(E, ((AB)C))\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6) = 6
$$

**Matrix after Iteration 3, $\mathbf{D}^{(3)}$:**

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(3)} = \begin{array}{c|cc}
 & ((AB)C) & (DE) \\ \hline
((AB)C) & 0 & 6 \\
(DE) & 6 & 0
\end{array}
$$

### 3.2.5 Final Merge — Iteration 4

- **Merge:** Combine $((AB)C)$ and $(DE)$.
- **Height:** $h(\text{root}) = 6/2 = \mathbf{3}$.
- **Branch length from root down to $((AB)C)$:** $3 - 2 = \mathbf{1}$.
- **Branch length from root down to $(DE)$:** $3 - 2 = \mathbf{1}$.

## 3.3 Final Tree — ASCII Representation (Newick + Diagrammatic)

**Newick format:**

$$
(((A:1,\; B:1):1,\; C:2):1,\; (D:2,\; E:2):1);
$$

**Visual depiction (root at top):**

```
Height 3 ───────────────────────────── Root
             ┌─────────────────────┴─────────────────────┐
          [1]                                           [1]
Height 2 ──┴──                                       ───┴──
       ((AB)C)                                     (DE)
       ┌───┴───┐                                  ┌────┴────┐
    [1]      [2]                                [2]        [2]
Height 1 ─┴──  │                                  │          │
         (AB)  C                                  D          E
         ┌─┴─┐
       [1] [1]
Height 0 ─A   B
```

## 3.4 Summary Table of Merge History

| Iteration | Pair Merged | Distance | New Height | Branch from Parent |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | (A, B) | 2 | 1 | A: 1, B: 1 |
| 2 | ((AB), C) | 4 | 2 | (AB): 1, C: 2 |
| 3 | (D, E) | 4 | 2 | D: 2, E: 2 |
| 4 | (((AB)C), (DE)) | 6 | 3 | ((AB)C): 1, (DE): 1 |

## 3.5 Verification of the Ultrametric Property

Compute the path lengths from the root (height 3) to every leaf:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell(A) &= 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \\
\ell(B) &= 1 + 1 + 1 = 3 \\
\ell(C) &= 1 + 2 + 0 = 3 \\
\ell(D) &= 1 + 2 + 0 = 3 \\
\ell(E) &= 1 + 2 + 0 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

All five paths equal **3** — confirming the tree is **ultrametric**, as UPGMA guarantees under its molecular-clock assumption.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 UPGMA Algorithm Flowchart (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start: Read n taxa and distance matrix D"] --> B["Display initial D"]
    B --> C{"More than 1 cluster remaining?"}
    C -- "No" --> Z["Output final rooted tree"]
    C -- "Yes" --> D["Scan matrix for smallest dij"]
    D --> E["Tie? Pick any minimum pair"]
    E --> F["Merge i and j into new cluster u"]
    F --> G["Compute height h_u = dij / 2"]
    G --> H["Compute d_uk = di,k + dj,k / 2 for all k"]
    H --> I["Remove rows/columns i and j"]
    I --> J["Insert row/column for new cluster u"]
    J --> K["Record merge event i,j at height h_u"]
    K --> C
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the UPGMA Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["Input Layer"]
        I1["Pairwise Distance Matrix D"]
        I2["Number of Taxa n"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["UPGMA Engine"]
        P1["Preprocessor: Symmetry Check D_ij = D_ji"]
        P2["Minimum-Finder Module: argmin dij"]
        P3["Merge Constructor: form cluster u"]
        P4["Distance Updater: arithmetic mean"]
        P5["Height Calculator: h = dij/2"]
        P6["Merge-Log Recorder"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT["Output Layer"]
        O1["Newick String"]
        O2["Rooted Tree Diagram"]
        O3["Merge History Table"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P3 --> P5
    P4 --> P2
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P2
    P6 --> O1
    P6 --> O2
    P6 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Iteration State Machine — Distance Matrix Evolution

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> InitialMatrix: D0 with 5 taxa
    InitialMatrix --> AfterIteration1: Merge A,B at d=2, h=1
    AfterIteration1 --> AfterIteration2: Merge AB,C at d=4, h=2
    AfterIteration2 --> AfterIteration3: Merge D,E at d=4, h=2
    AfterIteration3 --> AfterIteration4: Merge final two at d=6, h=3
    AfterIteration4 --> FinalRootedTree: Ultrametric tree of height 3
    FinalRootedTree --> [*]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**Define UPGMA. State any two assumptions of the algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

> **UPGMA (Unweighted Pair Group Method with Arithmetic Mean)** is an agglomerative hierarchical clustering algorithm that constructs a **rooted phylogenetic tree** from a distance matrix by repeatedly merging the two clusters with the **smallest pairwise distance** and updating the remaining distances using the **arithmetic mean** of the distances of the merged pair to every other cluster.
>
> **Assumptions:**
> 1. **Molecular clock hypothesis** — all lineages evolve at a constant, equal rate, so the tree is **ultrametric**.
> 2. **Pairwise distances are accurate and satisfy the ultrametric inequality** $d(x,z) \le \max(d(x,y), d(y,z))$ for all triples $(x,y,z)$.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**How is the height of a newly created internal node decided in the UPGMA algorithm? Why is the resulting tree rooted?**

**Model Answer:**

> When two clusters $i$ and $j$ are merged, the height of the resulting internal node is set to **half the distance** at which they were merged:
> $$h(u) = \frac{d_{ij}}{2}$$
> Because every internal node carries a well-defined **time/height** coordinate and all leaves lie at height **zero**, the resulting tree has an unambiguous **root** at the top — the node representing the very last merge. Hence UPGMA always returns a **rooted** tree (in contrast to Neighbor-Joining, which returns an unrooted tree).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Adapted]**

Given the following pairwise distance matrix for **four taxa** W, X, Y, Z:

$$
\mathbf{D} = \begin{array}{c|cccc}
 & W & X & Y & Z \\ \hline
W & 0 & 2 & 6 & 6 \\
X & 2 & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
Y & 6 & 6 & 0 & 4 \\
Z & 6 & 6 & 4 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**(a)** Construct the UPGMA phylogenetic tree step by step. Show every intermediate distance matrix and explicitly state the height of each new internal node. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw the final rooted tree, label all nodes with their heights, and write its **Newick representation**. Verify that the resulting tree is **ultrametric** by computing the path length from the root to each leaf. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Iteration 1**

Scan the upper triangle of $\mathbf{D}$. Minimum distance is $d(W,X) = 2$.

- Merge W and X into cluster $(WX)$.
- Height: $h(WX) = 2/2 = \mathbf{1}$.
- Branch lengths: both W and X have length $1$.

Update distances using the arithmetic mean:

$$
\begin{aligned}
d((WX), Y) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d(W,Y) + d(X,Y)\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6) = 6 \\
d((WX), Z) &= \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d(W,Z) + d(X,Z)\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6) = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Matrix after Iteration 1:**

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(1)} = \begin{array}{c|ccc}
 & (WX) & Y & Z \\ \hline
(WX) & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
Y & 6 & 0 & 4 \\
Z & 6 & 4 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Iteration 2**

Minimum distance is $d(Y,Z) = 4$.

- Merge Y and Z into cluster $(YZ)$.
- Height: $h(YZ) = 4/2 = \mathbf{2}$.
- Branch lengths: both Y and Z have length $2$.

Update distance:

$$
d((YZ), (WX)) = \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl[d(Y,(WX)) + d(Z,(WX))\bigr] = \tfrac{1}{2}(6 + 6) = 6
$$

**Matrix after Iteration 2:**

$$
\mathbf{D}^{(2)} = \begin{array}{c|cc}
 & (WX) & (YZ) \\ \hline
(WX) & 0 & 6 \\
(YZ) & 6 & 0
\end{array}
$$

**Iteration 3 — Final Merge**

Merge $(WX)$ and $(YZ)$ with $d = 6$.

- Height: $h(\text{root}) = 6/2 = \mathbf{3}$.
- Branch lengths: $(WX)$ has length $3 - 1 = 2$; $(YZ)$ has length $3 - 2 = 1$.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Final rooted tree:**

```
Height 3 ─────────────────────────── Root
                ┌────────────────────┴────────────────────┐
             [2]                                       [1]
Height 2 ─────┴────                                  ───┴──
            (WX)                                     (YZ)
            ┌─┴─┐                                   ┌────┴────┐
         [1] [1]                                 [2]        [2]
Height 1 ─ W   X                                  Y          Z
Height 0 ─ (leaves)
```

**Newick format:**

$$
((W:1,\; X:1):2,\; (Y:2,\; Z:2):1);
$$

**Ultrametric verification — root-to-leaf path lengths:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell(W) &= 2 + 1 + 0 = 3 \\
\ell(X) &= 2 + 1 + 0 = 3 \\
\ell(Y) &= 1 + 2 + 0 = 3 \\
\ell(Z) &= 1 + 2 + 0 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

All four leaves lie at total distance **3** from the root → tree is **ultrametric** ✓

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **UPGMA algorithm** in detail with its pseudocode-style steps. Clearly state the role of the **arithmetic mean** in distance updating. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare UPGMA with **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** with respect to (i) molecular-clock assumption, (ii) rooting of the output tree, (iii) computational complexity, and (iv) robustness to unequal evolutionary rates. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Detailed Algorithm:**

1. **Input:** A symmetric $n \times n$ distance matrix $\mathbf{D}$ containing pairwise distances $d_{ij}$ for taxa $i, j \in \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$.
2. **Initialize:** Create $n$ singleton clusters $C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_n$. Each cluster has height $h(C_i) = 0$.
3. **Repeat** until only one cluster remains:
    - **(a)** Scan $\mathbf{D}$ and locate the pair $(i^*, j^*)$ minimizing $d_{ij}$.
    - **(b)** Merge $i^*$ and $j^*$ into a new cluster $u$.
    - **(c)** Set the height of $u$ as $h(u) = d_{i^*j^*}/2$.
    - **(d)** For every remaining cluster $k$, update the distance using the arithmetic mean:
    $$d(u, k) = \frac{d_{i^*, k} + d_{j^*, k}}{2}$$
    - **(e)** Delete rows/columns of $i^*$ and $j^*$ from the matrix; insert a new row/column for $u$.
    - **(f)** Record the merge $(i^*, j^*, h(u))$ in the tree-building log.
4. **Output:** A rooted tree whose internal nodes carry the recorded heights.

**Role of the Arithmetic Mean:**

The arithmetic mean is the *defining operation* of UPGMA. It expresses the principle that **every original cluster is treated as a single equal entity** when computing its distance to any third cluster — regardless of how many taxa the cluster internally contains. The constant denominator **2** is what makes UPGMA "**unweighted**": if the denominator depended on cluster size, the algorithm would become **WPGMA** (weighted).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

| Property | **UPGMA** | **Neighbor-Joining (NJ)** |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Molecular-clock assumption | **Required** (assumes equal rates) | **Not required** (corrects for rate variation via $Q$-matrix) |
| (ii) Rooting of output tree | **Always rooted** (root is the last merge) | **Unrooted tree** (requires outgroup to root) |
| (iii) Computational complexity | $O(n^3)$ naive; $O(n^2)$ optimized | $O(n^3)$ naive; $O(n^2)$ with heuristics |
| (iv) Robustness to unequal rates | **Poor** — biased when rates vary | **Good** — designed to handle unequal rates |

**Key conclusion:** Use **UPGMA** for clock-like data (e.g., closely related viral or bacterial sequences) where speed and a natural rooted tree are wanted; use **NJ** when rates are suspected to vary across lineages.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to halve the distance** when assigning node heights. Many students write the merge distance as the height — this loses 1–2 marks per question.
> 2. **Using a weighted average** (dividing by 2+1 etc.) when the matrix has multi-taxon clusters — this is WPGMA, not UPGMA, and is a textbook definition error.
> 3. **Not showing intermediate matrices** — KTU examiners require every distance update to be shown step by step.
> 4. **Drawing unrooted trees** for UPGMA output — UPGMA is **rooted by construction**.
> 5. **Failing to verify ultrametricity** in the final tree (root-to-leaf paths should all be equal).
> 6. **Omitting the Newick string** — the Newick format is a frequently tested representation worth 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Confusing UPGMA with single-linkage or complete-linkage clustering** — these are *other* hierarchical methods (WPGMA uses the mean; single uses min; complete uses max).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **UPGMA** = **U**nweighted **P**air **G**roup **M**ethod with **A**rithmetic mean.
- It is **agglomerative** (bottom-up) and **hierarchical**.
- At every step, merge the **pair with the smallest distance** in the current matrix.
- **Node height** = (merge distance) / 2.
- **Distance update**: $d_{u,k} = (d_{i,k} + d_{j,k}) / 2$ — denominator is **always 2**, never the cluster sizes.
- Output is a **rooted**, **ultrametric** tree.
- **Assumes a molecular clock** — fails when substitution rates vary across lineages.
- Time complexity is **$O(n^3)$** in the naive form, $O(n^2)$ with optimization.
- Total root-to-leaf path length is **identical for every leaf** (ultrametric property).
- The **Newick string** is the standard textual representation of the tree.
- Compare with **WPGMA** (weighted mean), **NJ** (no clock, unrooted), and **single/complete linkage** (min/max instead of mean).
- KTU exam hot-spots: numerical worked examples, Newick format, ultrametric verification, complexity, and the UPGMA vs NJ comparison.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Neighbour Joining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Neighbour Joining

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Neighbour-Joining (NJ)** is a **bottom-up (agglomerative) hierarchical clustering algorithm** used in computational phylogenetics to reconstruct an **unrooted additive evolutionary tree** from a matrix of pairwise evolutionary distances between *n* operational taxonomic units (OTUs). It was proposed by **Saitou and Nei (1987)** as an improvement over UPGMA, specifically because it does **not** assume a **molecular clock** (i.e., it does not require all lineages to evolve at the same rate).

Mathematically, NJ seeks the pair of taxa $(i, j)$ that are **true evolutionary neighbours** — that is, the pair that shares an internal node with **no other intermediate taxa** between them — by minimizing a **net divergence criterion** encoded in the **Q-matrix**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties of the NJ Algorithm:**
> - It is a **distance-based** method (requires a fully filled pairwise distance matrix).
> - It produces an **unrooted** tree.
> - It has **no clock assumption** — branch lengths are free to vary.
> - Time complexity: $O(n^3)$ for *n* taxa.
> - It guarantees recovery of the **correct tree** if the input distances are **additive** (tree-like).

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Map Maker's Shortcut"**

Imagine you are a **cartographer** drawing a map of **six villages** scattered across a valley, and you only have the **straight-line road distances** between every pair of villages. Two villages may appear close on the map simply because they are both near a **central hub**, not because they are actually neighbouring each other.

The **Neighbour-Joining algorithm** is like a smart map-maker who first **corrects for this "hub effect"** by computing a **net divergence score (Q-matrix)** that penalizes villages that merely have many close neighbours, and then groups together only the **genuine neighbours** — those that, once joined, would not leave any other village stranded between them.

In each round, NJ:
1. Looks at all possible pairs,
2. Picks the pair with the **smallest corrected distance** (lowest Q-value),
3. **Fuses** them into a new composite "district",
4. **Recomputes** the distances from this new district to all remaining villages,
5. Repeats until **only two districts** remain, which is the final unrooted tree.

This is fundamentally different from **UPGMA**, which blindly joins the closest pair at every step (and gets fooled by hub-like taxa). NJ, in contrast, is **rate-aware** — it asks, *"If I join these two, will the resulting tree remain additive?"*

---

## Physical / Mathematical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Pairwise distance $d(i, j)$** — typically measured in **substitutions per site** (evolutionary distance), e.g., using the **Jukes–Cantor** or **Kimura 2-parameter** model.
- **Number of taxa $n$** — a dimensionless integer.
- **Net divergence $Q(i, j)$** — has the same units as $d(i, j)$.
- **Branch length $\delta(i)$** — same units as $d(i, j)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## The "Why" Behind the NJ Algorithm

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Insight:** Raw pairwise distances $d(i, j)$ are **biased** by the **total branch length** emanating from each taxon. A taxon that has a very long branch to its ancestral node will appear **artificially distant** to every other taxon. The **Q-matrix** subtracts this background "divergence noise" so that the algorithm can identify **true cherry-picked neighbours** in the tree.

---

## Step-by-Step Operational Logic of the NJ Algorithm

**Step 1 — Input:** A symmetric $(n \times n)$ distance matrix $\mathbf{D} = [d(i, j)]$ with zeros on the diagonal.

**Step 2 — Compute row sums (net divergences from each taxon):**
$$r(i) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} d(i, k)$$

**Step 3 — Build the Q-matrix:**
$$Q(i, j) = (n - 2) \cdot d(i, j) - r(i) - r(j)$$

**Step 4 — Select the pair $(i^*, j^*)$ such that $Q(i^*, j^*)$ is the minimum (most negative).**

**Step 5 — Define a new internal node $u$** that joins $i^*$ and $j^*$. Compute the two branch lengths:
$$\delta(i^*) = \frac{d(i^*, j^*)}{2} + \frac{r(i^*) - r(j^*)}{2(n - 2)}$$

$$\delta(j^*) = d(i^*, j^*) - \delta(i^*)$$

**Step 6 — Compute distance from the new node $u$ to every other taxon $k$:**
$$d(u, k) = \frac{d(i^*, k) + d(j^*, k) - d(i^*, j^*)}{2}$$

**Step 7 — Replace rows/columns $i^*$ and $j^*$ with a single row/column for $u$**, reducing the matrix size from $n \times n$ to $(n - 1) \times (n - 1)$.

**Step 8 — Repeat Steps 2–7** until only **2 nodes** remain, at which point the algorithm terminates and returns the **unrooted binary tree** with all branch lengths.

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol / Term** | **Formula / Definition** | **Units** | **Purpose** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $d(i, j)$ | Input pairwise evolutionary distance | substitutions/site | Direct measurement |
| $n$ | Number of taxa in current matrix | dimensionless | Determines divisor $(n-2)$ |
| $r(i)$ | $\sum_{k=1}^{n} d(i, k)$ | substitutions/site | Background divergence of taxon $i$ |
| $Q(i, j)$ | $(n-2) \cdot d(i, j) - r(i) - r(j)$ | substitutions/site | Net divergence; used to pick neighbours |
| $\delta(i^*)$ | $\dfrac{d(i^*, j^*)}{2} + \dfrac{r(i^*) - r(j^*)}{2(n-2)}$ | substitutions/site | Branch length from $i^*$ to new node $u$ |
| $\delta(j^*)$ | $d(i^*, j^*) - \delta(i^*)$ | substitutions/site | Branch length from $j^*$ to new node $u$ |
| $d(u, k)$ | $\dfrac{d(i^*, k) + d(j^*, k) - d(i^*, j^*)}{2}$ | substitutions/site | Distance from new node $u$ to other taxa |
| Stopping rule | $n = 2$ | — | Final two nodes are joined with $d(u, k) / 2$ each |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Exams:** The Q-matrix formula is the single most testable expression. Memorize:  
> **"Q = (n−2)·distance − sum of row i − sum of row j"**  
> Always use `(n - 2)`, not `(n - 1)`.

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

NJ trees are the workhorse of:
- **Molecular epidemiology** — tracking viral lineages (e.g., SARS-CoV-2 variant trees).
- **Drug target discovery** — identifying orthologous protein families.
- **Conservation biology** — reconstructing species divergence histories.
- **Forensic bioinformatics** — microbial source tracking in outbreak investigations.
- **Pipeline tools:** Implemented in **PHYLIP**, **MEGA**, **PAUP***, **R (ape package)**, and **Biopython**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Worked Numerical Example (4 Taxa)

Consider four taxa **A, B, C, D** with the following distance matrix (in substitutions per site):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{D}^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
  & A & B & C & D \\
A & 0 & 8 & 5 & 7 \\
B & 8 & 0 & 9 & 6 \\
C & 5 & 9 & 0 & 10 \\
D & 7 & 6 & 10 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Round 1: From $n = 4$ down to $n = 3$

**Step 1 — Compute row sums $r(i)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r(A) &= 0 + 8 + 5 + 7 = 20 \\
r(B) &= 8 + 0 + 9 + 6 = 23 \\
r(C) &= 5 + 9 + 0 + 10 = 24 \\
r(D) &= 7 + 6 + 10 + 0 = 23
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the Q-matrix using $Q(i, j) = (n - 2) \cdot d(i, j) - r(i) - r(j)$, with $n = 4 \Rightarrow (n - 2) = 2$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(A, B) &= 2(8) - 20 - 23 = 16 - 43 = -27 \\
Q(A, C) &= 2(5) - 20 - 24 = 10 - 44 = -34 \\
Q(A, D) &= 2(7) - 20 - 23 = 14 - 43 = -29 \\
Q(B, C) &= 2(9) - 23 - 24 = 18 - 47 = -29 \\
Q(B, D) &= 2(6) - 23 - 23 = 12 - 46 = -34 \\
Q(C, D) &= 2(10) - 24 - 23 = 20 - 47 = -27
\end{aligned}
$$

**Q-matrix summary:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{Q}^{(4)} = \begin{bmatrix}
  & A & B & C & D \\
A & - & -27 & -34 & -29 \\
B & - & - & -29 & -34 \\
C & - & - & - & -27 \\
D & - & - & - & -
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Select minimum Q:** Two pairs are tied at $-34$: $(A, C)$ and $(B, D)$. We select the lexicographically first pair: **$(A, C)$** with $d(A, C) = 5$.

**Step 4 — Compute branch lengths $\delta(A)$ and $\delta(C)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(A) &= \frac{d(A, C)}{2} + \frac{r(A) - r(C)}{2(n - 2)} \\
         &= \frac{5}{2} + \frac{20 - 24}{2(2)} \\
         &= 2.5 + \frac{-4}{4} = 2.5 - 1.0 = 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(C) &= d(A, C) - \delta(A) = 5 - 1.5 = 3.5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute distance from new node $U = (A, C)$ to remaining taxa $B$ and $D$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(U, B) &= \frac{d(A, B) + d(C, B) - d(A, C)}{2} = \frac{8 + 9 - 5}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6 \\
d(U, D) &= \frac{d(A, D) + d(C, D) - d(A, C)}{2} = \frac{7 + 10 - 5}{2} = \frac{12}{2} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Construct reduced $(3 \times 3)$ distance matrix:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{D}^{(3)} = \begin{bmatrix}
  & U & B & D \\
U & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
B & 6 & 0 & 6 \\
D & 6 & 6 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Round 2: From $n = 3$ down to $n = 2$

**Step 1 — Compute row sums:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r(U) &= 0 + 6 + 6 = 12 \\
r(B) &= 6 + 0 + 6 = 12 \\
r(D) &= 6 + 6 + 0 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Build the Q-matrix, with $n = 3 \Rightarrow (n - 2) = 1$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(U, B) &= 1(6) - 12 - 12 = -18 \\
Q(U, D) &= 1(6) - 12 - 12 = -18 \\
Q(B, D) &= 1(6) - 12 - 12 = -18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — All three pairs are tied at $-18$.** Select $(U, B)$ with $d(U, B) = 6$.

**Step 4 — Compute branch lengths:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(U) &= \frac{6}{2} + \frac{r(U) - r(B)}{2(1)} = 3 + \frac{12 - 12}{2} = 3 + 0 = 3 \\
\delta(B) &= 6 - 3 = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute distance from new node $V = (U, B)$ to the remaining taxon $D$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
d(V, D) &= \frac{d(U, D) + d(B, D) - d(U, B)}{2} = \frac{6 + 6 - 6}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Final two-node branch lengths:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta(V) &= \frac{d(V, D)}{2} = \frac{3}{2} = 1.5 \\
\delta(D) &= 1.5
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Final Unrooted Tree Topology

The reconstructed unrooted tree is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Topology:} \quad ((A, C), B), D
\end{aligned}
$$

with the following branch lengths (in substitutions per site):

| **Branch** | **Length** |
|---|---|
| $A \rightarrow U$ | 1.5 |
| $C \rightarrow U$ | 3.5 |
| $U \rightarrow V$ | 3.0 |
| $B \rightarrow V$ | 3.0 |
| $V \rightarrow D$ | 1.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** The total path length from $A$ to $D$, for example, should equal $d(A, D) = 7$:  
> $\delta(A) + \delta(C) \text{ in } A \to U \to C$ (we go $A \to U \to V \to D$):  
> $1.5 + 3.0 + 1.5 = 6.0$. Hmm, that does not match $d(A, D) = 7$ — this is because the distance matrix is **not perfectly additive**. In real applications, NJ finds the tree that is **closest** to the observed distances in a least-squares sense, not exact.

---

## Summary of Key Derivations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $(n-2)$ appears in Q-matrix:** The factor $(n-2)$ arises from the **Four-Point Condition** — for any four taxa, the sum of the two larger pairwise distances equals the sum of the two smaller ones in a true additive tree. This condition has $(n-2)$ degrees of freedom, which is exactly the divisor needed to normalize the Q-matrix so that the **minimum** correctly identifies **true cherry neighbours** regardless of rate heterogeneity.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
