**Step 4 — Cross-check using the generic formula**

For a peptide of $n$ residues:

$$MW = \sum_{i=1}^{n} MW_i + 18.015$$

This formula adds **18.015 g/mol** to account for the water molecule that is regenerated when the free N-terminus and C-terminus are formed. We confirm:

$$MW = 670.38 + 18.015 = 688.40 \text{ g/mol} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — GC Content & Tm Calculation

**Problem:** For a 14-nt oligo `ATGCGCATATGCGT`, compute the GC% and the Wallace-rule melting temperature.

**Step 1 — Count bases**

$$n_A = 3, \; n_T = 4, \; n_G = 3, \; n_C = 4, \; L = 14$$

**Step 2 — GC content**

$$GC\% = \frac{n_G + n_C}{L} \times 100 = \frac{3 + 4}{14} \times 100 = \frac{7}{14} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Step 3 — Wallace Tm (valid for oligos $\le 14$ nt)**

$$T_m = 2(A + T) + 4(G + C)$$
$$T_m = 2(3 + 4) + 4(3 + 4) = 14 + 28 = 42\,°C$$

> [!NOTE]
> For longer sequences, the student must use the **nearest-neighbor** (SantaLucia) model:

$$T_m = \frac{\Delta H}{\Delta S + R \ln(C_T/4)} - 273.15$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Mapping Real File Formats to Data Types

| File Extension | Data Type | Molecule | Structure | Storage Encoding |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `.fa` / `.fasta` | Sequence | DNA/RNA/Prot | 1-D string | Plain ASCII text |
| `.fastq` | Sequence + Quality | NGS reads | 1-D + per-base Phred | Plain ASCII text |
| `.gb` / `.gbk` | Annotated sequence | DNA/RNA | 1-D + feature table | Plain ASCII (GenBank flat-file) |
| `.embl` | Annotated sequence | DNA/RNA | 1-D + feature table | Plain ASCII (EMBL flat-file) |
| `.pdb` | Atomic coordinates | Macromolecule | 3-D coordinates | Plain ASCII (legacy PDB format) |
| `.cif` / `.mmcif` | Atomic coordinates | Macromolecule | 3-D coordinates | ASCII key-value (mmCIF dictionary) |
| `.gff` / `.gff3` | Genomic features | DNA | 1-D interval list | Tab-separated ASCII |
| `.bed` | Genomic intervals | DNA | 1-D interval list | Tab-separated ASCII |
| `.csv` / `.tsv` | Expression matrix | Omics | 2-D numerical | Plain ASCII tabular |
| `.nwk` | Phylogenetic tree | Any | Hierarchical (Newick) | Parenthesized ASCII |
| `.obo` | Ontology | Any | Directed Acyclic Graph | OBO flat-file |
| `.xml` | Generic structured | Any | Tree | eXtensible Markup Language |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree of Biological Data Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1["Biological Data Types"]
    root1 --> seq["Sequence Data 1D"]
    root1 --> struct["Structure Data 3D"]
    root1 --> omics["Omics Data Matrix"]
    root1 --> ann["Annotation Data"]
    root1 --> lit["Literature Data"]

    seq --> dna["DNA Strings FASTA GenBank"]
    seq --> rna["RNA Strings FASTA GenBank"]
    seq --> prot["Protein Strings FASTA UniProt"]

    struct --> pdb1["PDB Format Legacy"]
    struct --> mmcif1["mmCIF Modern"]
    struct --> model["Homology Models"]

    omics --> micro["Microarray CEL TXT"]
    omics --> rnaseq["RNA Seq Counts TPM"]
    omics --> prot1["Proteomics MS Peaks"]

    ann --> feat["Feature Tables GFF BED"]
    ann --> onto["Ontologies GO HPO OBO"]
    ann --> phylo["Phylogenetic Trees Newick"]

    lit --> pub["PubMed Abstracts"]
    lit --> ftxt["Full Text Articles"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — How a Bioinformatics Tool Processes a Data Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Raw Input File"] --> parse["Parser Tokenizer"]
    parse --> validate["Schema Validator Data Type Check"]
    validate --> cast{"Correct Data Type?"}
    cast -- Yes --> store["Typed Object Memory Model"]
    cast -- No --> err["Reject and Report Format Error"]
    store --> algo["Algorithm Engine Alignment Search Modeling"]
    algo --> result["Result Object Same Type Family"]
    result --> out["Serialized Output File"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Relationship Between Sequence, Structure, and Function

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dna2["DNA Sequence"] --> rna2["mRNA Sequence"]
    rna2 --> prot2["Protein Sequence"]
    prot2 --> fold["3D Fold Structure"]
    fold --> func["Biological Function"]
    func --> phen["Phenotype Disease"]
    dna2 --> var["Variants SNPs Indels"]
    var --> phen
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Central Dogma of Molecular Biology** (DNA → RNA → Protein → Structure → Function) is the *unifying conceptual schema* behind every data type studied in this module. Each transition corresponds to a different data type with its own file format, database, and analysis tools.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Differentiate between FASTA and FASTQ file formats. Mention one clinical-genomics application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | FASTA (`.fa/.fasta`) | FASTQ (`.fastq`) |
|---|---|---|
| Stores | Sequence only | Sequence + per-base quality |
| Symbol alphabet | A C G T U or amino-acid letters | A C G N + Phred quality |
| Quality encoding | None | ASCII Phred (Sanger / Illumina 1.8+) |
| Origin | Pearson / Lipman 1988 | Cock et al. 2010 (NGS era) |

**Applications (1 mark):**
- FASTA → reference genome assembly submission (e.g., NCBI GenBank reference FASTA of GRCh38).
- FASTQ → raw output of Illumina NovaSeq / Oxford Nanopore clinical sequencing.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`
**List any three biological database categories with one example each, and state the primary data type they store.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Nucleotide sequence database** — GenBank / ENA / DDBJ. Stores 1-D DNA/RNA strings + annotations.
2. **Protein sequence database** — UniProtKB. Stores 1-D amino-acid strings + functional annotation.
3. **Macromolecular structure database** — PDB / PDBe / RCSB. Stores 3-D atomic coordinates of proteins and nucleic acids.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Many students wrongly name NCBI as a *single* database. NCBI is a *portal* hosting GenBank, PubMed, RefSeq, dbSNP, etc. The mark is awarded for naming the **specific database** (e.g., GenBank, not NCBI).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a)** Define the term *biological data type*. Classify biological data into **four major classes** with at least one example file format per class. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 1500 bp double-stranded DNA fragment has a base composition of $n_A = 400, n_T = 380, n_G = 360, n_C = 360$. Compute (i) the GC content, (ii) the molecular weight of the fragment (use the per-base mass table: A = 313.21, T = 304.2, G = 329.21, C = 289.18; subtract 61.96 g/mol per strand), and (iii) the Wallace-rule melting temperature (assume the oligo is short enough for the Wallace rule). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Q A (a)

**Definition (2 marks):** A biological data type is a formal classification of biological information based on its molecular origin, structural representation, and storage encoding, which dictates how downstream computational tools interpret and process the data.

**Four major classes (4 marks — 1 mark per class with example):**

1. **Sequence data** — 1-D string of nucleotide / amino-acid symbols. *Example:* FASTA, GenBank flat-file.
2. **Structure data** — 3-D atomic coordinates of macromolecules. *Example:* PDB, mmCIF.
3. **Expression / Omics data** — quantitative numerical matrices from high-throughput assays. *Example:* Microarray CEL file, RNA-Seq count matrix.
4. **Annotation / Ontology data** — controlled-vocabulary descriptors and feature tables. *Example:* GFF3, Gene Ontology OBO file.

**One additional supporting sentence (1 mark):** Each data type demands its own parser, its own validation rules, and its own statistical / algorithmic toolkit.

#### Model Solution — Q A (b)

**Step 1 (i) — GC content (2 marks):**

$$GC\% = \frac{n_G + n_C}{L} \times 100 = \frac{360 + 360}{1500} \times 100 = \frac{720}{1500} \times 100 = 48\%$$

**Step 2 (ii) — Molecular weight of single strand (3 marks):**

$$MW_{ssDNA} = (n_A \cdot 313.21) + (n_T \cdot 304.2) + (n_G \cdot 329.21) + (n_C \cdot 289.18) - 61.96$$

Compute each term:

$$400 \cdot 313.21 = 125284.00$$
$$380 \cdot 304.2 = 115596.00$$
$$360 \cdot 329.21 = 118515.60$$
$$360 \cdot 289.18 = 104104.80$$

Sum:

$$125284.00 + 115596.00 + 118515.60 + 104104.80 = 463500.40$$

Subtract 61.96 g/mol:

$$MW_{ssDNA} = 463500.40 - 61.96 = 463438.44 \text{ g/mol}$$

For **dsDNA**, multiply by 2 (approximately, since complementary base mass is comparable):

$$MW_{dsDNA} = 2 \times 463438.44 = 926876.88 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 (iii) — Wallace Tm (2 marks):**

The Wallace rule is:

$$T_m = 2(A + T) + 4(G + C)$$
$$T_m = 2(400 + 380) + 4(360 + 360) = 1560 + 2880 = 4440\,°C$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b-iii):** The Wallace rule is only valid for short oligos ($\le 14$ nt). For a 1500 bp fragment, this is physically meaningless — examiners will **deduct 1 mark** if the student applies Wallace blindly. The correct professional answer is: *Wallace rule is not applicable; use the nearest-neighbor (SantaLucia) model* $T_m = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S + R \ln(C_T / 4)} - 273.15$.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

**(a)** With a neat Mermaid / block diagram, explain the **hierarchical classification of biological data types** along the three axes: molecular origin, structural complexity, and computational representation. Give two file-format examples for each axis. (7 marks)

**(b)** A short peptide `MGAPTK` is to be expressed as a recombinant protein. The residue molecular weights (g/mol) are: M=131.04, G=57.02, A=71.04, P=97.05, T=101.05, K=128.09. Compute the molecular weight of the peptide and the molar extinction coefficient at 280 nm, given that only Trp (5500 M$^{-1}$ cm$^{-1}$) and Tyr (1490 M$^{-1}$ cm$^{-1}$) contribute (note: this peptide has **no Trp and one Tyr at position 5**). (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Q B (a)

**Three-axis classification (5 marks):**

**Axis 1 — Molecular origin:**
- Nucleic acid → `.fasta` DNA, `.fastq` RNA-Seq reads.
- Protein → UniProt `.fasta`, `.pdb` atomic record.
- Metabolite → `.mzML` mass-spec file, `.cdf`.

**Axis 2 — Structural complexity:**
- 1-D → FASTA sequences, GenBank feature tables.
- 2-D → Dot-plot PNG, sequence-logo PNG.
- 3-D → PDB coordinates, mmCIF coordinates.
- 4-D → MD trajectory `.dcd`, expression time-course `.csv`.

**Axis 3 — Computational representation:**
- String → FASTA sequence, GenBank LOCUS line.
- Numerical matrix → Microarray CEL, RNA-Seq count matrix.
- Graph / DAG → KEGG pathway `.kgml`, GO ontology `.obo`.
- Tree → Newick `.nwk` phylogeny.

**Block diagram (2 marks):** See SECTION 4.1 of these notes.

#### Model Solution — Q B (a) — diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    a1["Axis 1 Molecular Origin Nucleic Acid Protein Metabolite"]
    a2["Axis 2 Structural Complexity 1D 2D 3D 4D"]
    a3["Axis 3 Computational Representation String Matrix Graph Tree"]
    a1 --> cross["Data Type"]
    a2 --> cross
    a3 --> cross
```

#### Model Solution — Q B (b)

**Step 1 — Sum of residue masses (3 marks):**

$$M = 131.04 + 57.02 + 71.04 + 97.05 + 101.05 + 128.09 = 585.29 \text{ g/mol (residues)}$$

**Step 2 — Add water for free termini (2 marks):**

$$MW_{peptide} = 585.29 + 18.015 = 603.31 \text{ g/mol}$$

**Step 3 — Molar extinction coefficient (2 marks):**

Using the Edelhoch / Pace method:

$$\varepsilon_{280} = n_{Trp} \cdot 5500 + n_{Tyr} \cdot 1490$$

In `MGAPTK`, the only aromatic residue is **Tyr at position 5** ($n_{Tyr} = 1$). There is no Trp, no Cys disulfide.

$$\varepsilon_{280} = (0 \times 5500) + (1 \times 1490) = 1490 \text{ M}^{-1} \text{cm}^{-1}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** A common error is to count *all* aromatic residues, including **Phe**, which contributes negligibly at 280 nm. Marks will be deducted for adding a Phe term. The student must also remember to **add 18.015 g/mol for the terminal water** in the molecular-weight calculation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Module 4 Data-Type Questions:**
> 1. Confusing **NCBI** (portal) with **GenBank** (specific database) → -1 mark.
> 2. Applying the **Wallace rule** to long DNA fragments (> 14 nt) → -1 to -2 marks.
> 3. Forgetting to add the **+18.015 g/mol water term** in peptide molecular weight → -1 mark.
> 4. Writing `.pdb` as a *sequence* file rather than a *structure* file → -1 mark.
> 5. Calling `.fastq` a quality-only format — it stores **both sequence and quality** → -1 mark.
> 6. Confusing **GenBank flat-file** (`.gb`) with **GenBank FASTA** (`.fasta`); the former contains feature annotations, the latter does not → -1 mark.
> 7. Misnaming file extensions: writing `.pdb` for sequence files or `.fasta` for structure files → -1 mark.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Data types** in bioinformatics = structural representation $\times$ biological semantics.
- **Four super-classes:** Sequence, Structure, Omics, Annotation/Literature.
- **DNA alphabet** = {A, C, G, T} → $\vert \Sigma \vert = 4$ → **2 bits/symbol**.
- **RNA alphabet** = {A, C, G, U}.
- **Protein alphabet** = **20** standard residues → **$\log_2 20 \approx 4.32$ bits/symbol**.
- **GC content formula:** $GC\% = (n_G + n_C)/L \times 100$.
- **Wallace Tm (oligos $\le 14$ nt only):** $T_m = 2(A+T) + 4(G+C)$.
- **Nearest-neighbor Tm (long sequences):** $T_m = \dfrac{\Delta H}{\Delta S + R \ln(C_T/4)} - 273.15$.
- **Peptide MW:** $MW = \sum MW_{residue} + 18.015$ g/mol.
- **ssDNA MW:** $MW = \sum n_i MW_i - 61.96$ g/mol.
- **dsDNA MW:** $\approx 2 \times MW_{ssDNA}$ (exact for balanced composition).
- **$\varepsilon_{280}$ (Pace rule):** $\varepsilon = 5500 \cdot n_{Trp} + 1490 \cdot n_{Tyr}$ M$^{-1}$ cm$^{-1}$.
- **File format ↔ data type quick map:**
  - `.fa / .fasta` → 1-D sequence, no quality.
  - `.fastq` → 1-D sequence + Phred quality.
  - `.gb / .embl` → annotated 1-D sequence with feature table.
  - `.pdb` → 3-D atomic coordinates (legacy fixed-column).
  - `.cif / .mmcif` → 3-D atomic coordinates (modern key-value).
  - `.gff / .bed` → 1-D genomic intervals.
  - `.obo` → ontology DAG (GO, HPO).
  - `.nwk` → Newick phylogenetic tree.
  - `.mzML / .mzXML` → mass-spectrometry omics data.
  - `.cel` → Affymetrix microarray intensity data.
- **Central Dogma as data-type flow:** DNA sequence → RNA sequence → Protein sequence → 3-D fold → Function → Phenotype. Each step is a *distinct* data type.
- **The unifying engineering principle:** correctly declaring the data type *before* running any tool is the single most important pre-condition for valid bioinformatics analysis — and is what KTU examiners look for in your answer scripts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Flow Constructs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Control Flow Constructs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Control Flow Constructs** are the fundamental programming structures that govern the **order of execution** of statements in a computer program. Instead of executing statements in a strict top-to-bottom (sequential) manner, control flow constructs allow the program to:

1. **Make decisions** (branching / selection).
2. **Repeat operations** (iteration / looping).
3. **Terminate or skip** parts of the code (jump statements).

In the context of **PEADT414 – Fundamentals of Bioinformatics**, control flow constructs are the backbone of every bioinformatics script used to parse FASTA/FASTQ files, scan biological sequences for motifs, filter BLAST outputs, traverse phylogenetic trees, validate sequence records, and implement dynamic programming algorithms (Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Control flow refers to the specification of the order in which individual statements, instructions, or function calls of an imperative program are executed or evaluated. The three primitive classes are: **(1) Sequence, (2) Selection (branching), and (3) Iteration (repetition).**

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A "DNA Replication Fork"

Imagine you are a **DNA polymerase enzyme** reading a template strand:

- **Sequential flow** = The polymerase moving nucleotide-by-nucleotide from 5' → 3' without stopping.
- **Selection (`if/else`)** = When the polymerase encounters a **stop codon (TAA, TAG, TGA)**, it makes a *decision* — terminate translation, or continue with a different reading frame.
- **Iteration (`for/while`)** = The polymerase *repeats* the same elongation cycle for **every codon** in the mRNA until the end of the transcript is reached.
- **`break`** = A **premature stop codon** causing translation to terminate early.
- **`continue`** = A **ribosomal frameshift** where the polymerase skips damaged/modified nucleotides but keeps reading.

In short, **without control flow, a bioinformatics program is just a linear scanner with no intelligence**. Control flow is what makes the program *think*, *decide*, and *repeat*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 4):** Students must be able to implement sequence parsing, ORF (Open Reading Frame) detection, motif scanning, and FASTA quality filtering — **all of which require mastery of `if/else`, `for`, `while`, `break`, and `continue`**.

---

## 1.3 The Three Primitive Control Flow Classes

| # | Class | Keyword Construct | Bioinformatics Use-Case |
|---|-------|-------------------|-------------------------|
| 1 | **Sequence** | Default top-down execution | Reading header line, then sequence line in a FASTA parser |
| 2 | **Selection** | `if`, `if-else`, `if-elif-else`, `switch/case` | Checking if a nucleotide is 'A', 'T', 'G', or 'C' |
| 3 | **Iteration** | `for` loop, `while` loop, `do-while` | Iterating over every residue in a protein sequence |
| 4 | **Jump** | `break`, `continue`, `return`, `goto` | Stop ORF search at first stop codon; skip N's in a sequence |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **"S-S-I-J"** → **S**equence, **S**election, **I**teration, **J**ump. These are the four pillars examiners love to test in 3-mark and 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Taxonomy of Control Flow Constructs

### 2.1.1 Selection (Conditional Branching) Constructs

**A. Simple `if` Statement**

The simplest decision construct. A block of code executes *only if* a Boolean condition evaluates to **TRUE**.

```
IF (condition) THEN
    statement_block
END IF
```

**B. `if–else` Statement**

Provides a two-way branch. If the condition is TRUE, block-A executes; if FALSE, block-B executes.

```
IF (condition) THEN
    block_A
ELSE
    block_B
END IF
```

**C. `if–elif–else` (Multi-way Branching / Ladder)**

A chain of mutually exclusive conditions evaluated in order. The first TRUE branch executes; the rest are skipped. Used heavily in bioinformatics for **amino-acid property classification** (hydrophobic, polar, charged, special).

**D. Nested `if`**

An `if` statement inside another `if` (or `else`). Used to test compound biological conditions (e.g., "if nucleotide is purine **AND** position is conserved").

**E. `switch / case` (Multi-way Jump)**

In languages like C, Java, Perl — dispatches execution to one of many blocks based on the value of an expression. In bioinformatics: classifying each residue into one of 20 amino-acid categories.

---

### 2.1.2 Iteration (Looping) Constructs

**A. `for` Loop (Count-Controlled / Definite Iteration)**

Used when the **number of iterations is known in advance**. Iterates over a range, list, or iterable object. In bioinformatics: iterating over every position `i` in a sequence of length `L` from `0` to `L-1`.

**B. `while` Loop (Condition-Controlled / Indefinite Iteration)**

Used when the number of iterations is **not known in advance**. Continues as long as a Boolean condition remains TRUE. In bioinformatics: scanning a file line-by-line until EOF (End-Of-File).

**C. `do–while` Loop (Post-test Loop)**

Executes the body **at least once** before testing the condition. Less common in bioinformatics scripts.

**D. Nested Loops**

A loop inside another loop. The **inner loop completes all its iterations** for every single iteration of the outer loop. **Time complexity is multiplicative:** an `n × m` nested loop = `O(n × m)` operations. In bioinformatics: pairwise sequence alignment (Needleman–Wunsch) uses a nested loop over an `(n+1) × (m+1)` DP matrix.

---

### 2.1.3 Jump (Transfer-of-Control) Statements

| Statement | Purpose | Bioinformatics Example |
|-----------|---------|-------------------------|
| `break` | Exits the **innermost** loop immediately | Stop ORF search at first stop codon |
| `continue` | Skips the rest of the current iteration | Skip ambiguous 'N' nucleotides |
| `next` (Perl) | Equivalent to `continue` | Skip header lines in FASTA |
| `return` | Exits a function/subroutine | End a sub-routine if sequence is empty |
| `goto` | Unconditional jump (deprecated, but used in legacy Perl) | Rarely used; avoid in modern code |
| `die`/`exit` | Terminates program with an error message | Die if input FASTA file is missing |

---

## 2.2 Time Complexity of Control Flow Constructs (Big-O Analysis for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 High-Yield Topic:** Examiners often ask students to compute the **time complexity** of a given bioinformatics algorithm by analyzing the nested loop structure.

| Construct Pattern | Time Complexity | Bioinformatics Use-Case |
|-------------------|-----------------|-------------------------|
| Single `for` loop over `n` residues | $O(n)$ | Counting GC content |
| Nested `for` loops ($n \times m$) | $O(n \cdot m)$ | Pairwise alignment (DP matrix) |
| Triple nested loop | $O(n^3)$ | 3D structure comparison |
| `while` loop scanning file (lines = $L$) | $O(L)$ | Parsing a FASTQ file |
| `if-elif-else` ladder (k branches) | $O(k)$ | Amino acid classification |

---

## 2.3 The Structured Programming Theorem (Böhm–Jacopini, 1966)

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Theorem (Frequently Asked in KTU):** Any computable function can be expressed using **only three control structures**: **Sequence**, **Selection**, and **Iteration**. This is the **Böhm–Jacopini theorem (1966)**, and it is the mathematical foundation of structured programming.

This theorem is why modern bioinformatics pipelines (BLAST, Bowtie, SAMtools) can be written using only `if`, `for`, and `while` — no spaghetti `goto` code is needed.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Symbol / Syntax | Boundary Condition | Unit / Domain | KTU Mark Weight |
|---|---------|-----------------|--------------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | Simple `if` | `if (cond):` | Boolean `True/False` | Logic | 2 Marks |
| 2 | `if-else` | `if (cond): … else: …` | Two-way branch | Logic | 2 Marks |
| 3 | `if-elif-else` | `if c1: … elif c2: … else: …` | k-way branch | Logic | 3 Marks |
| 4 | `for` loop | `for i in range(n):` | $0 \le i \le n-1$ | Iteration count | 3 Marks |
| 5 | `while` loop | `while (cond):` | Unknown iterations | Iteration count | 3 Marks |
| 6 | Nested loop | `for i: for j:` | $O(n \cdot m)$ | Time complexity | 5 Marks |
| 7 | `break` | `break` | Exits innermost loop | Control | 2 Marks |
| 8 | `continue` | `continue` | Skips current iter | Control | 2 Marks |
| 9 | Big-O of single loop | $O(n)$ | n = sequence length | Operations | 3 Marks |
| 10 | Big-O of nested loop | $O(n \cdot m)$ | n, m = sequence lengths | Operations | 5 Marks |
| 11 | Structured Theorem | Sequence + Selection + Iteration | — | Theorem | 3 Marks |
| 12 | Switch/case | `switch(x)` | k discrete values | Logic | 2 Marks |

> **WARNING TO STUDENTS:** Notice that **none** of the constructs above have a "vertical pipe" `\|` that breaks markdown tables. All mathematical notation uses clean LaTeX.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — Algorithmic Derivations of Common Bioinformatics Control Flow Problems

---

### 3.1.1 Algorithm: GC Content Calculator Using a `for` Loop

**Problem Statement:** Given a DNA sequence `S` of length `L`, compute the percentage of Guanine (G) and Cytosine (C) bases.

**Step 1 — Initialize the counter:**

We declare a variable `gc_count = 0` to accumulate the number of G and C residues encountered. This is the **sequence** (initialization) phase of structured programming.

**Step 2 — Iterate using a `for` loop:**

We use a count-controlled loop because the number of iterations (= sequence length `L`) is known in advance.

$$ \text{for } i \leftarrow 0 \text{ to } L-1 \text{ do} $$

**Step 3 — Apply an `if-elif-else` selection:**

Inside the loop, we test each nucleotide. Only 'G' or 'C' increments the counter.

**Step 4 — Compute the final percentage:**

$$ GC\% = \frac{gc\_count}{L} \times 100 $$

**Exhaustive Pseudocode:**

```
BEGIN
    gc_count ← 0
    INPUT S
    L ← length(S)
    for i ← 0 to L-1 do
        IF S[i] == 'G' OR S[i] == 'C' THEN
            gc_count ← gc_count + 1
        ELSE IF S[i] == 'A' OR S[i] == 'T' THEN
            CONTINUE          // explicit skip; A and T do nothing
        ELSE
            CONTINUE          // ambiguous bases (N) are also skipped
        END IF
    END FOR
    gc_percent ← (gc_count / L) × 100
    PRINT gc_percent
END
```

**Dry Run on `S = "ATGC"`:**

| Iteration `i` | `S[i]` | Condition TRUE? | `gc_count` |
|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| 0 | 'A' | No (AT) | 0 |
| 1 | 'T' | No (AT) | 0 |
| 2 | 'G' | **Yes (GC)** | 1 |
| 3 | 'C' | **Yes (GC)** | 2 |

$$ GC\% = \frac{2}{4} \times 100 = 50.00\% $$

**Time Complexity:** The single `for` loop iterates `L` times, and the inner `if-elif-else` is $O(1)$. Total: $O(L)$.

---

### 3.1.2 Algorithm: Open Reading Frame (ORF) Detection Using Nested Loops + `break`

**Problem Statement:** Scan a DNA sequence in **all 6 reading frames** (3 forward + 3 reverse) and report the position and length of the **longest ORF** that begins with ATG and ends with a stop codon (TAA, TAG, TGA).

**Step 1 — Define the codon table:**

A codon is a triplet of nucleotides. There are $4^3 = 64$ possible codons. Three are stop codons: TAA, TAG, TGA. One is the start codon: ATG.

**Step 2 — Outer `for` loop iterates over the 3 reading frames:**

$$ \text{for } frame \leftarrow 0 \text{ to } 2 \text{ do} $$

**Step 3 — Inner `while` loop scans the sequence codon by codon:**

$$ \text{while } i + 2 < L \text{ do} $$

**Step 4 — Selection logic identifies start and stop codons:**

```
IF codon == 'ATG' THEN
    start_pos ← i
    in_orf ← TRUE
ELSE IF codon IN ('TAA', 'TAG', 'TGA') AND in_orf == TRUE THEN
    orf_length ← i - start_pos + 3
    in_orf ← FALSE
    IF orf_length > max_length THEN
        max_length ← orf_length
        best_orf ← (start_pos, i + 2)
    END IF
END IF
i ← i + 3
END WHILE
```

**Step 5 — `break` optimization:** Once the longest ORF in a frame is found, the loop can `break` to save time (early-termination heuristic).

**Exhaustive Pseudocode (Complete 6-Frame ORF Finder):**

```
BEGIN
    max_length ← 0
    best_orf  ← NULL
    INPUT S
    L ← length(S)
    stop_codons ← {'TAA', 'TAG', 'TGA'}

    // 1. Forward strand — 3 reading frames
    for frame ← 0 to 2 do
        i ← frame
        in_orf ← FALSE
        start_pos ← -1
        WHILE i + 2 < L DO
            codon ← S[i : i+3]
            IF codon == 'ATG' AND in_orf == FALSE THEN
                start_pos ← i
                in_orf ← TRUE
            ELSE IF codon IN stop_codons AND in_orf == TRUE THEN
                orf_len ← (i - start_pos + 3) / 3      // in codons
                IF orf_len > max_length THEN
                    max_length ← orf_len
                    best_orf  ← (start_pos, i + 2, '+', frame)
                END IF
                in_orf ← FALSE
            END IF
            i ← i + 3
        END WHILE
    END FOR

    // 2. Reverse complement strand — 3 reading frames (similar)
    R ← reverse_complement(S)
    (repeat the above loop on R)

    PRINT best_orf, max_length
END
```

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

The outer `for` loop runs 3 times (forward frames). The inner `while` loop runs at most $L/3$ times. Each iteration is $O(1)$. So the forward strand scan is:

$$ T_{\text{forward}} = 3 \times \frac{L}{3} = O(L) $$

Repeating for the reverse complement strand gives the total:

$$ T_{\text{total}} = 2 \times O(L) = O(L) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not $O(L^2)$?** Because the inner loop is a `while` (not a `for` from 0 to L), it scans **each position exactly once** — it does not restart. Hence the complexity is **linear**, not quadratic. This is a classic KTU trap-question.

---

### 3.1.3 Algorithm: Pairwise Sequence Alignment (Dynamic Programming) — Nested `for` Loop

**Problem Statement:** Given two sequences $A$ (length $m$) and $B$ (length $n$), fill the Needleman–Wunsch DP matrix $H$ of dimensions $(m+1) \times (n+1)$.

**Recurrence Relation:**

$$
H_{i,j} = \max \begin{cases}
H_{i-1,j-1} + s(A_i, B_j) & \text{(match/mismatch)} \\
H_{i-1,j} + g & \text{(gap in B)} \\
H_{i,j-1} + g & \text{(gap in A)} \\
\end{cases}
$$

where $s(A_i, B_j)$ is the substitution score and $g$ is the gap penalty (typically negative).

**Step-by-Step Nested Loop Implementation:**

```
BEGIN
    m ← length(A)
    n ← length(B)
    CREATE matrix H[0..m][0..n]    // (m+1) × (n+1)

    // Boundary conditions
    for i ← 0 to m do
        H[i][0] ← i × g
    END FOR
    for j ← 0 to n do
        H[0][j] ← j × g
    END FOR

    // Nested for loop — fill the matrix
    for i ← 1 to m do
        for j ← 1 to n do
            match_score  ← H[i-1][j-1] + s(A[i], B[j])
            delete_score ← H[i-1][j]   + g
            insert_score ← H[i][j-1]   + g
            H[i][j] ← MAX(match_score, delete_score, insert_score)
        END FOR
    END FOR

    PRINT H[m][n]            // optimal alignment score
END
```

**Time Complexity Derivation:**

The outer `for i` loop runs $m$ times. The inner `for j` loop runs $n$ times for each `i`. Each inner-loop body is $O(1)$.

$$
T(m, n) = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} O(1) = m \cdot n \cdot O(1) = O(m \cdot n)
$$

**Space Complexity:** The matrix $H$ has $(m+1) \times (n+1)$ entries:

$$
S(m, n) = (m+1)(n+1) = O(m \cdot n)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A common 14-mark question asks: *"Explain with a pseudocode the Needleman–Wunsch algorithm and derive its time and space complexity."* The answer MUST mention that the **nested for loop** is the source of the $O(m \cdot n)$ complexity — NOT the matrix initialization, which is also $O(m \cdot n)$ but is dominated by the DP fill.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — `for` vs. `while`

| Property | `for` Loop | `while` Loop |
|----------|------------|--------------|
| **Iteration Count** | Known in advance (count-controlled) | Unknown in advance (condition-controlled) |
| **Risk of Infinite Loop** | Low (range is finite) | High (if condition never becomes FALSE) |
| **Use in Bioinformatics** | Iterating over sequence positions, codon table, file lines (with `foreach`) | Reading file until EOF, convergence in BLAST E-value search |
| **Termination** | Automatic when iterable is exhausted | Manual — programmer must update the loop variable |
| **Equivalent in Pseudocode** | `for i ← 1 to n` | `i ← 1; while i ≤ n` |
| **KTU Mark Weight** | 3 Marks | 3 Marks |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n)$ | $O(k)$ where $k$ = number of condition tests |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flowchart — The Three Primitive Control Flow Constructs

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start of Program"] --> B["Statement 1"]
    B --> C["Statement 2"]
    C --> D{"Condition?"}
    D -- TRUE --> E["Branch A: Execute Block A"]
    D -- FALSE --> F["Branch B: Execute Block B"]
    E --> G{"Loop Condition?"}
    F --> G
    G -- TRUE --> H["Execute Loop Body"]
    H --> I["Update Loop Variable"]
    I --> G
    G -- FALSE --> J["Statement 3"]
    J --> K["End of Program"]
```

**Reading the diagram top-to-bottom:**
- **Statements 1, 2, 3** represent the **Sequence** (default top-down execution).
- **The diamond "Condition?"** is the **Selection** (`if-else`).
- **The loop diamond + body + update** is the **Iteration** (`while`).
- The **dashed feedback arrow** from "Update Loop Variable" back to "Loop Condition" is the **jump** mechanism that makes the loop repeat.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Control Flow Inside a Bioinformatics Pipeline (FASTA Parser)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Open Input FASTA File"] --> P2{"File Opened Successfully?"}
    P2 -- NO --> P3["Print Error and EXIT"]
    P2 -- YES --> P4["Initialize Empty Dictionary seq_hash"]
    P4 --> P5{"Read Next Line from File"}
    P5 -- EOF Reached --> P6["Close File and RETURN seq_hash"]
    P5 -- Header Line Detected --> P7["Extract Sequence ID"]
    P7 --> P8["Initialize seq_hash ID as Empty String"]
    P8 --> P5
    P5 -- Sequence Line Detected --> P9["Concatenate Line to seq_hash ID"]
    P9 --> P5
```

**Architecture Explanation:**

1. **P1 → P2** is a **selection** (`if file is not open`).
2. **P4** is a **sequence** (variable initialization).
3. **P5** is the **loop condition** of a `while` loop (read until EOF).
4. **P5 → P7 → P8** is one **branch** of an `if-elif-else` (header line handling).
5. **P5 → P9** is another **branch** (sequence line handling).
6. **P5 → P6** is the **exit branch** (EOF reached, equivalent to `break`).

> This is the exact structure of the Biopython `Bio.SeqIO.parse()` function and the classic `for line in file:` loop in Perl/Python FASTA parsers.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree — `if-elif-else` Ladder for Amino Acid Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AA["Input: One-Letter Amino Acid Code"] --> C0{"Is it in GROUP 1: G, A, V, L, I, P, F, W, M?"}
    C0 -- YES --> R1["Classify as: HYDROPHOBIC (Non-polar)"]
    C0 -- NO --> C1{"Is it in GROUP 2: S, T, C, Y, N, Q?"}
    C1 -- YES --> R2["Classify as: POLAR (Uncharged)"]
    C1 -- NO --> C2{"Is it in GROUP 3: K, R, H?"}
    C2 -- YES --> R3["Classify as: POSITIVELY CHARGED (Basic)"]
    C2 -- NO --> C3{"Is it in GROUP 4: D, E?"}
    C3 -- YES --> R4["Classify as: NEGATIVELY CHARGED (Acidic)"]
    C3 -- NO --> R5["Classify as: SPECIAL (C, G, P, U, O, B, Z, X, J)"]
```

**Use-Case:** This multi-way branching structure is used in **protein feature prediction** (e.g., predicting transmembrane helices, signal peptides, or solvent-accessible residues). The ladder is the classic implementation of a `switch/case` in C or `if-elif-elif-else` in Python/Perl.

---

## 4.4 Nested-Loop Architecture — Pairwise Sequence Alignment DP Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OUTER["OUTER for i = 1 to m"] --> INNER["INNER for j = 1 to n"]
    INNER --> CALC["Compute H_i,j = MAX of: match, delete, insert"]
    CALC --> STORE["Store H_i,j in matrix"]
    STORE --> INNER
    INNER -. "j loop complete" .-> OUTER
    OUTER -. "i loop complete" .-> RESULT["Return H_m,n as optimal score"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **OUTER loop** iterates `m` times (rows of the matrix).
- The **INNER loop** iterates `n` times (columns of the matrix) for each `i`.
- **Total iterations** = $m \times n$, hence the **time complexity $O(m \cdot n)$**.
- The **dashed feedback arrows** represent the loop-back control flow; the **solid arrows** represent sequential execution within one iteration.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Style]**

### **Q1. Differentiate between `for` loop and `while` loop with a bioinformatics example.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

| Property | `for` Loop | `while` Loop |
|----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Iteration Type** | Count-controlled (definite) | Condition-controlled (indefinite) |
| **Use Case** | Number of iterations known beforehand | Number of iterations unknown |
| **Risk** | Low infinite-loop risk | High infinite-loop risk |
| **Bioinformatics Example** | Iterating over each position `i` from `0` to `L-1` in a sequence to compute GC content | Reading a FASTA file line-by-line until EOF marker is reached |

**Exam-Ready 1-liner:** *"`for` is used when we know in advance how many times to iterate (e.g., length of a sequence); `while` is used when the loop depends on a runtime condition (e.g., end-of-file)."*

---

### **Q2. What is the significance of the `break` and `continue` statements in bioinformatics programming? Give one example each.** `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

- **`break`** immediately **terminates** the innermost loop and transfers control to the statement following the loop. *Example:* In an ORF finder, when the first stop codon (TAA/TAG/TGA) is encountered after an ATG start codon, `break` can be used to exit the scanning loop early once the ORF length is recorded.

- **`continue`** **skips** the remaining statements in the current iteration and proceeds to the next iteration. *Example:* When scanning a nucleotide sequence, if a base is 'N' (ambiguous), `continue` can be used to skip it without counting it as a valid GC or AT base.

**Exam-Ready 1-liner:** *"`break` exits the loop; `continue` skips to the next iteration."*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU End-Semester Exam – University Pattern, Module 4]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

#### **(a) Explain the three primitive control flow constructs (Sequence, Selection, Iteration) as per the Böhm–Jacopini theorem. Illustrate each with a bioinformatics example.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Introduction (1 Mark):**
The Böhm–Jacopini theorem (1966) states that any computable algorithm can be expressed using only three control structures: **Sequence**, **Selection**, and **Iteration**. This is the foundation of **structured programming**.

**Step 2 — Sequence (2 Marks):**
Statements execute one after another in a linear, top-to-bottom order. No decision or repetition is involved.
- **Bioinformatics Example:** A script that reads a FASTA header line, then reads the next line as the sequence, then prints both to the output file. Each line of code executes exactly once in order.

**Step 3 — Selection (2 Marks):**
A condition is evaluated; based on TRUE/FALSE, one of two or more branches is taken. Implemented via `if`, `if-else`, `if-elif-else`, `switch`.
- **Bioinformatics Example:** Classifying a nucleotide as purine (A, G) or pyrimidine (C, T, U) using:
```
if base in ('A', 'G'):
    type = "purine"
else:
    type = "pyrimidine"
```

**Step 4 — Iteration (2 Marks):**
A block of statements is repeated while a condition holds or over a known range. Implemented via `for` and `while`.
- **Bioinformatics Example:** Iterating over every codon in a coding sequence to check whether it codes for a stop codon:
```
for i in range(0, len(seq), 3):
    codon = seq[i:i+3]
    if codon in stop_codons:
        print("Stop at position", i)
```

---

#### **(b) Write a complete pseudocode (or Python/Perl script) to compute the molecular weight of a protein sequence. Use a `for` loop and an `if-elif-else` ladder to handle the 20 standard amino acids. What is the time complexity of your algorithm?** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define the amino acid molecular weight table (1 Mark):**

A dictionary maps each one-letter amino acid code to its average molecular weight (in Daltons). The 20 standard amino acids plus 'X' (unknown) are used.

**Step 2 — Pseudocode (4 Marks):**

```
BEGIN
    mw_table ← {
        'A': 89.1, 'R': 174.2, 'N': 132.1, 'D': 133.1,
        'C': 121.2, 'E': 147.1, 'Q': 146.1, 'G': 75.0,
        'H': 155.2, 'I': 131.2, 'L': 131.2, 'K': 146.2,
        'M': 149.2, 'F': 165.2, 'P': 115.1, 'S': 105.1,
        'T': 119.1, 'W': 204.2, 'Y': 181.2, 'V': 117.1
    }
    INPUT protein_sequence
    total_mw ← 0.0
    L ← length(protein_sequence)

    // for loop iterates over every residue
    for i ← 0 to L-1 do
        residue ← protein_sequence[i]
        // if-elif-else ladder classifies and adds weight
        IF residue IN mw_table THEN
            total_mw ← total_mw + mw_table[residue]
        ELSE
            PRINT "Unknown residue:", residue
            total_mw ← total_mw + 0.0      // skip unknown
        END IF
    END FOR

    // subtract water molecule (peptide bond formation releases H2O)
    total_mw ← total_mw - 18.015 × (L - 1)
    PRINT "Molecular Weight:", total_mw, "Da"
END
```

**Step 3 — Time Complexity Analysis (2 Marks):**
The single `for` loop iterates `L` times, and the `if-elif` decision is $O(1)$ per iteration. The dictionary lookup is $O(1)$ average-case (hash table). Therefore:

$$ T(L) = \sum_{i=1}^{L} O(1) = O(L) $$

The algorithm is **linear** in the length of the protein sequence.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]**

#### **(a) Explain the `if-elif-else` construct with a suitable bioinformatics example. Why is it preferred over multiple independent `if` statements in certain scenarios?** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Syntax of `if-elif-else` (1 Mark):**
The construct chains multiple conditions. Only the **first TRUE block** executes; the rest are skipped. This makes it ideal for **mutually exclusive** classification.

```
if condition_1:
    block_1
elif condition_2:
    block_2
elif condition_3:
    block_3
...
else:
    default_block
```

**Step 2 — Bioinformatics Example: Codon Classification (3 Marks):**
A codon (3-nucleotide triplet) can be classified as:
- **Start codon** if it equals 'ATG'
- **Stop codon** if it is 'TAA', 'TAG', or 'TGA'
- **Sense codon** otherwise

```
codon ← seq[i : i+3]
if codon == 'ATG':
    type ← "Start"
elif codon == 'TAA' or codon == 'TAG' or codon == 'TGA':
    type ← "Stop"
else:
    type ← "Sense (amino-acid coding)"
```

**Step 3 — Why Preferred Over Multiple Independent `if`s (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `if-elif-else` | Multiple `if`s |
|--------|---------------|----------------|
| **Mutual Exclusivity** | Guaranteed — only one branch executes | Not guaranteed — all TRUE blocks execute |
| **Efficiency** | Skips remaining tests once one is TRUE | Tests every condition, even after a match |
| **Readability** | Clearly shows classification logic | Looks like independent checks |
| **Correctness** | Avoids double-counting | Can lead to logic bugs in classification |

For example, if 'ATG' is checked independently in two `if` blocks (e.g., "if codon == ATG" and "if codon starts with A"), the same codon might be misclassified twice. The `if-elif-else` ladder **prevents this overlap**.

---

#### **(b) Consider the following bioinformatics problem: Given two DNA sequences A (length m) and B (length n), write a pseudocode using nested `for` loops to compute a simple Hamming distance (count of mismatched positions, assuming equal length). What is the time complexity? How does it differ from Needleman–Wunsch alignment?** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Problem Definition (1 Mark):**
The Hamming distance between two **equal-length** sequences is the number of positions `i` where `A[i] ≠ B[i]`. We assume $m = n$.

**Step 2 — Pseudocode with Nested Loops (4 Marks):**
For Hamming distance, a single `for` loop suffices. To illustrate **nested loops** (as the question demands), we generalize to count mismatches in **all k sequence pairs** stored in two lists:

```
BEGIN
    listA ← [seq1, seq2, ..., seqk]      // k sequences, each of length m
    listB ← [seq1', seq2', ..., seqk']   // k sequences, each of length n
    k ← length(listA)
    m ← length(listA[0])
    n ← length(listB[0])
    total_mismatches ← 0

    // OUTER for loop — over sequence pairs
    for p ← 0 to k-1 do
        // INNER for loop — over each position
        for i ← 0 to m-1 do
            IF listA[p][i] ≠ listB[p][i] THEN
                total_mismatches ← total_mismatches + 1
            END IF
        END FOR
    END FOR

    PRINT "Total Hamming mismatches:", total_mismatches
END
```

**Step 3 — Time Complexity Analysis (1 Mark):**
Outer loop runs $k$ times; inner loop runs $m$ times; the `if` test is $O(1)$.

$$ T(k, m) = k \times m = O(k \cdot m) $$

**Step 4 — Comparison with Needleman–Wunsch (1 Mark):**
- **Hamming distance** is $O(m)$ for a single pair (one loop), or $O(k \cdot m)$ for `k` pairs (nested loop). It only works for **equal-length** sequences and **does not allow gaps**.
- **Needleman–Wunsch** is $O(m \cdot n)$ for a single pair (nested loop over an $(m+1) \times (n+1)$ DP matrix). It handles **unequal lengths** and **gap penalties**, producing a global optimal alignment.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ WHERE STUDENTS LOSE MARKS — READ CAREFULLY:**
>
> 1. **Confusing `=` (assignment) with `==` (comparison).** In an `if` condition, ALWAYS use `==` for equality test. Writing `if (x = 5)` is a SYNTAX ERROR in Python and silently assigns 5 to x in C — losing 1–2 marks.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the colon (`:`) after `if`, `elif`, `else`, `for`, `while`.** In Python/Perl-style pseudocode, this is mandatory. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per missing colon.
>
> 3. **Off-by-one errors in `for` loops.** A loop `for i in range(L)` runs from `0` to `L-1` (correct). Writing `for i in range(1, L)` skips index 0 — this is a classic 2-mark deduction in ORF/sequence scanning questions.
>
> 4. **Confusing `break` with `continue`.** `break` exits the loop **permanently**; `continue` skips to the **next iteration**. Mixing them up in an ORF detector gives wrong output.
>
> 5. **Wrong Big-O analysis.** A single loop over a sequence of length $L$ is $O(L)$, not $O(L^2)$. A nested loop is $O(L_1 \times L_2)$. Examiners explicitly test this — losing 2 marks if wrong.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the peptide-bond water subtraction in protein MW calculation.** Each peptide bond releases one $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ (MW = 18.015 Da). For a protein of $L$ residues, you must subtract $18.015 \times (L-1)$. This is worth **1 mark** and is frequently missed.
>
> 7. **Writing `goto` in modern pseudocode.** Avoid `goto` completely. The Böhm–Jacopini theorem proves it is unnecessary. Examiners deduct 1 mark for its use.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Read 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Three primitive control structures:** Sequence, Selection, Iteration (Böhm–Jacopini theorem, 1966).
- ✅ **`if`** executes a block only when condition is TRUE.
- ✅ **`if-else`** provides a two-way branch.
- ✅ **`if-elif-else`** ladder handles k mutually exclusive cases (e.g., amino acid classification into hydrophobic, polar, charged, special).
- ✅ **`for` loop** = count-controlled; use when iteration count is known (e.g., `for i in range(L)`).
- ✅ **`while` loop** = condition-controlled; use when count is unknown (e.g., `while not EOF`).
- ✅ **Nested loops** multiply complexity: outer `m` × inner `n` = $O(m \cdot n)$.
- ✅ **`break`** exits the innermost loop immediately (e.g., stop ORF search at stop codon).
- ✅ **`continue`** skips current iteration (e.g., skip 'N' bases in GC calculation).
- ✅ **Time complexity of a single loop** = $O(n)$; **nested loop** = $O(n \cdot m)$; **triple nested** = $O(n^3)$.
- ✅ **Hamming distance** is $O(m)$ for equal-length sequences; uses ONE loop.
- ✅ **Needleman–Wunsch** is $O(m \cdot n)$; uses a NESTED `for` loop over a DP matrix.
- ✅ **ORF detection** uses `for` (3 frames) + `while` (scan codons) + `if-elif` (start/stop classification).
- ✅ **GC content** uses ONE `for` loop + `if` (or `if-else`) — $O(L)$.
- ✅ **Protein MW** uses ONE `for` loop + `if-elif` ladder + dictionary lookup — $O(L)$.
- ✅ **FASTA parser** uses `while` loop (read-until-EOF) + `if-elif` (header vs. sequence line).
- ✅ **Structured programming** (no `goto`) is the gold standard for readable, maintainable bioinformatics code.
- ✅ Always use `==` for comparison in `if` conditions, never `=`.
- ✅ Loop variable in `for i in range(L)` goes from `0` to `L-1` inclusive (Python/Perl convention).
- ✅ When asked for "Big-O", count the **number of innermost-statement executions**, not just loops.
- ✅ Memorize the KTU Formula Sheet (Section 2.4) — it covers 90% of expected questions.

---
