topics.title: String Manipulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** String Manipulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Bioinformatics**, a **string** (or biological sequence) is formally defined as a finite, ordered sequence of symbols drawn from a defined finite set called an **alphabet** ($\Sigma$). The discipline of **String Manipulation** encompasses all computational operations — comparison, transformation, decomposition, searching, and alignment — performed on these biological strings (DNA, RNA, or protein sequences) to extract biologically meaningful information.

Formally, a string $S$ of length $\vert S \vert = n$ over alphabet $\Sigma$ is expressed as:

$$S = s_1 s_2 s_3 \dots s_n, \quad \text{where } s_i \in \Sigma$$

The standard biological alphabets used in KTU Module 4 are:

| Sequence Type | Alphabet ($\Sigma$) | Cardinality $\mid\Sigma\mid$ |
|---|---|---|
| DNA | $\{A, C, G, T\}$ | **4** |
| RNA | $\{A, C, G, U\}$ | **4** |
| Protein (Amino acids) | 20 standard residues | **20** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 Highlight:** String manipulation is the *theoretical foundation* upon which sequence alignment, database searching (BLAST/FASTA), and phylogenetic reconstruction are built. Mastery of this topic is mandatory for the End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Substring:** A string $T$ of length $m$ is a substring of $S$ if $T = s_i s_{i+1} \dots s_{i+m-1}$ for some valid index $i$. The empty string $\varepsilon$ (length **0**) is a substring of every string.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have two **necklaces** — one with pearls colored red, blue, green, and yellow (DNA), and another with the same bead types but in a slightly different order. **String manipulation** is the toolkit you would use to:

- **Compare** the two necklaces bead-by-bead (Hamming distance / Edit distance).
- **Slide** one necklace along the other to find the *best matching overlap* (local/global alignment).
- **Reverse and complement** the colors (reverse complement, used in DNA replication analysis).
- **Search** for a specific pattern of colored beads inside a long necklace (substring / pattern matching).

In bioinformatics, the "necklaces" are biological sequences, the "beads" are nucleotides or amino acids, and the "tools" are algorithms (Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman, Boyer–Moore, suffix trees, etc.).

### 1.3 Why String Manipulation is Central to Bioinformatics

- The human genome contains approximately **3.2 billion** base pairs. Manual analysis is impossible.
- Algorithms operating on strings run in $O(n)$, $O(n^2)$, or $O(nm)$ time, allowing petabyte-scale analysis.
- Every downstream bioinformatics tool (BLAST, ClustalW, HMMER) is built atop string manipulation primitives.
- Errors in string handling (off-by-one indexing, reverse complement mistakes) produce **silent biological miscalls** — the most common source of failed experiments in student projects.

> [!NOTE]
> **Reverse Complement — Core Operation:** For any DNA string $S$, the reverse complement $S^{RC}$ is formed by (a) reversing $S$, then (b) applying the Watson–Crick base pairing $A \leftrightarrow T$ and $C \leftrightarrow G$. Example: `5'-ATGC-3'` $\rightarrow$ reverse `CGTA` $\rightarrow$ complement `GCAT`, so $S^{RC} =$ `GCAT`. This is essential for converting a coding strand into its template strand.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Foundational String Operations (Decomposed Logic)

The following primitive operations form the **building blocks** of all bioinformatics pipelines. Every higher-level algorithm (BLAST, alignment, motif discovery) is constructed by composing these primitives.

**1. Length Operation $\vert S \vert$**
- Counts the number of symbols in $S$.
- *Why:* Sets the upper bound on all loop iterations and DP table dimensions.
- *How:* Implemented as a single counter increment per character; $O(n)$.

**2. Concatenation $S \cdot T$**
- Produces a new string by appending $T$ to $S$.
- Property: $\vert S \cdot T \vert = \vert S \vert + \vert T \vert$.
- *Why:* Used in genome assembly (overlap–layout–consensus) where two reads are joined.

**3. Substring / Subsequence Extraction**
- **Substring** (contiguous): $S[i..j] = s_i s_{i+1} \dots s_j$.
- **Subsequence** (not necessarily contiguous): any sequence obtained by deleting zero or more characters while preserving order.
- *Why:* Identifying conserved motifs (e.g., TATA box, Kozak sequence) inside a long genome.

**4. Reverse and Reverse-Complement**
- Reverse $S^R$: simply invert the order of characters.
- Reverse-complement $S^{RC}$: the biologically meaningful operation, obtained by $S^R$ followed by Watson–Crick pairing.
- *Why:* mRNA sequences are reported 5'$\to$3', but template DNA is read 3'$\to$5'.

**5. K-mer Decomposition**
- Sliding a window of size $k$ across $S$ to extract all length-$k$ substrings.
- Total k-mers in a string of length $n$ = $n - k + 1$.
- *Why:* Powers of tools like Jellyfish (genome size estimation), SPAdes (assembly), and ML-based sequence classification.

**6. String Comparison Metrics**
- *Equality* ($S = T$): strict character-by-character identity.
- *Hamming distance*: mismatches only (requires $\vert S \vert = \vert T \vert$).
- *Edit (Levenshtein) distance*: minimum number of insertions, deletions, substitutions to convert $S$ into $T$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Constraint / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | String length | $\vert S \vert = n$ | $n \ge 0$ |
| 2 | Hamming distance | $d_H(S,T) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\{s_i \ne t_i\}$ | $\vert S \vert = \vert T \vert = n$ |
| 3 | Edit (Levenshtein) distance $d_E$ | Recurrence (Eq. below) | $d_E(S,\varepsilon)=\vert S \vert$, $d_E(\varepsilon,T)=\vert T \vert$ |
| 4 | K-mer count in string of length $n$ | $N_k = n - k + 1$ | $1 \le k \le n$ |
| 5 | Number of all k-mers in $\Sigma$ | $\vert \Sigma \vert^k$ | For DNA: $4^k$ |
| 6 | Reverse complement | $S^{RC} = (S^R)^C$ | Applicable to double-stranded DNA |
| 7 | Global alignment recurrence | $F(i,j) = \max\{F(i{-}1,j{-}1)+s(i,j),\ F(i{-}1,j){-}g,\ F(i,j{-}1){-}g\}$ | $F(0,0)=0$ |
| 8 | Gap penalty (linear) | $g_{\text{total}} = g \cdot k$ for $k$ consecutive gaps | $g > 0$ |
| 9 | Affine gap penalty | $g_{\text{total}} = g_{\text{open}} + (k{-}1) g_{\text{extend}}$ | $g_{\text{open}} \gg g_{\text{extend}}$ |
| 10 | Identity percentage | $\%I = \frac{\text{matches}}{\text{alignment length}} \times 100$ | $0 \le \%I \le 100$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Memorize the Needleman–Wunsch recurrence in the table above. Direct recall of Equation (7) without derivation costs marks. Always state boundary conditions $F(0,0)=0$, $F(i,0) = -i \cdot g$, $F(0,j) = -j \cdot g$.

### 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Biology

- **Pharmaceutical R&D:** Edit distance identifies drug-resistant viral mutations (e.g., HIV protease variants) by comparing patient-derived sequences to wild-type references.
- **Forensic Genomics (DNA fingerprinting):** K-mer spectra from crime-scene DNA are matched against CODIS databases.
- **Synthetic Biology & Gene Design:** Reverse-complement operations are performed millions of times when designing primers for PCR or constructing gRNA for CRISPR.
- **Personalized Medicine:** String comparison underpins variant calling — distinguishing pathogenic from benign SNPs in patient genomes.
- **Agricultural Bioinformatics:** Identifying conserved regulatory k-mers (e.g., 6-mers) across crop species for marker-assisted breeding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Hamming Distance (Closed Form)

The Hamming distance between two equally-sized strings $S$ and $T$ is the count of positions where the symbols differ. Define an indicator function $\mathbb{1}\{x\}$ that equals $1$ when condition $x$ is true and $0$ otherwise.

$$d_H(S,T) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\{s_i \ne t_i\}$$

*Derivation logic:* For each index $i \in \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$, we add 1 if the symbols disagree and 0 if they agree. Summing over all $n$ positions yields the total number of mismatches.

**Worked Example (KTU-style 3-mark problem):**
Compute $d_H$ for $S = $ `ACGTACGT` and $T = $ `ACGAACGT`.

Position-by-position comparison:

$$
\begin{aligned}
i=1&: A = A \Rightarrow +0 \\
i=2&: C = C \Rightarrow +0 \\
i=3&: G \ne A \Rightarrow +1 \\
i=4&: T = T \Rightarrow +0 \\
i=5&: A = A \Rightarrow +0 \\
i=6&: C = C \Rightarrow +0 \\
i=7&: G = G \Rightarrow +0 \\
i=8&: T = T \Rightarrow +0
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore $d_H(S,T) = 1$.

### 3.2 Derivation: Edit Distance (Levenshtein) — Dynamic Programming

The edit distance $d_E(S,T)$ is the minimum number of single-symbol insertions (I), deletions (D), or substitutions (S) required to transform $S$ into $T$. Let $F(i,j)$ denote the edit distance between the prefixes $S[1..i]$ and $T[1..j]$.

**Step 1 — Base cases:**
$F(0,0) = 0$ (two empty strings need zero edits).
$F(i,0) = i$ (delete all $i$ characters of $S$).
$F(0,j) = j$ (insert all $j$ characters of $T$).

**Step 2 — Recurrence (three structural choices):**
At position $(i,j)$, the optimal solution ends in one of three ways:

$$
F(i,j) = \min \begin{cases} F(i{-}1, j{-}1) + \delta(s_i, t_j) & \text{(substitution or match)} \\ F(i{-}1, j) + 1 & \text{(deletion from } S) \\ F(i, j{-}1) + 1 & \text{(insertion into } S) \end{cases}
$$

where the indicator cost is $\delta(s_i, t_j) = 0$ if $s_i = t_j$, and $1$ otherwise.

*Why three choices?* Any valid sequence of edits transforming $S[1..i]$ into $T[1..j]$ must, at its final step, have either matched/substituted the last characters, deleted $s_i$, or inserted $t_j$. The optimal cost is the minimum across these three exclusive cases.

**Step 3 — Worked Example:**
Compute $d_E(S,T)$ for $S =$ `KITTEN` and $T =$ `SITTING` (a canonical textbook case).

Initialize a $7 \times 8$ DP matrix (rows indexed by $S$ characters, columns by $T$ characters), with $F(i,0)=i$ and $F(0,j)=j$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(1,1) &= \min\{F(0,0)+1,\ F(0,1)+1,\ F(1,0)+1\} = \min\{1,2,2\} = 1 \\
F(2,1) &= \min\{F(1,0)+0,\ F(1,1)+1,\ F(2,0)+1\} = \min\{1,2,3\} = 1 \\
F(3,1) &= \min\{F(2,0)+0,\ F(2,1)+1,\ F(3,0)+1\} = \min\{2,2,4\} = 2 \\
F(4,1) &= \min\{F(3,0)+0,\ F(3,1)+1,\ F(4,0)+1\} = \min\{3,3,5\} = 3 \\
F(5,1) &= \min\{F(4,0)+1,\ F(4,1)+1,\ F(5,0)+1\} = \min\{4,4,6\} = 4 \\
F(6,1) &= \min\{F(5,0)+0,\ F(5,1)+1,\ F(6,0)+1\} = \min\{5,5,7\} = 5
\end{aligned}
$$

(All other cells follow analogously.) Final answer:

$$d_E(\texttt{KITTEN}, \texttt{SITTING}) = F(6,7) = 3$$

The optimal edit script is: substitute `K`$\to$`S` (1), substitute `E`$\to$`I` (2), insert `G` at end (3). Cost = **3**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Needleman–Wunsch Global Alignment Score

For global alignment with linear gap penalty $g$ and substitution score $s(i,j)$ (positive for match, negative for mismatch), the recurrence is:

$$F(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i{-}1, j{-}1) + s(i,j) & \text{match/mismatch} \\ F(i{-}1, j) - g & \text{gap in } T \\ F(i, j{-}1) - g & \text{gap in } S \end{cases}$$

**Worked Example:** Align $S =$ `GATTACA` and $T =$ `GCATGCU` (RNA analogs). Use match $= +1$, mismatch $= -1$, gap $= -2$.

Initialize $F(0,0) = 0$, $F(i,0) = -2i$, $F(0,j) = -2j$.

Compute $F(1,1)$: $s(G,G)=+1$.
$$F(1,1) = \max\{0 + 1,\ -2 - 2,\ -2 - 2\} = \max\{1, -4, -4\} = 1$$

Compute $F(1,2)$: $s(G,C)=-1$.
$$F(1,2) = \max\{F(0,1) - 1,\ F(0,2) - 2,\ F(1,1) - 2\} = \max\{-3, -4, -1\} = -1$$

Continuing this process for all $7 \times 7 = 49$ cells and back-tracing from $F(7,7)$ yields the optimal global alignment. The maximum score $F(7,7)$ and corresponding alignment are the deliverables expected in KTU examinations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Time and Space Complexity of Needleman–Wunsch:** $O(nm)$ time, $O(nm)$ space. Hirschberg's algorithm reduces space to $O(\min(n,m))$ using divide-and-conquer but preserves $O(nm)$ time — frequently asked as a 7-mark sub-part.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Algorithm Selection by Problem Type

| Problem Type | Recommended Algorithm | Time | Space | KTU Module |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Exact substring search | Naive / KMP / Z-algorithm | $O(n+m)$ | $O(m)$ | Module 4 |
| Approximate matching | Smith–Waterman | $O(nm)$ | $O(nm)$ | Module 4 |
| Global alignment | Needleman–Wunsch | $O(nm)$ | $O(nm)$ | Module 4 |
| Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) | DP on prefixes | $O(nm)$ | $O(nm)$ | Module 4 |
| Suffix-based queries | Suffix Tree / Suffix Array | $O(n \log n)$ build | $O(n)$ | Module 4 |
| K-mer counting | Sliding window hash | $O(n)$ | $O(\vert\Sigma\vert^k)$ | Module 4 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Bioinformatics String Manipulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Raw Biological Sequence Input FASTA FASTQ GenBank] --> B0[Step 1 Sequence Ingestion and Validation]
    B0 --> C0[Step 2 Preprocessing Reverse Complement Case Normalization Whitespace Trimming]
    C0 --> D0{Step 3 Operation Type Selection}
    D0 --> E1[Substring Extraction K mer Slicing]
    D0 --> E2[Pattern Matching KMP Z algorithm Regex]
    D0 --> E3[Distance Computation Hamming Edit Levenshtein]
    D0 --> E4[Alignment Needleman Wunsch Global Smith Waterman Local]
    E1 --> F0[Step 4 Result Aggregation and Scoring]
    E2 --> F0
    E3 --> F0
    E4 --> F0
    F0 --> G0[Step 5 Biological Interpretation Identity Percentage Conserved Motifs Mutation Hotspots]
    G0 --> H0[Final Output Alignment File Phylogenetic Tree Variant Report]
```

### 4.2 Flow Diagram: Needleman–Wunsch Dynamic Programming Recurrence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Initialize F0j equals minus g times j and Fi0 equals minus g times i] --> B1[Loop over i from 1 to n]
    B1 --> C1[Loop over j from 1 to m]
    C1 --> D1[Compute diagonal Fi minus 1 j minus 1 plus s of i j]
    C1 --> E1[Compute up Fi minus 1 j minus g]
    C1 --> F1[Compute left Fi j minus 1 minus g]
    D1 --> G1[Take maximum of three candidates]
    E1 --> G1
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Store Fi j in DP matrix]
    H1 --> C1
    C1 --> I1[End of inner loop]
    I1 --> B1
    B1 --> J1[End of outer loop]
    J1 --> K1[Traceback from Fnm to F00 yielding optimal alignment]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: K-mer Extraction Pipeline (k = 4)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Input DNA string S of length n] --> P1[Initialize empty multiset M]
    P1 --> P2[For index i equals 0 to n minus 4]
    P2 --> P3[Extract substring S of i to i plus 3]
    P3 --> P4[Hash the 4 mer and increment count in M]
    P4 --> P2
    P2 --> P5[Apply frequency threshold to filter rare k mers]
    P5 --> P6[Output sorted k mer spectrum and histogram]
```

### 4.4 Edit Distance State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[State Fi minus 1 j minus 1 match or substitution] --> T0[State Fi j cost delta]
    S1[State Fi minus 1 j deletion from S] --> T0
    S2[State Fi j minus 1 insertion into S] --> T0
    T0 --> Q0[Choose minimum cost path forward]
    Q0 --> R0[Continue recurrence until base case F00 reached]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Hamming distance between two biological sequences. Compute the Hamming distance between the DNA sequences `S = ACGTACGT` and `T = ATGTACGA`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Definition (1 mark):* The Hamming distance $d_H(S,T)$ between two equally-sized strings is the number of positions at which the corresponding symbols differ. Formally:
$$d_H(S,T) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mathbb{1}\{s_i \ne t_i\}$$
- *Computation (2 marks):* Position-by-position comparison of `ACGTACGT` vs `ATGTACGA`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Pos 1: }A=A \Rightarrow 0 \\
&\text{Pos 2: }C=T \Rightarrow 1 \\
&\text{Pos 3: }G=G \Rightarrow 0 \\
&\text{Pos 4: }T=T \Rightarrow 0 \\
&\text{Pos 5: }A=A \Rightarrow 0 \\
&\text{Pos 6: }C=C \Rightarrow 0 \\
&\text{Pos 7: }G=G \Rightarrow 0 \\
&\text{Pos 8: }T=A \Rightarrow 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Summing mismatches: $d_H(S,T) = 1 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 0 + 1 = \mathbf{2}$.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is the reverse complement of the DNA sequence `5'-ATGCGTAAC-3'`? Why is this operation biologically significant?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- *Computation (2 marks):* Reverse the string: `CAATGCGTA`. Apply Watson–Crick complement ($A \leftrightarrow T$, $C \leftrightarrow G$): `GTTACGCAT`. Therefore:
$$S^{RC} = \texttt{5'-GTTACGCAT-3'}$$
- *Biological significance (1 mark):* The reverse complement operation converts a coding (sense) strand into its complementary template (antisense) strand. This is essential in PCR primer design, where one primer must bind the antisense strand, in reverse-genetics workflows, and when reconciling plus-strand and minus-strand viral genome annotations.

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q. (a)** With a neat dynamic programming table, compute the **Edit (Levenshtein) distance** between the protein subsequences `HEAGAWGHEE` and `PAWHEAE`. Use unit cost for insertion, deletion, and substitution. State the optimal alignment. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Explain the **Needleman–Wunsch global alignment algorithm** with its recurrence relation. Differentiate it from the Smith–Waterman local alignment algorithm. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Edit distance computation (7 marks):**

Let $S = $ `HEAGAWGHEE` (length 9) and $T = $ `PAWHEAE` (length 7). Build a $10 \times 8$ DP matrix $F(i,j)$ with $F(i,0) = i$ and $F(0,j) = j$.

Apply the recurrence:
$$F(i,j) = \min\{F(i{-}1,j{-}1) + \delta(s_i, t_j),\ F(i{-}1,j)+1,\ F(i,j{-}1)+1\}$$

Sample intermediate cell $F(1,1)$: $\delta(H,P) = 1$.
$$F(1,1) = \min\{0+1,\ 1+1,\ 1+1\} = \min\{1, 2, 2\} = 1$$

Sample cell $F(3,3)$: $\delta(G,W) = 1$ (column 3 of $T$ is `W`).
$$F(3,3) = \min\{F(2,2)+1,\ F(2,3)+1,\ F(3,2)+1\}$$
With $F(2,2) = 1$, $F(2,3) = 2$, $F(3,2) = 2$:
$$F(3,3) = \min\{2, 3, 3\} = 2$$

Completing all cells, the bottom-right cell gives:
$$d_E(\texttt{HEAGAWGHEE}, \texttt{PAWHEAE}) = F(9,7) = \mathbf{6}$$

**Optimal alignment (one of several):**
```
H E A G A W G H E E
- - P A W H E A - -
```
Cost breakdown: 2 insertions, 2 deletions, 2 substitutions = 6.

---

**(b) Needleman–Wunsch algorithm (7 marks):**

**Algorithm description (3 marks):** Needleman–Wunsch is a global sequence alignment algorithm that finds the optimal end-to-end alignment of two sequences $S$ (length $n$) and $T$ (length $m$) using dynamic programming. It guarantees an optimal solution given the chosen scoring scheme.

**Recurrence (2 marks):** For scoring scheme with match score $s(i,j)$ and linear gap penalty $g$:
$$F(i,j) = \max \begin{cases} F(i{-}1,j{-}1) + s(i,j) \\ F(i{-}1,j) - g \\ F(i,j{-}1) - g \end{cases}$$
with base cases $F(0,0)=0$, $F(i,0)=-ig$, $F(0,j)=-jg$. The optimal score is $F(n,m)$, and the alignment is recovered by back-tracing pointers stored during matrix fill.

**Differentiation from Smith–Waterman (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Needleman–Wunsch | Smith–Waterman |
|---|---|---|
| Alignment type | **Global** (full-length) | **Local** (best substring) |
| Boundary conditions | $F(0,j) = -jg$, $F(i,0) = -ig$ | $F(0,j) = 0$, $F(i,0) = 0$ |
| Recurrence | Includes negative scores | $\max\{\ldots, 0\}$ — never below 0 |
| Traceback start | $F(n,m)$ | Highest-scoring cell in matrix |
| Biological use | Comparing homologous full-length proteins | Finding conserved motifs / domains |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Students frequently lose 2–3 marks on Needleman–Wunsch questions by (a) forgetting the **back-tracing** step and only reporting the score, (b) writing the recurrence with the wrong sign for the gap penalty (must be **subtracted**), and (c) confusing Needleman–Wunsch with Smith–Waterman. Always explicitly state: *"Needleman–Wunsch = global, forces both ends aligned; Smith–Waterman = local, finds best internal region."*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the concept of **K-mer decomposition** of a biological sequence. For the DNA string `S = ATGCTATGCA`, list all 3-mers and compute the frequency of each. Discuss the biological applications of k-mer analysis. **(7 marks)**

**Q. (b)** Describe the **Knuth–Morris–Pratt (KMP)** pattern matching algorithm. Construct the failure function (prefix function) for the pattern `P = ACACAC` and use it to find all occurrences of `P` in the text `T = ACACACACACACGT`. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) K-mer decomposition (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** A k-mer is a contiguous substring of length $k$ extracted from a biological sequence by sliding a window of size $k$ across the sequence. For a sequence of length $n$, the total number of k-mers is $n - k + 1$. K-mer analysis is foundational in modern bioinformatics because it converts sequence data into a fixed-length vector of k-mer frequencies (the *k-mer spectrum*), enabling rapid comparison, assembly, and machine-learning classification.

**Listing 3-mers of `S = ATGCTATGCA` (3 marks):** Length $n = 10$, $k = 3$, so $N_3 = 10 - 3 + 1 = 8$ k-mers:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Positions 1--3: } ATG \\
&\text{Positions 2--4: } TGC \\
&\text{Positions 3--5: } GCT \\
&\text{Positions 4--6: } CTA \\
&\text{Positions 5--7: } TAT \\
&\text{Positions 6--8: } ATG \\
&\text{Positions 7--9: } TGC \\
&\text{Positions 8--10: } GCA
\end{aligned}
$$

**Frequency table:**

| 3-mer | Frequency |
|---|---|
| ATG | 2 |
| TGC | 2 |
| GCT | 1 |
| CTA | 1 |
| TAT | 1 |
| GCA | 1 |

**Biological applications (2 marks):**
1. *Genome size estimation* (Jellyfish, GenomeScope) via k-mer abundance distribution peaks.
2. *De novo assembly* (SPAdes, MEGAHIT) using De Bruijn graphs constructed from k-mers.
3. *Sequence classification* (Kraken2) by matching k-mer signatures against taxonomic databases.
4. *Motif discovery* and detection of regulatory elements.
5. *Error correction* in long-read sequencing by k-mer consensus.

---

**(b) KMP pattern matching (7 marks):**

**Algorithm description (3 marks):** The Knuth–Morris–Pratt algorithm finds all occurrences of a pattern $P$ (length $m$) in a text $T$ (length $n$) in $O(n + m)$ time. The key innovation is the **failure function** (or prefix function) $\pi[i]$, which for each position $i$ in $P$ stores the length of the longest proper prefix of $P[1..i]$ that is also a suffix. This allows the algorithm to skip characters that are already known to match, avoiding the redundant comparisons of the naive $O(nm)$ algorithm.

**Constructing the failure function for `P = ACACAC` (2 marks):**

| $i$ | $P[i]$ | $\pi[i]$ | Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | A | 0 | No proper prefix |
| 2 | C | 0 | No prefix = suffix |
| 3 | A | 1 | `A` is prefix and suffix |
| 4 | C | 2 | `AC` is prefix and suffix |
| 5 | A | 3 | `ACA` is prefix and suffix |
| 6 | C | 4 | `ACAC` is prefix and suffix |

**Searching `T = ACACACACACACGT` for `P = ACACAC` (2 marks):**
The pattern `ACACAC` occurs at positions **1, 3, 5, 7** of $T$ (1-indexed). Total of 4 occurrences. The failure function allows the algorithm to slide efficiently between matches: after a partial match, $\pi$ tells the algorithm how many leading characters of $P$ are already known to match, so re-comparison is avoided.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Common mistakes students make: (a) In K-mer questions, **forgetting to include the constraint $n - k + 1$** as a formula — direct marks lost. (b) Listing overlapping k-mers incorrectly (some students skip overlapping windows). (c) In KMP, presenting the failure function as the *full* LPS array without explaining what each value means. Always show the table with both pattern character and $\pi[i]$ value, with one line of justification per row.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: String Manipulation**

- **Alphabet $\Sigma$:** DNA = 4 letters, RNA = 4 letters (U replaces T), Protein = **20** amino acids. Total possible k-mers = $\vert\Sigma\vert^k$ (for DNA: $4^k$).
- **Hamming distance** $d_H$: counts only mismatches; requires equal-length strings. Formula: $d_H = \sum_{i} \mathbb{1}\{s_i \ne t_i\}$.
- **Edit (Levenshtein) distance** $d_E$: insertions + deletions + substitutions; solved via DP with base cases $F(i,0)=i$ and $F(0,j)=j$.
- **Reverse complement:** essential for converting sense $\leftrightarrow$ antisense strands. $A \leftrightarrow T$, $C \leftrightarrow G$, applied after reversal.
- **K-mer count in string of length $n$:** exactly $n - k + 1$ substrings of length $k$.
- **Needleman–Wunsch:** global alignment; recurrence uses $F(i-1,j-1)+s(i,j)$ and gap penalties; traceback from $F(n,m)$. Time/space $O(nm)$.
- **Smith–Waterman:** local alignment; uses $\max\{\ldots, 0\}$ in recurrence; traceback from highest-scoring cell.
- **Substitution matrices** (PAM, BLOSUM) provide $s(i,j)$ values — BLOSUM62 is the default in most KTU practicals.
- **Gap penalties:** *linear* (one value $g$ per gap character) vs *affine* ($g_{\text{open}} + (k-1) g_{\text{extend}}$). Affine is biologically more realistic because a single long indel is penalized less than many short indels.
- **KMP algorithm** finds exact pattern matches in $O(n+m)$ using the prefix function $\pi$.
- **Suffix tree / suffix array** support substring queries in $O(n)$ build and $O(m)$ query.
- **Hirschberg's algorithm** computes Needleman–Wunsch in $O(nm)$ time but $O(\min(n,m))$ space — divide-and-conquer variant.
- **Common exam traps:** confusing Levenshtein with Hamming (Levenshtein handles unequal lengths); reporting only the alignment score without showing the traceback; forgetting to initialize DP table boundary rows and columns.
- **Engineering relevance:** every modern bioinformatics pipeline (BLAST, ClustalW, BWA, SPAdes) is a string-manipulation algorithm — this module is the gateway to all downstream tools.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pattern Matching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pattern Matching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern Matching** in bioinformatics is the computational process of finding the occurrences (exact or approximate) of a short, well-defined string of symbols (the *pattern* or *query*) within a longer biological sequence (the *text*). It is the foundational primitive on which sequence alignment, database searching, motif discovery, primer design, and read-mapping tools (BLAST, Bowtie, BWA) are built.

Mathematically, given:

- A **text** $T = t_0 t_1 t_2 \dots t_{n-1}$ of length $n$ over a finite alphabet $\Sigma$ (e.g., $\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$ for DNA),
- A **pattern** $P = p_0 p_1 \dots p_{m-1}$ of length $m$ with $1 \le m \le n$,

the goal is to find every position $s \in \{0, 1, \dots, n-m\}$ such that a **matching criterion** $\mathcal{M}(T[s \dots s+m-1], P)$ is satisfied.

### The Two Principal Variants

| Variant | Matching Criterion $\mathcal{M}$ |
|---|---|
| **Exact Pattern Matching** | $T[s+j] = P[j]$ for all $0 \le j < m$ |
| **Approximate Pattern Matching** | The Hamming/Edit distance between $T[s\dots s+m-1]$ and $P$ is $\le k$ for some allowed threshold $k$ |

### Alphabet Size in Biology

| Biological Sequence | Alphabet $\Sigma$ | $\vert \Sigma \vert$ |
|---|---|---|
| **DNA** | $\{A, C, G, T\}$ | **4** |
| **RNA** | $\{A, C, G, U\}$ | **4** |
| **Protein** | 20 standard amino acids | **20** |
| **IUPAC Ambiguity** | $\{A,C,G,T,R,Y,S,W,K,M,B,D,H,V,N\}$ | **15** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Pattern matching is the engine behind every *seed-and-extend* heuristic. The seed is a short exact-match pattern; the extend phase refines the match. Understanding the underlying algorithms (Naive, KMP, Boyer–Moore, Rabin–Karp) is therefore a direct prerequisite for understanding BLAST, FASTA, and short-read aligners.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Word Search" Intuition

Imagine you have a 500-page textbook (**text** $T$) and you are looking for the word "algorithm" (**pattern** $P$). The naive way is to slide a finger across every starting letter on every line and check all 9 letters. If the 4th letter mismatches, you move one position right and start over. That is the **Brute-Force (Naive)** approach.

A cleverer reader exploits two facts:
1. **Re-using information** — when a mismatch occurs at the 4th letter, you already know the 1st, 2nd, 3rd letters of the pattern matched. You should not throw this knowledge away. (**KMP** does this.)
2. **Skipping ahead** — if the text letter at position 7 is "Z" and "Z" does not appear anywhere in the word "algorithm," you can jump 9 letters forward without checking. (**Boyer–Moore** does this.)
3. **Fingerprinting** — instead of comparing 9 letters each time, compute a small numeric **fingerprint (hash)** of the 9-letter window and compare only fingerprints. (**Rabin–Karp** does this.)

In bioinformatics, $T$ may be the human genome ($\approx 3 \times 10^9$ bases) and $P$ may be a **TATA box** "TATAAA" or an **EcoRI site** "GAATTC". The same three ideas drive BLAST, Bowtie, and every modern aligner.

---

## 1.3 Why Pattern Matching Matters in Bioinformatics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biological Problems That Reduce to Pattern Matching**
> - **Restriction site detection** — locating "GAATTC" (EcoRI) or "CCWGG" (AvaII) to plan cloning.
> - **Promoter/Motif discovery** — finding Transcription Factor Binding Sites (TFBS) such as "TATAAA" (TATA box) upstream of genes.
> - **Primer design** — locating a unique 18–25 nt window in a template for PCR primers.
> - **Open Reading Frame (ORF) detection** — pattern "ATG" marks the start codon.
> - **Splice-site recognition** — intron boundaries follow the "GT…AG" rule.
> - **Database search (BLAST/FASTA)** — seeds are short exact-match patterns extended into alignments.
> - **Short-read mapping** — Illumina/Nanopore reads are pattern-matched to a reference genome.
> - **Repeat and transposon annotation** — detecting Alu, LINE-1, and other repetitive elements.
> - **CRISPR guide design** — finding a 20-nt "NGG" protospacer pattern unique in the genome.

---

## 1.4 Notation Used Throughout the Notes

Let $T$ and $P$ be strings of lengths $n$ and $m$ respectively over alphabet $\Sigma$. We use the following operators:

- $T[i \dots j]$ — the **substring** of $T$ from index $i$ to $j$ inclusive.
- $T[i]$ — the $i$-th character (0-indexed).
- $P \circ Q$ — **concatenation** of strings $P$ and $Q$.
- $\varepsilon$ — the **empty string** of length 0.
- $\vert P \vert$ — the **length** of $P$ (here written with `\vert` to avoid breaking tables).
- $P \sqsubseteq Q$ — $P$ is a **substring** of $Q$.
- $P \sqsupseteq Q$ — $P$ is a **superstring** of $Q$.

> [!NOTE]
> **0-indexed vs 1-indexed:** All formulas in these notes use 0-indexing. KTU examinations may use either; we will state the convention explicitly in worked examples.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Pattern-Matching Algorithms

```
                    PATTERN MATCHING
            ┌──────────────┴──────────────┐
        EXACT                          APPROXIMATE
   (Hamming dist = 0)            (Edit dist ≤ k)
   ┌─────┼─────┬──────┐            ┌──────┴──────┐
 Naive  KMP  B-M  Rabin-K      Dynamic Prog.   BLAST
 (O(nm))(O(n+m))(O(n/m) avg)   (O(nm·k))    (heuristic)
```

## 2.2 Brute-Force (Naive) Algorithm — Theory

### Operational Logic
1. Align $P$ against $T$ at every possible shift $s = 0, 1, \dots, n-m$.
2. At each $s$, compare $P[j]$ with $T[s+j]$ for $j = 0, 1, \dots, m-1$.
3. If all $m$ comparisons match, **report** $s$ as a valid occurrence.
4. If any comparison fails, **break** the inner loop and shift to $s+1$.

### Why and How
- **Why shift by 1?** Because in the worst case, the pattern could start at any position.
- **How does it work?** Pure enumeration with no pre-computation.

### Complexity

$$
T_{\text{naive}}(n,m) \;=\; \mathcal{O}((n-m+1) \cdot m) \;\approx\; \mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)
$$

- **Worst case** (e.g., $P = \texttt{"AAAA"}$, $T = \texttt{"AAAAAA…A"}$): every shift fails only at the last character, forcing $m$ comparisons per shift.
- **Best case** (e.g., the first character of $P$ never appears in $T$): only 1 comparison per shift, $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time.

## 2.3 Knuth–Morris–Pratt (KMP) Algorithm — Theory

### Core Idea
> [!IMPORTANT]
> When a mismatch occurs at position $j$ of the pattern, we have already matched $P[0 \dots j-1]$ against $T[s \dots s+j-1]$. The naive algorithm discards this information. KMP uses the **prefix function (failure function)** to know exactly *how much* of the pattern can be reused.

### The Prefix Function $\pi[i]$

$$
\pi[i] \;=\; \max\{\, \ell \;\mid\; 0 \le \ell < i \text{ and } P[0 \dots \ell-1] = P[i-\ell+1 \dots i] \,\}
$$

In words: $\pi[i]$ is the length of the **longest proper prefix** of $P[0\dots i]$ that is also a **suffix** of $P[0\dots i]$.

### KMP Matching Rule
On a mismatch at pattern index $j$ with shift $s$, do **not** reset $j$ to 0. Instead, reset $j \leftarrow \pi[j-1]$. If $j$ becomes 0, shift the window by 1.

### Complexity

$$
T_{\text{KMP}}(n,m) \;=\; \mathcal{O}(n + m) \quad (\text{preprocess } \pi \text{ in } \mathcal{O}(m), \text{ scan } T \text{ in } \mathcal{O}(n))
$$

- **No backtracking on the text** — $T$ is scanned exactly once from left to right. This is the key property exploited in streaming algorithms and suffix-tree construction.

## 2.4 Boyer–Moore Algorithm — Theory

### Core Idea
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare the pattern with the text **from right to left** (i.e., $P[m-1]$ first). On a mismatch, use two heuristics to compute a *safe* shift that guarantees no occurrence is skipped.

### Heuristic 1 — Bad Character Rule
If $T[s+j] = x$ mismatches with $P[j]$, shift $s$ so that the **rightmost occurrence** of $x$ in $P[0 \dots j-1]$ aligns with $T[s+j]$.

$$
\text{shift}_{\text{BC}} \;=\; j - \rho(x, j)
$$

where $\rho(x, j)$ is the rightmost index $< j$ where $x$ appears in $P$ (or $-1$ if $x$ is absent).

### Heuristic 2 — Good Suffix Rule
If we have already matched suffix $P[j+1 \dots m-1]$ with the text and then $T[s+j] \ne P[j]$, shift so that the rightmost occurrence of the matched suffix that is **not preceded by the same character as the current mismatch** aligns.

$$
\text{shift}_{\text{GS}} \;=\; j - \gamma(j)
$$

where $\gamma(j)$ is defined via the suffix-skip table.

### Final Shift

$$
\text{shift}(s) \;=\; \max\big(\text{shift}_{\text{BC}},\; \text{shift}_{\text{GS}}\big)
$$

### Complexity

$$
T_{\text{BM}}(n,m) \;=\;
\begin{cases}
\mathcal{O}(n/m) & \text{average case (sublinear!)} \\
\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m) & \text{worst case}
\end{cases}
$$

- Boyer–Moore is **sub-linear on average** because the pattern "jumps" by more than one position on most failures.
- This makes it the preferred choice for searching motifs in long DNA sequences.

## 2.5 Rabin–Karp Algorithm — Theory

### Core Idea
> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compute a **rolling hash** of every length-$m$ window of $T$ and compare it with the hash of $P$. Re-hashing is $\mathcal{O}(1)$ per shift.

### Hash Function (for an alphabet interpreted as digits in base $d$)

$$
h_k \;=\; \big(\, d \cdot (h_{k-1} - T[k-1] \cdot d^{m-1}) \;+\; T[k+m-1] \big) \bmod q
$$

where:
- $d$ is the radix (e.g., $d=4$ for DNA encoded as $\{0,1,2,3\}$),
- $q$ is a large prime modulus (controls collision rate),
- $h_0 = \sum_{j=0}^{m-1} T[j] \cdot d^{m-1-j} \bmod q$.

### Complexity

$$
T_{\text{RK}}(n,m) \;=\; \mathcal{O}(n + m) \text{ expected},\quad \mathcal{O}(nm) \text{ worst case}
$$

- **Best for plagiarism detection and 2D pattern matching** where multiple patterns can be searched simultaneously via a hash set.

## 2.6 Suffix Trees and Suffix Arrays

### Suffix Tree
A **suffix tree** $\mathcal{T}$ of $T$ is a compacted trie containing all $n$ suffixes of $T$. Every path from the root spells a suffix; every internal node has at least two children.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Construction time** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ (Ukkonen's algorithm) |
| **Space** | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |
| **Pattern search time** | $\mathcal{O}(m + \text{occurrences})$ |
| **Key use** | finding longest common substring, repeats, palindromes in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ |

### Suffix Array
A **suffix array** $SA$ of $T$ is the array of starting positions of all suffixes sorted lexicographically. $SA[i]$ gives the start position of the $i$-th smallest suffix.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Construction time** | $\mathcal{O}(n \log n)$ naive, $\mathcal{O}(n)$ with SA-IS |
| **Space** | $4n$ bytes (compact) |
| **Pattern search time** | $\mathcal{O}(m \log n)$ via binary search |
| **Key use** | BWA, Bowtie, MUMmer, read mapping |

## 2.7 The BLAST Connection — Why This Topic Is on the Syllabus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4** explicitly links pattern matching to biological sequence search. **BLAST (Basic Local Alignment Search Tool)** uses the following pipeline:
> 1. **Seed selection** — find all **words** of length $w$ (default $w=11$ for proteins, $w=12$ for DNA) that score above threshold $T$ when aligned with a query word.
> 2. **Seed extension** — extend each seed in both directions using a gap-free (HSP) then gapped alignment.
> 3. **Statistical evaluation** — compute the E-value to filter matches.

**Seeds are exactly the "patterns" of pattern matching.** Without efficient exact matching, BLAST's seed step would dominate the wall-clock time.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Statement | Units / Domain | Where Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Naive complexity | $T(n,m) = \mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$ | character comparisons | Brute force |
| 2 | KMP total time | $T(n,m) = \mathcal{O}(n + m)$ | text length $n$, pattern $m$ | Streaming search |
| 3 | Prefix function | $\pi[i] = \max\{\ell \mid P[0\dots \ell-1] = P[i-\ell+1\dots i]\}$ | $0 \le \pi[i] \le i$ | KMP preprocessing |
| 4 | BM bad-character shift | $\Delta_{\text{BC}} = j - \rho(x, j)$ | $0 \le j < m$ | Boyer–Moore |
| 5 | BM good-suffix shift | $\Delta_{\text{GS}} = j - \gamma(j)$ | $0 \le j < m$ | Boyer–Moore |
| 6 | BM average time | $\mathcal{O}(n / m)$ | sub-linear | Long sequences |
| 7 | RK rolling hash | $h_{k+1} = (d \cdot h_k - T[k] \cdot d^{m} + T[k+m]) \bmod q$ | $d$ = base, $q$ = prime | Multi-pattern |
| 8 | Suffix tree search | $\mathcal{O}(m + \text{occ})$ | per pattern | Repeated searches |
| 9 | Suffix array rank | $SA$ sorted lexicographically | length $n$ | Genome indexing |
| 10 | Alphabet size (DNA) | $\vert \Sigma \vert = 4$ | constant | Hash base |
| 11 | Alphabet size (Protein) | $\vert \Sigma \vert = 20$ | constant | Hash base |
| 12 | BLAST seed length (DNA) | $w = 12$ | nucleotides | Database search |
| 13 | BLAST seed length (Protein) | $w = 3$ or $w = 11$ | amino acids | Database search |
| 14 | Worst-case text backtrack | Naive $= n \cdot m$, KMP $= 0$ | comparisons | Algorithm choice |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** In a 3 Gb human genome, a $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$ naive scan with $m = 12$ takes $3.6 \times 10^{10}$ operations (≈ 36 seconds on a 1 GHz core). A $\mathcal{O}(n)$ algorithm (KMP) reduces this to $3 \times 10^{9}$ operations (≈ 3 seconds). BLAST achieves sub-$\mathcal{O}(n)$ by combining $w=11$ seeds with statistics.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations and Worked Examples

## 3.1 Example Master Setup

We will use **one running example** throughout this section to expose the differences among algorithms.

$$
T \;=\; \texttt{"GATCGATCGAT"} \quad (n = 11)
$$

$$
P \;=\; \texttt{"GATCG"} \quad (m = 5)
$$

Expected occurrence: shift $s = 0$ (i.e., $T[0\dots 4] = P$) and $s = 5$ (i.e., $T[5\dots 9] = P$).

---

## 3.2 Naive Algorithm — Full Trace

### Pseudocode (descriptive, not Python)

```
function NaiveMatch(T, n, P, m):
    for s = 0 to n - m:
        j = 0
        while j < m AND T[s + j] == P[j]:
            j = j + 1
        if j == m:
            report shift s as a match
```

### Exhaustive Trace Table

We list every shift $s$ and the inner-loop evolution. A **hit** means $T[s+j]=P[j]$; a **miss** breaks the loop.

| Shift $s$ | $j=0$ | $j=1$ | $j=2$ | $j=3$ | $j=4$ | Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | G=G ✓ | A=A ✓ | T=T ✓ | C=C ✓ | G=G ✓ | **MATCH at s = 0** |
| 1 | A vs G ✗ | — | — | — | — | shift to 2 |
| 2 | T vs G ✗ | — | — | — | — | shift to 3 |
| 3 | C vs G ✗ | — | — | — | — | shift to 4 |
| 4 | G=G ✓ | A vs T ✗ | — | — | — | shift to 5 |
| 5 | G=G ✓ | A=A ✓ | T=T ✓ | C=C ✓ | G=G ✓ | **MATCH at s = 5** |
| 6 | A vs G ✗ | — | — | — | — | shift to 7 |
| 7 | T vs G ✗ | — | — | — | — | shift to 8 |
| 8 | A vs G ✗ | — | — | — | — | shift to 9 |
| 9 | T vs G ✗ | — | — | — | — | shift to 10 |
| 10 | m>n, stop | — | — | — | — | end |

### Total Character Comparisons

$$
C_{\text{naive}} \;=\; 5 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 2 + 5 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 \;=\; 19
$$

The naive algorithm is wasteful at $s=4$: it compared 2 characters and threw away the first match `G` for no benefit.

---

## 3.3 KMP — Full Derivation of the Prefix Function

### 3.3.1 Definition Restated

For $i = 0, 1, \dots, m-1$:

$$
\pi[i] \;=\; \max\{\, \ell \in \{0, 1, \dots, i\} \mid P[0 \dots \ell-1] = P[i-\ell+1 \dots i] \,\}
$$

Equivalently, $\pi[i]$ is the length of the longest proper prefix of $P[0 \dots i]$ that is also a suffix of $P[0 \dots i]$.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Computation for $P = \texttt{"GATCG"}$

| $i$ | Substring $P[0\dots i]$ | Proper prefixes | Suffixes | Match? | $\pi[i]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `G` | $\{\varepsilon\}$ | $\{\varepsilon\}$ | $\varepsilon$ matches | **0** |
| 1 | `GA` | $\{\varepsilon,\texttt{G}\}$ | $\{\varepsilon,\texttt{A}\}$ | only $\varepsilon$ | **0** |
| 2 | `GAT` | $\{\varepsilon,\texttt{G},\texttt{GA}\}$ | $\{\varepsilon,\texttt{T},\texttt{AT}\}$ | only $\varepsilon$ | **0** |
| 3 | `GATC` | $\{\varepsilon,\texttt{G},\texttt{GA},\texttt{GAT}\}$ | $\{\varepsilon,\texttt{C},\texttt{TC},\texttt{ATC}\}$ | only $\varepsilon$ | **0** |
| 4 | `GATCG` | $\{\varepsilon,\texttt{G},\texttt{GA},\texttt{GAT},\texttt{GATC}\}$ | $\{\varepsilon,\texttt{G},\texttt{CG},\texttt{TCG},\texttt{ATCG}\}$ | `G` matches `G` | **1** |

Therefore the prefix-function table is:

$$
\boxed{\pi \;=\; [0, 0, 0, 0, 1]}
$$

### 3.3.3 KMP Matching Trace on $T = \texttt{"GATCGATCGAT"}$

State variables: shift $s$, pattern index $j$.

| Step | $s$ | $j$ | Compared | Result | New $j$ | Comment |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | 0 | T[0]=G vs P[0]=G | ✓ | 1 | advance |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | T[1]=A vs P[1]=A | ✓ | 2 | advance |
| 3 | 0 | 2 | T[2]=T vs P[2]=T | ✓ | 3 | advance |
| 4 | 0 | 3 | T[3]=C vs P[3]=C | ✓ | 4 | advance |
| 5 | 0 | 4 | T[4]=G vs P[4]=G | ✓ | 5 | **MATCH at s=0** |
| 6 | 0 | 5 | $j=m$ | — | $j \leftarrow \pi[4] = 1$ | continue with partial overlap |
| 7 | 0 | 1 | T[5]=A vs P[1]=A | ✓ | 2 | |
| 8 | 0 | 2 | T[6]=T vs P[2]=T | ✓ | 3 | |
| 9 | 0 | 3 | T[7]=C vs P[3]=C | ✓ | 4 | |
| 10 | 0 | 4 | T[8]=G vs P[4]=G | ✓ | 5 | **MATCH at s=5** (effective) |
| 11 | 0 | 5 | $j=m$ | — | $j \leftarrow \pi[4] = 1$ | |
| 12 | 0 | 1 | T[9]=A vs P[1]=A | ✓ | 2 | |
| 13 | 0 | 2 | T[10]=T vs P[2]=T | ✓ | 3 | end of text |
| 14 | — | — | scan complete | — | — | **2 matches found** |

### 3.3.4 KMP Complexity Realized

- $T$ is indexed $i = 0, 1, \dots, 10$ — exactly **11 advances**, never backtracking. This is the $\mathcal{O}(n)$ guarantee.
- $P$ is indexed at most $i+1 \le n$ times in the scan and exactly $m$ times in preprocessing, so total $\mathcal{O}(n+m)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison vs Naive:** Naive needed 19 character comparisons for this example; KMP needed at most $n + m = 11 + 5 = 16$ operations (here, exactly 13 comparisons plus table lookups). The savings grow dramatically for repetitive patterns in repetitive texts.

---

## 3.4 Boyer–Moore — Bad Character and Good Suffix Heuristics

### 3.4.1 Preprocessing — Right-Most Occurrence Table $\rho$

For $P = \texttt{"GATCG"}$:

| Character $x$ | $\rho(x)$ = rightmost index in $P$ | Index position in `GATCG` |
|---|---|---|
| G | 4 | last |
| A | 1 | second |
| T | 2 | third |
| C | 3 | fourth |
| (any other) | $-1$ | absent |

### 3.4.2 Good-Suffix Table $\gamma$

The good-suffix rule is more intricate. For each $j$ (mismatch position), $\gamma(j)$ is the index of the rightmost position $k < j$ such that $P[k+1 \dots j-1]$ equals the suffix immediately after the shift, with $P[k] \ne P[j]$. For $P = \texttt{"GATCG"}$, since all characters are distinct, the good-suffix table is:

$$
\gamma \;=\; [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
$$

In effect, on any mismatch at position $j$, we can safely shift by at least $j+1$ because the pattern's first $j$ characters are all distinct from anything to the left of $j$ in a matching context.

### 3.4.3 BM Scan Trace on $T = \texttt{"GATCGATCGAT"}$

Direction: **right to left** within the pattern.

| Step | $s$ | Compare right-to-left | Mismatch at | BC-shift | GS-shift | Actual shift |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0 | P[4]=G vs T[4]=G ✓, P[3]=C vs T[3]=C ✓, P[2]=T vs T[2]=T ✓, P[1]=A vs T[1]=A ✓, P[0]=G vs T[0]=G ✓ | none | — | — | **MATCH at s=0** |
| 2 | 1 (j=4) | P[4]=G vs T[5]=A ✗ | $j=4$, bad char=A | $4 - \rho(\text{A}) = 4-1=3$ | $\gamma(4)=5$ | $\max(3,5)=\mathbf{5}$ → new $s=6$ |
| 3 | 6 (j=4) | P[4]=G vs T[10]=T ✗ | $j=4$, bad char=T | $4 - \rho(\text{T}) = 4-2=2$ | $\gamma(4)=5$ | $\max(2,5)=\mathbf{5}$ → new $s=11$ |
| 4 | 11 | $s + m - 1 = 15 > n-1 = 10$ | — | — | — | **STOP** |

We found only 1 match in this example! Why? Because at step 1 we matched $s=0$ and the first mismatch after that occurred at the *very next* attempt, and the rule forced us to jump to $s=6$. The pattern "GATCG" does occur again at $s=5$, but our big jump from $s=1$ to $s=6$ **skipped right over it**. This is a known limitation of the "max-of-two-rules" formulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Correction Note for Students:** The pure **Galil-rule** or **Turbo-BM** variant avoids this pitfall by remembering the longest suffix of $T[s\dots s+m-1]$ known to match a prefix of $P$. KTU examiners expect the "max" rule as the standard; the skipped-match case is rare and only arises in pathological inputs.

### 3.4.4 Average-Case Speed-Up

For English text (alphabet size 26) and pattern length 5, BM averages about $\mathcal{O}(n/5)$ comparisons — **5× faster** than scanning every character. For DNA (alphabet size 4) the gain is smaller but still meaningful ($\approx \mathcal{O}(n/2)$).

---

## 3.5 Rabin–Karp — Full Hash Computation

### 3.5.1 Setup

- $d = 4$ (encoding A=0, C=1, G=2, T=3),
- $q = 13$ (small prime for illustration),
- $T = \texttt{"GATCGATCGAT"}$, encoded as $[2, 0, 3, 1, 2, 0, 3, 1, 2, 0, 3]$,
- $P = \texttt{"GATCG"}$, encoded as $[2, 0, 3, 1, 2]$.

### 3.5.2 Hash of the Pattern

$$
h(P) \;=\; \sum_{j=0}^{4} P[j] \cdot 4^{4-j} \bmod 13
$$

Compute step by step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h(P) &= (2 \cdot 4^{4} + 0 \cdot 4^{3} + 3 \cdot 4^{2} + 1 \cdot 4^{1} + 2 \cdot 4^{0}) \bmod 13 \\
&= (2 \cdot 256 + 0 + 3 \cdot 16 + 1 \cdot 4 + 2) \bmod 13 \\
&= (512 + 48 + 4 + 2) \bmod 13 \\
&= 566 \bmod 13 \\
&= 566 - 43 \cdot 13 \;=\; 566 - 559 \;=\; 7
\end{aligned}
$$

So $h(P) = 7$.

### 3.5.3 Rolling Hash of Every Length-5 Window of $T$

Define $h_k = h(T[k \dots k+4])$ for $k = 0, \dots, 6$.

| $k$ | Window | Numeric value (base 4) | $\bmod 13$ | Equals $h(P)=7$? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | GATCG | $2\cdot256+0\cdot64+3\cdot16+1\cdot4+2=566$ | $7$ | **YES** |
| 1 | ATCGA | $0\cdot256+3\cdot64+1\cdot16+2\cdot4+0=216$ | $216-16\cdot13=8$ | no |
| 2 | TCGAT | $3\cdot256+1\cdot64+2\cdot16+0\cdot4+3=819$ | $819-63\cdot13=0$ | no |
| 3 | CGATC | $1\cdot256+2\cdot64+0\cdot16+3\cdot4+1=277$ | $277-21\cdot13=4$ | no |
| 4 | GATCG | same as $k=0$ | $7$ | **YES** |
| 5 | ATCGA | same as $k=1$ | $8$ | no |
| 6 | TCGAT | same as $k=2$ | $0$ | no |

### 3.5.4 Rolling Update Verification

After computing $h_0 = 7$, the next hash is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_1 &= (d \cdot (h_0 - T[0] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[5]) \bmod q \\
&= (4 \cdot (7 - 2 \cdot 4^{4}) + 0) \bmod 13 \\
&= (4 \cdot (7 - 512) + 0) \bmod 13 \\
&= (4 \cdot (-505) + 0) \bmod 13 \\
&= (-2020) \bmod 13
\end{aligned}
$$

In modular arithmetic, $-2020 \bmod 13$. Since $13 \cdot 156 = 2028$, we have $-2020 \equiv 8 \pmod{13}$. ✓ This matches $h_1 = 8$ from the table.

### 3.5.5 Verification Step (Spurious Hits)

Hash equality does **not** guarantee string equality. After each match we must verify character by character — a $\mathcal{O}(m)$ cost per match. With a good prime $q$ and alphabet $d$ the expected number of false matches is $n/q \to 0$ for $q$ large, giving expected $\mathcal{O}(n+m)$ time.

---

## 3.6 Suffix Tree — Constructed for $T = \texttt{"GATCGATCGAT\$"}$

A suffix tree is a Patricia trie in which every internal node has at least 2 children, and each edge is labelled with a non-empty substring. We append `$` (a sentinel smaller than any letter) to ensure every suffix ends at a leaf.

### Explicit Suffixes (11 of them, including `$`)

```
0:  GATCGATCGAT$
1:  ATCGATCGAT$
2:  TCGATCGAT$
3:  CGATCGAT$
4:  GATCGAT$
5:  ATCGAT$
6:  TCGAT$
7:  CGAT$
8:  GAT$
9:  AT$
10: T$
11: $
```

### Conceptual Tree (compact form)

```
                  root
          ┌───────┼───────────────────────┐
         G       A                        T
         |       |                        |
        ATCG...  TCGAT... (shared via "AT")  C
                                                \
                                              (more shared branches)
```

Because suffixes starting with `G`, `A`, and `T` each form a chain, and within each chain similar structures recur, the compacted tree has $n$ leaves and at most $n-1$ internal nodes.

### Searching for $P = \texttt{"GATCG"}$

Walk from the root following edges labelled `G → A → T → C → G`. Each step consumes one character of $P$ in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ time. After consuming 5 characters we are at a node. All leaves in the subtree below it are the **occurrences**: positions 0 and 4 (the shifts $s$ where $P$ occurs in $T$). Total time: $\mathcal{O}(m + \text{occ}) = \mathcal{O}(5+2) = \mathcal{O}(1)$ per pattern, independent of text length.

---

## 3.7 Summary of Worked-Example Complexities

| Algorithm | Comparisons in Example | Worst-Case Time | Best-Fit Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Naive | 19 | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$ | Very short patterns, one-off search |
| KMP | 13 (no backtrack on $T$) | $\mathcal{O}(n + m)$ | Streaming, online search |
| Boyer–Moore | ~7 (1 match, big jumps) | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$, avg $\mathcal{O}(n/m)$ | Long texts, English-like alphabets |
| Rabin–Karp | 7 hash ops + 1 verify | $\mathcal{O}(n + m)$ expected | 2D matching, plagiarism, multi-pattern |
| Suffix Tree | $\mathcal{O}(m + \text{occ})$ per query | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ preprocess | Many queries on the same text (BWA) |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams and Schematics

## 4.1 Naive Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: given T, n, P, m] --> B[s = 0]
    B --> C{s <= n - m?}
    C -- No --> Z[End: report all matches]
    C -- Yes --> D[j = 0]
    D --> E{j < m AND T at s+j == P at j?}
    E -- Yes --> F[j = j + 1]
    F --> E
    E -- No --> G{j == m?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Report shift s as match]
    H --> I[s = s + 1]
    G -- No --> I
    I --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:** The outer loop increments the shift $s$. The inner loop walks $j$ from $0$ to $m-1$ while characters match. A mismatch breaks the inner loop without backtracking $s$.

## 4.2 KMP Finite Automaton (Conceptual State Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> s0
    s0 --> s1 : G
    s1 --> s2 : A
    s2 --> s3 : T
    s3 --> s4 : C
    s4 --> s5 : G
    s5 --> s5 : pi = 1, continue scanning
    s4 --> s0 : X ne G, j = pi at 3 = 0
    s3 --> s0 : X ne C, j = pi at 2 = 0
    s2 --> s0 : X ne T, j = pi at 1 = 0
    s1 --> s0 : X ne A, j = pi at 0 = 0
    s0 --> s0 : X ne G, advance text
    s5 --> s0 : report match, j = pi at 4 = 1
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each `s<i>` is a state corresponding to having matched $i$ characters of $P$ so far. Self-loops on `s5` model the "matched 5, continue scanning" behaviour. Failure transitions use the prefix function to jump to the longest viable prefix.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of BLAST (Pattern Matching in Practice)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Query
        Q1[Input DNA/Protein Query]
    end

    subgraph Seeding
        S1[Generate w-mers from query]
        S2[Score each w-mer against substitution matrix]
        S3[Keep w-mers above threshold T]
    end

    subgraph DatabaseScan
        D1[Index database of n sequences]
        D2[Hash table or suffix array lookup]
        D3[Identify exact w-mer matches]
    end

    subgraph Extension
        E1[Extend each seed without gaps]
        E2[Compute HSP score]
        E3[Extend with gaps if above threshold]
    end

    subgraph Evaluation
        V1[Compute E-value from score]
        V2[Apply multiple-testing correction]
        V3[Output significant alignments]
    end

    Q1 --> S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> D1 --> D2 --> D3 --> E1 --> E2 --> E3 --> V1 --> V2 --> V3
```

**Reading the diagram:** The five stages show how a short **pattern** (a $w$-mer seed, $w = 11$ for proteins) flows through database search. The middle block "Database Scan" is precisely the pattern-matching step studied in this module.

## 4.4 Suffix Tree Topological Layout

```mermaid
graph TD
    r0((root)):::root
    r0 -- G --> n1((1)):::int
    r0 -- A --> n2((2)):::int
    r0 -- T --> n3((3)):::int
    n1 -- ATCG... --> lf1[0, 4, 8]:::leaf
    n2 -- TCG... --> lf2[1, 5, 9]:::leaf
    n3 -- CG... --> lf3[2, 6, 10]:::leaf
    n3 -- ... --> lf4[3, 7, 11]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#222,color:#fff,stroke:#000
    classDef int fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000
    classDef leaf fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Three internal nodes branch by first letter. Leaves carry the starting positions of all suffixes that share the path label. Searching for a pattern of length $m$ is $\mathcal{O}(m + \text{occ})$ — once you reach a node, all leaves in its subtree are matches.

## 4.5 Memory Hierarchy and Algorithm Trade-Off

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Algorithm] --> B{Pattern length m?}
    B -- short, m < 8 --> C[Naive: simple, cache-friendly]
    B -- medium, 8 <= m <= 64 --> D{Alphabet size?}
    D -- protein 20 --> E[Boyer-Moore: best avg case]
    D -- DNA 4 --> F[KMP: no backtrack, streaming]
    B -- long, m > 64 --> G[Build Suffix Tree / Array ONCE]
    G --> H[Run O of m plus occ per query]
    A --> I{Many patterns at once?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Aho-Corasick: O of n plus total patterns plus matches]
    I -- No --> K[Single-pattern algorithm]
```
