# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank and Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question A.1  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define pattern matching. Distinguish between exact and approximate pattern matching with one bioinformatics example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Pattern matching** is the problem of locating the occurrences of a short string $P$ (the *pattern*) of length $m$ within a longer string $T$ (the *text*) of length $n$.
>
> - **Exact matching** requires the substring of $T$ to be *identical* to $P$ at every position. **Example:** Locating the EcoRI restriction site "GAATTC" in a plasmid sequence to plan a cloning experiment.
> - **Approximate matching** permits a small number of mismatches or gaps (Hamming or edit distance $\le k$). **Example:** Searching a database with a short read that has sequencing errors, using BLAST, which tolerates mismatches via the seed-and-extend heuristic.

*(Examiner's note: 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for exact + example, 1 mark for approximate + example.)*

### Question A.2  `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**What is the prefix (failure) function in the Knuth–Morris–Pratt algorithm? State its role and compute it for $P = \texttt{"ABABCA"}$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **prefix function** $\pi[i]$ gives the length of the longest proper prefix of $P[0\dots i]$ that is also a suffix of $P[0\dots i]$. It allows KMP to skip comparisons that are guaranteed to fail, achieving $\mathcal{O}(n + m)$ time.

**Computation for $P = \texttt{"ABABCA"}$:**

| $i$ | Substring | Longest prefix = suffix | $\pi[i]$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | `A` | $\varepsilon$ | 0 |
| 1 | `AB` | $\varepsilon$ | 0 |
| 2 | `ABA` | `A` | 1 |
| 3 | `ABAB` | `AB` | 2 |
| 4 | `ABABC` | $\varepsilon$ | 0 |
| 5 | `ABABCA` | `A` | 1 |

Therefore $\pi = [0, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1]$.

*(1 mark definition + role, 2 marks computation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question is internally optional. We present **Question A** and **Question B** as fully independent alternatives. Each carries two sub-parts worth 7 marks each.

---

### 🔹 Question A  `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the Knuth–Morris–Pratt (KMP) algorithm. Construct the prefix function for the pattern $P = \texttt{"AABAACAABAA"}$ and show the step-by-step matching of $P$ in $T = \texttt{"AABAACAABAABAABAA"}$. Comment on its time complexity.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Algorithm Idea (2 marks):**
KMP improves over the naive algorithm by **avoiding re-examination of text characters already matched**. On a mismatch at $P[j]$, the algorithm does not restart the pattern at index 0; instead, it shifts the pattern so that the longest prefix of $P$ that is also a suffix of the matched portion aligns with the corresponding text window. This shift is determined by the precomputed **prefix function** $\pi$.

**Step 2 — Prefix Function Table for $P = \texttt{"AABAACAABAA"}$ (3 marks):**

Let $m = 11$. We compute $\pi[i]$ for $i = 0, \dots, 10$.

| $i$ | $P[i]$ | Substring $P[0\dots i]$ | Longest matching prefix = suffix | $\pi[i]$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | A | A | $\varepsilon$ | 0 |
| 1 | A | AA | A | 1 |
| 2 | B | AAB | $\varepsilon$ | 0 |
| 3 | A | AABA | A | 1 |
| 4 | A | AABAA | AA | 2 |
| 5 | C | AABAAC | $\varepsilon$ | 0 |
| 6 | A | AABAACA | A | 1 |
| 7 | A | AABAACAA | AA | 2 |
| 8 | B | AABAACAAB | AAB | 3 |
| 9 | A | AABAACAABA | AABA | 4 |
| 10 | A | AABAACAABAA | AABAA | 5 |

Therefore $\pi = [0, 1, 0, 1, 2, 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]$.

**Step 3 — Matching Trace (1 mark):**

For $T = \texttt{"AABAACAABAABAABAA"}$ (length 17), the algorithm finds the pattern at shifts $s = 0$ and $s = 8$ (i.e., the substring `AABAACAABAA` occurs at $T[0\dots 10]$ and at $T[8\dots 18]$ — but $T$ is only 17 long, so the second occurrence is actually $T[8\dots 16]$ contains `AABAACAABAA`... let's verify: $T[8] = A, T[9]=A, T[10]=B, T[11]=A, T[12]=A, T[13]=C, T[14]=A, T[15]=A, T[16]=B$ — wait, $T[16] = B$ but $P[8] = B$ ✓. Continuing: $T[17]$ is out of range, so we cannot match $P$ at $s=8$ fully. We re-examine: $T = \texttt{"AABAACAABAABAABAA"}$ has length 17. A length-11 window starting at $s=8$ would be $T[8\dots 18]$ which exceeds 16. So only **one occurrence** at $s = 0$ is reported. The algorithm makes at most $n + m = 28$ operations.

**Step 4 — Complexity Comment (1 mark):**
Total time is $\mathcal{O}(n + m)$: $\mathcal{O}(m)$ to build $\pi$ and $\mathcal{O}(n)$ to scan the text. **No character of $T$ is ever re-examined**, making KMP ideal for streaming inputs.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the Boyer–Moore algorithm. Explain the bad-character and good-suffix heuristics with an example. Why is Boyer–Moore often preferred for searching motifs in long biological sequences?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motivation (1 mark):**
The Boyer–Moore (BM) algorithm scans the text $T$ left-to-right but compares the pattern $P$ with the text **right-to-left** within each window. On a mismatch, it uses one or both of two **heuristics** to compute a *safe* shift (one that cannot skip a real occurrence). The result is sub-linear average-case time $\mathcal{O}(n/m)$.

**Step 2 — Bad-Character Heuristic (2 marks):**
Suppose at shift $s$, the rightmost comparison $P[j]$ vs $T[s+j]$ mismatches with $T[s+j] = x$ (the "bad character"). Shift $P$ so that the rightmost occurrence of $x$ in $P[0\dots j-1]$ aligns with $T[s+j]$. Formally:

$$
\Delta_{\text{BC}} \;=\; j - \rho(x, j)
$$

If $x$ does not appear in $P[0\dots j-1]$, $\rho(x, j) = -1$ and we shift by $j+1$, *past* the bad character.

**Worked micro-example:** $P = \texttt{"GCAGAGAG"}$, $T = \texttt{"…G C A T C G C A G A G A G…}$". At $s$ with $j=7$ (last position), $T[s+7] = \text{T}$ but $P[7] = \text{G}$. The bad-character rule looks for the rightmost `T` in $P[0\dots 6]$; there is none, so the shift is $7+1 = 8$.

**Step 3 — Good-Suffix Heuristic (2 marks):**
Let $U = P[j+1 \dots m-1]$ be the part of the pattern that has already matched (the "good suffix"). On a mismatch, we shift $P$ so that the rightmost occurrence of $U$ in $P[0\dots m-2]$ that is **preceded by a different character** than $P[j]$ aligns with the text. Formally:

$$
\Delta_{\text{GS}} \;=\; j - \gamma(j)
$$

If no such occurrence exists, we shift $P$ past the suffix entirely, aligning the longest prefix of $P$ that is also a suffix of $U$ with the text.

**Step 4 — Combining the Heuristics (1 mark):**
The actual shift is $\Delta = \max(\Delta_{\text{BC}}, \Delta_{\text{GS}})$, the maximum of the two safe shifts. This guarantees that no possible match is missed.

**Step 5 — Why BM is Preferred in Bioinformatics (1 mark):**
Long biological sequences (chromosomes, contigs) are searched repeatedly with many short motifs. The average $\mathcal{O}(n/m)$ behaviour means that for $m = 12$ (typical BLAST seed) the search is **12× faster** than a single pass through the genome. Since biological alphabets are small ($\vert\Sigma\vert = 4$ for DNA, $20$ for proteins), the bad-character rule still triggers frequent large jumps, and the algorithm exploits modern CPU cache behaviour because the pattern is small and frequently re-read.

---

### 🔹 Question B  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**What is a suffix tree? Construct the suffix tree for $T = \texttt{"BANANA\$"}$ and use it to find all occurrences of $P = \texttt{"ANA"}$. Explain why suffix trees are useful in bioinformatics applications like BLAST and MUMmer.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (1 mark):**
A **suffix tree** $\mathcal{T}$ of a text $T$ of length $n$ is a compacted trie (or Patricia tree) containing all $n$ suffixes of $T$. Every root-to-leaf path spells a unique suffix, and every internal node has at least two children. The `\$` sentinel ensures no suffix is a prefix of another.

**Step 2 — Suffixes of $T = \texttt{"BANANA\$"}$ (1 mark):**

```
0: BANANA$
1: ANANA$
2: NANA$
3: ANA$
4: NA$
5: A$
6: $
```

**Step 3 — Compacted Suffix Tree (4 marks):**

```
                          (root)
              ┌──────────────┼──────────────┐
             B              A               N
             |              |               |
        (edge label)        (edge label)    (edge label)
        ANANA$             NA$              ANA$
                           |                |
                          A$               $
                          |  
                         $ (leaf 5)
```

To be precise, the fully compacted tree is:

```
                          (root)
        ┌──────────────┬────────────┬──────────────┐
        B              A            N              $
        |              |            |              |
       ANANA$       (split)       ANA$            (leaf 6)
                   ┌──┴──┐         |
                  NA$   $         $
                  |    (leaf 5)  (leaf 3)
                 $ (leaf 4)
```

The internal node under `A` branches into `NA$` (leaf 1) and `$` (leaf 5). The internal node under `N` branches into `ANA$` (leaf 3) and `$` (leaf 6, but this is at the root, the "$" child).

**Step 4 — Searching for $P = \texttt{"ANA"}$ (1 mark):**
From the root, follow edges `A → N → A`. We arrive at an internal node whose subtree contains **leaves 1, 3** (i.e., suffixes starting at positions 1 and 3). Both leaves are reached by reading `ANA`, so the pattern occurs at shifts $s = 1$ and $s = 3$. **Search time:** $\mathcal{O}(m + \text{occ}) = \mathcal{O}(3+2) = \mathcal{O}(1)$ after $\mathcal{O}(n)$ preprocessing.

**Step 5 — Bioinformatics Relevance (mark embedded above):**
Suffix trees enable $\mathcal{O}(n)$-construction, $\mathcal{O}(m)$-search, and immediate extraction of:
- **Longest common substrings** (used in MUMmer for genome alignment),
- **Maximal repeats** (used in repeat masking),
- **Longest common extension queries** (used in BWA for read mapping).

This is why modern aligners and assemblers are built on suffix arrays (memory-efficient cousins of suffix trees).

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Describe the Rabin–Karp algorithm. For $T = \texttt{"2359023141526739921"}$ and $P = \texttt{"31415"}$, take $d = 10$ and $q = 13$. Find all occurrences of $P$ in $T$ using the rolling hash. Discuss its time complexity and one bioinformatics application.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Algorithm Idea (1 mark):**
Rabin–Karp uses a **rolling hash function** to compute a fingerprint of every length-$m$ window of $T$ in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ amortized time per window. Windows whose hash equals the pattern's hash are candidates; each candidate is verified character-by-character.

**Step 2 — Hash of the Pattern (1 mark):**
Interpret the digits of $P$ as a base-10 number:

$$
h(P) \;=\; (3 \cdot 10^4 + 1 \cdot 10^3 + 4 \cdot 10^2 + 1 \cdot 10 + 5) \bmod 13
$$

Compute:

$$
31415 \bmod 13
$$

$$
31415 \;=\; 13 \cdot 2416 + 7 \;\Rightarrow\; h(P) = 7
$$

**Step 3 — Initial Hash $h_0$ of $T[0\dots 4] = \texttt{"23590"}$ (1 mark):**

$$
h_0 = (2 \cdot 10^4 + 3 \cdot 10^3 + 5 \cdot 10^2 + 9 \cdot 10 + 0) \bmod 13
$$

$$
23590 = 13 \cdot 1814 + 8 \;\Rightarrow\; h_0 = 8
$$

Since $h_0 = 8 \ne 7$, no match at $s=0$.

**Step 4 — Rolling Hash Updates (2 marks):**
For $k = 1, 2, \dots, n-m$:

$$
h_{k+1} \;=\; \big( d \cdot (h_k - T[k] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[k+m] \big) \bmod q
$$

with $d^{m-1} = 10^4 = 10000$.

Let me tabulate the rolling computation:

| $k$ | Window | Numeric | $\bmod 13$ | = 7? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 23590 | 23590 | 8 | no |
| 1 | 35902 | 35902 | $35902 = 13\cdot 2761+9$ → 9 | no |
| 2 | 59023 | 59023 | $59023 = 13\cdot 4540+3$ → 3 | no |
| 3 | 90231 | 90231 | $90231 = 13\cdot 6940+11$ → 11 | no |
| 4 | 02314 | 2314 | $2314 = 13\cdot 178+0$ → 0 | no |
| 5 | 23141 | 23141 | $23141 = 13\cdot 1780+1$ → 1 | no |
| 6 | 31415 | 31415 | **7** | **YES** |
| 7 | 14152 | 14152 | $14152 = 13\cdot 1088+8$ → 8 | no |
| 8 | 41526 | 41526 | $41526 = 13\cdot 3194+4$ → 4 | no |
| 9 | 15267 | 15267 | $15267 = 13\cdot 1174+5$ → 5 | no |
| 10 | 52673 | 52673 | $52673 = 13\cdot 4051+12$ → 12 | no |
| 11 | 26739 | 26739 | $26739 = 13\cdot 2056+11$ → 11 | no |
| 12 | 67399 | 67399 | $67399 = 13\cdot 5184+7$ → 7 | **YES** (spurious?) |
| 13 | 73992 | 73992 | $73992 = 13\cdot 5691+9$ → 9 | no |
| 14 | 39921 | 39921 | $39921 = 13\cdot 3070+11$ → 11 | no |

**Verification:** At $k = 6$ the window is `31415` = $P$, so a **true match** at $s=6$. At $k=12$ the window is `67399` which is **not** $P$, so it is a *spurious hit* (hash collision $\bmod 13$). The verify step catches it.

**Step 5 — Reported Occurrences (1 mark):** Real match at $s = 6$ only.

**Step 6 — Complexity and Application (1 mark):**
- **Expected time:** $\mathcal{O}(n + m)$ with a well-chosen prime $q$.
- **Worst-case time:** $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$ if every window collides with $h(P)$.
- **Bioinformatics application:** Rabin–Karp excels at **multi-pattern search** (e.g., scanning a DNA sequence for a set of 1000 transcription-factor motifs simultaneously) because all pattern hashes can be pre-stored in a hash set, and each text window is checked against all of them in $\mathcal{O}(1)$ expected time. This is the basis of tools like **Genscan** and **PROSITE-scan**.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning and Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in Pattern-Matching questions**
> 1. **Confusing prefix and suffix in the KMP $\pi$ function.** The value $\pi[i]$ is the *longest proper prefix* of $P[0\dots i]$ that is **also a suffix** of $P[0\dots i]$. Many students mistakenly use "longest suffix" of $P[0\dots i]$ — that is the same thing only by accident; the correct reading is "longest prefix that happens to also occur as a suffix." Deduct 1 mark if the wording is sloppy.
> 2. **Forgetting the `\$` sentinel in suffix trees.** Without the sentinel, a suffix can be a strict prefix of another suffix, breaking the tree structure. Deduct 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Off-by-one in prefix-function table.** $\pi[0]$ is *always* 0. If you write $\pi[0] = 1$, the entire table is wrong.
> 4. **Boyer–Moore: omitting the `max` rule.** Students often present only the bad-character rule. The full algorithm requires `max(bad-character, good-suffix)`. Deduct 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Rabin–Karp: failing to verify.** Hash equality is necessary but not sufficient. Always perform a character-by-character check before reporting a match. Deduct 1 mark.
> 6. **Mixing 0-indexed and 1-indexed notation.** State your convention explicitly in the first line of the solution.
> 7. **Complexity claims without justification.** "It is $\mathcal{O}(n)$" is not enough — write "the text is scanned exactly once, hence $\mathcal{O}(n)$, and the prefix function is built in $\mathcal{O}(m)$."
> 8. **Skipping the trace.** KTU examiners award marks for *showing* the alignment at each step. A naked formula is not a derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Pattern Matching**
>
> **Foundations**
> ☐ Pattern matching = find all positions $s$ in $T$ of length $n$ where $P$ of length $m$ matches a length-$m$ window.
> ☐ Two flavours: **exact** (identity) and **approximate** (edit distance $\le k$).
> ☐ DNA alphabet $\vert\Sigma\vert = 4$; protein alphabet $\vert\Sigma\vert = 20$.
>
> **Algorithms and Complexities**
> ☐ **Naive (Brute Force):** $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$ worst case, $\mathcal{O}(n)$ best case, no preprocessing.
> ☐ **Knuth–Morris–Pratt (KMP):** $\mathcal{O}(n + m)$, no backtrack on $T$. The **prefix function** $\pi[i]$ is the longest proper prefix of $P[0\dots i]$ that is also a suffix of $P[0\dots i]$.
> ☐ **Boyer–Moore (BM):** Right-to-left pattern scan, two heuristics:
>  - **Bad-character rule:** $\Delta_{\text{BC}} = j - \rho(x, j)$.
>  - **Good-suffix rule:** $\Delta_{\text{GS}} = j - \gamma(j)$.
>  - Final shift: $\max(\Delta_{\text{BC}}, \Delta_{\text{GS}})$. Average $\mathcal{O}(n/m)$, worst $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot m)$.
> ☐ **Rabin–Karp:** Rolling hash, base $d$, prime modulus $q$. Recurrence: $h_{k+1} = (d \cdot (h_k - T[k] \cdot d^{m-1}) + T[k+m]) \bmod q$. Expected $\mathcal{O}(n + m)$. **Always verify** hash hits.
> ☐ **Suffix Tree:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ construction (Ukkonen), $\mathcal{O}(m + \text{occ})$ per query.
> ☐ **Suffix Array:** $SA[i]$ = position of $i$-th lexicographically smallest suffix. $\mathcal{O}(n)$ construction, $\mathcal{O}(m \log n)$ binary search.
>
> **Bioinformatics Connections**
> ☐ BLAST uses **exact $w$-mer seeds** (pattern matching) followed by gapped extension.
> ☐ Restriction enzymes (EcoRI "GAATTC"), TATA box, splice sites "GT…AG" are all exact patterns.
> ☐ Modern aligners (BWA, Bowtie) use **Burrows–Wheeler Transform + FM-index**, which is built on **suffix array** pattern matching.
> ☐ Suffix trees are used for finding the **longest common substring** (MUMmer genome alignment) and the **shortest absent word** (genome assembly QC).
>
> **Common Traps**
> ☐ Hash equality ≠ string equality — Rabin–Karp requires verification.
> ☐ KMP's $\pi[0] = 0$ always.
> ☐ Suffix trees need a `$` sentinel.
> ☐ Boyer–Moore's shift is the **maximum** of two rules, not the sum.
> ☐ Naive complexity is $n \cdot m$, not $n + m$ — do not confuse.
> ☐ State indexing convention (0 vs 1) in the first line of every answer.
>
> **One-Line Takeaway**
> ☐ *"Pattern matching is the algorithmic seed of every bioinformatics search: naive search wastes information, KMP remembers it, BM skips ahead, Rabin–Karp fingerprints, and suffix trees index the universe of all possible searches."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Arrays
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Arrays

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

An **Array** is a fundamental, contiguous, homogeneous, linear data structure that stores a fixed-size sequential collection of elements of the **same data type** in adjacent memory locations. Each element is uniquely identified by an integer index, with indexing typically starting at `0` (in C/C++/Java/Python) and ending at `n-1`, where `n` is the array size.

In the context of **Fundamentals of Bioinformatics (PEADT414)**, arrays are the foundational storage primitive for biological sequence data, such as:
- **DNA sequences** (A, T, G, C nucleotides)
- **RNA sequences** (A, U, G, C)
- **Protein sequences** (20 standard amino acids)
- **Microarray gene expression intensity values**
- **Position-Specific Scoring Matrices (PSSM)**

$$A = \{a_0, a_1, a_2, \dots, a_{n-1}\}, \quad a_i \in \Sigma$$

where $\Sigma$ is the alphabet (e.g., $\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$ for DNA) and $a_i$ denotes the element at index $i$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Arrays in bioinformatics are not just storage containers; they form the **computational backbone** for string-based sequence alignment, scoring matrices, and k-mer indexing algorithms. Mastering arrays is mandatory before progressing to dynamic programming alignments (Needleman–Wunsch, Smith–Waterman).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of an **array as a row of numbered lockers** in a school corridor.

- Each **locker (cell)** holds one item of the same kind (e.g., one test tube).
- Every locker has a **unique number (index)** painted on its door, starting from 0.
- The lockers are **physically placed one beside the other (contiguous)** — so if you know locker #5 and the size of each locker, you can walk directly to locker #8 without searching.
- You **cannot insert a new locker in the middle** without shifting all the subsequent ones (this is the famous *O(n)* insertion cost).

Now translate this to bioinformatics:
- Imagine a DNA sequence `ATGCCTGA` stored in a character array. Locker `0` = 'A', Locker `1` = 'T', …, Locker `7` = 'A'.
- To fetch the base at position `i`, the computer performs one arithmetic operation: `address = base_address + (i × element_size)`. This is why array access is **O(1)** — instant, like knowing exactly which locker to open.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Notation | Use Case in Bioinformatics |
|---|---|---|
| **Time complexity of access** | **O(1)** | Random nucleotide retrieval |
| **Time complexity of search** | **O(n)** | Linear pattern scan in sequence |
| **Space complexity** | **O(n)** | Storage of $n$ residues |
| **Standard DNA alphabet** | **$\Sigma = \{A, C, G, T\}$, $\vert \Sigma \vert = 4$** | DNA sequence arrays |
| **Standard Protein alphabet** | **$\Sigma_{aa}$, $\vert \Sigma_{aa} \vert = 20$** | Amino acid arrays |
| **Memory per char (C)** | **1 byte = 8 bits** | Sequence storage estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "homogeneous"?** All elements must be of the same type because the computer pre-calculates the memory offset between consecutive elements. If elements had variable sizes (heterogeneous), this offset would not be constant, and random access would break.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Arrays (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

Arrays are broadly classified along three orthogonal axes:

1. **By Dimensionality**
   - **One-Dimensional Array (1-D / Linear / Vector)**: A simple line of elements. *Bioinformatics use:* storing a raw DNA sequence.
   - **Two-Dimensional Array (2-D / Matrix)**: A grid of rows × columns. *Bioinformatics use:* **PAM/BLOSUM substitution matrices**, **dot plots** for sequence comparison.
   - **Multi-Dimensional Array (n-D)**: Generalization to n axes. *Bioinformatics use:* **microarray gene expression data** (genes × samples × time points).

2. **By Memory Allocation Strategy**
   - **Static Array**: Size fixed at compile-time. Stored in the **stack** segment. Fast allocation, no flexibility.
   - **Dynamic Array**: Size resizable at runtime (e.g., `std::vector` in C++, `list` in Python). Stored in the **heap** segment. Slower allocation but flexible.

3. **By Access Pattern**
   - **Dense Array**: All positions utilized. Used for short sequence reads.
   - **Sparse Array / Sparse Matrix**: Most positions are zero. Used for **k-mer frequency tables** when $\vert \Sigma \vert^k$ is huge but actual observed k-mers are few.

---

## 1.5 Core Operations on Arrays

For a sequence $A$ of length $n$, the following primitive operations form the building blocks of every downstream bioinformatics algorithm:

| Operation | Description | Time Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| **Traversal** | Visit every element $a_i$ in order | **O(n)** |
| **Insertion** | Add element at position $i$, shifting $a_i \dots a_{n-1}$ right | **O(n)** worst case |
| **Deletion** | Remove element at position $i$, shifting $a_{i+1} \dots a_{n-1}$ left | **O(n)** worst case |
| **Search (Linear)** | Scan for a value or pattern | **O(n)** |
| **Access (Random)** | Retrieve $a_i$ via index | **O(1)** |
| **Update** | Modify $a_i \leftarrow v$ | **O(1)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory Layout & Address Calculation (The "Why" Behind O(1) Access)

The single most important theoretical property of an array is that it occupies a **contiguous block of physical memory**. When the array is declared, the system reserves $n \times s$ bytes, where $n$ is the number of elements and $s$ is the size (in bytes) of one element.

### 2.1.1 The Address Formula

For a 1-D array `A` of base address $B$, element size $s$ (in bytes), and index $i$:

$$\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + (i \times s)$$

For a 2-D array stored in **row-major order** (C, C++, Java, Python default), with $R$ rows, $C$ columns, and element size $s$:

$$\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + ((i \times C) + j) \times s$$

For **column-major order** (Fortran, MATLAB, R):

$$\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + ((j \times R) + i) \times s$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why is this O(1)?** Because computing the address is just **one multiplication and one addition** — regardless of whether $i = 0$ or $i = 1{,}000{,}000$. There is no traversal, no loop, no search. This is the foundation of array's unbeatable access speed, and it is *exactly* why BLAST, sequence hashing, and PSSM lookups rely on arrays.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Structured Logic of Array Operations

### Step 1: Declaration (Memory Reservation)
- The compiler/interpreter reserves $n \times s$ contiguous bytes.
- For a Python list (dynamic array of references), each element is actually an 8-byte pointer; for a `numpy` array of `int8`, each element is 1 byte.

### Step 2: Indexing (Direct Address Computation)
- The base address $B$ is stored in a register.
- For index $i$, the hardware computes $B + i \cdot s$ in a single cycle.
- No bounds check in low-level C (hence buffer-overflow vulnerabilities); bounds-checked in Python/Java.

### Step 3: Insertion / Deletion (Cascade Shift)
- Insertion at index $i$ requires shifting $n - i$ elements one position right.
- Deletion at index $i$ requires shifting $n - i - 1$ elements one position left.
- This **shift cost** is what makes arrays *unsuitable* for applications with heavy middle-end insertions, where **linked lists** or **balanced trees** are preferred.

### Step 4: Resizing (Dynamic Arrays)
- When a dynamic array is full, a new array of size $2n$ is allocated, all elements are copied, and the old array is freed. This is the **amortized O(1)** trick (geometric growth series).

---

> The following table is a **rapid-revision compendium** of every array-related formula, equation, and rule a PEADT414 student must memorize. **All $\vert \cdot \vert$ are absolute-value bars written as `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.**

| 11 | Dynamic array amortized insert | $T_{amort} = O(1)$ | time units | `vector` / `list.append` |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Address Computation for a 1-D Array

**Problem Setup:** Consider a C array declaration `char dna[1000];` storing a DNA sequence. The base address assigned by the compiler is $B = 1000$ (in bytes). The size of `char` is $s = 1$ byte. Find the memory address of the nucleotide at index $i = 437$.

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**

We use the general 1-D address formula:

$$\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + (i \times s)$$

Substituting the numerical values $B = 1000$, $i = 437$, $s = 1$:

$$\text{Addr}(A[437]) = 1000 + (437 \times 1)$$

Carrying out the multiplication:

$$\text{Addr}(A[437]) = 1000 + 437$$

Performing the addition:

$$\text{Addr}(A[437]) = 1437 \text{ bytes}$$

**Conclusion:** The nucleotide at index 437 is stored exactly at byte 1437, just **one CPU cycle** away from the base address.

> **Conversion Logic:** Because each `char` is exactly 1 byte, multiplying by 1 is a no-op. For a 64-bit integer array where $s = 8$, the same index would point to byte $1000 + 437 \times 8 = 4496$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Address Computation for a 2-D Row-Major Array (Substitution Matrix)

**Problem Setup:** A BLOSUM62 substitution matrix is declared in C as `int blosum[24][24];`. The base address is $B = 2000$ bytes, and `int` size is $s = 4$ bytes. Compute the address of `blosum[10][15]`, which stores the score for a particular amino acid pair.

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**

The row-major formula is:

$$\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + ((i \times C) + j) \times s$$

where $C$ is the total number of columns. Here $C = 24$, $R = 24$, $i = 10$, $j = 15$, $s = 4$:

$$\text{Addr}(A[10][15]) = 2000 + ((10 \times 24) + 15) \times 4$$

Evaluating the inner parenthesis:

$$(10 \times 24) + 15 = 240 + 15 = 255$$

Multiplying by $s = 4$:

$$255 \times 4 = 1020$$

Adding the base offset:

$$\text{Addr}(A[10][15]) = 2000 + 1020 = 3020 \text{ bytes}$$

**Conclusion:** The score `blosum[10][15]` is located at byte 3020. This is the **exact mechanism** by which BLAST retrieves substitution scores in O(1) time.

> **Conversion Logic:** The expression $i \times C$ skips over $i$ complete rows. Each row has $C$ elements, each of size $s$ bytes. Then $+j$ moves into the current row by $j$ elements.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Memory Footprint of a Microarray

**Problem Setup:** A gene-expression microarray measures expression of $G = 20{,}000$ genes across $S = 50$ samples. Each intensity is a 32-bit float ($s = 4$ bytes). Compute total memory in MB.

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**

Total number of cells in the 2-D array:

$$N = G \times S = 20{,}000 \times 50$$

$$N = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ cells}$$

Total memory in bytes:

$$M = N \times s = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 4 = 4{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

Converting to megabytes (1 MB = $2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576$ bytes):

$$M_{MB} = \frac{4{,}000{,}000}{1{,}048{,}576} \approx 3.815 \text{ MB}$$

**Conclusion:** A single microarray experiment with 20,000 genes × 50 samples consumes about **3.82 MB** — easily stored in an array, and an order of magnitude smaller than the modern **scRNA-seq** matrices (which can be **GBs**, hence the need for **sparse array** representations).

> **Conversion Logic:** Always multiply $G \times S \times s$ first, then divide by $1024^2$ for binary megabytes (or $10^6$ for decimal MB). The KTU convention assumes binary (MiB ≈ MB).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Amortized Cost of Dynamic Array Append

**Problem Setup:** A dynamic array starts at capacity $n_0 = 1$ and doubles capacity whenever full. Prove that the amortized cost of a single `append` operation is $O(1)$.

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**

Let $C_n$ be the total number of element-copy operations performed during the first $n$ appends. Copy events occur only when the array is resized, at powers of two: $n = 1, 2, 4, 8, \dots, 2^k$.

At resize event $2^k$, the previous array of size $2^{k-1}$ must be copied. So the cost at event $2^k$ is $2^{k-1}$ copy operations.

Summing copy costs from $k = 1$ to $k = m$ where $n = 2^m$:

$$C_n = \sum_{k=1}^{m} 2^{k-1} = 1 + 2 + 4 + \dots + 2^{m-1}$$

Using the geometric series identity:

$$C_n = 2^m - 1 = n - 1$$

Amortized cost per operation:

$$T_{amort} = \frac{C_n + n}{n} = \frac{(n - 1) + n}{n} = \frac{2n - 1}{n}$$

Taking the limit as $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} T_{amort} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2n - 1}{n} = 2 = O(1)$$

**Conclusion:** Each `append` costs at most a small constant on average, even though occasional resizes are expensive.

> **Conversion Logic:** The sum collapses because the geometric series $1 + 2 + 4 + \dots + 2^{m-1}$ is always just one less than the next power of two. This is the *single most important* reason Python lists and C++ `std::vector` feel "fast" despite occasional resizes.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Information-Theoretic Encoding Length of a Sequence

**Problem Setup:** An array of $L$ nucleotides uses how many bits, assuming a uniform DNA alphabet? How many bits if we instead store an amino acid sequence with alphabet size 20?

**Step-by-Step Logical Deduction:**

For uniform alphabet of size $\vert \Sigma \vert$, the minimum bits per symbol is:

$$b = \log_2 \vert \Sigma \vert$$

For DNA, $\vert \Sigma \vert = 4$:

$$b_{DNA} = \log_2 4 = 2 \text{ bits/nucleotide}$$

For amino acids, $\vert \Sigma \vert = 20$:

$$b_{AA} = \log_2 20 \approx 4.3219 \text{ bits/amino acid}$$

Total bits for a sequence of length $L$:

$$B = L \times b$$

For a 3-billion bp human genome stored as 2-bit packed DNA:

$$B_{genome} = 3 \times 10^9 \times 2 = 6 \times 10^9 \text{ bits} = 750 \text{ MB}$$

**Conclusion:** The human genome fits in roughly **750 MB** when 2-bit packed — a critical result that justifies storing entire genomes in RAM (in-memory array operations) for modern aligners like Bowtie and BWA.

> **Conversion Logic:** Each symbol needs $\lceil \log_2 \vert \Sigma \vert \rceil$ bits in a *practical* packed representation, but the information-theoretic minimum is exactly $\log_2 \vert \Sigma \vert$ bits. The packed representation can be implemented using **bit-arrays** (e.g., a C++ `std::bitset`), a special array variant.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Operation Complexity vs. Bioinformatics Use

| Operation | Time | Space | Bioinformatics Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Random access $A[i]$ | **O(1)** | O(1) | Retrieve base at SNP position |
| Linear scan | **O(n)** | O(1) | Count GC content of a sequence |
| Insert at position $i$ | **O(n)** | O(1) | Introduce gap in a sequence |
| Delete at position $i$ | **O(n)** | O(1) | Remove adapter from read |
| 2-D access $M[i][j]$ | **O(1)** | O(1) | Look up BLOSUM score |
| Build reverse complement | **O(n)** | O(n) | Prepare antisense strand |
| Find motif (naive) | **O(n·m)** | O(1) | Brute-force pattern match |
| Find motif (KMP/Z-alg) | **O(n+m)** | O(n) | Efficient pattern match |

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Insertion Cascade (Why Insertion Is O(n))

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0["Step 0:\nA T G C T A\nIndices 0 1 2 3 4 5"]:::step
    S1["Step 1:\nInsert 'X' at index 2\nShift A[2..5] right by 1"]:::step
    S2["Step 2:\nA T X G C T A\nIndices 0 1 2 3 4 5 6"]:::step

    S0 ==> S1 ==> S2

    S0 --- N0["Position 2: G"]:::hl
    S1 --- N1["Positions 2-5 shift right"]:::hl

```

**Interpretation:** To insert a single element at position 2, four elements (positions 2, 3, 4, 5) had to be shifted. For an array of length $n$, inserting at the worst position (index 0) requires shifting all $n$ elements — hence $O(n)$.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define an array. List any two advantages and one limitation of arrays when used for storing biological sequences.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **array** is a linear, contiguous, homogeneous data structure that stores a fixed-size collection of elements of the same data type, where each element is identified by an integer index.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Random access in O(1) time** — A specific nucleotide at position $i$ can be retrieved using the address formula $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i \cdot s$ in a single step. This is exploited by BLAST and BLOSUM lookup.
2. **Cache-friendly contiguous layout** — Sequential traversal of a sequence (e.g., for GC-content calculation) maximizes CPU cache hits, making array scans faster than linked-list scans.

**One limitation:**
1. **Fixed size at compile time (for static arrays)** — Storing variable-length sequencing reads (e.g., 100 bp vs. 300 bp Illumina reads) wastes memory or requires padding. Dynamic arrays solve this but introduce amortized resize cost.

> **Mark split:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for two advantages, 1 mark for one limitation.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**What is the time complexity of (i) accessing an element in a 1-D array, (ii) linear search in an unsorted array, and (iii) inserting an element at the beginning of a static array of size n? Justify each in one line.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Operation | Complexity | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Access $A[i]$ | **O(1)** | Address computed directly as $B + i \cdot s$, no traversal needed |
| (ii) Linear search | **O(n)** | Worst case requires scanning all $n$ elements to find the target |
| (iii) Insertion at the beginning | **O(n)** | All $n$ existing elements must be shifted one position to the right |

> **Mark split:** 1 mark per correct sub-part with justification.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the memory representation of a 1-D and a 2-D array with suitable diagrams. Derive the address calculation formula for both. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain how arrays are used to represent a biological sequence and a substitution matrix. Compute the memory required to store a BLOSUM62 matrix (24×24 integers) and a DNA sequence of 10 million base pairs. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

##### Part (a) — 1-D and 2-D Array Memory Representation (7 marks)

**1-D Array Representation:**

A 1-D array $A$ of $n$ elements is stored in a single contiguous block of memory of size $n \cdot s$ bytes, where $s$ is the per-element size. The starting address is the base address $B$.

```
Address:    B       B+s     B+2s    ...     B+(n-1)s
Index:       0       1       2      ...       n-1
Content:    A[0]    A[1]    A[2]    ...    A[n-1]
```

The address of element $A[i]$ is:

$$\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + (i \times s) \quad \text{for } 0 \leq i < n$$

> (1 mark for diagram, 1 mark for formula)

**2-D Array Representation (Row-Major):**

A 2-D array $A[R][C]$ is also stored in a single contiguous block, but logically visualized as a grid of $R$ rows × $C$ columns. The C/C++/Java convention (row-major) lays out the matrix row by row in memory:

```
Address:   B    B+s   B+2s ... B+(C-1)s   B+Cs   B+(C+1)s ...
            ----- Row 0 (A[0][*]) -----   ----- Row 1 (A[1][*]) -----
Index:      0      1     2    ...   C-1       0       1
Content:  A[0][0] A[0][1] ...    A[0][C-1]  A[1][0] A[1][1] ...
```

The address of $A[i][j]$ in row-major order is:

$$\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + ((i \times C) + j) \times s$$

For column-major order (used by Fortran, MATLAB, R):

$$\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + ((j \times R) + i) \times s$$

> (2 marks for row-major diagram, 1 mark for row-major formula, 1 mark for column-major formula, 1 mark for noting the order convention difference)

---

##### Part (b) — Arrays in Bioinformatics + Memory Computation (7 marks)

**Biological Sequence in a 1-D Array:**

A DNA sequence `S = "ATGCCTGA"` of length $n = 8$ is stored in a character array of size 8:

| Index `i` | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 | 6 | 7 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `S[i]` | A | T | G | C | C | T | G | A |

To fetch the base at position $i$ (e.g., $i = 3$), only one memory access is needed: $S[3] = $ 'C'.

**Substitution Matrix in a 2-D Array:**

A BLOSUM62 sub-matrix for amino acids $\{A, R, N, D\}$ is a 4×4 grid where `M[i][j]` is the log-odds score of substituting amino acid $i$ with amino acid $j$. For example:

$$M[2][3] = \text{score of substituting } N \rightarrow D$$

In BLAST, this lookup happens millions of times per alignment and must be O(1) — exactly the strength of a 2-D array.

> (2 marks for biological sequence explanation, 2 marks for substitution matrix explanation)

**Memory Computation:**

**BLOSUM62 matrix:** $R = 24$, $C = 24$, $s = 4$ bytes (assuming 32-bit int):

$$M_{BLOSUM} = R \times C \times s = 24 \times 24 \times 4$$

$$M_{BLOSUM} = 576 \times 4 = 2304 \text{ bytes} \approx 2.25 \text{ KB}$$

**DNA sequence:** $L = 10{,}000{,}000$ bp, $s = 1$ byte (char):

$$M_{DNA} = L \times s = 10{,}000{,}000 \times 1 = 10{,}000{,}000 \text{ bytes}$$

$$M_{DNA} = \frac{10{,}000{,}000}{1024^2} \approx 9.54 \text{ MB}$$

> (1.5 marks for BLOSUM computation, 1.5 marks for DNA computation)

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Differentiate between static and dynamic arrays. Explain with a diagram how dynamic array resizing works and prove that the amortized cost of `append` is O(1). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of arrays in storing (i) DNA sequences, (ii) protein sequences, and (iii) microarray gene-expression data. For each, specify the dimensionality, element data type, and a typical size. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

##### Part (a) — Static vs. Dynamic Arrays + Amortized Append (7 marks)

**Static vs. Dynamic Arrays — Comparison Table:**

| Property | Static Array | Dynamic Array |
|---|---|---|
| Size | Fixed at compile time | Resizable at runtime |
| Memory location | Stack | Heap |
| Allocation cost | O(1) (single allocation) | Amortized O(1) (with resizes) |
| Resizing | Not possible without recompilation | Possible via `realloc`, `vector::push_back`, `list.append` |
| Examples in code | `int arr[100];` (C) | `std::vector<int> v;` (C++), `list = []` (Python) |
| Memory overhead | Minimal (just the array) | Extra capacity slots + occasional copy |
| Use in bioinformatics | Fixed-length k-mers, codon tables | Variable-length reads, growing alignment tables |

> (3 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for noting the location difference)

**Dynamic Array Resizing — Diagram:**

```
Initial:  [ _ _ _ ]              capacity = 3, size = 0
After 3 appends: [A T G]         capacity = 3, size = 3 (FULL)
Resize:   [A T G _ _ _ _]        new capacity = 6, copy 3 elements
After 6 appends: [A T G C T G]   capacity = 6, size = 6 (FULL)
Resize:   [A T G C T G _ _ _ _ _ _]   new capacity = 12, copy 6 elements
```

The growth factor is typically 2 (geometric), though Python uses ~1.125 for some lists and Java's `ArrayList` uses 1.5.

> (1 mark for the diagram)

**Amortized Cost Proof:**

Total cost of $n$ appends, where resize events occur at sizes $1, 2, 4, \dots, 2^k$ for $n = 2^k$:

$$C_{total} = n + (1 + 2 + 4 + \dots + 2^{k-1}) = n + (2^k - 1) = n + (n - 1) = 2n - 1$$

Amortized cost per append:

$$T_{amort} = \frac{C_{total}}{n} = \frac{2n - 1}{n} < 2$$

$$T_{amort} = O(1)$$

> (2 marks for the proof)

---

##### Part (b) — Arrays in DNA, Protein, and Microarray Storage (7 marks)

| Biological Data Type | Dimensionality | Element Data Type | Typical Size | Why Array? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| (i) DNA sequence | **1-D** | `char` (or 2-bit packed) | $10^3$ – $3 \times 10^9$ bases | Random base access in O(1), cache-friendly scanning |
| (ii) Protein sequence | **1-D** | `char` (20-letter alphabet) | $10^2$ – $10^4$ residues | Index-based amino acid lookup; basis for alignment DP |
| (iii) Microarray expression | **2-D** | `float` (4 bytes) | $20{,}000$ genes × 50 samples = $10^6$ cells | Simultaneous O(1) lookup of gene-sample intensity pairs |

> (2 marks per row × 3 rows = 6 marks; 1 mark for the introductory framing paragraph)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing row-major and column-major order:** C/C++/Java use row-major, while MATLAB/R use column-major. Address formulas differ — examiners specifically test this.
> 2. **Forgetting the element size `s` in address calculation:** Many students write $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i$, omitting $s$. This loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up amortized vs. worst-case cost of dynamic array append:** Worst case is $O(n)$ (during resize), but **amortized** is $O(1)$. Examiners look for the word "amortized."
> 4. **Forgetting to specify units** in memory calculations (bytes vs. KB vs. MB). Always state the unit.
> 5. **Writing $O(1)$ for array search:** Only random *access* is O(1); *search* (finding a value) is $O(n)$ in an unsorted array.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Pin this section to your revision wall the night before the exam.**

- [x] An **array** is a **contiguous, homogeneous, fixed-size** collection indexed by integers.
- [x] **Address formula (1-D):** $\text{Addr}(A[i]) = B + i \cdot s$
- [x] **Address formula (2-D, row-major):** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (i \cdot C + j) \cdot s$
- [x] **Address formula (2-D, col-major):** $\text{Addr}(A[i][j]) = B + (j \cdot R + i) \cdot s$
- [x] **Random access** is **O(1)**; this is the array's *superpower*.
- [x] **Linear search** is **O(n)**; **insertion/deletion at middle** is **O(n)**.
- [x] **Static arrays** live on the **stack**; **dynamic arrays** live on the **heap**.
- [x] **Dynamic array amortized append** is **O(1)** via geometric growth (e.g., ×2).
- [x] **DNA alphabet** $\vert \Sigma \vert = 4$; **Protein alphabet** $\vert \Sigma \vert = 20$.
- [x] **Bits per symbol:** $b = \log_2 \vert \Sigma \vert$ (theoretical minimum).
- [x] **In bioinformatics, arrays are used for:** sequences (1-D), substitution matrices (2-D), microarray data (2-D), atomic coordinates (3-D).
- [x] **Sparse arrays** save memory when $\rho = \frac{nnz}{R \cdot C}$ is very small (e.g., k-mer tables).
- [x] **Reverse complement** of sequence $A$ is $A'[i] = \text{complement}(A[n-1-i])$, computed in **O(n)** using an array.
- [x] **Always** state units (bytes / KB / MB) in memory calculations and complexity classes in time/space analyses.

> **Final KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** When asked "explain how arrays are used in bioinformatics," always structure your answer in **three layers** — (1) **what is stored** (sequence/matrix/tensor), (2) **how it is laid out** (1-D / 2-D / n-D, row vs. column-major), and (3) **why an array is the right choice** (O(1) access, cache locality, simple address arithmetic). Examiners award full marks only when all three layers are explicitly addressed.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lists and Hashes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Lists and Hashes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Lists (Arrays) — Formal Definition In **Perl** — the dominant scripting language of legacy and production bioinformatics pipelines (e.g., **Bioperl**, **Ensembl API**, **NCBI legacy scripts**) — a **list** is an **ordered collection of scalar values** accessed by a contiguous integer index. Once stored in a named variable, it is called an **array** (`@array_name`). Internally, Perl arrays are dynamically-sized, **sparse-friendly** structures backed by an AV (Array Value) body and a pointer table. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the context of PEADT414, lists are used to store biological sequences (DNA/RNA/protein strings), parsed FASTA records, lists of gene IDs, BLAST hit scores, and exon coordinates from **GFF/GTF** files. **Formal Properties of a Perl List/Array:** - **Indexing:** Integer-based, **0-based** (first element has index 0, last is `$#arr`). - **Order:** Strict insertion order is **preserved**. - **Heterogeneity:** A list may contain scalars, numbers, strings, and references (mixed). - **Auto-vivification:** Array grows/shrinks on demand; no pre-allocation required. - **Symbol sigil:** `@` for whole array, `$` for individual element (e.g., `$seq[2]`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** A Perl array is like a **test-tube rack in a molecular biology lab**. Each slot has a fixed numeric position (slot 0, slot 1, slot 2, ...), and you can drop a different sample (a DNA string, a p-value, a coordinate) into any slot. The rack remembers the order you placed the tubes in, and you can grab any tube instantly if you know its slot number. ### 1.2 Hashes (Associative Arrays) — Formal Definition A **Perl hash** is an **unordered collection of scalar values indexed by unique string keys** rather than integers. It is a direct implementation of a **hash table** data structure with **O(1) average-case** lookup complexity. Hashes are the workhorse of bioinformatics for **fast lookups** (e.g., codon-to-amino-acid translation, gene-ID-to-sequence mapping). **Formal Properties of a Perl Hash:** - **Key Type:** Always **stringified** (numbers are converted to strings internally). - **Key Uniqueness:** Each key is unique; re-assignment overwrites the value. - **Order:** **No guaranteed order** of iteration (use `sort` on keys if order required). - **Access:** `$hash{$key}` retrieves value; `${$hash{$key}}` is illegal — must use intermediate scalar. - **Symbol sigil:** `%` for whole hash, `$` for individual element (e.g., `$gc{$gene_id}`). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):** Think of a hash like a **lab notebook with labeled sticky notes** — instead of locating a sample by its slot number (as in arrays), you locate it by its **label** (e.g., the gene name "BRCA1" or the codon "ATG"). You stick a new note (key→value) anywhere in the notebook, and when you need it back, you call out the label and instantly get the data. The notebook doesn't care about the order of notes — only the labels matter. ### 1.3 Why Lists and Hashes Matter in Bioinformatics | Data Structure | Bioinformatics Use-Case | Performance Need | |---|---|---| | **List `@seqs`** | Storing reads from a FASTQ file | Sequential iteration | | **List `@orfs`** | Open Reading Frames from a genome scan | Indexed access | | **Hash `%codon`** | Codon → Amino Acid translation table | O(1) lookup | | **Hash `%gc_content`** | Gene-ID → GC% mapping | O(1) lookup | | **Hash of Arrays (HoA)** | Gene → list of exon coordinates | Compound lookup | > [!NOTE] > KTU students should remember: **~70% of Bioperl-style scripts** are essentially a loop over an array combined with a hash-based lookup table. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 List (Array) Construction Syntax $$\text{List} = \begin{cases} (\text{scalar}_1,\ \text{scalar}_2,\ \ldots,\ \text{scalar}_n) & \text{Standard tuple notation} \\ \text{qw/word1 word2 word3/} & \text{Quote-word shortcut} \\ [\text{ref}_1,\ \text{ref}_2,\ \ldots] & \text{Anonymous array reference} \end{cases}$$ - **`qw()`** — splits a string on whitespace, **no interpolation**, no commas needed. Highly used in bioinformatics for FASTA header words, taxonomy tokens. - **Anonymous array `[]`** — returns a **scalar reference** to a new array; essential for nested data structures (e.g., list of gene records). ### 2.2 List Core Operations — Functional & Complexity Analysis $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{indexed access}} &= O(1) \\ T_{\text{push/pop}} &= O(1)\ \text{amortized} \\ T_{\text{shift/unshift}} &= O(n) \\ T_{\text{splice (mid)}} &= O(n) \\ T_{\text{grep/map/sort}} &= O(n \log n)\ \text{(sort dominates)} \\ T_{\text{reverse}} &= O(n) \\ T_{\text{join/split}} &= O(n) \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why `shift/unshift` is O(n):** Removing/inserting at the head requires Perl to **reindex every remaining element**, making it the **most expensive** list operation. In **streaming FASTQ parsing**, always use a **while loop with ``** rather than building and unshifting a list. ### 2.3 Hash Construction Syntax $$\text{Hash} = \begin{cases} \%h = (\text{key}_1 \Rightarrow \text{val}_1,\ \text{key}_2 \Rightarrow \text{val}_2,\ \ldots) & \text{Standard} \\ \%h = (\text{key}_1,\text{val}_1,\ \text{key}_2,\text{val}_2,\ \ldots) & \text{Flat key,val list} \\ \text{my \%h;} & \text{Empty hash declaration} \end{cases}$$ - The **fat comma `=>`** is identical to a comma but **auto-quotes** the left operand — a major readability win for biological key names like `'chromosome_1' => 'ATGC...'`. - Hash entries are stored in **insertion order internally in modern Perl ≥ 5.18** (deterministic iteration), but **never rely on this for logic** — use `sort(keys %h)` explicitly. ### 2.4 Hash Core Operations & Complexity $$\begin{aligned} T_{\text{lookup}} &= O(1)\ \text{average,}\ O(n)\ \text{worst-case (collision)} \\ T_{\text{insert}} &= O(1)\ \text{average} \\ T_{\text{delete}} &= O(1)\ \text{average} \\ T_{\text{keys/values}} &= O(n) \\ T_{\text{exists}} &= O(1) \\ T_{\text{each}} &= O(1)\ \text{per call,}\ O(n)\ \text{full traversal} \end{aligned}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Hash Function Behind Perl:** Perl uses **Bob Jenkins' lookup3 / SDBM / FNV-1a** (version-dependent). The hash slot index is computed as: > $$\text{slot} = \text{hash}(\text{key string}) \bmod \text{bucket\_count}$$ > When load factor exceeds threshold, **rehashing** occurs — this is why **iterating a hash with `each()` during insertion** is undefined behavior. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | Construct | Syntax | Time Complexity | Bioinformatics Application | |---|---|---|---| | Array declaration | `@seq = ();` | O(1) | Initialize empty sequence list | | Hash declaration | `%map = ();` | O(1) | Initialize codon table | | `qw//` | `qw/A T G C/` | O(n) build | Nucleotide alphabet | | `$a[$i]` | Index access | **O(1)** | Get base at position $i | | `$a[-1]` | Negative index | O(1) | Last base of sequence | | `@a[1..3]` | Array slice | O(k) | Sub-sequence extraction | | `push @a, $x` | Append | O(1) amortized | Add new read | | `pop @a` | Remove last | O(1) | Stack pop | | `shift @a` | Remove first | **O(n)** | Avoid in hot loops | | `splice @a, $i, 1` | Remove at $i | O(n) | Exon removal in simulation | | `grep { /ATG/ } @a` | Filter | O(n) | Find ORFs | | `map { tr/ATGC/UACG/ } @a` | Transform | O(n) | Reverse complement | | `sort @a` | Sort | O(n log n) | Sort by E-value | | `reverse @a` | Reverse | O(n) | Invert coordinate list | | `$h{$k}` | Hash lookup | **O(1)** avg | Translate codon | | `keys %h` | All keys | O(n) | List all gene IDs | | `values %h` | All values | O(n) | All GC contents | | `exists $h{$k}` | Key check | O(1) | Validate gene ID | | `delete $h{$k}` | Remove entry | O(1) avg | Remove obsolete record | | `each %h` | Iterator | O(1) per call | Stream processing | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** In genome annotation pipelines (e.g., **GFF → BED** converters), an array of hashes (`@genes`) is the standard representation: each hash holds `chr`, `start`, `end`, `strand`, `attributes` for one feature. ### 2.6 Slice Operations — A Common KTU Exam Trap $$\begin{aligned} @a[1,3,5] &\rightarrow \text{array slice} \rightarrow \text{returns LIST} \\ \$a[1,3,5] &\rightarrow \text{last element ONLY (scalar context trap)} \\ @a[1..3] &\rightarrow \text{range slice} \rightarrow \text{returns 3 elements} \\ @h{'a','b'} &\rightarrow \text{hash slice} \rightarrow \text{returns LIST of values} \\ \$h{'a'} &\rightarrow \text{single value (scalar)} \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > The **scalar-context slice trap** is the #1 reason KTU students lose marks. `@a[1,3,5]` in **list context** returns three values; in **scalar context** it returns the **last** value only. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Analytical Matrix — List vs Hash Decision Framework To **derive** when to use a List vs a Hash, evaluate the **access pattern** of the data: | Question | If YES → Use | If NO → Use | |---|---|---| | Do you access by **integer position**? | **List** | Hash | | Do you access by **string label** (gene ID, codon)? | **Hash** | List | | Does the **order of insertion matter**? | **List** | Hash | | Do you have **duplicate keys**? | **List of pairs** | Hash (keys must be unique) | | Is the dataset **>1 M records**? | **Hash** for O(1) | List is O(n) search | | Are you building a **lookup table**? | **Hash** | List | | Are you doing **sequential iteration only**? | **List** (lower memory) | Hash | ### 3.2 Worked Example — Codon-to-Amino-Acid Translation **Problem:** Build a hash that translates the codon `ATG` to `Methionine (M)` and translate the DNA sequence `ATGGCATAA`. **Step 1: Declare the codon table hash using fat comma.** $$\%codon = (\text{'ATG'} \Rightarrow \text{'M'},\ \text{'TGG'} \Rightarrow \text{'W'},\ \text{'CAT'} \Rightarrow \text{'H'},\ \text{'TAA'} \Rightarrow \text{'*'})$$ **Step 2: Iterate the DNA string in steps of 3 (codon-sized frames).** $$\begin{aligned} \$dna &= \text{'ATGGCATAA'} \\ \$len &= \text{length}(\$dna) = 9 \\ \text{frames} &= \{(0,3),\ (3,6),\ (6,9)\} \\ \text{codons} &= \{\text{'ATG'},\ \text{'TGG'},\ \text{'CAT'},\ \text{'TAA'}\} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: For each codon, perform a hash lookup.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{protein} &= \text{''} \\ \text{for}\ i &= 0;\ i < \$len;\ i += 3\ \{ \\ \text{codon} &= \text{substr}(\$dna,\ i,\ 3) \\ \text{protein} &= \text{protein} \cdot \$codon\{\$codon\} \\ \} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Trace the computation.** $$\begin{aligned} i=0 &: \text{codon} = \text{substr}(\text{'ATGGCATAA'},\ 0,\ 3) = \text{'ATG'} \\ &\phantom{:}\ \$codon\{\text{'ATG'}\} = \text{'M'} \Rightarrow \text{protein} = \text{'M'} \\ i=3 &: \text{codon} = \text{substr}(\text{'ATGGCATAA'},\ 3,\ 3) = \text{'TGG'} \\ &\phantom{:}\ \$codon\{\text{'TGG'}\} = \text{'W'} \Rightarrow \text{protein} = \text{'MW'} \\ i=6 &: \text{codon} = \text{substr}(\text{'ATGGCATAA'},\ 6,\ 3) = \text{'CAT'} \\ &\phantom{:}\ \$codon\{\text{'CAT'}\} = \text{'H'} \Rightarrow \text{protein} = \text{'MWH'} \\ i=9 &: \text{STOP} \rightarrow \text{codon} = \text{'TAA'} \\ &\phantom{:}\ \$codon\{\text{'TAA'}\} = \text{'*'} \Rightarrow \text{protein} = \text{'MWH*'} \end{aligned}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Final Result:** `ATGGCATAA` → protein string `MWH*` (M = Met, W = Trp, H = His, * = STOP). ### 3.3 Worked Example — List Operations on Gene Coordinates **Problem:** A list of exon coordinates from a GFF file is given as `@exons = (100, 250, 500, 800)`. Compute: (a) the number of exons, (b) the length of each exon, (c) the cumulative span. **Step 1: Count the elements.** $$\text{scalar}(@exons) = 4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n_{\text{exons}} = 4$$ **Step 2: Iterate in pairs to compute per-exon length.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{Exon 1}: & \quad [100,\ 250) \Rightarrow 250 - 100 = 150\ \text{bp} \\ \text{Exon 2}: & \quad [250,\ 500) \Rightarrow 500 - 250 = 250\ \text{bp} \\ \text{Exon 3}: & \quad [500,\ 800) \Rightarrow 800 - 500 = 300\ \text{bp} \\ \text{Total}: & \quad 150 + 250 + 300 = 700\ \text{bp} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Express as a loop pseudocode.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{for}\ (\$i = 0;\ \$i < \text{scalar}(@exons);\ \$i += 2)\ \{ \\ \$len &= \$exons[\$i+1] - \$exons[\$i] \\ \text{push}\ @lengths,\ \$len \\ \} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4: Final state.** $$\begin{aligned} @exons &= (100,\ 250,\ 500,\ 800) \\ @lengths &= (150,\ 250,\ 300) \\ \text{Total gene span} &= \$exons[-1] - \$exons[0] = 800 - 100 = 700\ \text{bp} \end{aligned}$$ ### 3.4 Hash Slicing Derivation — Multi-Gene GC% Lookup **Problem:** Compute the GC content of three genes at once using a hash that stores `gene_id → sequence`. **Step 1: Define the hash.** $$\%seq = (\text{'BRCA1'} \Rightarrow \text{'ATGCGCATTA'},\ \text{'TP53'} \Rightarrow \text{'GGGGCCATAA'},\ \text{'EGFR'} \Rightarrow \text{'ATATGCGCGC'})$$ **Step 2: Slice the hash to retrieve three sequences at once.** $$\begin{aligned} @selected &= @seq\{\text{'BRCA1'},\ \text{'TP53'},\ \text{'EGFR'}\} \\ &= (\text{'ATGCGCATTA'},\ \text{'GGGGCCATAA'},\ \text{'ATATGCGCGC'}) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Apply GC% formula to each element.** $$\text{GC\%} = \frac{\text{count}(\text{G}) + \text{count}(\text{C})}{\text{total length}} \times 100$$ **Step 4: Numerical evaluation.** $$\begin{aligned} \text{BRCA1} &: \text{GC count} = 4\ (\text{C})\ +\ 1\ (\text{G}) = 5,\ \text{len} = 10 \Rightarrow \frac{5}{10} \times 100 = 50.0\% \\ \text{TP53} &: \text{GC count} = 5\ (\text{G})\ +\ 2\ (\text{C}) = 7,\ \text{len} = 10 \Rightarrow \frac{7}{10} \times 100 = 70.0\% \\ \text{EGFR} &: \text{GC count} = 3\ (\text{G})\ +\ 3\ (\text{C}) = 6,\ \text{len} = 10 \Rightarrow \frac{6}{10} \times 10 = 60.0\% \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{@gc = (50.0,\ 70.0,\ 60.0)}$$ ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Array (List) Memory Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A0["@bases slot 0
A"] --> A1["@bases slot 1
T"] --> A2["@bases slot 2
G"] --> A3["@bases slot 3
C"] --> A4["@bases slot 4
T"] --> ASIZE["#bases = 5"] IDX0["$bases[0]"] -.-> A0 IDX1["$bases[1]"] -.-> A1 IDXN["$bases[-1]"] -.-> A4 SLICE["@bases[1..3]"] -.-> A1 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Hash Bucket Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB KEY1["key ATG"] --> HASH1["hash function
lookup3"] KEY2["key TGG"] --> HASH2["hash function
lookup3"] KEY3["key TAA"] --> HASH3["hash function
lookup3"] KEY4["key CAT"] --> HASH4["hash function
lookup3"] HASH1 --> SLOT0["bucket 0
ATG to M"] HASH2 --> SLOT1["bucket 1
TGG to W"] HASH3 --> SLOT2["bucket 2
TAA to STOP"] HASH4 --> SLOT0 ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Functional Data Flow: Codon Translation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD INPUT["Input DNA String
ATGGCATAA"] --> LOOP["Iterate i in 0 3 6"] LOOP --> SUB["substr dna i 3
codon extraction"] SUB --> LOOKUP["hash lookup
codon table"] LOOKUP --> AA["Amino Acid M W H STAR"] AA --> CONCAT["Concatenate to
protein string"] CONCAT --> OUTPUT["Output protein
MWH STAR"] CODON["hash codon
64 entries"] -.-> LOOKUP ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block — Decision Tree: List vs Hash ```mermaid flowchart TD START["Need to store data"] --> Q1{"Access by
integer position?"} Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"Access by
string key?"} Q1 -- NO --> USEHASH["Use HASH"] Q2 -- YES --> Q3{"Order
important?"} Q2 -- NO --> USELIST["Use LIST"] Q3 -- YES --> HOAL["Hash of Arrays
HoA"] Q3 -- NO --> HASHONLY["Use HASH"] Q1 -- SOMETIMES --> AOH["Array of Hashes
AoH"] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]** **Differentiate between a Perl list and a Perl hash with respect to indexing, order preservation, and access complexity.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | List / Array | Hash | |---|---|---| | Indexing | Integer (0-based) | String key (always stringified) | | Order | Insertion order preserved | No guaranteed order | | Access Complexity | O(1) by position | O(1) average, O(n) worst case | | Sigil | `@` for whole, `$` for element | `%` for whole, `$` for element | | Key Type | Auto-integers | Any scalar (stringified) | | Use in Bioinformatics | Sequence storage, FASTQ reads | Codon table, gene-ID lookup | (Each row carries 0.5 marks. Total = 3 marks. Award full marks for any 3 distinguishing features with examples.) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted)]** **What is the output of the following Perl snippet? Justify your answer.** `my @a = qw/ATG TGG CAT TAA/; print scalar(@a[1,3]);` **Model Answer (3 marks):** - `@a[1,3]` is a **list slice** containing two elements: `('TGG', 'TAA')`. - When forced into **scalar context** (as required by `print scalar(...)`), Perl returns the **last element of the slice** (a documented edge case for slices in scalar context — actually it returns the count of elements in scalar context, which is 2). $$\text{scalar}((@a[1,3])) = 2 \quad \text{(number of elements in the slice)}$$ > **Correction note for KTU evaluators:** `scalar(@a[1,3])` returns **2** (the slice length), NOT `'TAA'`. The *array element* in scalar context returns its value, but a *slice* in scalar context returns the **count**. KTU students frequently write `TAA` here — this is a 1-mark penalty. **Output:** `2` **Justification (1 mark):** Slices in scalar context return the number of elements. The fact that `TAA` is a stop codon is a red herring. --- ### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) **Important KTU Note:** Module 4 of PEADT414 (as per the 2024 scheme ESE template) contains **TWO sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students answer **EITHER** Question A OR Question B in full (14 marks), not both. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU Model Paper 2024 – Q4(a) Adapted]` **(a)** Explain in detail the various ways to **construct a Perl array (list)** with at least three distinct syntaxes. Provide a **biologically meaningful example** for each. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the **time complexity** of major array operations — `push`, `pop`, `shift`, `unshift`, `splice`, and `sort` — and justify with a brief **bioinformatics scenario** where choosing the wrong operation causes a pipeline slowdown. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **(i) Parenthesized comma-separated list — 2.5 marks** $$\text{my}\ @nucleotides = (\text{'A'},\ \text{'T'},\ \text{'G'},\ \text{'C'})$$ - Most explicit, supports interpolation of variables. - Use case: Building a list of variant IDs from a VCF file: `my @variants = ('rs1234', 'rs5678', 'rs9012');` **(ii) `qw()` quote-word construct — 2.5 marks** $$\text{my}\ @amino = \text{qw/Ala Val Leu Ile Pro/}$$ - No commas, no quoting needed, whitespace-delimited. - **No interpolation** (variables inside `qw()` are literal text). - Use case: Hard-coding the genetic code codons: `my @stop_codons = qw/TAA TAG TGA/;` **(iii) Anonymous array reference — 2 marks** $$\text{my}\ \$gene\_struct = [\text{'BRCA1'},\ 17,\ 41197695,\ 41276113]$$ - Returns a **scalar reference** to a new array. - Use case: Nested data — array of gene records where each record is itself an array reference. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** | Operation | Time Complexity | Why | Bioinformatics Scenario | |---|---|---|---| | `push @a, $x` | O(1) amortized | Adds to end; AV body is pre-allocated | **Ideal** for reading FASTQ records into memory one by one | | `pop @a` | O(1) | Removes from end; no reindexing | Stack-based parsing of RNA secondary structure | | `shift @a` | **O(n)** | Removes from head; **reindexes all elements** | **Avoid** in pipelines with millions of reads — use a while-loop with `<>` instead | | `unshift @a, $x` | **O(n)** | Inserts at head; reindexes all | **Avoid** in hot loops; build a normal list and `reverse` it once at the end | | `splice @a, $i, 1` | O(n) | Worst case O(n) when removing near the start | Exon removal simulation in alternative splicing studies | | `sort @a` | O(n log n) | Comparison-based sort | Sorting BLAST hits by E-value before reporting | **Justification — 2 marks:** If a student is building a list of 10 million FASTQ reads and uses `shift` in a loop to process one at a time, the total cost is: $$T_{\text{total}} = \sum_{k=1}^{10^7} k \approx 5 \times 10^{13}\ \text{operations}$$ This is **catastrophic** for a production pipeline. The correct pattern is: $$\text{while}\ (\text{my}\ \$read = ) \rightarrow \text{process in streaming fashion, O(1) per read}$$ $$\boxed{T_{\text{total}} = O(n)\ \text{instead of}\ O(n^2)}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU Model Paper 2024 – Q4(b) Adapted]` **(a)** Explain the **construction of a Perl hash** with three different syntaxes. Build a **codon-to-amino-acid lookup table** as a worked example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **time complexity of hash operations** — lookup, insert, delete, `keys`, `values` — and explain how **hash-based codon lookup is faster than array-based search** for genome-scale translation. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** **(i) Fat-comma construction — 2.5 marks** $$\begin{aligned} \%codon &= ( \\ &\text{'ATG'} \Rightarrow \text{'Met(M)'},\ \\ &\text{'TGG'} \Rightarrow \text{'Trp(W)'},\ \\ &\text{'CAT'} \Rightarrow \text{'His(H)'},\ \\ &\text{'TAA'} \Rightarrow \text{'STOP'},\ \\ &\text{'TAG'} \Rightarrow \text{'STOP'},\ \\ &\text{'TGA'} \Rightarrow \text{'STOP'} \\ &); \end{aligned}$$ - `=>` auto-quotes the left side, visually clean. - KTU students must show **at least 3 codons** for full marks. **(ii) Flat key,value list — 2.5 marks** $$\%codon = (\text{'ATG'},\ \text{'Met'},\ \text{'TGG'},\ \text{'Trp'},\ \text{'CAT'},\ \text{'His'})$$ - Alternating key,value pairs. - Use case: Loading a translation table from a flat-file via `split`. **(iii) Incremental assignment — 2 marks** $$\begin{aligned} \text{my}\ \%codon; \\ \$codon\{\text{'ATG'}\} = \text{'Met'}; \\ \$codon\{\text{'TGG'}\} = \text{'Trp'}; \\ \$codon\{\text{'CAT'}\} = \text{'His'}; \end{aligned}$$ - Best when populating dynamically — e.g., parsing NCBI's `genetic_code` file. - Use case: Building a hash from a `while ()` loop reading a TSV codon table. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** | Operation | Complexity | Reason | |---|---|---| | `$h{$k}` (lookup) | **O(1) average** | Hash function maps key directly to bucket | | `$h{$k} = $v` (insert) | O(1) average | Amortized; may trigger rehash on load factor | | `delete $h{$k}` | O(1) average | Direct bucket manipulation | | `keys %h` | **O(n)** | Must walk all buckets | | `values %h` | **O(n)** | Same as keys | | `exists $h{$k}` | O(1) | Bucket probe, no return | **Numerical justification — 3 marks:** Suppose we translate a chromosome of length $L = 250 \times 10^6$ bases (human chr1). Number of codons: $n = L/3 \approx 8.33 \times 10^7$. **Array-based linear search (no hash):** $$T_{\text{array}} = n \times c = 8.33 \times 10^7 \times 64\ \text{comparisons} = 5.33 \times 10^9\ \text{ops}$$ **Hash-based lookup:** $$T_{\text{hash}} = n \times 1 = 8.33 \times 10^7\ \text{ops}$$ $$\text{Speedup} = \frac{T_{\text{array}}}{T_{\text{hash}}} = 64 \times$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing `$#arr` (last index) with `scalar(@arr)` (count).** These differ by 1. Award 0 if both are written as "array length." > 2. **Forgetting that hash keys are always strings.** A student who writes `$h{42}` and expects integer key optimization is **wrong** — Perl auto-stringifies to `$h{"42"}`. > 3. **Using `each %h` while modifying the hash** is undefined behavior — will not be penalized in theory, but in code questions, deduct 1 mark. > 4. **Confusing `%hash` (whole hash) with `@hash` (whole hash as array of pairs).** Deduct 1 mark. > 5. **Writing hash slice as `@hash{$k}` (correct)** vs **`@$hash{$k}` (dereferenced slice, also correct)** — accept both. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **List = ordered, integer-indexed** (0-based). Hash = **unordered, string-keyed**. - **Sigil rule:** `@` for whole array, `%` for whole hash, `$` for both individual array element **and** individual hash value (context-dependent). - **Array access:** `$arr[$i]` — single scalar. `@arr[1,3,5]` — list slice. `scalar(@arr[1,3,5])` returns **count = 3**, not the last element. - **Hash access:** `$h{$key}` — single value. `@h{$k1, $k2}` — hash slice returning a list of values. - **Time complexity rules of thumb:** - Array index access = O(1). Array search = O(n). - Hash lookup/insert/delete = O(1) average. Hash full traversal = O(n). - `shift`/`unshift` = O(n) — **avoid in hot loops** (streaming FASTQ parsing). - **`qw()`** does **no interpolation**; comma list **does interpolate**. - **Hash keys are always stringified** — never integers. - **`exists` checks the key**, not the truthiness of the value. Use `defined` for the latter. - **Bioinformatics use-case pairings (high-frequency KTU topic):** - Array → FASTQ/FASTA records, ORF list, BLAST hit list, exon coordinates. - Hash → Codon table, gene-ID → sequence map, taxonomy ID → name, restriction enzyme → cut site. - **HoA (Hash of Arrays)** → Gene → list of exons. - **AoH (Array of Hashes)** → List of GFF feature records. - **Default value of uninitialized array element** = `undef`. Default value of uninitialized hash value = `undef`. - **Sorting hashes:** Always use `sort { $a cmp $b } keys %h` — never assume insertion order across Perl versions. - **Memory:** A hash with 1 M keys occupies roughly **40–60 MB** in modern Perl (16 bytes/key + bucket overhead). Plan accordingly for genome-scale projects. - **List context vs scalar context** is the **#1 source of subtle bugs** — `localtime()` in list context returns 9 elements, in scalar context returns the formatted date string.

topics.title: File Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEADT414 - FUNDAMENTALS OF BIOINFORMATICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** File Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**File Handling** in the context of bioinformatics refers to the systematic, programmatic creation, opening, reading, writing, appending, and closing of data files that store biological information. In the KTU 2024 syllabus for *PEADT414 – Fundamentals of Bioinformatics*, file handling is treated as the bridge between raw biological databases (FASTA, GenBank, FASTQ, PDB, GFF, etc.) and computational analysis pipelines written in high-level languages (predominantly **Python**).

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, a student must be able to:
- Open and close files safely using appropriate **file modes**.
- Read biological sequence and annotation files line-by-line, record-by-record, or as a whole stream.
- Write processed biological results back to disk in standard or custom formats.
- Handle exceptions (`FileNotFoundError`, `IOError`, `PermissionError`) gracefully.
- Use **context managers** (`with` statement) to ensure resource cleanup.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Wording):**
> "File handling is the process of interacting with secondary storage devices through a programming language to persistently read, modify, or store biological data in structured formats such as FASTA, GenBank, or FASTQ."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **biobank** that stores millions of patient DNA samples in a giant refrigerated vault. A researcher cannot "teleport" the entire vault onto her workbench; instead, she:
1. **Opens** a specific drawer (open the file).
2. **Takes out** only the sample she needs (read the file).
3. Performs an experiment (analyze the sequence).
4. **Files a report** back into the cabinet (write the file).
5. **Closes and locks** the drawer to preserve integrity (close the file).

File handling is the **digital equivalent** of this workflow. The biological data is the "sample," the file on disk is the "drawer," and your Python script is the "researcher."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in Bioinformatics:**
> A single human genome sequence file (FASTQ, paired-end, 30x coverage) is approximately **120 GB**. You **cannot** load the entire file into RAM at once. File handling techniques (streaming, chunked reading, indexed access) are therefore not just convenient — they are **computationally mandatory**.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in Bioinformatics File Handling

| Metric / Constant | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Average FASTA record size** | ~1 KB to 10 KB | Line-based record delimiter is `>` |
| **Human genome (FASTA)** | ~3.2 GB | Cannot be loaded into RAM naively |
| **FASTQ read record size** | ~200–500 bytes | 4 lines per record (header, seq, +, qual) |
| **PDB file row length** | 80 characters (legacy) / up to 199 (modern) | Fixed-width format |
| **GenBank flat-file header** | Starts with `LOCUS` keyword | Followed by **mandatory 12 fields** |
| **Newline character (universal)** | `\n` (LF, **0x0A**) or `\r\n` (CRLF, **0x0D 0x0A**) | OS-dependent |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of File Handling

File handling in any language is built on three foundational pillars. Understanding these pillars is **mandatory** for KTU 2024 evaluations.

#### Pillar 1: The File Object (Handle)
- A file object is a **wrapper** around an OS-level file descriptor.
- It is created by calling the built-in `open()` function.
- It maintains an **internal pointer** (the file cursor) that tracks the current read/write position.
- The pointer starts at byte offset **0** for read mode and is **truncated to 0** for write mode.

#### Pillar 2: The File Mode String
The `open()` function accepts a mode string. The KTU 2024 board frequently tests the combinations below.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Default mode in Python is `'r'` (read text mode).** Forgetting to specify mode is one of the most common errors in KTU answer sheets.

#### Pillar 3: The Operation Methods
- **Read operations**: `read()`, `readline()`, `readlines()`, iteration.
- **Write operations**: `write()`, `writelines()`, `print()` with `file=` argument.
- **Pointer operations**: `seek(offset, whence)`, `tell()`.
- **Lifecycle operations**: `close()`, `flush()`.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why open a file?** Because the OS kernel manages persistent storage as files, and the user-space program needs a controlled channel to access them.
- **Why specify a mode?** To declare intent — opening a 3 GB reference genome in `'w'` mode will **silently overwrite** the data, which is catastrophic in production pipelines.
- **Why close a file?** To flush the in-memory write buffer to disk and release the OS file descriptor. Forgetting `close()` can lead to data loss when the script terminates abruptly.
- **Why use `with`?** It guarantees that `__exit__()` is called, which calls `close()` — even if an exception occurs midway.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax | Return Type | Use Case in Bioinformatics |
|---|---|---|---|
| Open file | `f = open(path, mode)` | `<class '_io.TextIOWrapper'>` | Generic |
| Read all | `data = f.read()` | `str` (text) / `bytes` (binary) | Small reference sequences |
| Read N chars | `data = f.read(n)` | `str` | Chunked FASTQ reading |
| Read one line | `line = f.readline()` | `str` (with `\n`) | FASTA header parsing |
| Read all lines | `lines = f.readlines()` | `list[str]` | Whole-file annotation |
| Write string | `f.write("ACGT")` | `int` (chars written) | Saving aligned sequences |
| Write lines | `f.writelines(list_of_str)` | `None` | Batch GenBank export |
| Move pointer | `f.seek(offset)` | `int` (new position) | Random access in BAM index |
| Get position | `pos = f.tell()` | `int` | Debugging |
| Check closed | `f.closed` | `bool` | Defensive coding |
| Safe open | `with open(path, mode) as f:` | context manager | **KTU-recommended pattern** |

### 2.4 Complete File Mode Matrix

| Mode | Read | Write | Create | Truncate | Pointer | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `'r'` (default) | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Start | Reading FASTA |
| `'w'` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Start | Saving new results |
| `'a'` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | End | Appending to log file |
| `'x'` | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | Start | Exclusive create (fail if exists) |
| `'r+'` | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | Start | In-place editing |
| `'w+'` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | Start | Truncate then read/write |
| `'a+'` | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | End | Append and verify |
| `'rb'` | ✅ (bytes) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Start | Reading `.pdb`, `.bam` |
| `'wb'` | ❌ | ✅ (bytes) | ✅ | ✅ | Start | Writing binary indices |
| `'rt'` | ✅ (str) | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | Start | Default text reading |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** When a question mentions "raw binary data such as PDB coordinates or BAM alignments," expect the answer to use `'rb'` or `'wb'`. When it mentions "FASTA sequence processing," expect `'r'` and `'w'`.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering & Science

- **Clinical Genomics Pipelines**: Tools like **BWA**, **SAMtools**, and **GATK** open large BAM/CRAM files in binary streaming mode to perform variant calling on terabyte-scale datasets.
- **Pharmaceutical Drug Discovery**: PDB files (3D protein structures) are parsed by **Biopython**'s `PDBParser` to compute RMSD values during molecular docking simulations.
- **Agricultural Biotechnology**: FASTA files containing crop genome assemblies (e.g., Oryza sativa) are read line-by-line to build **k-mer indices** for marker-assisted selection.
- **Environmental Metagenomics**: FASTQ files from soil or ocean microbiome samples (often **hundreds of GB**) are processed using chunked file reads to perform **taxonomic classification** using Kraken2.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: From File Path to Closed File (Lifecycle)

The complete file-handling lifecycle can be modeled as a 7-stage state machine. Every KTU programming question can be decomposed into these stages.

| Stage | Action | State Variable | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Acquire path | `path: str` | `path = "ref/human.fasta"` |
| 2 | Open with mode | `f: TextIOWrapper` | `f = open(path, 'r')` |
| 3 | Read header | `header: str` | `header = f.readline()` |
| 4 | Read body | `seq: str` | `seq = ''.join(f.readlines())` |
| 5 | Process | `result: str` | `gc = (seq.count('G') + seq.count('C')) / len(seq)` |
| 6 | Write output | `bytes_written: int` | `f_out.write(f"{header}\t{gc:.4f}\n")` |
| 7 | Close | `f.closed → True` | `f.close()` |

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Reading a FASTA File Record-by-Record
